parts.label: All chapters
parts.tagline: Generated from build_cd_expandables.py on the complete dentures build script.

blocks.text: An accurate diagnostic cast is the substrate for everything downstream. The tray on which you take the final impression sits on this cast, so this cast determines tray contour, spacer extent, and ultimately the border of the finished denture.

items: **Stock edentulous trays** — metal or plastic; multiple sizes. Try in dry first.
**Alginate** + **rubber bowl** + **flat spatula**. uses Jeltrate or Identic.
**Water** at room temperature (or slightly cool to slow set if you need more working time).
**Cheek retractor**, mouth mirror, gauze.
**Sticky wax / utility wax** for tray extensions and tongue-space block-out.
**Alginate spray adhesive** — sprayed inside tray before loading (prevents alginate detachment on removal).
Confirm patient is not actively gagging today; have nitrous available if anxious.
Try multiple tray sizes dry; pick one that covers ridge + extends 2–3 mm into vestibule on all sides.
Extend short trays with utility wax built up on the borders.
Pre-spray tray with alginate adhesive; let dry ~30 sec.
Patient seated upright, head against headrest, bib in place.
**Mix water + powder** per manufacturer ratio. Spatulate vigorously until smooth + drip-free (no lumps, no air bubbles).
**Load the tray** from posterior to anterior.
Retract the lip with the cheek retractor (or your finger).
**Seat tray posterior-first, then rotate anterior into place**. Head slightly forward.
Have patient say "ah" gently while material is still loose — engages the soft palate to capture the vibrating-line area.
**Hold steady until set.** Verify by touching residue in bowl — should snap cleanly without sticking or stringing.
**Remove with a quick, even pull** straight down. Don't rock.
Inspect immediately: voids, rugae captured, borders rounded, hamular notches present bilaterally.
Tray covers residual ridge to retromolar pads bilaterally. Verify retromolar pad coverage by trying in dry.
**Plan for the tongue space:** either fill with sticky wax / utility wax before loading, or load alginate into the tongue space immediately after removing the tray (less common, harder to control).
Mix + load alginate same as maxillary.
Patient lifts the tongue.
**Seat tray**: posterior first, then rotate anterior.
Patient slowly protrudes and retrudes the tongue once or twice during set — engages genioglossus for floor-of-mouth capture.
**Hold until set**; remove evenly.
Inspect: retromolar pad bilaterally, lingual sulcus extension, no tears.
**Pour within 15 minutes** of taking the impression — alginate dehydrates and shrinks.
Type III stone (microstone) for diagnostic casts.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Patient gags:** raise chair, redirect breathing through nose, position patient slightly forward, work fast. Apply topical anesthetic spray to the soft palate if gagging persists. **Alginate sets before tray seated:** over-spatulation — spatulate less and seat faster. **Borders thin or absent:** tray too small / not extended. Try a bigger tray or extend with utility wax.

blocks.text: The custom tray gives even, controlled space for the final impression material and lets you border-mold the periphery. Built on the diagnostic cast, single thickness Triad over a wax spacer, with selective relief.

items: **Diagnostic cast** from your alginate impression.
**Red and blue pencils** for outlines.
**Baseplate wax** — one sheet for the spacer; offcuts of wax for blocking undercuts; offcuts of Triad for handles / finger rests.
**Light-cured Triad** tray material — one sheet.
**Vaseline** — separator on cast (not after spacer placement).
**Light chamber** for cure, **acrylic burs** for finishing.
**Red pencil:** trace the tray border at the depth of the vestibule on buccal + labial. At posterior: vibrating line area (maxillary) or pear-shaped retromolar pad (mandibular).
**Blue pencil:** draw a line 2 mm INSIDE the red. This is where the tray border will sit, leaving 2 mm of vestibular space for border-molding material.
Posterior extension gets RED line only (no 2 mm blue offset there).
**Red marks for relief areas:** mid-palatal suture, incisive papilla, mandibular lingual tori, mylohyoid ridge, knife-edge ridge zones.
**Vaseline the entire cast.** Thin uniform film.
Drip wax with a #7 spatula to block out undercuts and relief areas. Smooth contour.
**Vaseline OVER the block-out wax** — this seals the wax to the cast and lets the spacer release later.
**One sheet of baseplate wax**. Warm slightly to soften (water bath or warm hands).
**Adapt it** to the cast surface — don't drip molten wax; press the warm sheet onto the cast.
Cut with a #11 scalpel along the blue line. Sharp edges trimmed.
**DO NOT Vaseline after this step.** The spacer must stay inside the tray through border molding — if you Vaseline now, the spacer will fall out at the chair.
**One sheet of light-cured Triad** tray material.
Press over the spacer + cast. Adapt to all surfaces, no air bubbles.
Trim excess with sharp scalpel along the red line.
**Maxillary:** vertical handle over the central incisor area. Should not impinge on lip closure.
**Mandibular:** finger rests in the bicuspid area, bilateral. No taller than **2/3 of the retromolar pad height** (impingement = tray won't seat in mouth).
Build from Triad offcuts. Press into the surface; light cure after addition.
**Light cure ~1 min** in the light chamber.
Wait for cooling.
Remove tray from cast carefully — lift off, don't pry.
**Cure again off the cast** with intaglio side up. Fully polymerizes the inner surface.
Acrylic burs (carbide) on slow speed.
Smooth all borders — no sharp edges.
**Try the tray on the cast**: should seat fully with the spacer retained inside.
Confirm frenal and muscle clearance on the cast.

blocks.title: What's on the Custom Tray sheet
blocks.body: Even thickness (single Triad sheet). Handle / finger rest functional, no interference with lip / cheek movement. Spacer retained inside the tray. Frenal + muscle clearance confirmed on cast. Borders smooth, no sharp edges, no air voids. Tray inserts unobstructed on the cast.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Spacer falls out:** you Vaseline'd after step 3. Remake spacer; don't apply more Vaseline. **Triad doesn't adapt cleanly:** not warmed enough; or air pocket. Re-warm, re-adapt. **Tray won't release from cast:** insufficient block-out wax in undercuts. Remake. **Handle too tall — tray won't seat in mouth:** reduce with acrylic bur until lip closes naturally. **Borders sharp on tongue side:** over-trimmed. Smooth more, or add a thin layer of Triad and re-cure.

blocks.text: Border molding records the periphery of the denture-bearing area under function. Each region of the border is shaped by a different muscle complex; each requires a specific movement to record accurately.

items: **Custom tray** (instructor-approved 1–2 days ahead).
**Green-stick compound** (modeling plastic) — melts at 122–124 °F. Trimmable, additive.
**Hanau torch** + lighter / matches for flame.
**Warm-water bath** at ~135 °F for tempering.
**Wax knife or Bunsen-heated knife** for trimming between regions.
**Thompson stick** (indelible) for marking the vibrating line.
**Cheek retractors**, mouth mirror.
Trim back the buccal/labial border of the tray with acrylic bur to ~2–3 mm short of the depth of the vestibule (the blue-line offset from custom tray fab).
Dry the tray completely — compound won't stick to a wet tray.
Heat compound stick in Hanau torch flame, rotating constantly. Once it bends, remove from flame.
Add compound to the target border region of the tray in a rolling motion.
**Temper:** dip the tray with the soft compound into the 135 °F water bath for a few seconds — outside is workable, bulk stays warm.
**Anterior labial flange.** Add compound to anterior labial. Insert tray. Patient lifts upper lip outward and down; operator massages cheek across the labial frenum.
**Buccal flange (canine to first molar).** Compound on buccal segment. Cheek stretched outward, down, forward.

**Distobuccal flange (coronoid challenge).** Compound on distobuccal. Patient opens wide + excursions to the working side. Coronoid process slides forward; impingement leaves curved indentation on molded compound.
**Distobuccal extension to hamular notch.** Patient opens wide. Capture the hamular notch — critical for posterior seal.

**Posterior border.** Mark vibrating line with Thompson stick (patient says "ah"). Confirm tray ends just anterior to the line. **Do not form PPS in green-stick** — that's done in PVS during the final wash.
**Contralateral side** — repeat all five regions.
**Anterior labial flange.** Patient lifts lower lip outward and up; operator massages cheek across the labial frenum (more active than maxillary).
**Buccal flange (canine to first molar).** Cheek stretched outward and up.
**Distobuccal flange (masseter / buccinator).** Patient closes against operator's finger on the tray; masseter + buccinator define posterior buccal extension.
**Distal extension.** Tray ends on the pear-shaped retromolar pad. Capture the entire pad, but don't extend onto the retromolar incline.
**Lingual flange (anterior, middle, posterior).** Patient protrudes the tongue forward; then licks L cheek + R cheek; then licks the contralateral palate (records retromylohyoid space).
**Contralateral side** — repeat.
Heat compound: ~10 sec in flame, rotating.
Add to tray: 5–10 sec rolling.
Temper in water bath: 2–3 sec.
Insert + patient movement: ~15 sec.
Set in mouth: ~30 sec (compound cools and hardens).
Inspect + trim before next region: 30 sec.
Total per region: ~1.5–2 min. Six regions per arch = 10–15 min per arch.
Borders are **symmetric** left to right.
Borders are **even in thickness** anterior to posterior.
Surface is **smooth, dull, no shiny spots** (shiny = unmolded).
**No tray show-through** anywhere along the border.
Anterior borders are slightly thinner than posterior (lip vs. cheek differential).

blocks.title: Salari's verdict
blocks.body: *"Sharp borders are underextended borders."* If the green-stick comes to a thin sharp edge, you didn't reach the depth of the vestibule. Add more compound and remold.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Compound won't stick to tray:** tray wet. Dry completely; reapply. **Compound is too soft when patient moves:** didn't temper enough. Hot compound deforms instead of recording. Dip longer in 135°F water bath. **Compound is brittle / cracks during movement:** over-cooled. Re-warm briefly in flame. **Patient gags on maxillary posterior:** trim back the posterior of the tray ~2 mm before re-trying. Avoid impinging the soft palate during border molding. **Asymmetric borders L vs R:** patient's movements were uneven (e.g., one cheek pulled harder than the other). Redo the deficient side. **Tray showed through compound in a region:** needs more compound there. Add + re-mold that region.

blocks.text: Once the border is molded in green-stick, the inner surface of the tray takes a wash of PVS to capture the residual ridge and supporting tissues in detail. Selective-pressure philosophy: pressure on primary stress-bearers, relief over secondary and relief areas (via the spacer-created intaglio shape).

items: **Border-molded custom tray** (just completed in step above).

**PVS light or medium body** per Salari's review (the deck has both "most viscous heavy" and "light/medium" references — practical reading: light/medium for the wash when borders are already molded in green-stick).
**PVS dispenser gun** + cartridge + fresh mixing tip.
**Tray adhesive** appropriate for the PVS brand (brush + dappen dish; use new brushes each visit — no double-dipping).
**#8 round acrylic bur** for the rugae escape vent (maxillary).
Gauze for drying tissue + storing impression after.
Trim back excess green-stick from the *inside* of the border (a thin sliver) so the wash material has somewhere to flow. **Don't trim the outside** — that's your molded periphery.
**Remove the wax spacer.** The spacer is the most-forgotten step. Without removing it, the wash will be uniformly thick everywhere and the cast won't reproduce primary stress-bearing areas.
**Drill the rugae escape vent** (maxillary only): #8 round acrylic bur at the mid-palatal suture in the rugae area. Lets trapped air vent during seating — prevents palatal voids.
Apply tray adhesive to the intaglio. Let it dry per manufacturer (typically 60–90 sec).
Use a NEW brush from the drawer; **do NOT double-dip into the adhesive bottle** (infection control).

blocks.text: Step 2 — Prep the patient + load tray

items: Warn patient: PVS takes 6 full minutes to set. Have paper towel + suction ready.
**Dry the tissues** with gauze. PVS is hydrophobic — saliva pools become voids.
Mix PVS: pump pea-size discard, attach fresh mixing tip.
**Load a thin layer** (~2 mm) of light body into the tray intaglio.
Use a tongue depressor to roll the PVS from the inside of the tray UP and OVER the molded compound at the border (incorporates the wash into the border seal).
Have the patient hold cheek retractors.
Retract cheek + lip on one side; seat tray using the same path of insertion you used during border molding; retract opposite side.

**After ~30 seconds**, have the patient repeat all the border-molding movements (lip stretches, tongue movements for mandibular). This pushes wash material into the muscle-controlled periphery so it sets in functional position.
**Hold the tray stable** with cover-fingers spanning the entire border-molded material on both sides. **Don't grip the tray itself** — finger pressure deforms the wash.
**Wait for full set:** 6 minutes per standard PVS. Verify on the bowl.
Remove with a quick, even pull straight down.
**Sharp borders = underextended** (Salari).
**Thick rolled borders = overextended.**
Voids in the palate or on the ridge = retake.
Tray show-through anywhere on the border = retake.
Tray malposition (off-midline, canted) = retake.

blocks.title: Remake triggers (don't send to lab)
blocks.body: Tray malposition (off-midline, canted) · large void in any supporting area · incorrect border length (over or under) · wrong material consistency (too thin, too thick, double-dispensed) · patient movement during set · uneven mix (base/catalyst streaks).

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **PVS sets before you can repeat movements:** work faster on seating; or use a slower PVS variant. Aim to start movements within 30 sec of seating. **Voids in palate (maxillary):** rugae vent missing or too small. Drill a larger vent (still #8 bur). **Voids over ridge:** spacer didn't get removed before adhesive. Re-make. **Patient swallows during set:** tray shifts. Re-make. Next time, redirect breathing through nose + don't talk to patient during set. **Sharp anterior border:** tray was too short in vestibule; border molding compound didn't capture depth. Re-mold green-stick anterior + retake.

chapters.title: Boxing, pouring, record bases, wax rims

blocks.text: Between the final impression and Appointment 3 (jaw relation records), the lab does four things: boxes and pours the master cast, scribes the PPS, fabricates a record base, and builds wax rims to standard dimensions.

items: Mix equal parts pumice and plaster (~1.5 cup each) to a thick putty.
Spread the putty flat on the bench.
Press the impression into the putty so **3 mm of putty extends lateral to the borders** and **at least 2 mm of border is exposed in height** above the putty.
Adapt **boxing wax** around the periphery. Top of the boxing wax should be **at least 16 mm above the highest point** of the impression area — gives a cast base 15–20 mm thick.
Seal the boxing wax to the periphery of the land area with a heated #7 spatula. Water-test the seal for leaks.
Type III stone (microstone) at the correct W:P ratio.
Vacuum mixer to eliminate air bubbles.
Pour slowly along one edge, letting stone flow across the impression by gravity. Vibrate gently to settle.
Fill to the top of the boxing wax.
Let set fully (per manufacturer, usually 45 min).
Remove the boxing wax, soften green-stick / PVS in warm water, separate impression from cast carefully.
Trim the cast base parallel to the ridge. **Land area no more than 3 mm wide.**

blocks.text: Transfer the vibrating-line mark from the impression to the cast (or re-mark from anatomic landmarks). **#8 round bur** to form the posterior PPS terminus along the vibrating line. **Rescribe to a depth of 1–1.5 mm**, carving smoothly with a #7 spatula, transitioning from deeper posteriorly to shallower anteriorly. The anterior border of the PPS lies just anterior to the hard-palate / soft-palate attached-tissue junction (Valsalva line). This shape becomes a positive bead seal on the processed denture.

Lubricate the cast, block out major undercuts with wax, adapt Triad on the cast, light-cure on a rotating table at 400–500 nm for ~10 min. Finish smooth. The record base must be stable on the cast and stable in the mouth.

items: **Maxillary:** 22 mm anterior height, 4–6 mm posterior width. Occlusal surface **flat and even**. Facial contour canine to canine flat and meets the occlusal surface at a sharp angle.
**Mandibular:** 18 mm anterior height, 3–4 mm posterior width.
Line **8 mm anterior to the center of the incisive papilla** — outermost facial surface of the maxillary centrals.
Line across the **crest of the mandibular ridge** — mandibular tooth position.
**Two-thirds the height of the retromolar pad** — posterior occlusal-plane reference.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Boxing wax leaks during pour:** water-test seal before pouring. Heated #7 spatula to re-seal any gaps. **Stone has bubbles / voids in cast:** not using vacuum mixer, or W:P off. Re-pour from impression. **Impression won't separate from cast:** green-stick still hard. Soften in warm water bath; gently lift. **Cast chips at edges:** separated too early (before full set). Wait 45 min minimum. **PPS too deep / too shallow:** remeasure on patient; rescribe. **Record base rocks on cast:** undercut block-out wax was insufficient. Remove, block out more, re-adapt Triad.

chapters.title: Face-bow record on the edentulous patient

blocks.text: On the edentulous patient the face-bow uses the maxillary record base + wax rim as the engagement surface, since there are no teeth. The anterior reference is taken at the relaxed lip rather than a tooth incisal edge.

items: **Arbitrary ear-bow** for the Mark 320 (standard).
**Bite fork** matched to the face-bow.
**Hanau torch** + flame source — for softening wax rim surface.
**Sticky wax** as backup adhesion between fork and rim.
**Maxillary record base + wax rim** (contoured at the jaw-relation visit).
Mirror, marker (43 mm reference), ruler.
Verify bow is fully assembled, no missing knobs.
Patient seated upright, head firm against headrest, eyes on a fixed point straight ahead, shoulders relaxed.
Have maxillary record base + wax rim already verified in mouth (good fit, contoured).

blocks.text: Step 1 — Secure bite fork to maxillary wax rim

items: **Option A (preferred):** soften the maxillary wax rim's upper surface with the torch flame — brief pass, soften but don't melt. Press the bite fork's prongs into the warm wax. Chill with cool air to fix.
**Option B (backup):** sticky wax along the fork-rim interface.
Verify the fork is rigidly attached — should not rock or shift.
Fork stem should project anteriorly + slightly downward.

blocks.text: Step 2 — Position record base + bite fork in patient's mouth

items: Insert the maxillary record base with attached bite fork.
Patient stabilizes the assembly by light bite or cotton-roll support — mandibular teeth shouldn't be needed if edentulous, but you can have patient close lightly on cotton rolls behind the fork stem.
Position the ear-bow earpieces in the external auditory meatus.
**Both earpieces seated symmetrically** — asymmetric earpiece engagement is the #1 cause of canted maxillary mountings.
Eyeball from the front: bow appears level across the face.
Slide the transfer arm onto the bite fork's stem. Tighten the transfer-arm locking knob finger-tight.
**Anterior reference for edentulous: 43 mm above the relaxed lip.**
Measure with a ruler on the patient: from the lower border of the upper lip (relaxed), go up 43 mm. Mark with the marker.
Adjust the orbital pointer (or reference plate per your facebow's design) to touch this point.
The bow should now be roughly parallel to the Frankfort horizontal plane.
Tighten the top central knob.
Tighten each side knob.
Tighten transfer-arm knob if not already.
Loosen the bite fork from the patient's mouth — the rim + fork come together.
Remove the entire face-bow assembly as a unit. Don't touch any joint.
Transport to lab. Do NOT loosen any joint in transit.

blocks.title: Why accuracy matters
blocks.body: **5 mm of vertical deviation from the true hinge axis** produces **~0.2 mm of AP displacement at the second molar**. That's enough to mismount occlusion by a noticeable amount — you'll feel it as a high or low occlusal plane at try-in.

blocks.title: Preclinical lab shortcut
blocks.body: In the preclinical lab, students use a **face-bow transfer jig** rather than an actual face-bow, and an **interocclusal mounting jig** rather than a clinical CR record. This standardizes the mountings across the cohort. In clinic, the real face-bow comes back.

blocks.text: The Mark 320 is a Class III semi-adjustable arcon articulator. Only condylar inclination is adjustable; Bennett angle is fixed at 15°.

items: **Upper member** with the maxillary mounting plate.
**Lower member** with the mandibular mounting plate and the condylar housings.
**Condylar inclination thumbscrew** — adjusts 0–60°. default for tooth setup: 30°.
**Centric latch** — locks the articulator into terminal hinge for setup; loosened for excursion checks.
**Incisal pin and table** — sets vertical dimension and anterior guidance. Original white table for setup; black table is the customized incisal table built from the patient's protrusive record.
**Mark 320 articulator** with mounting plates.
**Mounting stone** (Type II plaster or fast-setting mounting plaster).
**Face-bow assembly** with maxillary record base + wax rim + bite fork, fresh from clinic.
**Mandibular record base + wax rim** with CR record (V-points indexed in 2 mm groove).
Sticky wax to stabilize casts on plates while stone sets.
Spatula for stone, rubber bowl, water.
Open the articulator. Verify centric latch is engaged + incisal pin reads 0.
Set **condylar inclination thumbscrew to 30°** (default for setup; refined later by protrusive record at try-in).
Bennett angle is fixed at 15° on the Mark 320 (no adjustment).
Use the WHITE incisal table at this stage (the black one is the customized incisal table built later from the protrusive record).
Mounting plates: attach upper + lower; cut retention grooves into the underside of each plate to grip the mounting stone.
Affix the face-bow assembly to the articulator per manufacturer procedure (slots / earpiece equivalents on the bow attach to corresponding receptacles on the articulator).
Once locked, the maxillary record base + bite fork is at the correct spatial position relative to the upper member.
Place the maxillary cast under the bite fork — the wax rim sits in the indentations of the fork.
Verify the cast is stable; if it rocks, faculty assist.
Stabilize cast against bite fork with sticky wax (small dots, not flowing onto wax rim contact areas).
Mix mounting stone to a creamy consistency (about 30 sec).
Apply stone to the underside of the upper mounting plate and the top of the maxillary cast.
Close the upper member down so the plate engages the stone.
Wait full set (~10–15 min for mounting stone).
Trim excess flush with the mounting plate edge.
With maxillary mounted, place the mandibular record base + wax rim in occlusion with the maxillary, indexed via the V-points + 2 mm groove (the CR record from the jaw-relation visit).
Verify: maxillary V-points seat cleanly into mandibular groove. No rocking.
**Flip the articulator** (or use a counter-balance / stabilization frame) so the mandibular plate faces up.
Apply mounting stone to underside of lower mounting plate + top of mandibular cast.
Close to engage. Wait full set.
Trim flush.
Flip back upright. Engage centric latch.
With centric latch engaged: maxillary + mandibular wax rims should occlude exactly as they did in the patient at the jaw-relation visit.
Incisal pin should rest on the incisal table at 0.
Loosen centric latch and articulate through excursions — should be smooth, no binding.
Cross-check against any face-bow remount jig (if one was made later).

blocks.title: Articulator setting reference
blocks.body: **Condylar inclination:** 30° default; refined by protrusive record at try-in. **Bennett:** 15° fixed (not adjustable on the Mark 320). **Incisal pin:** 0 mm at setup VDO.

blocks.text: Vertical Dimension of Occlusion is the inter-arch height when the teeth are in maximum intercuspation. For the edentulous patient, no MI exists — VDO must be reconstructed from non-occlusal cues. Standard teaching: **VDO = VDR − 2–3 mm**. Use multiple methods and cross-check.

blocks.title: Five methods (expand)
blocks.body:
**Method 1 — Physiologic rest position (VDR).** Patient sits upright, eyes forward, relaxed. Measure the inter-pupillary distance between two reference marks (one on the nose, one on the chin) with a Boley gauge. **Subtract 2–3 mm to get VDO.** Freeway space (interocclusal rest space) is normally 2–4 mm.

**Method 2 — Phonetics: the 60–70 count.** Patient counts from 60 to 70. The teeth (or wax rims) should **not touch** — this is the closest speaking space, normally ~1 mm. If they touch, VDO is too high; reduce.

**Method 3 — Esthetics.** Nasolabial angle 90–95° in men, 100–105° in women. Lip support natural; philtrum visible; no overstretched or collapsed appearance. Lip vermilion plump but not everted.

**Method 4 — Patient perception.** Ask: does this feel natural? Comfortable? Too tight? Too open? The patient's proprioception is often surprisingly accurate, especially if they wore a previous denture they liked. Use as a sanity check.

**Method 5 — Previous denture comparison.** If the patient had a previous denture they liked, measure its VDO with a Boley gauge as a reference. **Caveat:** if their previous denture was wrong (collapsed VDO from years of wear), repeating that error doesn't help. Cross-check against the other four methods.

blocks.title: What goes wrong at VDO determination
blocks.body: **Too high.** Patient feels "crammed," rims hit at 60–70 count, lips look stretched, philtrum loses depth. **Too low.** Patient looks aged, marionette lines deepen, drooling possible, eating efficiency drops, freeway space >4 mm. Both are caught at try-in or delivery — better caught here at the jaw-relation visit.

blocks.text: Centric Relation is the maxillomandibular relationship in which the condyles are in their most superior position in the glenoid fossae, against the posterior slope of the articular eminence. **CR still exists in the edentulous patient** — it's a joint position, not a tooth relationship. Standard practice: register CR with the bimanual chin technique.

items: **Regisil PB** cartridge (PVS bite registration) + dispenser gun + fresh mixing tip.
Maxillary record base + wax rim with **V-notches cut** (two per side: one horizontal, one diagonal).
Mandibular record base + wax rim with **2 mm-deep groove** cut to receive the V-points.
Cotton rolls for patient comfort during set.
Thompson stick for marking VDR reference points (if not already done).

blocks.text: Step 1 — Set up patient + practice the closure

items: Seat patient upright, head against headrest, eyes forward.
Verify VDO already established from wax rim contouring step.
Place hands: **all four fingers on the inferior border of the mandible (fingertips on BONE, not soft tissue)**; **thumbs lightly on the symphysis, touching at midline**.
Patient opens about 35 mm (about two-fingers-wide; less than maximal).
Patient relaxes the jaw.
**Soft upward lift on the inferior border** + **downward thumb force on the symphysis**. This seats the condyles in the fossae — terminal hinge.
**Do NOT shove the condyles back** — that's forced retrusion, not CR.
Close to first tooth contact (or first rim contact).
Open, repeat 3–5 times. Patient should relax into the closure on cue without active jaw movement.
Verify maxillary wax rim has V-notches: two per side, one horizontal + one diagonal, cut deep enough that PVS will lock into them.
Verify mandibular wax rim has the 2 mm groove.
Pump pea-size discard from Regisil PB cartridge.
Attach fresh mixing tip.
Apply Regisil PB **into the V-notches** on the maxillary rim (NOT onto the mandibular rim — the V-points are the registration features).
**Working time: ~45 sec** from start of dispensing.
Hands back in bimanual position (chin lift + thumb press).
Guide patient to close at the practiced rate.
**Stop closure when the rims touch lightly** — do NOT let patient bite past the recorded VDO. The mandibular rim should index into the Regisil-filled V-notches.
Patient holds while material sets.
**Set time: 2–3 minutes** for Regisil PB. Hold steady; any movement during set distorts.
After set, have patient open slowly.
Remove the maxillary rim with the registration in place.
**Inspect:** maxillary V-points should be cleanly indexed by the mandibular groove. PVS should show sharp negative impressions of the V-points, no smearing, no double impressions.
Verify the bite reg seats and releases cleanly on the mandibular rim.
If smeared, double-imaged, or partial → retake.

blocks.title: Forced retrusion vs. CR
blocks.body: If you push the condyles back instead of guiding upward into the fossae, you get a posterior position that isn't the true terminal hinge. At delivery, the patient bites past your record, the bases lift, and the prosthesis fails. **The lift is soft and upward, NOT hard and back.**

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Patient bites past the record:** VDO setting was off or patient closed too fast. Retake with slower guided closure. **Record is smeared / double:** patient moved during set or you released the bimanual grip. Retake; keep hands on chin throughout. **V-points didn't capture:** not enough Regisil in the notches, or wax rim still mobile. Re-cut deeper V-notches and retake. **Patient can't relax the jaw:** common at first. Have patient stick out tongue, swallow, then close — resets neuromuscular pattern. **Patient consistently bites in eccentric / off-midline:** repeat practice closures with stronger upward lift on the inferior border. Faculty assist if persistent.

blocks.text: Tooth selection happens at the jaw-relation visit, once you've contoured the wax rim and established the patient's facial proportions. uses **Portrait IPN** denture teeth for conventional CDs and **Trubyte Classic** for interim immediate dentures.

Based on facial form — Williams' classification (square, tapering, ovoid, square-tapering). The maxillary anterior 6 teeth should mirror the patient's facial shape: square face → square teeth; tapering face → tapering teeth.

Based on the **combined width of the maxillary anterior 6 teeth**. Measure the patient's intercommissural distance (corner of mouth to corner of mouth) — the maxillary anterior 6 should span approximately this distance from canine to canine. Use the mold-card templates to pick a stock tooth set matching this width.

items: Select at the chair, in **natural light** if possible.
Hold the shade tab against the patient's complexion.
Show shade options to the patient; let them weigh in (denture-tooth shade selection is more collaborative than crown shade selection because the patient sees the result for years).
Generally darker than a young dentition — matches the natural age-related darkening.

blocks.text: Selection follows from the articulation scheme:

items: **33° BBA** — 33° anatomic posteriors (max + mand).
**Monoplane neutrocentric** — 0° flat-plane posteriors.
**Monoplane + balancing ramp** — 0° flat-plane posteriors plus acrylic balancing ramps built distal to the second molars.
**Lingualized (alternative not on the grading sheets)** — 20° maxillary, 0° mandibular.

blocks.title: Common pitfalls
blocks.body: Teeth too large — over-fill the lip, look unnatural. Teeth too small — sunken, aged look. Shade too light — obviously prosthetic in an older patient. Patient buy-in matters — show options.

blocks.text: Setup happens in the pre-clinic lab on the mounted articulator. Follow the canonical order and the position rules — pulling teeth out of sequence forces you to redo earlier teeth. Plan on two-to-three lab sessions of 1.5–2 hours each. Trying to do this in one sitting produces a setup you don't love.

items: **Articulator with mounted casts** (Mark 320), face-bow record preserved, CR record verified at jaw-relation visit.
**Maxillary wax rim** contoured (already has midline, smile line, incisal edge position, lip support marks from the rim contouring visit).
**Mandibular wax rim** contoured to the determined occlusal plane (2/3 retromolar pad height posteriorly).
**Denture teeth:** Portrait IPN anteriors + posteriors in the patient's chosen mould and shade. Verify the prescription card before pulling teeth from stock.
**Baseplate wax + setup wax** (softer, easier to flow around tooth necks).
**Hanau / Bunsen wax torch** or hot waxing instrument. Wax knife. PKT instruments (1, 2, 3). Le Cron carver. #7 wax spatula.
**Toothbrush** (for facial stippling), warm-water cup, gauze.
**Caliper / Boley gauge** for verifying inter-tooth distances and tooth-to-rim height.
**Articulating paper** (thin, blue or red) and AccuFilm II for centric contact verification at the end.
Articulator centric latch engaged; incisal pin set to the recorded VDO; condylar settings entered from the protrusive record if available.
Maxillary cast secure on its mounting plate (no rock); wax rim still firmly seated on the cast.
Mandibular cast secure; mandibular wax rim seated with the previously-recorded 2/3 retromolar pad height marks visible.
Tooth mould prescription card next to the articulator — verify mould number on each tooth card before opening foil packs.
Adequate workspace: bench top clear, articulator at eye level, good task lighting.

blocks.text: **(1) Maxillary anteriors → (2) Mandibular anteriors → (3) Mandibular posteriors → (4) Maxillary posteriors.**

Why this order: the maxillary rim has already been contoured for incisal edge position, midline, and lip support — the maxillary anteriors drop into that. The mandibular anteriors register against the maxillary anteriors. The mandibular posteriors set on the recorded occlusal plane (2/3 retromolar pad height). The maxillary posteriors register against the mandibular posteriors in Class I molar.
Step 1 — Set the maxillary central (15 min)

items: **1a.** Melt wax with the Hanau torch in a 12×6 mm window on the right side of the maxillary rim, just lateral to the marked midline. Don't strip the whole rim — the surrounding wax is your registration of incisal edge position.

**1b.** Trim the gingival aspect of the tooth (with a sharp wax knife or a small bur) so the tooth seats fully into the wax window. The marked incisal edge line on the rim is the target for the tooth's incisal edge.
**1c.** Press the tooth into the warm wax. Long axis parallel to the long axis of the face (the central is the "straight" tooth — no tilt).
**1d.** Facial surface lands on the 8 mm-anterior-to-incisive-papilla reference line.
**1e.** Mesial of the central touches the midline.
**1f.** Reflow setup wax around the cervical to seal the tooth to the rim. Wipe excess off the facial.
**1g.** Step back. View from the front: long axis vertical, midline on. View from incisal: facial surface follows the labial arc of the rim.

blocks.text: Step 2 — Set the maxillary lateral (10 min)

items: **2a.** Melt wax in a 10×6 mm window distal to the central.

**2b.** Press the lateral so its incisal edge sits **0.5 mm *higher*** (more gingival) than the central. Use the caliper or eyeball — the lateral is the only anterior whose incisal edge is not at the occlusal plane.
**2c.** Rotate the distal aspect slightly back (palatally). The lateral has more distal rotation than the central.
**2d.** Long axis: slight distal tilt of the incisal edge (the cervical leans slightly mesial).
**2e.** Reflow setup wax around the cervical. View from the front: the lateral incisal edge sits below the central's by ~0.5 mm.

blocks.text: Step 3 — Set the maxillary canine (10 min)

items: **3a.** Position: at the corner of the mouth, lined up with the canine eminence marked on the rim.
**3b.** **Cusp tip at the occlusal plane** — same height as the central incisal edge.
**3c.** Rotate the distal aspect back so only the mesial half of the facial surface is visible from straight on (creates the "corner of the smile" effect).
**3d.** Long axis: cervical leans slightly distal (cusp tip more mesial than cervical — opposite tilt of the lateral).
**3e.** Reflow setup wax. View from the front: central + lateral + canine form a smooth, slightly upward arc.

blocks.text: Step 4 — Mirror to contralateral side (15 min)

Set the left central, lateral, canine in the same sequence (Steps 1–3). Verify symmetry with a caliper: distance from midline to canine cusp tip should be equal R vs. L. Verify incisal edge heights: both centrals at occlusal plane, both laterals 0.5 mm shorter, both canine cusps at occlusal plane.

**Faculty checkpoint 1:** Show the faculty your six maxillary anteriors. They will check midline, symmetry, incisal edge plane, canine eminence, and whether the smile looks natural. *Do not proceed to mandibular anteriors until anterior setup is approved.*
Step 5 — Set the mandibular anteriors (20 min)

items: **5a.** Close the articulator into centric. Mark on the mandibular rim where each maxillary anterior incisal edge lands.
**5b.** Open the articulator. Melt wax in a window for the mandibular central.
**5c.** Set the mandibular central so its incisal edge sits **1 mm below** the maxillary central incisal edge (1 mm vertical overlap).
**5d.** Long axis: **perpendicular to the mandibular plane** (the mandibular anteriors are upright; no tilt).
**5e.** Position B-L: facial surface should sit so there's **~1 mm horizontal overlap** (the maxillary incisal edge is 1 mm labial to the mandibular incisal edge).

**5f.** **Crest-of-ridge check:** the long axis of the mandibular central should pass through or just lingual to the crest of the residual ridge. *Setting it buccal to the crest will rock the denture every time the patient bites.*
**5g.** Set the mandibular lateral and canine in the same horizontal plane as the central (all three mandibular anteriors at the same incisal height).
**5h.** Mirror to contralateral side.

**5i.** Close in centric. **Verify: no tooth contact between maxillary and mandibular anteriors in centric.** Light contact only in protrusion. If there's centric contact, mandibular anteriors are set too tall — reduce or remove.

blocks.text: Step 6 — Set the mandibular posteriors (25 min)

The mandibular posteriors define the occlusal plane. The maxillary posteriors will register against them. Each mandibular posterior tooth's **central fossa** must sit **over the crest of the residual ridge**.

items: **6a.** Identify the crest of the mandibular ridge on the cast. Mark it lightly with a pencil from the canine area to the retromolar pad.
**6b.** Melt a wax window for the mandibular first premolar.

**6c.** Set the first premolar: central fossa over the crest; long axis perpendicular to the occlusal plane; both B and L cusps lightly contacting a flat-plane indicator (or the metal occlusal plate on the articulator).
**6d.** Set the second premolar in the same way, immediately distal to the first.
**6e.** Set the first molar: central fossa over the crest; the mesiobuccal cusp should align with the maxillary canine's distal aspect (anticipating Class I).

**6f.** Set the second molar. **Critical:** the second molar must sit on flat ridge, not on the retromolar incline. If the cast shows ridge rising toward the retromolar pad, *shorten the distal extension* — do not climb the incline.
**6g.** Mirror to contralateral side. Verify symmetry with a caliper.
**6h.** All four mandibular posteriors should contact the flat plane simultaneously. Use a flat metal indicator on the occlusal surfaces to check.

blocks.text: Step 7 — Set the maxillary posteriors (25 min)

items: **7a.** Close the articulator into centric. The maxillary posteriors will register against the now-set mandibular posteriors.
**7b.** Melt a wax window for the maxillary first premolar.
**7c.** Set the maxillary first premolar so its **palatal cusp seats into the central fossa** of the mandibular first premolar.
**7d.** Set the maxillary second premolar against the mandibular second premolar in the same way.
**7e.** Set the maxillary first molar in **Class I**: maxillary MB cusp seats into the buccal groove of the mandibular first molar.
**7f.** Set the maxillary second molar.

**7g.** **Cheek-bite prevention rule:** the maxillary lingual cusps should sit over the crest of the mandibular ridge (not lateral to the mandibular buccal cusps). If the maxillary teeth are set too buccal, the patient bites their cheek.
**7h.** Mirror to contralateral side.
**7i.** **Compensating curve for 33° BBA**: build mm of wax under the posterior cusps per the Week 14 huddle. Mandibular fossae sit on the curve, not on a flat plane.
**8a.** Close the articulator into centric.
**8b.** Place thin articulating paper between the teeth. Tap-tap-tap (no grinding).
**8c.** Remove paper. Inspect contact dots: **≥2 contacts per side** in centric is the BBA grading-sheet minimum. Distribute contacts on multiple teeth, not all on one molar.
**8d.** If a tooth has no contact, gently warm wax around its base and reseat it slightly higher.
**8e.** If a tooth has a heavy contact (paper rips through), warm the wax and reseat it slightly lower (or trim wax beneath).
**8f.** Re-verify until contacts are even.

blocks.text: Step 9 — Verify protrusive + lateral balance (15 min, 33° BBA only)

items: **9a.** Loosen centric latch. Move the articulator into protrusion.
**9b.** Verify **bilateral posterior contact** in protrusion (Christensen's phenomenon eliminated by the compensating curve).
**9c.** Verify **anterior contact** in protrusion.
**9d.** Move into working excursion R. Verify working-side contact + balancing-side contact.
**9e.** Move into working excursion L. Same check.
**9f.** Adjust compensating curve depth (add or remove wax beneath posteriors) until balance is achieved.
**9g.** *For Monoplane:* skip this step entirely. Monoplane has no balance requirement.
**9h.** *For Monoplane + balancing ramp:* wait until processing — the ramps are acrylic, added at the lab-remount stage.
**10a. Interdental papillae:** sculpt flat or slightly concave between each pair of teeth. Use a PKT instrument or a small wax carver.

**10b. Facial gingival contours:** develop natural-looking root prominences (canine eminence is the most important — it displaces the lip away from the biting zone and supports the corner of the mouth).
**10c. Stippling:** dab the facial wax surfaces with a clean toothbrush to create a stippled texture. Mimics attached gingiva.
**10d. Lingual contour (mandibular):** concave, with a slight gingival roll. Tongue space matters — over-bulky lingual flange causes tongue irritation.
**10e. Palate thickness:** ~3 mm. Smooth, no air bubbles. Don't make the palate so thin it flexes; don't make it so thick it crowds the tongue.
**10f. Posterior palatal seal area:** leave the PPS area un-thickened — it will be defined at the wax try-in visit on the patient.
**10g. Final inspection:** no wax on facial enamel, no sharp wax edges, no air voids. The wax denture should look like a natural denture-ready prosthesis.
**Never strip the whole wax rim.** Melt wax only where the next tooth goes. The surrounding rim is your registration of position.

**If a tooth doesn't fit B-L:** trim the wax base first (cervical adjustment). Still tight? Trim the lingual aspect of the tooth, not the facial. Lingual trimming is invisible; facial trimming ruins the contour.
**Butt joint** at the wax-tooth interface; no wax on the facial enamel. Wax on the facial = unesthetic and won't survive flasking.
**Keep wax warm but not liquid.** Liquid wax floods around the tooth and obscures position; cold wax shatters when you press the tooth.
**Reflow, don't add cold.** If you need to adjust, reheat the existing wax rather than dripping new wax in.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Maxillary anteriors look crooked from the front:** midline is off. Pull the central, re-mark midline on the rim from the philtrum reference, reset. **Mandibular anteriors hit in centric:** they're too tall. Warm the wax around the cervical and reseat them lower — do not grind the incisal edges (changes appearance, doesn't fix the cantilever). **Denture rocks during posterior setup:** you set a tooth buccal to the crest. Pull it, re-mark the crest, reset on-crest. **Second molar sits on retromolar incline:** shorten the distal extension — do not climb the incline. The 2nd molar is the most dispensable tooth in the setup. **Wax rim cracking during torch use:** wax is too cold or the rim is too thin. Add baseplate wax to thicken before reheating. **Tooth keeps drifting B or L after pressing:** the wax window is too wide. Trim wax to a tighter window before reseating — the wax should grip the tooth on all sides.

blocks.title: Faculty checkpoints to plan for
blocks.body: **After Step 4** (maxillary anteriors complete): faculty checks midline, smile, lip support. **After Step 6** (mandibular posteriors complete): faculty checks crest-of-ridge alignment, occlusal plane height, no climb up retromolar pad. **After Step 9** (centric + balance verified): faculty checks centric contacts, balance, Class I, Hanau's quint compliance. Plan your lab sessions so you stop at one of these checkpoints — don't be halfway through a tooth when faculty is available.

blocks.text: Three schemes are taught at, with one variant. Each scheme has an indication and a grading sheet. Hanau's quint is the theoretical framework that explains why each scheme works (or doesn't) for a given patient.
Hanau's quint — five factors affecting occlusal balance

items: Condylar inclination
Incisal guidance
Orientation of the occlusal plane
Depth of the compensating curve
Cuspal inclination / height

blocks.text: **Theilemann formula (qualitative):** Balance = (Incisal guidance × Condylar inclination) / (Occlusal plane × Cuspal inclination × Compensating curve).

What you can manipulate: cuspal inclination (tooth selection), compensating curve depth (build-up of posterior wax during setup), and incisal guidance (vertical and horizontal anterior overlap). What you cannot manipulate: condylar inclination (patient anatomy, set by the protrusive record) and occlusal plane orientation (constrained by esthetic and functional landmarks).

items: **Indication:** good ridges, good neuromuscular control, prior anatomic wearer, transition from natural dentition.
**Posterior teeth:** 33° anatomic, max + mand.
**Compensating curve** built per the Week 14 huddle.
**Goal:** bilateral contact in centric, in working, in balancing, and in protrusive excursions.
**Grading:** 0/0.5/1 point per parameter; out of 20 points; pass at 16+.
**Indication:** resorbed ridges, poor neuromuscular control, bruxism, Class II / III maxillomandibular relationship, prior 0° wearer.
**Posterior teeth:** 0° flat-plane.
**No compensating curve, no anterior vertical overlap.**
**Goal:** stable centric, no excursive balance required.
**Grading:** PASS / FAIL. Gross deviation if anteriors contact in MI.
**Indication:** as monoplane, but balance is desired (e.g., for stability without anatomic tooth wear concerns).
**Posterior teeth:** 0° flat-plane.
**Acrylic balancing ramps** added distal to the mandibular second molars to allow posterior contact in excursions.
**Anterior overlap:** 1–2 mm vertical and horizontal.
**Posterior horizontal overlap:** 1/3 of tooth.
**Grading:** PASS / FAIL. Working = contact on working-side ramp + anteriors; balancing = contact on balancing ramp; protrusive = contact on posterior acrylic ramp bilaterally + anterior contact.

blocks.text: Week 14 huddle: compensating-curve build-up for 33° BBA

items: *The specific mm values below are illustrative of the gradient (anterior 0 → posterior elevated); the clinic huddle-key values may differ slightly. Verify against your current Week 14 huddle key.*
**Mandibular 2nd molar central fossa:** 1.5–2.0 mm above the metal occlusal plate.
**Mandibular 1st molar central fossa:** 1.0–1.5 mm above the plate.
**Mandibular 2nd premolar central fossa:** 0.5–1.0 mm above the plate.
**Mandibular 1st premolar central fossa:** 0.2–0.5 mm above the plate.
**Mandibular canine cusp tip:** 0 mm (on the plate).
**Mandibular incisors:** 0 mm (on the plate, contacting opposing during protrusion).

blocks.text: The curve runs anterior-low to posterior-high so that during protrusion + lateral excursions the posterior teeth maintain contact (balancing-side contacts), preserving denture stability. The exact values vary slightly by articulator setting + tooth mold; the relative gradient (anterior 0 → posterior 2 mm) is what matters.

blocks.title: How to physically build the curve
blocks.body: Wax up the mandibular posteriors to the target height using the metal occlusal plate as the 0-reference + a wax ruler or pre-scribed cast lines as the height-reference. Verify with the upper teeth set against the lower wax — in protrusion + working/balancing excursions, the cusps should track smoothly without lift-off. If they lift, the curve is too shallow; if they jam, too steep. Re-wax until smooth excursive tracking is achieved before processing.

blocks.text: Values are mm above the metal occlusal plate. Mandibular central fossa sits over the crest of the ridge; maxillary lingual cusps over the same crest; central grooves of all four posteriors aligned.

The last reversible step. Once the wax dentures go to processing, the tooth positions and the PPS are locked in PMMA. Use this visit to verify everything — visual inspection, fit, esthetics, phonetics, VDO, CR, protrusive, PPS extent, and patient consent. Plan 1.5–2 hours.

items: **From the lab:** wax trial dentures on mounted casts, articulator (Mark 320), face-bow record preserved.
**For phonetics + esthetics:** handheld mirror for the patient, retraction cheek retractors (optional), good ambient + task lighting.
**For VDO:** Boley gauge or digital caliper, dots / marks on the patient's nose tip + chin point if not already marked from the JR visit.
**For CR + protrusive:** Regisil PB cartridge + dispenser gun + fresh mixing tip, bite registration paste alternative (e.g., ZOE), articulating paper.
**For PPS:** indelible (T-band) marker, Hanau torch + green stick / softening flame, sharp #7 wax spatula, mouth mirror, gauze.
**For documentation:** intraoral camera or phone for shade + esthetic photos, consent form on iPad / printout.
**For the lab return:** remount jig materials (plaster, separator, small dappen dish, brush), bite registration storage box, lab prescription form.

blocks.text: Pre-appointment setup (10 min before patient arrives)

items: Verify wax dentures came back from setup faculty-approved (signed grading sheet).
Confirm both bases articulate together cleanly on the Mark 320 in centric (incisal pin contacts the table, centric latch engages without prying).
Inspect intaglio and borders with a thumb pad: no sharp wax edges, no air voids, no debris.
Soak the trial dentures in lukewarm water 5 min before seating — cold wax is brittle and easier to fracture in the mouth.
Set up the bracket table: mirror, Boley, Regisil gun loaded, T-band marker uncapped, articulating paper ready, camera ready.
Confirm interpreter availability if needed.

blocks.text: Step 1 — Visual + tactile inspection of the wax bases (5 min)

items: **1a.** Bases smooth: run thumb pad around all flange borders. No sharp edges, no rough wax.
**1b.** No debris in the intaglio. Wipe with damp gauze if needed.
**1c.** No air bubbles or voids in the palate. Hold to light — any pinholes mean the lab needs to repair before chairside use.
**1d.** Wax-tooth interface intact: no teeth loose or rotated since setup approval. Light digital pressure on each tooth confirms it's seated in the wax.
**1e.** If anything fails inspection, *do not proceed*. Send back to the lab for repair and reschedule.
**2a. Seat each denture individually first.** Maxillary alone, then mandibular alone. Frenal clearance on insertion — if a frenum catches, mark with marker and relieve wax chairside.

**2b. Retention check (each arch alone).** Maxillary should hold against gentle downward tug; mandibular should remain seated when patient relaxes. Denture adhesive permissible *only for evaluation* — don't deliver if retention requires adhesive.
**2c. Articulate both together.** Check stability in centric: no rock, no rebound when patient bites.

**2d. Facial proportions.** Stand the patient up or have them sit erect. Lower 1/3 of the face should be proportional to upper and middle 1/3. Philtrum length, nasolabial fold depth, marionette lines should look natural — not over-extended (cushy cheeks) or under-supported (sunken).

**2e. Midline check.** Patient closes lips, relaxes. Dental midline should align with facial midline (philtrum / nose tip). If off by >0.5 mm, mark for the lab to correct — or accept if the patient's facial midline itself is asymmetric.
**2f. Incisal edge position.** Lip at rest: 2–4 mm of maxillary incisal edge visible (less for males, more for younger / female patients). Smile: incisal edge follows the curve of the lower lip.
**2g. Smile line + cant.** Verify the incisal plane is parallel to the interpupillary line. Hold a stick / ruler horizontally across the eyes; the incisal edges should match.
**2h. Lip support test.** Add a thin strip of wax to the labial flange. Ask the patient: better, worse, same? Observe philtrum — deeper or shallower? Adjust labial flange thickness accordingly.

**2i. Characterization.** Ask the patient if they want the anteriors to look perfectly straight (younger / more glamorous) or with minor rotation, slight recession, slight staggering (more natural, age-appropriate). Mark adjustments for the lab in pencil on the cast or note on the lab Rx.

**3a. F / V sounds.** Have the patient say "fifty-five Fridays." The incisal edges of the maxillary anteriors should rest on the **wet-dry line** of the lower lip. *Too far labial → F sounds like Th (lisp). Too far lingual → F sounds dull.*

**3b. S sounds.** Have the patient count from 60 to 70 ("sixty, sixty-one, sixty-two..."). Listen and observe. *Whistle → teeth too far labial or median palatal groove too deep. Lisp → teeth too far buccal-palatal.* **No tooth-on-tooth contact during S** — if you hear clicking, vertical dimension is too high or anteriors interfere.
**3c. M sounds.** "Mama, Memphis, Monday." This puts the lips in their closest natural rest position — useful for confirming rim profile and lip support.
**3d. E sounds.** "Eee." The incisal edges should rest *lightly* against the vermilion of the lower lip. Too long → teeth poke the lower lip; too short → no lip contact.
**3e. Document findings.** If phonetics fail, the teeth need to be repositioned by the lab — don't try to grind chairside. Schedule a second wax try-in if needed.
**4a.** Have the patient sit erect, head unsupported, looking straight ahead.
**4b.** Measure **VDR** (vertical dimension at rest) with the Boley gauge from the nose-tip mark to the chin-point mark. Patient relaxes lips closed.
**4c.** Have the patient close to centric with the trial dentures in. Measure **VDO** from the same two reference marks.
**4d.** **VDR − VDO ≈ 3 mm** is the target freeway space.
**4e.** Cross-verify with phonetic 60–70 count: at correct VDO, the closest speaking distance is ~1 mm and there's no clicking.
**4f. If VDO too high (freeway <2 mm):** patient looks "over-faced," lips don't close at rest, teeth click in speech. Need to reduce VDO — lab grinds posteriors or resets at lower VDO.
**4g. If VDO too low (freeway >4 mm):** patient looks "collapsed," deep nasolabial fold, no support. Need to increase VDO — lab raises posteriors or remounts on a new VDR−3 record.
**5a.** Bring the patient into CR using the same technique as the JR visit (chin guidance, bimanual manipulation, or tapping into the most-retruded position).

**5b.** Place Regisil PB on the mandibular posterior teeth only. Have the patient close to first contact — **do not** close fully into the wax try-in's centric (you're testing whether the trial denture's centric matches the patient's actual CR, not stamping the trial denture's position into the record).
**5c.** Set ~30 seconds. Remove the record carefully.
**5d.** Take the dentures back to the lab area. Loosen the centric latch on the articulator. Seat the new CR record between the mounted casts.
**5e. The maxillary teeth must fall exactly into the indentations** in the new record. If they do, CR was correct at the JR visit — proceed to Step 6.

**5f. If they don't fall exactly into the record**, CR was wrong at the JR visit. **Remount the mandibular cast** on the new CR record before sending to processing — otherwise the processed dentures will have a built-in centric error.
**5g.** Document the remount on the lab Rx.
**6a.** Place Regisil PB on the maxillary posterior teeth (or mandibular, lab preference).
**6b.** Have the patient protrude 5–6 mm forward (you can guide them: "slide your lower jaw forward as far as you comfortably can, then back off slightly").
**6c.** Patient closes into the Regisil with the jaw protruded.
**6d.** Set ~30 seconds. Remove carefully.
**6e.** This record goes to the lab. The lab will set the articulator's horizontal condylar guidance to match the patient's protrusive condylar path.

**6f. Why this matters:** without this record, the lab remount and the clinical remount are working with the default 30° condylar setting — not the patient's actual anatomy. Excursive balance won't reproduce in the mouth.

blocks.text: Step 7 — PPS extent determination on the patient (10 min)

items: **7a.** Seat the maxillary trial denture. Patient says **"ah"** — observe the vibrating line at the junction of hard and soft palate. Mark with indelible marker on the trial denture intaglio.

**7b. Valsalva maneuver:** patient pinches nose, closes lips, gently blows. The soft palate balloons forward, demarcating the **anterior** PPS border (the most-forward point that won't lift off the denture during function).

**7c. Classify House Class I–III** based on soft palate angle (Class I = flat, generous PPS zone; Class III = steep, narrow PPS zone, more critical to extend to the vibrating line without overextending).
**7d. Mark the PPS area** on the trial denture intaglio: anterior border (Valsalva line), posterior border (vibrating line, "ah" line). Hamular notches at the lateral extents.
**7e. Dimensions for the lab to carve:**
Hamular notch area: 1.0–2.0 mm wide × 1.0 mm deep.
Near the hard-soft palate junction: 4.0–6.0 mm wide × 1.5 mm deep.
Mid-palatal: 2.0–3.0 mm wide × 1.0 mm deep.
**7f.** Document on the lab Rx that the PPS has been marked on the intaglio of the trial denture.
**8a.** Hand the patient the mirror. Walk them through: shape, position, characterization, shade.
**8b.** Photograph for the chart (smile, repose, profile).
**8c.** Explicit verbal: "Are you happy with how these look? Once they go to processing, we can't change the tooth positions or the shade without remaking the dentures."
**8d.** Patient signs the informed consent for prosthodontics. Document the conversation in Axium.
**8e.** If patient hesitates, *do not push through*. Schedule a second try-in with modifications — remakes from post-processing complaints are much more expensive.

blocks.text: Step 9 — Recall + maintenance plan + lab dispatch (10 min)

items: **9a.** Discuss the post-insertion follow-up schedule with the patient: delivery + 24-hour check + 1-week check, then 3–6 month recall.
**9b.** Discuss home care: brush dentures with denture brush daily, soak in denture cleanser overnight (or in water), leave dentures out for 6–8 hours per day to let tissues rest.
**9c.** Pour the remount jig (see callout below) before pulling the trial denture off the cast.
**9d.** Pour the clinical remount cast (alginate impression of patient with trial dentures in place — this becomes the lab's reference for finishing).
**9e.** Fill out the lab Rx: patient name in each prosthesis, acrylic shade, lab remount procedure, remount jig + clinical remount cast attached, finish + polish instructions.
**9f.** Send to the lab. Schedule delivery for 2–3 weeks out.

blocks.title: Preserve the face-bow record before processing
blocks.body: Before pulling the trial denture off the mounted maxillary cast, make a **face-bow remount jig**: plaster built against the maxillary tooth occlusal surfaces while the cast is on the articulator. This lets the lab remount the processed maxillary denture to the articulator after deflasking. Without the jig, the lab remount can't restore the original face-bow position.

blocks.title: What goes to the lab with the prescription
blocks.body: Patient's name in each prosthesis. Acrylic shade. Lab remount procedure. Remount jig (the plaster key). Clinical remount casts. Finish and polish. Instruction to **remount the maxillary cast to the articulator using the clinical remount jig**.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Trial denture rocks on insertion:** base doesn't fit. Check for sharp wax in the periphery, frenum impingement, or PPS too thick. PIP-equivalent (mark with marker, look for blanching, relieve) chairside or send back to the lab. **Patient says "teeth look too big":** tooth mould was wrong, or vertical proportion (gingival height) is short. Show photos pre-extraction if available. Offer to remake with smaller mould (rare; usually patient adjusts after delivery). **VDO is off by >2 mm:** remake the mandibular wax rim at corrected VDO and re-record CR. Don't try to fudge it — processed dentures with wrong VDO are unwearable. **CR doesn't match the new record:** remount mandibular cast on new CR before processing. Common cause: patient bit into a comfortable habitual posture at JR visit rather than true CR. **Patient hates the look:** *do not send to processing.* Schedule a second try-in. Identify the specific complaint (too white, too straight, midline off, too short, etc.). One re-try-in is far cheaper than a remake. **Wax denture fractures during try-in:** repair with sticky wax if minor, otherwise return to lab for relining the base. Don't proceed with a fractured base.

blocks.text: After try-in approval, the trial denture goes to the lab for flasking, wax elimination, packing with acrylic, curing, deflasking, lab remount, and finish/polish. uses the SR Ivocap injection-molding system for heat-cured PMMA. This is primarily lab work — you may do some of the steps yourself, depending on which lab session you're assigned to. Knowing the process matters because you'll need to evaluate the returned dentures and the lab remount before delivery.

items: **Trial dentures** on mounted casts (faculty-approved at wax try-in).
**Face-bow remount jig** + clinical remount cast (from the wax try-in visit).
**Ivocap flask** (upper + lower halves) + closure bolts.
**Type III stone** for investing.
**Tin-foil substitute** (sodium alginate) + brush + dappen dish.
**SR Ivocap capsule** (20 g polymer + 30 mL monomer pre-portioned) in the patient's prescribed shade.
**Cap Vibrator** (for mixing the capsule).
**Ivocap injection unit + pressurizing chamber + boiling water bath.**
**Deflasking tools:** mallet, plaster knife, dental ratchet.
**Acrylic burs** (carbide, fine cross-cut) for trimming flanges.
**Polishing kit:** pumice + slurry, rag wheel, high-shine compound, lathe.
**Articulator (Mark 320)** for the lab remount.
**Articulating paper** (thin) + AccuFilm II for equilibration.
Trial dentures are faculty-approved and signed off at the wax try-in visit.
Remount jig present (preserves face-bow record after deflasking).
Clinical remount cast present (preserves the mandibular CR for the lab).
Lab Rx specifies shade, finish + polish protocol, remount instructions.
Patient's name visible on the lab Rx and ready to be embedded in each denture.
**1a.** Lubricate the cast with separator (light coat of petroleum jelly or commercial separator). Don't get separator on the wax.

**1b. Lower half:** mix Type III stone, pour into the lower-half flask. Embed the cast base in the stone up to the level of the gingival flange margin. Vibrate to release air. Smooth the top surface of the stone.
**1c.** Let the lower half set ~20 min until firm.
**1d.** Apply **separating medium** (tin-foil substitute or commercial separator) to the set stone surface. This prevents the upper-half stone from bonding to the lower-half stone.
**1e. Upper half:** assemble the upper flask onto the lower. Mix more stone, pour over the wax denture, covering teeth and the full base. Vibrate. Smooth.
**1f.** Let the upper half set ~25 min until firm.
**1g.** Inspect: the flask should be filled to the rim, no voids visible at the joint, no stone overflowing the flask edge.
**2a.** Place the closed flask in a hot-water bath at 100°C for 4–5 min — this melts the wax.
**2b.** Open the flask with the plaster knife / mallet. The upper half (with the teeth) separates from the lower half.
**2c.** Wash all wax out with boiling water, then with detergent (alkaline soap) until the stone is clean. No greasy film.

**2d. Inspect:** teeth remain embedded in the upper-half stone, in their original mounted positions. Stone base remains in the lower half. No wax residue anywhere. If a tooth comes out, retrieve it carefully — you'll need to reseat it before injection.
**2e.** Rinse both halves with cold water. Pat dry — not bone-dry, residual moisture aids the next step.
**3a.** Brush tin-foil substitute (sodium alginate) onto all **stone surfaces** that will contact the acrylic. Coat the lower-half intaglio and the upper-half soft-tissue impression.

**3b. Do NOT coat the denture-tooth bonding surfaces** — the lingual / palatal aspects of each tooth need to chemically bond to the new acrylic. If you coat them, the teeth fall out of the cured denture.
**3c.** Let dry to a slight film (~3 min).
**4a. SR Ivocap capsule:** 20 g polymer + 30 mL monomer, pre-dosed in a sealed capsule in the patient's shade.
**4b.** Insert capsule into the Cap Vibrator. Run for **5 min** to mix evenly. The capsule will warm and the contents become a uniform doughy slurry.
**4c.** Close the flask. Bolt down with the closure ring. Verify no gap at the seam.
**4d.** Attach the capsule to the flask injection port.
**4e.** Place the assembly in the Ivocap pressurizing chamber. Set to the prescribed pressure (per the unit's spec sheet, usually ~6 bar).
**4f.** Pressurize. The injection forces the acrylic from the capsule through the flask sprue and into all the empty spaces left by the wax.
**4g. Cure in boiling water bath for 35 min** while still under pressure. The heat polymerizes the acrylic; the pressure prevents porosity and shrinkage.
**4h. Cool the flask gradually** — pull the assembly out of the boiling water and let it cool to room temp on the bench (don't quench in cold water; thermal shock causes porosity and warpage).
**5a.** Unbolt the flask.
**5b.** Separate the upper and lower halves. The cured denture is sandwiched between the two stone halves.
**5c.** Carefully chip / pry the stone off the cameo (outer) surface using the plaster knife and mallet. *Avoid hitting the denture itself.*
**5d.** Continue removing stone in small pieces until the denture is fully exposed.
**5e.** The stone **cast** typically remains adherent to the intaglio — **this is intentional**, it's needed for the lab remount.
**5f.** Inspect: teeth firmly bonded, no fractures, no voids in the acrylic, base intact.

**6a.** Mount the deflasked **maxillary** denture (with cast still adherent) back onto the original articulator using the **face-bow remount jig** — the plaster key fits the occlusal surfaces and registers the original maxillary position.
**6b.** Mount the deflasked **mandibular** denture (with cast still adherent) using the **clinical remount cast** + CR record. Centric latch engaged.
**6c.** Close the articulator into centric. Observe: **VDO has typically opened slightly** due to processing dimensional change.
**6d. Use articulating paper** to mark centric contacts. Look for heavy marks (prematurities) — usually in the posteriors.
**6e. Equilibrate to restore VDO and develop even contacts.** Adjust cusp inclines, central fossae, marginal ridges.
**6f. *Do not reduce the centric-holding cusps*** — the maxillary lingual cusps and the mandibular buccal cusps hold the bite. Reducing them collapses VDO.

**6g. Excursive equilibration follows BULL** (Buccal of Uppers, Lingual of Lowers) — for the non-centric-holding cusps, adjust the inclines to allow free excursive movement without creating posterior interferences.
**6h.** Re-verify after each adjustment. Continue until centric contacts are even bilaterally and excursions are smooth.
**7a.** Remove the casts from the intaglio (careful tap with the plaster knife, then warm-water soak if needed).
**7b. Trim flange borders** smooth and rounded with acrylic carbide burs at low speed. Round the borders — sharp edges irritate tissue.
**7c. Trim excess acrylic** at the periphery, around the labial frenum notch, around the buccal frenum notches.
**7d. Verify PPS preserved** on the maxillary intaglio — don't trim it away.
**7e. Smooth with progressively finer burs**, then with pumice slurry on a rag wheel.

**7f. High-shine the cameo surface** with high-shine compound on a clean rag wheel. The cameo (outer) surface should reflect light cleanly — this is what the patient sees and what oral hygiene relies on.

**7g. Do NOT polish the intaglio surface.** The matte texture from the stone increases retention by capillary attraction with saliva. Polishing the intaglio creates a glossy surface that doesn't hold saliva and reduces fit.
**7h.** Verify patient name engraved or labeled in each denture (typically on a small label set into the maxillary palatal area before processing).
**7i.** Disinfect, package for the clinic delivery visit.

blocks.title: What you should evaluate on the returned dentures before delivery
blocks.body: **Tooth positions match the approved wax try-in** — if a tooth shifted during processing, the lab needs to repair before delivery. **VDO matches the lab remount equilibration.** Confirm the incisal pin still touches the table. **Centric contacts even bilaterally** (articulating paper test on the articulator before unmounting). **No porosity** in the acrylic — especially in the palate. White cloudy patches = porosity = remake. **No tooth fractures.** **Borders smooth, rounded, no sharp edges.** Run thumb pad around every flange. **Cameo polished, intaglio matte.** **PPS preserved on maxillary intaglio.**

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Porosity in the cured acrylic:** insufficient pressure during cure, or thermal shock during cool-down. Most porosity is in the palate (thickest section). Small porosity → smooth and polish. Large porosity → remake. **Tooth fell out of the upper-half stone after wax elimination:** reseat carefully using a tiny dot of sticky wax on the cast position before closing the flask — the new acrylic will bond the tooth in place. **Denture won't release from the stone after deflasking:** tin-foil substitute was inadequate. Soak in warm water, gentle pry with the plaster knife. Avoid hitting the acrylic directly. **VDO opens by >3 mm at the lab remount:** the closure ring wasn't tight enough during injection — acrylic flashed at the seam. Equilibrate to recover what you can; if >3 mm, remake. **Patient's denture name label is missing or wrong:** attach a new label before final polish — Patient name required in every removable prosthesis (forensic identification + chain of custody). **Cameo polish looks streaky:** contamination on the rag wheel. Re-pumice, then re-polish with clean compound on a fresh wheel.

blocks.text: At delivery you seat and finish the processed dentures, then send the patient home with care instructions. Work through it in order: check esthetics, refine the posterior palatal seal and the borders, fit the intaglio with PIP, check occlusion, polish, and educate the patient last.

items: **From sterilization:** exam kit, lab handpiece + lab burs, patient goggles.
**From the lab return:** the finished, polished maxillary + mandibular dentures.

**In clinic:** PIP paste + brush + PIP remover, Mizzy spray, disclosing wax, Thompson stick, horseshoe articulating paper, denture cleanser sample, post-op written instructions, denture brush, denture storage box.
Confirm the lab returned the **finished, polished maxillary + mandibular dentures**, ready to seat. (No casts or jigs to expect — the master casts get destroyed during processing.)
Print post-op instructions.
Before seating, run a finger over the intaglio and borders and smooth any sharp or rough spots.
Seat both dentures and ask the patient about any immediate areas of concern — pain, sharp, tight.
Hand the patient a mirror and get their approval of the esthetics. Be positive and encouraging.
With the dentures out, have the patient say "Ah" and mark the vibrating line with a wet Thompson stick.
Seat the maxillary denture and check how far its posterior border extends past the vibrating line.
Reduce any overextension with a bur, then bevel it to a gradual feather edge toward the posterior border.

blocks.text: **Border-extension check:** gently pull the cheeks and have the patient lift the tongue — the denture shouldn't unseat — then run a finger along the border for anything impinging. **To find overextended borders, use disclosing wax:** roll it into thin ropes, mold them along the borders (no more than ~2 mm thick), seat the denture through border-molding movements while you manipulate the cheeks, then remove and look for show-through — wherever the wax has thinned to nothing is overextended. Reduce those spots with a lab bur, re-apply wax, and repeat until there's no show-through. Smooth and polish adjusted borders.

items: Dry the intaglio.
Apply a **thin, uniform layer of PIP** to the intaglio, brushing all in one direction so show-through is easy to read.
Spray **Mizzy** on the residual ridge so the paste doesn't stick to the mucosa (don't let the mouth go dry).
Seat with **light, even hand pressure on the first molars** — do NOT have the patient bite. ~5 seconds.
Remove and inspect. **Show-through — where the PIP is pushed away to nothing — marks a pressure point.**
Reduce those spots conservatively with a lab bur. Re-apply PIP and repeat until the layer is uniform and undisturbed.
Repeat for the other denture.
Mark occlusion, lateral movements, and protrusion with **horseshoe articulating paper**.
Reduce denture teeth that hit too high with a lab bur; re-evaluate and repeat. Easiest to pick one arch and adjust only that arch.
**Goals:** even contact on both sides on multiple/all posterior teeth; in protrusion, a posterior contact on each side plus anterior contact.
Keep it light at delivery — the patient still has to adapt, and the intaglio will need more adjustment at the 24-hour follow-up.

blocks.text: Polish the **outside** of the dentures with a rag wheel + pumice in the support lab. **Never polish the intaglio** — it changes the fit.

items: **First night:** keep the dentures in until the 24-hour follow-up so we can see where they rub. After that, **remove them at night** and keep them in water.
**Eat** soft foods and small bites to start.
**Speak:** practice by reading aloud in front of a mirror.
**Saliva:** expect a temporary increase in flow at first — it settles.
**Soreness** is an expected tissue response; sore spots stay tender for a few days even after we adjust them — that's normal.
**Wash the dentures after eating.**
**Clean** with a denture brush + denture toothpaste (not regular toothpaste — too abrasive), brushing off all food and adhesive; **massage or brush the gums** for stimulation.
**Soak in water when out** (or Polident + water). **Never hot water** — it warps the acrylic.
**If the denture feels loose,** a small amount of denture adhesive (Polident / Polygrip) is fine.
**Bring the dentures to each visit** for ultrasonic cleaning.
**Next visit:** 24-hour follow-up (sooner if a severe adjustment was needed; ~3 days if minor).

blocks.title: Issues at delivery
blocks.body: **Denture won't seat:** reapply PIP; identify an undercut intaglio area or an overextended border. **Patient gags on insertion:** posterior border too thick or overextended — reduce with a carbide bur, smooth. **Sore spot on insertion:** mark with the Thompson stick BEFORE removing the denture; transfer to the intaglio; PIP-adjust.

blocks.text: The named anatomical landmarks expects you to identify on the diagnostic cast and in the patient's mouth. Each has a classification (primary support / secondary support / relief / border) that dictates how the denture is designed and how the impression is taken.

**Maxilla — primary:** hard palate lateral to midline + tuberosity (per ACP; book lists tuberosity as secondary). **Secondary:** posterior residual ridge + rugae (per book/ACP). **Relief:** incisive papilla, mid-palatal suture, greater palatine foramen, fovea palatinae, anterior residual ridge.

**Mandible — primary:** buccal shelf. **Secondary:** residual ridge (if inverted U). **Relief:** flabby or knife-edge ridge. **Peripheral / limiting:** retromolar pad (book = peripheral; ACP = primary supporting — source conflict).
Working ridge-to-vestibule, anterior to posterior — these are the landmarks every maxillary denture must respect (record or relieve).

items: **Labial frenum** — narrow soft-tissue attachment at the midline of the labial vestibule. **Relieve** in denture flange; impingement causes ulceration.
**Buccal frenum(s)** — left and right, in the buccal vestibule, typically at the canine-premolar region. **Relieve** in flange.
**Labial vestibule** — the depth from the alveolar ridge to the labial mucosa. **Record fully** at border molding — the denture flange must extend to its full reflection for retention.
**Buccal vestibule** — same as labial but along the cheek. **Record fully**; depth varies with masseter activity.
**Maxillary tuberosity** — bilateral bony bulges distal to the second molar site. **Record completely** — primary support area; engagement here resists displacement during function.

**Hamular notch** — depression between the tuberosity and the hamulus of the pterygoid plate. **Record at full depth** — defines the posterior border of the denture and is critical for posterior palatal seal placement.
**Pterygomandibular raphe** — fibrous band running from the hamulus down to the mandibular retromolar area. **Avoid impinging**; relieve in the flange if it tracks against the denture in function.
**Hard palate** — the bony roof. **Record fully** with full coverage; primary support area for the maxillary denture.
