**Soft palate (vibrating line)** — the junction between hard and soft palate, identified by asking the patient to say 'ah'. **Posterior border of the denture sits ~1–2 mm anterior** to the vibrating line.
**Fovea palatinae** — paired pits 1–2 mm posterior to the vibrating line, marking openings of mucous glands. **Visual landmark** — denture posterior border should be at or slightly anterior to these.
**Posterior palatal seal (PPS)** — engineered zone of 1–2 mm tissue compression at the posterior border. **Carved into the master cast** before processing; provides the suction seal.

**Incisive papilla** — pear-shaped soft-tissue mound at the midline, immediately palatal to the central incisors. **Relieve** — overlies the nasopalatine foramen + nerve; pressure causes burning + paresthesia.
**Rugae** — irregular ridges of palatal mucosa anterior to the midpalatine raphe. **Reproduce** — important for phonetics + tongue orientation.
**Midpalatine raphe (median palatine suture)** — bony ridge along the palatal midline. **Relieve if prominent** — non-resilient bone with thin mucosa; pressure causes midline ulceration.

**Maxillary torus (torus palatinus)** — bony exostosis at the midpalatine raphe, if present. **Relieve generously** OR refer for surgical removal pre-prosthetically if it interferes with denture seating.
**Residual alveolar ridge** — the bony crest after extraction. **Primary support area** in the anterior; resorption rate varies (Class I–IV House classification).

blocks.text: The mandibular ridge has less primary support area and more functional displacement than the maxilla — every landmark is biomechanically significant.

items: **Labial frenum** — midline labial attachment. **Relieve.**
**Buccal frenum(s)** — bilateral in the buccal vestibule. **Relieve.**
**Lingual frenum** — midline lingual attachment from tongue to floor of mouth. **Relieve generously**; high attachment + thin frenum compromise denture stability.

**Buccal shelf (external oblique ridge area)** — flat bony shelf between the mylohyoid line laterally and the alveolar ridge medially, posterior to the mental foramen. **Primary support area** for the mandibular denture — record fully + extend the flange to engage it.

**Retromolar pad** — pear-shaped soft-tissue cushion distal to the last molar site, overlying the retromolar triangle. **Cover with the denture flange to the distal half**; primary support area + identifies the posterior limit.

**Mylohyoid ridge** — bony crest along the lingual surface of the mandible, running from the third molar area forward and downward to the genial tubercles. **Relieve** — sharp ridge + thin mucosa; flange impinges if not relieved. Most common source of lingual soreness.

**External oblique ridge** — bony crest running from the anterior border of the ramus down to the buccal side of the mandibular body. **Use as the lateral border of the buccal shelf** during border molding.

**Mental foramen** — opening for the mental nerve, typically apical to the second premolar site on the lateral cortex. **Relieve** if it lies under the denture (post-resorption it can sit directly under the flange).
**Genial tubercles** — small bony elevations on the lingual aspect of the mandibular midline. **Relieve** if prominent post-resorption.
**Alveololingual sulcus** — the space between the residual ridge and the tongue. **Record at full depth** during border molding with the patient's tongue forward, lateral, and at rest.
**Retromylohyoid space** — the area distal to the mylohyoid ridge, between the ridge and the retromolar pad. **Extend the lingual flange into this space** for retention.
**Mandibular torus (torus mandibularis)** — bony exostosis on the lingual aspect of the premolar region, if present. **Relieve generously** OR refer for surgical removal pre-prosthetically.

blocks.text: The Posterior Palatal Seal is the bead-shaped indentation on the cast that creates a positive border seal on the processed denture. It compensates for polymerization shrinkage and seats the denture firmly against soft palate tissue when the patient closes.

On the soft palate, hamular notch to hamular notch, marking the division between immovable and movable soft palate during "ah". At midline, the vibrating line passes **anterior to the fovea palatinae**. The denture's posterior border usually ends right at the vibrating line (occasionally 1–2 mm posterior).

Lies between the vibrating line (posterior border) and the hard-palate / soft-palate attached-tissue junction (anterior border, found with **Valsalva** — patient pinches nose, gently blows; soft palate balloons forward, marking the anterior line).
Shape: **butterfly** due to the posterior nasal spine projecting inferiorly at midline.

Purpose: (1) compensate for polymerization shrinkage of acrylic; (2) compensate for minor functional movement of the soft palate; (3) prevent food and air intrusion under the posterior border; (4) aid retention; (5) reduce gagging sensation by tucking the posterior border under the soft palate edge.

items: Transfer the vibrating-line mark from the impression to the cast (or re-mark from anatomic landmarks).
**#8 round bur** to form the posterior PPS terminus along the vibrating line.
Rescribe to a **depth of 1–1.5 mm** with the #7 spatula.
Carve PPS smoothly: **deeper posteriorly, shallower anteriorly**.
The processed denture inherits this shape as a positive bead.

blocks.text: PPS dimensions at the trial denture stage

items: Hamular notch area: 1.0–2.0 mm wide × 1.0 mm deep.
Near the hard-soft palate junction: 4.0–6.0 mm wide × 1.5 mm deep.
Mid-palatal area: 2.0–3.0 mm wide × 1.0 mm deep.

blocks.text: House's classification ranks the soft-palate's anatomy by how much functional movement the soft palate makes on phonation ('ah'). Greater movement = less soft-tissue area at rest where the PPS can engage = harder retention. Identify the class BEFORE designing the posterior border + PPS.

items: **House Class I — High vault, broad palate.** Soft palate is mostly horizontal at rest, makes minimal movement on 'ah'. Posterior border can extend ~5 mm past the vibrating line; PPS area is broad + easy to engineer. **Retention prognosis:** excellent.

**House Class II — Medium vault, average movement.** Soft palate has intermediate slope, moves moderately on 'ah'. Posterior border at the vibrating line, PPS area narrower. **Retention prognosis:** good with careful PPS design.

**House Class III — Flat / shallow vault, extensive soft-palate movement.** Soft palate drops sharply (steep angle) and moves significantly on 'ah'. Posterior border MUST be anterior to the vibrating line; PPS area is narrow and resists deep scribing. **Retention prognosis:** difficult — implant retention often indicated.

blocks.title: Identifying House class in 30 seconds
blocks.body: Have the patient open + say 'ah' while you look at the soft palate. Watch the vibrating line move. **Class I:** vibrating line barely moves (1–2 mm), soft palate stays nearly horizontal. **Class II:** moderate movement (3–5 mm), soft palate hinges down at the junction. **Class III:** dramatic movement (6+ mm), soft palate flies down at a steep angle. Document the class on the diagnostic note — your lab Rx for PPS design depends on it.

blocks.text: Greater functional movement of the soft palate makes a denture harder to retain because the seal is harder to keep. Class III patients may need more aggressive PPS or, in some cases, may be better served by implants.
Phonetic problems at try-in or delivery point to specific tooth positions or contours that need adjustment. The vocabulary below maps the sound to the cause to the fix.

**Practice matters.** The patient's tongue and lips need to relearn positions with the new denture. Many transient phonetic issues at delivery resolve over the first 1–2 weeks of consistent reading practice. Adjust only if the issue persists past the 1-week follow-up.

When the patient mispronounces or struggles with a specific sound, the diagnosis usually points to one or two tooth-position errors. Run through each sound at the wax try-in BEFORE processing — corrections in wax are free; corrections in PMMA are remake-territory.

items: **Patient whistles on /s/** → anterior teeth too far forward OR teeth set too widely apart. The air stream accelerates through the narrow gap creating a whistle. **Fix:** move centrals slightly lingual or close interproximal spacing.

**Patient lisps on /s/ (sounds like 'th')** → anterior teeth too far palatal OR vertical overlap excessive. The tongue can't form the narrow /s/ stream. **Fix:** move centrals slightly labial OR reduce vertical overlap.
**Patient slurs /s/** → VDO too high; tongue can't reach the proper position. **Fix:** reduce VDO, recheck.
**Sound is muffled** → maxillary anteriors too far palatal; lower lip can't reach the incisal edge. **Fix:** move centrals labially until lip contact is on the incisal edge.
**Sound is harsh / over-articulated** → maxillary anteriors too far labial; lip wraps over the labial surface. **Fix:** move centrals palatally.
**Patient bites their lower lip** → vertical overlap too aggressive. **Fix:** reduce overlap.
**Patient can't protrude tongue between teeth** → vertical overlap excessive OR maxillary anteriors too palatal. **Fix:** reduce overlap OR move anteriors labially.
**Sound is unclear** → mandibular anteriors too retracted; tongue has no anterior reference point. **Fix:** verify mandibular anterior position relative to ridge.
**Patient struggles to bring lips together** → VDO too high. **Fix:** reduce VDO. This is THE most common VDO-too-high indicator — bilabials don't lie.
**Sound is breathy / weak** → labial flange too thick; lips don't make full contact. **Fix:** thin the labial flange.
**Open-mouth speech (vowels sound nasal or muffled)** → VDO too low; tongue compresses against palate. **Fix:** increase VDO.
**Distorted vowels** → tongue space limited (denture base too thick lingual, or mandibular anteriors set too palatal). **Fix:** verify lingual flange contour + anterior position.

blocks.title: The 'count from 60 to 70' test
blocks.body: Have the patient slowly count 60–70 at the wax try-in. This sequence exercises /s/ (60 'sixty'), /f/, /v/, /th/ ('three', 'four'), bilabials ('seven', 'six'), and vowels. Listen for any sound that requires conscious effort — that's a phonetic problem your tooth setup needs to correct BEFORE processing.

chapters.title: Types of complete dentures & immediate scenarios

blocks.text: the source material recognizes four distinct complete-denture treatment paths, each suited to a different clinical scenario. Which one you recommend depends on how many teeth remain, what the patient can tolerate surgically, and how long they can go without a prosthesis.

items: **Already fully edentulous** → CCD. Standard 7-appointment sequence.

**Few anterior teeth, patient wants one definitive prosthesis** → **CID.** Extract posteriors first, heal 4 wk (retain opposing PMs for VDO), insert at the anterior extraction day. Soft relines → hard reline at 3–4 mo.

**Multiple teeth, esthetics matter, patient can afford two prostheses** → **IID.** Same workflow as CID but with cheaper Trubyte Classic teeth, labeled "Temporary Denture." New CCD at 6–12 mo with full try-in.

**Sound canine roots can be saved, hygiene is good** → **IOD.** Endo + decoronate to dome; fabricate denture over roots. Better bone preservation, retention, proprioception. Root caries is the long-term risk.
**Poor surgical candidate / uncooperative / can't manage immediate workflow** → controlled extractions + 6 mo healing → CCD. Don't push an immediate on someone who can't manage it.
**Severely resorbed mandible (PDI IV), patient values stability** → consider 2-implant overdenture (McGill Consensus 2002) as first-line rather than conventional CD.
**Maintenance of patient appearance** — no edentulous phase.
Muscle support and tone, VDO, jaw relationship, and face height maintained.
Less post-operative pain (denture acts as a splint).
Duplication of existing tooth positions is easier.
Less psychological adaptation for the patient.
Can be relined with tissue conditioners during healing.
Special impression and cast-surgery methods required.
Higher risk of errors in CR record and VDO (no anterior try-in to verify against).
Errors in anterior arrangement — esthetic outcome is unpredictable.
More appointments, higher cost.
Impaired speech and mastication during early healing.
Patients with poor health or significant surgical risk.
Patients uncooperative or unable physically / mentally to manage an immediate denture.
Acute infection requiring resolution before surgery.
Inability to attend the close post-insertion follow-up schedule.
Fit will change as ridges heal — tissue conditioner / adhesives may be required.
Discomfort, soreness expected.
Temporary difficulty eating and speaking.
**Unpredictable esthetics** — you can't trial anteriors in the mouth before extraction.
Increased salivation, new chewing sounds, possible gagging.
**Do NOT remove the denture for the first 24–48 hours.**
Tissue conditioning, relines, adjustments, and a new set of dentures after healing are the patient's financial responsibility.
After healing (6–12 mo), a completely new CCD may be required.
**Diagnostic stock trays + alginate** (same as conventional CD start).
**Type III stone** for diagnostic cast; **Type IV stone** for master cast (high strength for the cast-surgery step).
**Custom tray materials** — Triad VLC + baseplate wax, plus **hard block-out wax** for tooth undercuts (RPD-style block-out).
**PVS impression material** — light body + medium/heavy body, automix gun, intraoral tips, tray adhesive.
**Green stick compound** for border molding (edentulous areas only).
**Cast saw / wax knife / lab handpiece with acrylic burs** for the cast-surgery step (shaving extraction teeth off the master cast).
**Articulator (Mark 320)**, face-bow, bite fork, Regisil PB.
**Tooth mould prescription** — Trubyte Classic for IID (cheaper, designed for the temporary phase); Portrait IPN for CID / IOD (definitive).
**Clear acrylic + Triad sheets** for the surgical guide fabrication.
**Surgical-day materials** — the immediate denture itself, denture adhesive (in case retention is poor day-of-extraction), Tempbond NE, post-op instructions printed.
Identify which teeth go and which (if any) stay. For complete IID: all remaining teeth marked.
Coordinate the extraction date with OS — the immediate denture must be ready BEFORE the extraction visit. Build in lab time.
Pre-extraction photos of patient's natural teeth for shade + position reference. Photograph in repose + full smile + side profile.
Patient consent for the limitations: unpredictable anterior esthetics, no try-in possible, reline schedule, eventual replacement at 6–12 mo (IID) or hard reline (CID).
Plan the same-day flow: OS extracts → student trial-seats the IID → OS sutures → deliver. Reserve a 3-hour slot.

blocks.text: Stage 1 — Diagnostic impressions + first-surgery posterior extractions

items: **1a.** Take preliminary alginate impressions of the existing dentition with stock trays. Pour Type III stone → diagnostic cast that shows the patient's entire current dentition.

**1b.** Treatment plan: identify the teeth being extracted at the first surgical appointment (typically posterior teeth) and the teeth being extracted at the final surgical / delivery appointment (typically anteriors).

**1c. First surgical appointment — remove POSTERIOR teeth.** Address tori, tuberosities, frenum attachments, prominent undercuts at this visit. **Retain opposing premolars where possible** to preserve VDO during healing — without posterior occlusal stops, VDO collapses and the final immediate denture is at the wrong vertical.
**1d.** Heal 3–4 weeks. Patient comes back with posteriors gone, anteriors still in place, ready for the master impression.

blocks.text: Stage 2 — Custom tray fabrication for teeth-present cases

The diagnostic cast still has the patient's anterior teeth in plaster. The custom tray must accommodate those teeth + the now-edentulous posterior ridge, AND release cleanly from the cast (tooth undercuts can lock impression material in place). The differences from a conventional CD custom tray are concentrated in block-out + spacer thickness.

items: **2a. Block out tooth undercuts.** Each remaining tooth has buccal, lingual, and proximal undercuts. Press hard block-out wax into each undercut on the cast, flush with the vertical surface of the tooth. Same technique as RPD block-out. Goal: tray release path is unobstructed.
**2b. Mark the planned tray border** on the cast: 2 mm short of the deepest sulcus all around.

**2c. Lay baseplate wax spacer.** convention: **1 mm (single sheet) over edentulous areas; 2–3 mm (two sheets) over dentate areas**. **Why the difference:** the impression material needs *uniform thickness* across the whole arch when the tray seats. Teeth project 1–2 mm further from the underlying bone than the gingival / alveolar tissue does — so if you used 1 mm of spacer everywhere, the tray would land at 1 mm clearance over the ridge but potentially 0 mm clearance over the teeth (impression material couldn't flow there). The thicker spacer over teeth compensates for their height, so when the tray seats, the impression material is the same thickness everywhere — same setting profile, same distortion, reliable impression. **Secondary reason:** dentate areas use *light body* PVS around the teeth (captures cervical / occlusal detail, no compression of the periodontal ligament), which needs more room to flow. Edentulous areas use *medium / heavy body* for slight functional displacement of the soft tissue (a denture-supportive impression), which needs less standoff — too much standoff and the heavy body sets thick, distorting the ridge form.

**2d. Cut tissue-stop openings** through the spacer at canine + first molar positions. Where teeth are present, the stops can sit on incisal edges or occlusal cusp tips. Where the ridge is already edentulous, stops sit on the ridge crest. Three to four stops total.
**2e. Apply Triad VLC** over the wax. Press firmly so resin contacts the cast (or tooth) through the stop openings — positive seat stops.
**2f.** Trim borders 2 mm short of vestibule. Add anterior handle + finger rests at first-molar locations.
**2g. Cure** 2 min cameo + flip + 1 min intaglio.
**2h. Remove the baseplate wax** from the intaglio.

**2i. Test-seat the tray on the cast.** It should release cleanly along the path of insertion (your block-out wax did its job), seat with ~2 mm uniform clearance from teeth + tissue, and contact the cast firmly at each tissue stop. If it locks, find the un-blocked-out undercut, fix on the cast, re-test.

blocks.title: Why block-out matters for teeth-present trays
blocks.body: Without block-out, impression material flows into the buccal / lingual / proximal undercuts of each tooth and locks the impression onto the patient. You can't remove it without tearing the material or hurting the patient. Block-out wax on the diagnostic cast prevents this — the resulting tray has the right shape to release cleanly. The same logic applies to the master impression: light body PVS will flow into undercuts intraorally and lock onto the teeth unless the technique is right (light body around tooth, set with minimal seating force, remove along the path of insertion with a quick snap).

blocks.text: Stage 3 — Master impression around standing teeth

items: **3a.** Try in the custom tray dry. Verify borders 2 mm short of vestibule, no impingement on frenum, tissue stops contact firmly.
**3b. Border mold the edentulous regions only.** Standard green stick protocol. Don't try to border-mold around standing teeth — the impression material captures the natural gingival sulcus directly.
**3c. Reduce border molding to 0.5 mm** with a scalpel.
**3d.** Apply PVS adhesive to the entire intaglio. Dry 10 min.

**3e.** Syringe **light body PVS** around the standing teeth FIRST — circumferentially around each tooth, into the gingival sulcus, over the occlusal surface. This captures the detail. Then around the edentulous ridge.
**3f. Load tray with medium or heavy body PVS** in the body / palate area. Don't mix bodies in a way that traps air at the interface.
**3g.** Seat the tray with **light pressure**. Don't compress the soft tissues. Hold for 5 minutes through full set.
**3h. Remove with a quick straight pull along the path of withdrawal** (the same direction you blocked out the undercuts for). Slow rocking can distort the impression at the tooth undercut areas.

**3i.** Inspect: each standing tooth captured clean and circumferentially, no voids around the cervical, no torn material, edentulous areas captured clean. Re-impress if any standing-tooth area shows a void.

blocks.text: Stage 4 — Pour master cast + duplicate cast for surgery simulation

items: **4a.** Box the impression (rim with utility wax around the borders, baseplate wax sheet over the top to form a vessel for the pour).
**4b. Pour with Type IV stone.** High strength is needed because the next step physically removes teeth from this cast.
**4c.** Wait 45–60 minutes for full set.
**4d.** Separate impression from cast carefully (soak in warm water first if border molding compound is brittle).
**4e. Trim cast to horseshoe form,** preserve land area + vestibule depth.

**4f. Duplicate the master cast** with alginate impression + second Type IV pour. **You need the duplicate** — the original master cast is preserved as a record of the pre-extraction state, and the duplicate is what you do cast surgery on.

blocks.text: On the DUPLICATE cast, simulate the post-extraction ridge anatomy. This is where the master cast becomes the simulated post-surgical state — you'll set teeth on this modified cast for the immediate denture.

items: **5a.** Mark each tooth to be extracted on the duplicate cast with a pencil.
**5b. For each marked tooth, recess the labial edge to the incisal third mark** — this simulates anticipated post-extraction labial bone resorption (the first 2 weeks of healing).
**5c. Cut down each tooth to the gingival margin level** using the lab handpiece with an acrylic bur OR the cast saw. Work carefully — you're removing plaster down to the simulated ridge.
**5d. Round the labial aspect of each socket** from the gingival margin to the middle third — anticipates ridge contour after healing.
**5e. Round the lingual aspect** of each socket similarly.

**5f. Recess each socket 1 mm** below the rest of the ridge — the actual extraction socket fills with blood clot + heals from the base up, so the immediate prosthesis sits slightly proud of where the final ridge will be.
**5g.** Smooth all transitions with sandpaper or a fine acrylic bur.

**5h.** The cast now simulates the immediate post-extraction state. Note: this is an approximation — actual post-extraction ridges resorb in patterns the cast can't perfectly predict, which is why post-delivery relines are needed.

blocks.title: Cast surgery is approximate, not perfect
blocks.body: The cast-modification step is the best engineering approximation of post-extraction anatomy that can be done before the surgery actually happens. Real ridges resorb in three dimensions, with bone often collapsing more buccally than the cast predicts. Expect to reline the immediate denture with tissue conditioner at 4–6 weeks — the cast simulation gets you 80% of the way there; the soft reline corrects the remaining 20%.

blocks.text: Stage 6 — Face-bow, jaw relation, tooth setup

items: **6a. Face-bow on patient.** The patient still has anterior teeth in front, so the bite fork registers against the existing maxillary teeth + (already-extracted) posterior wax rim. No maxillary record base needed for the dentate area.

**6b. Bite registration in CR.** Use Regisil between the existing posterior teeth (if any retained) or the wax rim, with the patient guided into CR. **CR, not MIP** — immediate dentures are still planned in CR even when natural teeth are present.
**6c. Mount on Mark 320** using the face-bow + bite reg (protocol).
**6d. Set posterior teeth FIRST** on the now-modified cast. Standard tooth setup principles apply for the posterior region.

**6e. Posterior wax try-in.** Verify posterior occlusion, VDO, posterior phonetics. This is the ONLY try-in step for complete IID — the anterior teeth can't be tried in because the patient still has them.

**6f. Set anterior teeth on the cast-surgery cast.** Reference the photos of the patient's natural teeth + their existing tooth positions on the original (un-modified) master cast. Set anteriors to the new midline + smile line based on facial landmarks, not the existing teeth (since those are going).
**6g.** No anterior try-in possible. Document the planned setup with photos for the chart.
**7a.** Duplicate the cast-surgery cast (alginate + Type III pour).
**7b. Vacuform clear acrylic over the duplicate** using a thermoplastic forming machine (or fabricate a stent from Triad clear).
**7c.** Trim the surgical guide to cover the planned post-surgery ridge form only.
**7d.** The OS uses this guide intraoperatively to verify that bone reduction matches the cast-surgery plan. Important when alveoloplasty is needed.

blocks.text: Standard SR Ivocap processing. Lab remount on the cast-surgery cast. Patient name in each prosthesis — for IID, the patient's name is followed by "TEMP" or "Temporary Denture" per convention.

items: **9a.** Patient seated in the OS chair with the surgical team.
**9b.** OS extracts the planned anterior (and any remaining) teeth per the surgical plan. Alveoloplasty as needed, referencing the surgical guide.
**9c. STOP before suturing.** Student dentist trial-seats the immediate denture.
**9d.** Check for sharp acrylic edges or pressure points on the just-extracted sockets. Adjust intaglio with an acrylic bur where needed — small adjustments only at this stage.
**9e.** Once the denture seats cleanly and the sockets aren't being impinged, hand off to OS for hemostasis + suturing.
**9f. Insert the denture for delivery.** Patient may need denture adhesive initially if retention is poor (the just-extracted ridge is swollen and the cast simulation isn't perfectly accurate).

**9g. The 24-hour rule.** **DO NOT REMOVE the denture for 24 hours.** Acts as a hemostatic bandage, shapes the immediate post-extraction ridge, controls post-op swelling. Removing early risks clot dislodgement + tissue collapse.
**9h.** Discharge instructions: soft diet, no rinsing for 24 hours (then warm saline rinses), return for 24-hour follow-up.
**24-hour follow-up:** remove denture for the first time, assess sockets, irrigate, PIP for pressure spots, adjust intaglio, reinsert. Patient now keeps denture out at night.
**1-week follow-up:** sockets healing, soft tissue swelling reducing. More PIP + intaglio adjustment as the denture settles into the actual (not simulated) ridge.
**3-week follow-up:** further adjustments. Patient's ridge has resorbed somewhat; denture may rock slightly.
**4–6 week follow-up:** apply **tissue conditioner (soft reline material)** to the intaglio. Takes up the space created by ridge resorption. Replace tissue conditioner every 2–4 weeks as needed.
**3-month visit:** assess ridge stability. If still resorbing, continue tissue conditioning. If stable, plan for definitive treatment.
**6–12 month definitive plan:**
**IID:** fabricate a new conventional CD (full wax try-in now possible since patient is fully edentulous).
**CID:** hard reline the existing immediate denture as the definitive prosthesis.
**IOD:** hard reline + assess root health; replace if rampant root caries develops.

blocks.title: verdict on the 24-hour rule
blocks.body: *"Do not remove until 24-hour follow-up. Controls swelling — bandage effect."* The denture acts as a splint over the extraction sockets, applying gentle pressure and reducing post-operative edema. Removing it early risks tissue collapse and loss of fit.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Master impression locks on standing teeth at removal:** tooth undercuts weren't blocked enough in tray fabrication, or removal force was too slow/rocking. Light body trapped in undercut tears at removal. Re-pack cord, re-impress with stronger snap pull along the path of withdrawal. **Master cast fractures during cast surgery:** stone was Type III not Type IV, or wasn't given full set time. Re-pour from impression. **Cast surgery sockets don't match the post-extraction reality:** ridge resorbed more aggressively than the cast simulation predicted. Tissue conditioner reline at the first post-op visit to take up the actual ridge contour. **Immediate denture won't seat at day-of-surgery delivery:** usually swelling. Trim intaglio sharp spots with acrylic bur; use denture adhesive if needed; the denture will fit better as swelling reduces over 24–48 hours. If it absolutely cannot seat: reline chairside with tissue conditioner. **Patient pulls denture out at home in the first 24 hours:** ridge starts resorbing in patterns that no longer match the cast. Reline ASAP with tissue conditioner at the 24-hour visit (or earlier). Re-educate on the 24-hour rule. **Anterior tooth setup doesn't match what the patient looks like in pre-op photos:** setup ignored shade/mold reference or set anteriors to wrong midline. Minor differences can be ground chairside; major mismatches require remaking. For IID this is acceptable since the definitive CD will be made at 6–12 mo with a full try-in. **VDO is wrong at delivery:** retained posterior teeth weren't preserved during the first surgery (Stage 1c). Without posterior occlusal stops, VDO drifted during healing. May need remake.

blocks.text: Distinct from the tooth-root overdenture (IOD) covered above. The implant-supported overdenture (sometimes "IOD-implant") sits on 2 or 4 implants placed in the resorbed ridge, retained by mechanical attachments (Locator, ball, bar) rather than soft-tissue suction. **McGill Consensus (2002):** the 2-implant mandibular overdenture is the first-line standard of care for the edentulous mandible — not conventional CD. The surgical workflow lives in the Implants guide; this section covers what the CD-side clinician does.

items: **Locator attachments** — default. Patrix (male) screws into the implant; matrix (female nylon insert) sits in the denture base. Multiple retention strengths via colored inserts (clear, pink, blue, green, red — weakest to strongest). Easy to replace inserts as they wear (every 6–18 months).

**Ball attachments** — older but still in use. Ball patrix on the implant; metal housing + rubber O-ring in the denture. Lower profile than Locator. O-rings wear faster and require frequent replacement.

**Bar-retained** — a precision-milled bar connects the implants (typically 4-implant), with clips in the denture engaging the bar. More retention + stability but takes up more space and is harder to clean.
**Conus / telescope crowns** — precision-milled inner crowns on the implants; outer crowns embedded in the denture. Excellent retention; high cost; lab-intensive.

blocks.text: **The CD-side workflow when implants are already placed + osseointegrated**

items: **Standard CD workflow modified at the custom tray step:** on the diagnostic cast (with healing abutments or locator patrices visible), block out around each abutment / patrix with wax so the custom tray releases cleanly. The tray covers the full ridge as in conventional CD; the attachments live underneath.

**Final impression:** standard PVS technique. The impression captures the implant-level positions (or attachment positions) along with the ridge anatomy. Healing abutments / locator patrices register in the impression directly.
**Master cast pouring:** standard Type IV stone. The cast now has the implant analogs or attachment positions in plaster.
**Face-bow + jaw relation:** standard CD protocol. The patient is edentulous from a denture-fabrication perspective; the implants are below the soft tissue and don't affect face-bow registration.

**Tooth setup + wax try-in:** standard CD protocol on the modified cast. The denture base has space planned for the attachment housings — consult the lab Rx for the exact attachment system + housing dimensions.
**Processing:** standard Ivocap. Lab leaves space for the housings on the intaglio; housings are *not* processed into the denture initially.
**Attachment housing pickup at delivery (or shortly after):** the critical step that distinguishes implant-overdenture delivery from conventional CD delivery.
**Verify the denture seats fully** on the soft tissue without the attachment housings engaged. Adjust intaglio with acrylic bur for any pressure spots (PIP first).

**Place the female matrix (housing) on each implant patrix.** For Locator: snap the metal housing + nylon insert combo onto each Locator patrix. For ball: thread the metal housing + O-ring onto each ball abutment.

**Block out the area immediately around each abutment** on the patient's tissue with rubber dam material or block-out tape, so cold-cure acrylic doesn't flow under the gingiva and lock the denture in.
**Apply cold-cure acrylic** (typically the same pink shade as the denture base) into the corresponding holes on the denture intaglio.
**Seat the denture over the housings + tissue.** Patient bites lightly into MI — do NOT have them bite hard; the cold-cure should fill but not displace the housings.
**Wait full self-cure** 10–15 min.

**Remove the denture from the mouth.** Inspect: each housing should now be embedded in the denture intaglio at the correct position. If any housing pulled out of the denture, repeat the pickup for that one.
**Trim excess cold-cure** with acrylic burs. Polish.
**Test seating + retention.** Each insertion should engage all housings with a positive snap. Retention strength is determined by the insert color (Locator) or O-ring material (ball).
**1-week follow-up:** verify patient is using the attachments correctly (some patients need coaching on the "snap" sensation). PIP if any pressure spots.
**3-month follow-up:** assess attachment wear. Locator inserts last 6–18 months depending on patient hygiene + chewing patterns. Replace as needed.

**Annual recall:** attachment evaluation + replacement (chairside, 5 minutes), peri-implant health check (probing, radiographs), denture base assessment (reline if ridge has resorbed under the denture even though attachments fix the position).

blocks.title: Implant-overdenture vs conventional CD — the patient counseling
blocks.body: Patients often don't understand that an implant-supported overdenture is still a **removable** denture — they take it out at night, brush it like a denture, and the attachments are maintenance items. The implants give it secure positioning, NOT a fixed prosthesis. If the patient wants something that stays in 24/7, that's an implant-supported fixed prosthesis (All-on-X) — a different treatment plan entirely, much higher cost. Have this conversation before referring for implant placement.

blocks.text: When a denture loses fit through ridge resorption or surface wear but the occlusion, VDO, MM relation, esthetics, and teeth are still acceptable, relining or rebasing salvages the prosthesis. When any of those criteria fail, the case is a new denture.

items: **Reline** — resurface the tissue side (intaglio) with new base material; existing denture base retained.
**Rebase** — lab process replacing the entire denture base material; teeth retained in original position.
**Repair** — reassemble fractured pieces or replace a fractured tooth.
Immediate denture 3–6 months after fabrication (initial ridge resorption).
After implant placement (implant-overdenture conversion).
Residual ridge resorption over years of wear.
Socioeconomic constraints precluding a new denture when occlusion is otherwise acceptable.
Abused tissue (except for temporary soft reline as tissue conditioning).
Excessive resorption beyond what reline can rebuild.
Poor esthetics.
Unsatisfactory maxillomandibular relation.
TMJ pain.
Major speech problems.
Worn or broken teeth.
Incorrect occlusal plane.
Severe osseous undercuts requiring surgery.

blocks.text: What reline / rebase can and cannot improve

Reline / rebase only fixes the tissue-side of the denture. Anything wrong with the occlusal side or the cameo surface is outside what these procedures can do. Misdiagnose this and you'll relined-then-remade in the same year.

items: **Loss of tissue adaptation** (ridge resorption since delivery). The intaglio doesn't match the current ridge contour — new acrylic on the intaglio restores adaptation.
**Minor border seal loss** (flange under-extension by 1–2 mm). New border-molded acrylic at the flange restores the seal.
**Minor posterior palatal seal loss** (PPS shallow, suction weak). New acrylic + re-scribed PPS area restores suction.
**Hygiene-related contour issues** (denture rough on the intaglio from cleaning, polish issues). New acrylic is smooth + cleanable.
**Soft-tissue compatibility improvement** (allergic reaction to a specific acrylic formulation can be resolved with a different acrylic at reline).
**Worn occlusal scheme.** If the denture teeth are worn flat, the occlusion can't be restored with reline. **Required:** clinical remount + selective grinding (if minor) OR remake (if extensive).
**Lost VDO.** Vertical dimension was wrong at delivery or has been lost through wear. Reline puts the wrong VDO back into the same position. **Required:** remake.
**Esthetic complaints.** Wrong tooth shade, wrong tooth shape, midline cant, wrong incisal display. Reline doesn't touch the cameo surface. **Required:** remake.
**Tooth fracture or loss.** Replace the tooth (separate procedure); reline doesn't address it.

**Major flange loss** (flange under-extended by 3+ mm or flange completely missing in a region). The required border-molded reline becomes effectively a new flange — a partial remake. **Required:** lab repair OR remake.

**Phonetic problems** (lisping, whistling, lip-bite, gagging from posterior border). These are tooth-position or denture-extension issues that reline doesn't fix. **Required:** remake or major chairside adjustment.

**Patient adaptation issues** (denture-handling errors, dexterity problems, psychological barriers). Reline doesn't change the patient. **Required:** behavioral counseling, caregiver training, or psychological referral.
**Parafunctional damage** (denture fractured from bruxing, accelerated wear). Reline ignores the underlying force. **Required:** nightguard + remake of the damaged denture.
**Material distortion** (denture warped from heat exposure post-delivery). The intaglio is wrong shape; new acrylic on the intaglio reproduces the wrong shape. **Required:** remake.

blocks.title: The 90-day rule
blocks.body: If a denture needs reline within 90 days of delivery, the problem isn't ridge resorption — it's a fit error at delivery that wasn't caught. Diagnose what the actual problem is before relining; otherwise you're masking the symptom and the patient returns at 6 months with the same complaint.

items: **1.** Send patient home with their old denture if available — current denture is going to the lab for 2 weeks.

**2.** Evaluate + adjust the denture chairside before reline (see Denture #7 Adjustment workflow): CC review, IOE + palpate, Thompson stick + PIP paste, posterior palatal seal check, border extension, occlusion.
**3.** Clean the denture thoroughly. If gross, use the ultrasonic machine in the support lab.

**4.** Try the denture into the patient's mouth + note orientation (incisal display, alignment) — you need to position the denture correctly when taking the impression; mis-orientation alters VDO + phonetics.
**5.** Relieve the denture intaglio: use lab burs to remove **0.5 mm uniformly**.
**6.** Shorten the flanges: **1 mm uniformly**.
**7.** Place relief holes: **one relief hole in the center of the maxillary palate**; **no relief hole for mandibular**.
**8.** Load a thin layer (1–2 mm) of **light body PVS** into the denture; use the mixing spatula to roll the light body up + over the flanges.
**9.** Seat the denture in the correct orientation. Have patient bite down. Border mold.
**10.** Confirm gingival shade: check the original lab script for the gingival shade used. If you can't find it, match with the gingival shade guide.
**11.** Submit lab Rx specifying heat-cured acrylic resin, shade (e.g. L199-OR), and posterior palatal seal as indicated.
**12.** Codes: **D5750** (Reline complete max — laboratory) or **D5751** (Reline complete man — laboratory).

blocks.text: **Acrylic fracture:** super-glue reassemble the fractured pieces; stone or lab putty in the intaglio to support the assembly; drill mechanical retention along the fracture line; MMA + repair acrylic; pressure pot 20 min; finish, polish, deliver.
**Tooth fracture:** remove the remainder of the broken tooth; replace with a new denture tooth at the lab.
Chairside repair procedures (what to actually do)

Three repairs you'll see at the clinic, with the actual procedural steps. Patient calls / walks in with a fractured denture — same-day chairside fixes are possible for the first two; the third needs lab work.

items: **Examine the fragments.** If they reapproximate cleanly (no missing chips), proceed with chairside cold-cure repair. If teeth are missing or fragments don't reapproximate, send to lab.
**Reapproximate the two halves with sticky wax** (yellow). Hold them in correct alignment along the fracture line. The wax goes on the cameo (outer) surface only — not the intaglio.
**Pour a stone matrix** of the assembled denture's intaglio with Type III stone. Wait 30 min for full set. This gives you a stable cast that holds the fragments in proper alignment.
**Remove the sticky wax**. The denture halves are now held in alignment by the stone matrix.
**Bevel each side of the fracture line** with an acrylic bur, creating a V-shaped groove ~3 mm wide at the surface, tapered to ~1 mm at the depth. Better mechanical interlock for the repair acrylic.
**Mix cold-cure (self-cure) PMMA** per manufacturer (powder + liquid, dappen dish, dropper). Working time ~2 min before it sets.
**Apply the cold-cure mix into the V-groove** with a small spatula. Overfill slightly. Wet the surface with monomer to bond cleanly to the existing acrylic.
**Allow self-cure** ~5–10 min until hard.
**Finish** with acrylic burs: remove excess, smooth the repair area to match the surrounding contour.
**Polish** the repair with pumice slurry on a rag wheel, then high-shine compound. Repair should be invisible to the patient.
**Test seat in the patient.** Verify fit hasn't changed (it shouldn't have if you matched the fragments correctly). Check occlusion with articulating paper, adjust if needed.

blocks.text: **Repair 2 — Tooth fell out of the denture.**

items: **If the patient has the original tooth in hand** and the socket in the denture is intact: chairside re-attachment is feasible. If the socket is damaged or the tooth is broken, lab work.
**Verify socket fit.** Dry the denture socket, dry the tooth, test-seat. Tooth should sit in the socket in its original position.
**Roughen both surfaces** with an acrylic bur — the back of the tooth (the diatoric / ridge-lap area) AND the socket walls. Creates a chemical bond surface.
**Mix cold-cure PMMA** in the matching shade (Trubyte / Portrait shade A2 or whatever matches the patient's denture).
**Apply cold-cure to the socket**, then press the tooth into place. Overflow slightly — you'll trim later.
**Hold the tooth in position** with finger pressure for ~5 min through initial set.
**Allow full self-cure** 10–15 min.
**Finish + polish** as in Repair 1.
**Test seat + verify occlusion** in the patient.
**If the broken flange piece is intact**: same procedure as midline fracture (Repair 1) — reapproximate with sticky wax, stone matrix, bevel + cold-cure.
**If the broken flange piece is missing or shattered**: send to lab for rebase (rebase indication). Trying to rebuild a flange chairside from cold-cure rarely produces a clinically-acceptable result.

**Either way: investigate the cause.** Recurrent flange fractures suggest occlusal interference, denture-rocking from poor fit, or patient bites against the flange repeatedly. Reline or remake may be more sustainable than repeated repairs.

blocks.title: When repair WON'T work — refer for remake
blocks.body: Multiple denture teeth fractured · cracks through the palate · fracture pattern suggests metal mesh in the denture (some processed dentures have mesh reinforcement; cold-cure won't bond to it) · denture base is significantly worn or thin · patient's VDO has changed and the denture no longer fits even if intact. Repair is a band-aid — if any of these are present, a new denture is the right answer.

blocks.title: Materials for chairside repair
blocks.body: **Cold-cure PMMA** (Caulk Repair Acrylic or equivalent) — powder/liquid system. Shade should match the existing denture base (typically pink shade). **Sticky wax** (yellow, melted with the Hanau torch) for reapproximating fragments. **Type III stone** for the matrix during midline repairs. **Acrylic burs** (cross-cut carbide, fine grit) for trimming + finishing. **Pumice slurry + rag wheel + high-shine compound** for polish.

chapters.title: Wax types used in CD work

blocks.text: The same chapter has you reach for "baseplate wax" one minute and "utility wax" the next without explaining which is which. This page is the decoder. At, the bench typically stocks four waxes (plus a fifth, sticky wax, for tiny anchoring jobs). Inlay wax is also on the bench but used only for cast restorations, not denture work.

items: **Block out an undercut on a tooth (cast)** → **pink hard baseplate wax**.
**Custom tray spacer over the cast** → **pink hard baseplate wax**. *Same material as block-out, just used in sheet form over the cast surface.*
**Build up a short stock tray border** → **red utility wax (rope)**.
**Box an impression before pouring stone** → **red utility wax built up** OR **red/pink boxing-wax strip**.
**Seal a tray edge or fill the tongue space** → **red utility wax**.
**Make a record base (when not using Triad VLC)** → **pink hard baseplate wax**.
**Build a wax rim on the record base** → **pink hard baseplate wax** (stacked sheets or pre-formed wax-rim blocks).
**Cut a wax window to set a denture tooth** → reflow the existing **pink baseplate wax** rim.
**Stick the bite fork to the wax rim before face-bow** → **yellow sticky wax** (small dabs).
**Hold a fallen tooth in place during processing** → **yellow sticky wax** (tiny dot).
**Stabilize a cast on a mounting plate while stone sets** → **yellow sticky wax**.

blocks.title: Why "block-out wax" and "spacer wax" sound different but aren't
blocks.body: Both jobs are done with the same pink hard baseplate wax. "Block-out" describes the *function* — filling an undercut so the impression / tray can release. "Spacer" describes a different function — creating uniform standoff between cast and tray for the impression material. The reason you'll hear both terms in the same chapter is that immediate-denture trays use baseplate wax for both purposes simultaneously: first as block-out in each tooth's undercuts, then as a layered spacer over the whole cast. One material, two roles.

blocks.title: What's on your bench at
blocks.body: Pink hard baseplate wax (in sheets, in a stack or roll), red utility wax (in rope form), and yellow sticky wax (in stick form, melted with the Hanau torch). Boxing wax strips may or may not be stocked depending on the lab session — if not, utility wax substitutes fine. Triad VLC sheets (pink/peach, sometimes ivory) are not wax — they're light-cure resin used for the structural tray body and handles, layered *over* the wax. Inlay wax (blue/green) is on the bench but for cast metal restorations only — you won't touch it during CD work.

blocks.text: The four waxes you'll actually use

items: **Baseplate wax (pink wax sheets):** stiff, pink/red, supplied as 0.05–0.10 inch sheets. **Used for:** record bases, wax rims, occlusal rims, denture-form try-in setups. Melting point ~50–55 °C; softens with mild flame or warm water bath. The workhorse — 80% of your CD wax volume.

**Modeling wax (utility wax / rope wax):** soft, pliable, sticky, supplied in coils. **Used for:** beading the master cast before mounting, adapting wax-rim seal to the cast, building up a posterior palatal seal area, sealing record-base voids. Lower melting point ~35–40 °C; remains pliable at room temperature.

**Sticky wax:** dark amber, brittle when cold but adheres when warmed. **Used for:** spot-fixing fragments together (broken cast repair, holding teeth in position during pickup, repairing fractured denture chairside). NOT a structural material — for short-term spot bonds only. Melting point ~65 °C.

**Lab / boxing wax (red strip wax):** rigid red strip ~5 cm wide. **Used for:** boxing the final impression to create the master-cast pour box. Forms a wax wall around the impression so stone can be poured to controlled height. Melting point ~50 °C; soldering iron preferred over open flame.
**Setting denture teeth:** baseplate wax (pink) on the record base.
**Sealing the wax rim to the cast:** modeling wax / utility wax.
**Boxing the final impression:** lab / boxing wax (red strip).
**Holding a wax-rim section together for try-in:** sticky wax (spot bonds).
**Repairing a fractured denture chairside:** sticky wax to position, then cold-cure PMMA for the structural repair.
**Beading the master cast:** modeling wax around the periphery before pouring the second cast.

chapters.title: Loose denture differential (why won't it stay in?)

blocks.text: Patient's most common complaint, with the broadest differential. The right fix depends on the cause — reline-when-it-should-be-remake wastes a visit and money; remake-when-reline-would-work is worse. This page is the diagnostic ladder.
Diagnostic sequence (use this at the chair)

items: **Listen first.** When did it start being loose? Was it ever good? What activity makes it worst? — their words narrow the differential.
**Inspect without the denture in.** Ridge form, tissue color (denture stomatitis suggests poor fit + hygiene), saliva quality, mobile-mucosa landmarks, frenum positions.
**Inspect with the denture in (static).** Does it suction on insertion? Does the PPS engage? Are borders at the functional sulcus or over/under?
**Inspect with the denture in (function).** Have the patient speak, swallow, smile widely, open wide, push tongue side-to-side. Watch for displacement and identify which movement breaks the seal.
**PIP the intaglio.** Hollow areas (no PIP wiped away) = ridge resorption. Heavy pressure spots = over-extension or occlusal interference reflected to intaglio.
**Articulating paper on the teeth.** Uneven contact = occlusal interference. Even contact = occlusion isn't the cause.
**Boley gauge for VDO.** If VDR−VDO is outside 2–4 mm, VDO is wrong.
**Decide on fix.** Match the diagnostic finding to the cause list below.

blocks.title: When the differential lands on "remake"
blocks.body: VDO is wrong by >2 mm · CR/MIP discrepancy not fixable by remount · borders wrong everywhere · ridge resorption so severe that reline can't restore retention · tissue surface worn through · patient never adapted in 6+ months despite correctly-fitting denture. In all of these, reline is a band-aid that delays the inevitable. Counsel the patient honestly.

blocks.text: 11 causes of a loose denture, ranked by frequency

When a patient walks in saying 'my denture is loose,' run the differential before reaching for any reline material. Reline of an occlusally-failing denture buys ~3 months; reline of a tissue-resorption case buys 1–2 years. Knowing why determines what.

items: **Occlusal scheme drift** (most common at 6–12 months post-delivery). Worn denture teeth shift the cusp-fossa relationship; the patient functions on one side; the denture tilts on the ridge. **Diagnose:** articulating paper shows uneven posterior contact OR worn cusps visible on inspection. **Fix:** clinical remount + selective grinding OR remake if wear is extensive.

**Ridge resorption** (most common at 2+ years post-delivery, or immediately after IID). The bone has remodeled; the denture intaglio no longer matches the ridge contour. **Diagnose:** PIP shows generalized contact loss; tissue is not blanching anywhere. **Fix:** reline (hard or soft) OR remake.

**Lost border seal** (frenum, flange wear, over-relief). The peripheral seal of the denture broken by overzealous adjustment, accidental flange trimming, or tissue change at a frenum site. **Diagnose:** retention test with patient closing + lateral force shows immediate dislodgement; specific area shows no tissue contact at the border. **Fix:** add wax to identify the problem area, lab repair or reline.

**Lost posterior palatal seal** (gradual). The PPS area has resorbed or wax was over-thinned at processing. **Diagnose:** PIP shows no contact at the PPS area; suction is weak under tongue pressure. **Fix:** wax addition to the PPS area; lab cure or chairside hard reline of just that zone.

**Parafunctional behavior** (clenching, bruxing on the denture). Often undiagnosed — patient doesn't know they grind. **Diagnose:** ask about morning soreness, clenching habit; check for masseter hypertrophy; look at wear pattern on the denture. **Fix:** nightguard + behavioral counseling; may need denture remake with reinforced design.

**Xerostomia** (medication, Sjögren's, radiation). Interfacial surface tension is reduced; suction is compromised even with a perfectly-fitting denture. **Diagnose:** ask about dry mouth + medication list; observe tissue dryness. **Fix:** saliva substitute, longer flanges if anatomy permits, implant retention as definitive solution.

**Tongue or facial muscle compensation lost** (post-stroke, dementia, neurologic). The patient previously stabilized the denture with muscle control; that control is gone. **Diagnose:** neurological history; observe tongue rest position. **Fix:** caregiver-supported insertion + cleaning; consider implant retention.

**Hyperplastic tissue / chronic inflammation** under the denture. Tissue has overgrown or chronic inflammation has changed the support contour. **Diagnose:** intraoral exam shows boggy, displaceable tissue; denture seats unevenly on irritated areas. **Fix:** denture removal for 1–2 weeks to allow tissue recovery, tissue conditioner liner, surgical reduction of hyperplastic tissue if persistent.

**Material distortion** (processing error, heat exposure post-delivery). The denture base itself has warped — patient ran it through dishwasher, left it on a heater, etc. **Diagnose:** check the intaglio against the working cast (if available) — gap indicates distortion. **Fix:** remake (distortion is not correctable).

**Patient denture-handling error** (incorrect insertion, biting denture into place, removing with twist instead of break-seal). **Diagnose:** ask the patient to demonstrate insertion / removal. **Fix:** re-instruct on the POI script; have the patient practice.

**Psychogenic** (rare; denture phobia, somatic complaint). All other causes ruled out; denture objectively fits well. **Fix:** referral to psychology or psychiatric evaluation; in some cases a behavior-modification program around denture wear.

blocks.text: Patient calls or walks in within the first 4–6 weeks of delivery with a specific complaint. Don't guess. Match the complaint to the cause to the fix — most are routine adjustments at the 24-hour or 1-week visit.

blocks.title: The 24-hour, 1-week, 1-month, 3-month cadence
blocks.body: Schedule four follow-ups by default: 24-hour (pain check + obvious adjustments), 1-week (PIP + occlusal recheck), 1-month (complaint review + most chronic issues surface here), 3-month (final adjustment + reline-need assessment). Patients who skip follow-ups end up with worse long-term outcomes — they self-adjust by chewing on one side, the denture wears unevenly, and you see them at year 2 needing a remake instead of a tune-up.

blocks.title: Triage rule for the 24-hour follow-up
blocks.body: Patient comes in complaining of everything (loose, sore, hurts to bite, looks bad). Triage in order: **(1) PIP for pressure spots** (relieve any) → **(2) occlusal check + equilibration** (BULL rule) → **(3) border evaluation** (functional movements) → **(4) VDO verification** → **(5) phonetic check**. Address the worst issue first; don't try to fix everything in one visit if it requires decisions like remake. Reassure the patient that adaptation takes 4–6 weeks.

blocks.text: **Complaint by complaint.** Every CD patient returns with at least one complaint in the first month. The pattern is so reliable that you can prepare them in advance — and your job is to triage quickly: which complaints need adjustment, which need patient education, which signal a remake.

items: **'It hurts at the gums in the back' (posterior ridge soreness):** PIP locates the pressure spot; adjust intaglio. If diffuse, suspect occlusal disharmony loading one quadrant — selective grinding.
**'It hurts at my cheek attachment' (buccal frenum):** flange over-extension or impingement; relieve flange at the frenum.
**'It hurts at the back of my mouth, both sides':** retromolar pad or hamular notch impingement; relieve flange.
**'It hurts at the front of my mouth, midline':** incisive papilla or midpalatine torus pressure; relieve intaglio over the papilla.
**'It hurts everywhere':** new wearer adaptation (counsel + persist) OR ill-fitting denture (PIP + relieve OR remake).
**'I can't chew certain foods':** counsel realistic expectations (raw carrots, steak — never as easy as natural teeth); verify occlusion is balanced and posterior teeth aren't worn.
**'Food gets stuck under my denture':** border seal weak; PIP + reline OR check posterior palatal seal.
**'My denture clicks when I talk or eat':** VDO too high (most common) OR posterior teeth out of contact in MIP. Reduce VDO selectively.
**'I bite my cheek / tongue':** insufficient horizontal overlap (cheek bite) or anterior teeth set too lingual (tongue bite). Adjust occlusal scheme.
**'My teeth look too big / too white / wrong shape':** typically only correctable at try-in or remake; counsel that minor adjustments are possible (incisal contouring, surface stains in some cases).
**'My lips look thin / different':** verify labial flange thickness; adding wax then re-evaluating shows what's possible without remake.
**'My teeth show too much / not enough':** occlusal plane height adjustment OR remake if substantial.
**'I can't say S' (whistling or lisping):** see the relevant chapter — typically anterior tooth position issue. Adjust in wax if try-in, otherwise counsel adaptation or remake.
**'My mouth feels full / I talk too thick':** denture base too thick on lingual or palatal; adjust by thinning.
**'I sound like I have something in my mouth':** normal first 1–2 weeks; reassess at 1 month if persistent.
**'I gag when I put my denture in':** posterior border too thick OR too far back. Verify it's anterior to vibrating line; thin posterior border edge.
**'My mouth burns':** allergic reaction (rare) OR residual monomer (cure issue — return to lab) OR ill-fitting denture causing tissue inflammation. Diagnose tissue response.
**'I have a metallic taste':** rare; consider material reaction or oral infection; refer for evaluation.

blocks.text: Patient factors that aren't covered by the standard workflow but show up regularly in clinic. Each one changes the technique or the materials — knowing the modifications saves an appointment.

items: **Behavioral / positional:** sit patient upright (not supine); have them concentrate on slow nasal breathing; have them lift one leg or count backwards from 100 (distraction); salt on the tip of the tongue (interrupts the reflex pathway).
**Tray selection:** smaller tray, posterior-extended only if essential; for stock trays, choose one size smaller than ideal and accept some short extensions you'll border-mold.

**Material technique:** use fast-set alginate or fast-set PVS; load tray light so it doesn't flow back into the throat; seat the tray *posterior first* rather than anterior first (closes off the gag trigger area).
**Pharmacologic:** 20% benzocaine topical on the soft palate 5 min before impression. Some clinics use intranasal lidocaine spray (off-label) or pre-procedural ondansetron (anti-emetic).
**Long-term:** for denture wearers with chronic gag, palate-reduction (horseshoe maxillary denture, removes palatal coverage from the gag-trigger zone) — trade-off is reduced retention.

**Denture retention strategies:** denture adhesive is appropriate as a baseline (not a workaround for poor fit). Patient should sip water frequently. Saliva substitutes (Biotène Oral Balance gel, Oasis spray, MouthKote, BioXtra) used as needed.
**Pharmacologic:** pilocarpine (Salagen, 5 mg PO TID) or cevimeline (Evoxac, 30 mg PO TID) for Sjögren's patients who can tolerate cholinergic side effects. Refer to medicine.
**Soft-lining:** permanent soft liner (Permasoft, Mollosil) can improve retention via compliance with mucosa; replace every 1–2 yr.

**Implant-supported overdenture:** 2-implant mandibular overdenture is the gold standard for severe xerostomia with bone available. Mechanical retention (locator / ball attachments) bypasses the suction problem entirely.
**Hygiene + caries risk:** xerostomic patients have high caries risk on remaining teeth. Fluoride trays / chlorhexidine rinses if any natural teeth remain.

blocks.text: Denture-anxious patient (first-time wearer, or trauma from previous denture)

items: **Pre-treatment counseling:** set realistic expectations — dentures function at 30% of natural dentition; adaptation takes 4–6 weeks; sore spots are expected and adjustable. Patient signs informed consent acknowledging these limitations.

**Trial denture in person, not just photos:** at the wax try-in, give patient a full 15 minutes to wear the trial denture, walk around, drink water, speak. Anxiety often surfaces in the trial visit if you create the space.
**Step the patient through delivery slowly:** show the finished denture out of mouth, let them touch it, walk through the insertion sequence verbally before doing it.
**Adhesive as a confidence aid (NOT a fit substitute):** let the patient use adhesive for the first 1–2 weeks if they want. Counsel that the goal is to wean off as they adapt.
**Quick-access follow-up:** schedule 24-hour and 1-week follow-ups before they leave delivery. Give them the clinic phone number for unscheduled concerns.

**Refer for behavioral support if severe:** patients with prior dental trauma or chronic anxiety benefit from cognitive-behavioral therapy or anxiolytic (short-term diazepam pre-appointment) — coordinate with their physician.

blocks.text: Limited dexterity (arthritis, stroke, Parkinson's, advanced age)

items: **Insertion / removal aids:** denture adhesive helps patients who can't generate firm seating pressure. the esthetic-veneer literaturet-retained attachments (Locator) make removal easier than tight suction.

**Single-arch denture vs. complete:** if the patient can't manage two dentures, prioritize the maxillary (better retention from palate) and consider implant-supported lower if mandibular is essential.

**Hygiene aids:** dishwashing-style denture brush (easier grip than a toothbrush); ultrasonic denture cleaner for at-home (replaces fine manual cleaning); denture-cleaning tablets in a soak cup at the bedside.
**Caregiver involvement:** identify and educate the caregiver if patient lives in assisted living or has cognitive decline. Provide written instructions, large-font, with photos.

blocks.text: **Risk:** medication-related osteonecrosis of the jaw (MRONJ) following extractions. Higher risk with IV (zoledronic acid for cancer patients); lower but real risk with oral (alendronate for osteoporosis). Risk doesn't end when patient stops the drug — bisphosphonates persist in bone for years.

items: **Pre-extraction medical consult mandatory.** Document indication, duration, drug holiday (if any) negotiated with the prescribing physician.
**Atraumatic surgical technique:** minimize bone trauma during extraction; primary closure if possible; antibiotic prophylaxis per protocol.

**Defer immediate denture for high-risk patients:** the cast-surgery + immediate-delivery pressure on the surgical site is contraindicated for IV bisphosphonate patients. Plan delayed delivery after 8–12 weeks of healing under physician supervision.
**Existing denture wearers on bisphosphonates:** monitor for traumatic ulcers (denture-induced ulceration can develop into MRONJ); reline aggressively to ensure no pressure points.

blocks.text: TMD patient considering a new denture

items: **Resolve TMD first if possible.** A new denture made on TMD-altered CR will be wrong as soon as TMD resolves or worsens.
**If chronic / non-resolving TMD:** make the denture at the patient's current functional position (typically MIP, not strict CR). Document this decision.
**Avoid increasing VDO:** TMD patients are sensitive to vertical changes. Match existing VDO closely.
**Nightguard option:** if patient bruxes (common in TMD), incorporate a hard-acrylic nightguard plan from the start.

blocks.title: When to refer rather than treat
blocks.body: Severe TMD with internal derangement → PG prosth or oromaxillofacial pain clinic. Active MRONJ → OMFS. Severe gag reflex with psychogenic component → behavioral / anxiolytic pre-treatment with their physician. Allergy workup → oral medicine + allergist for patch testing. Patient who has had >2 prior failed dentures → investigate root cause (anatomy, neuromuscular, psychogenic) before attempting a third.

blocks.text: The chapter index above is organized by topic; this page is organized by *patient.* Walk into the operatory, identify which pathway your patient is on, then read the linked chapters in the listed order. Several pathways cross into the RPD or Implants guides — references in italics with the guide name spell that out.
Pathway 1 — Fully edentulous for years, conventional CD
Patient has been wearing an old denture or no denture; ridges are healed; no anterior or posterior teeth remaining. The canonical 7-appointment workflow with no surgical phase.

**Reading order:** diagnostic impression → custom tray → border molding → final impression → boxing + record bases + wax rims → face-bow → mounting → VDO → CR registration → tooth selection → tooth setup → articulation scheme → wax try-in → processing → delivery + PIP.
Pathway 2 — Has remaining teeth, needs them out + temporary denture (IID)
The most common scenario at. Patient has anterior teeth (and maybe a few posteriors); plan is to extract anteriors at the surgical visit and deliver the immediate denture same-day.

**Reading order:** in full (Stages 1–10). Cross-refs to: for boxing/pouring the master cast, for tooth setup on the modified cast, for processing, and for the post-delivery PIP. Add for the 24-hour follow-up complaint triage.
Pathway 3 — Upper edentulous + lower partially edentulous (CD + RPD combination)

Patient has no upper teeth but retains some lower teeth (typical Kennedy III or distal-extension case). Treatment is two prostheses in parallel: complete upper denture + lower removable partial. Coordinate VDO and occlusion across both arches simultaneously.
**Reading order — CD side:** → → → → → face-bow → mounting (against the lower RPD framework + wax rim) → setup → delivery.

**Reading order — RPD side:** *RPD Deep Dives guide:* face-bow → mount diagnostic casts → survey cast → custom tray → single-step impression → design form → lab Rx → PIP + Mizzy → occlusal adjustment → POI delivery script.

**Coordination:** take the face-bow + bite registration with the upper denture base + lower RPD framework with wax rim in place simultaneously. Mount both casts together. Tooth setup on the upper denture references the lower RPD's wax rim for occlusal plane orientation. Process both in parallel; deliver both at the same appointment.
Pathway 4 — Existing denture loose / patient complaint
Patient walks in with a complaint — loose, painful, food impacting, sores, can't speak. Diagnose first, fix second.
**Reading order:** loose-denture differential OR post-delivery complaints (depending on complaint) → for chairside PIP + occlusal adjustment → for reline / rebase / repair if diagnosis points there.
Pathway 5 — Patient wants implant-supported lower denture

Patient has been wearing a conventional lower denture and hates the looseness, OR is presenting for new-denture treatment and asks about implants. 2-implant mandibular overdenture is the McGill Consensus first-line for resorbed lower ridges.

**Reading order:** for the "types" decision tree (CCD vs IID vs IOD vs IOD-on-implants) → *Implants guide* for the surgical workflow (consult, surgical placement, healing, attachment-level impression, attachment loading) → back to CD: – modified to incorporate the implant-attachment housings in the denture base.

**Key modifications for the CD steps:** custom tray must accommodate the healing abutments (blocked out with wax on the cast); the final impression captures the implant-level positions; the denture base has space for attachment housings (Locator, ball, bar) which are added at delivery or shortly after via chairside pickup with cold-cure PMMA.

Patient has multiple lost teeth, failing existing restorations, possibly endo issues, and wants a comprehensive plan. Multi-disciplinary by definition: phase 1 (caries control, perio, endo), phase 2 (extractions, prosth foundation), phase 3 (final restorations). Each phase is months long.

**Reading order — planning phase:** *Indirect guide* escalation continuum + material selection matrix + restorability assessment frameworks → CD for denture options if any arch becomes edentulous → *RPD guide* design form for any partial planning.

**Reading order — execution phase:** chapter-by-chapter through the appropriate guide for each procedure (fixed work in Indirect Parts 1–5, removable work in CD/RPD), with the CD edge-case management consulted for any patient factors (gag, xerostomia, anxious, etc.) that affect the whole plan.
Pathway 7 — Sound canine roots, IOD candidate

Patient has 2–4 sound roots remaining (typically canines) with good periodontal support. Retain the roots for an overdenture rather than extracting. Better bone preservation, retention, and proprioception than a conventional CD.
**Reading order:** IOD section → endo treatment + decoronation of retained roots (refer to endo) → – with the custom tray accommodating the decoronated root domes (block out with wax on the cast).
Patient has never worn a denture or has had a traumatic prior experience. Behavioral + expectation management is the dominant treatment factor.
**Reading order:** edge-case patient management (denture-anxious section) FIRST → then the standard CCD or IID pathway depending on case → post-delivery complaints for early-adaptation support.
Sjögren's, post-radiation, polypharmacy. Retention is the dominant challenge.
**Reading order:** xerostomia section → standard CCD pathway with modifications (consider 2-implant overdenture for mandibular) → loose-denture differential at follow-ups.
Patient walks in with a fractured denture (midline, tooth fell out, broken flange).

**Reading order:** repair procedures (chairside midline / tooth / flange) → if remake indicated, the standard CCD pathway. If recurrent fractures suggest poor fit, also consult loose-denture differential to fix the root cause.

blocks.title: How to use this page
blocks.body: Identify which pathway matches your patient. Read the linked chapters in order — each click jumps to the chapter (chapter references are hyperlinked in oxblood). Stop reading any chapter as soon as it stops applying to your specific case; the pathways list is a *study order*, not a procedural script. Faculty checkpoints and clinical judgment always supersede the listed sequence.

chapters.title: Attachment systems for implant overdentures + tooth-supported overdentures

blocks.text: Overdentures retain via attachments rather than tissue suction. The choice of attachment determines retention force, ease of replacement, and the chairside maintenance schedule. Five systems dominate clinical practice; each has its place and none is universally best.
Locator (the current default for 2-implant mandibular overdenture)

items: **Construction:** Patrix (male) screws into the implant; matrix (female nylon insert) sits in the denture base. Multiple retention strengths via colored inserts (clear, pink, blue, green, red — weakest to strongest).
**Retention range:** 1.5–5 lb (mild to extra-strong).
**Pickup:** at delivery or chairside reline. Block out the patrix path with the supplied black processing inserts during pickup so the nylon doesn't get caught in PMMA.

**Replacement schedule:** nylon inserts wear and lose retention; replace every 6–12 months on average heavy use, 12–24 months light use. Patient can be taught self-replacement with proper instruction.
**Strengths:** low vertical profile (works in tight inter-arch space), forgiving of divergent implant angulations (up to 40° between two implants), retention adjustable.
**Weaknesses:** nylon inserts wear out (recurring maintenance cost), can over-retain causing housing fracture if too strong an insert is used.

blocks.text: Ball attachment (older standard, still in use)

items: **Construction:** Ball patrix screws into the implant; matrix is a metal housing with a retentive cap that grips the ball.
**Retention range:** 2–4 lb. Adjusted by squeezing the metal cap closer to the ball (more retention) or spreading it (less).
**Pickup:** at delivery with cold-cure PMMA around the metal housings.
**Strengths:** simpler than Locator, cheaper per attachment, durable metal-on-metal connection.

**Weaknesses:** higher vertical profile (needs more inter-arch space), less forgiving of angulation differences (>10° between implants causes binding), retention adjustment is mechanical (squeeze) and irreversible.
**Construction:** A bar connects 2 or more implants. Clips in the denture base engage the bar for retention.
**Retention range:** Variable; typically 4–8 lb (strong).
**Indications:** 4+ implants, severely resorbed ridge where stress distribution matters, cases requiring rigid splinting of implants.
**Pickup:** lab-fabricated, picked up in the denture at delivery.
**Strengths:** splints implants together (load distribution), high retention, durable.

**Weaknesses:** lab cost (custom bar fabrication), takes more inter-arch space than studs (Locator/ball), more difficult to clean (requires interproximal brushes around the bar), patient must remove the prosthesis daily to clean the bar.
**Construction:** A magnetic keeper (patrix) is attached to the implant abutment or root cap. The denture contains a complementary magnet (matrix).
**Retention range:** 1–2 lb (low).
**Indications:** Limited use — patients with very low dexterity who cannot manage higher retention forces, or as backup retention for tissue-supported dentures with sound canine roots.
**Strengths:** Easy seat/release. Low force on abutments.
**Weaknesses:** Corrodes in oral environment over years (replace at 5–7 years). Low retention compared to other systems; not adequate for primary retention on most cases.
**Construction:** Stud patrix on the implant; rubber O-ring or plastic insert in the denture matrix.
**Retention range:** Adjustable via insert color (similar to Locator).
**Indications:** Older or budget alternative to Locator. Some practices prefer the simpler design.
**Strengths:** Inexpensive, simple chairside replacement of inserts.
**Weaknesses:** Less forgiving of angulation than Locator. Some studies show shorter insert lifespan.
**2-implant mandibular overdenture with parallel implants in canine regions:** Locator (default) or ball.
**2-implant mandibular with >10° angulation between implants:** Locator (handles up to 40° divergence).
**4-implant overdenture or severely resorbed mandible:** Bar attachment.
**Patient with very low dexterity:** Locator with weakest nylon (clear), magnet attachments if needed.
**Patient with retained natural canine roots (tooth-supported overdenture):** Custom-cast copings + ball or O-ring attachments. Locators can fit on root copings too.

blocks.title: The maintenance reality
blocks.body: Implant overdentures have a hidden chairside-time cost: insert replacement, housing relines, attachment cleaning. Expect a recall visit every 6–12 months for these patients. Budget 20–30 minutes per visit for routine attachment maintenance. The patient should be counseled at delivery that this is part of owning an implant overdenture; surprise charges at year 1 cause complaints.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Insert tears during seating:** the patrix has a sharp edge (worn) or the insert size is wrong. Replace with new insert; if recurs, replace the patrix. **Denture rotates around one attachment:** the other attachment is non-functional (worn insert, housing crack, abutment screw loose). Diagnose and fix the non-functional side. **Retention force decreases over months:** normal nylon wear. Replace inserts with same color first; only escalate to higher retention if patient reports inadequacy. **Patient cannot seat denture independently:** retention is too high for their dexterity. Step down insert color one level.

blocks.text: *Where this fits in the workflow:* Used in cd-implant-supported-lower, cd-iod-canine-roots, cross-cd-iod-implants, and rpd-implant-hybrid pathways. Pickup of attachments references (processing) and (reline/rebase/repair) for the chairside technique; ongoing maintenance lives in (complaints).*

Tissue conditioners are soft, pliable materials applied to the intaglio surface of a denture chairside to relieve soreness, condition irritated tissue before final impression, or serve as an interim liner between fabrication phases. They're not the same as a lab-cured permanent soft liner — these are short-term materials measured in weeks, not years.

items: **Coe Comfort (GC America):** tissue conditioner per the clinic Materials reference. Powder + liquid, mixed chairside. Pliable for ~4–6 weeks. The clinic default for IID transition relines + chronic soreness conditioning.
**Coe Soft (GC America):** soft reline material per the clinic Materials reference. Use when a longer-lasting soft surface is needed (1–3 months pliability).
**GC Reline:** hard reline material per the clinic Materials reference. Used for definitive chairside hard reline.
**Visco-gel / Lynal (general knowledge, not on clinic Materials reference):** historical tissue conditioners similar to Coe Comfort. Clinic may differ from these specific brands.

**Patient with acute soreness in a single area (1-week complaint):** Coe-Soft or Lynal, spot-applied with PIP-identification first to localize the area. Often resolves the spot soreness in 24–48 hours.

**IID patient at 2-week post-op:** Coe-Soft or COE-Comfort full reline of the IID intaglio. Conditions the tissue as the ridge remodels; replaced every 3–4 weeks until ready for hard reline at 3–6 months.

**Patient with poor tissue health (chronic inflammation under existing denture):** apply tissue conditioner full intaglio, replace weekly for 2–3 weeks to allow tissue to heal before final impression for new denture or reline.
