**D9230** — Inhalation of nitrous oxide / analgesia (if used).
**Continuation of D5213 / D5214** — no separate code at try-in (bundled).
**D2950** if a build-up was added to an abutment chairside (rare).
**Continuation of D5213 / D5214** — no separate code.
**Document patient sign-off** in chart for medico-legal purposes.
**D5213 / D5214** — final completion (the milestone code triggers full insurance billing).
**D9430** — office visit for observation, no other service performed (for 24-hour and 1-week follow-ups).
**D5670** — replace all teeth and acrylic on cast metal framework, maxillary (only if remake at this stage).
**D5671** — same, mandibular.
**D5511** — Repair broken complete denture base, maxillary.
**D5512** — Repair broken complete denture base, mandibular.
**D5520** — Replace missing or broken teeth — complete denture (each tooth).
**D5611** — Repair resin denture base, mandibular.
**D5612** — Repair resin denture base, maxillary.
**D5621** — Repair cast framework, mandibular.
**D5622** — Repair cast framework, maxillary.
**D5630** — Repair or replace broken clasp.
**D5650** — Add tooth to existing partial denture.
**D5660** — Add clasp to existing partial denture.

blocks.title: Insurance milestone billing — when to submit
blocks.body: Most insurance plans pay D5213 / D5214 on a 50/50 split between initial-impression milestone and delivery milestone. Submit the initial portion when the final impression is taken (Appointment 3) and the delivery portion when the prosthesis is delivered (Appointment 6). Submitting all at once delays payment or risks denial. Confirm with your billing office which codes trigger which payment percentage in your specific cohort year.

blocks.text: The mistakes students reliably make at each appointment. Use as a pre-appointment sanity check.

items: Treatment plan approved in Axium? Without approval you can't submit a lab Rx.
Phase I (perio + caries) verified complete? Patient may need a re-eval if not recent.
Schedule enough time — 2 hours minimum, 3 if you're new to facebow.
Instructor signed off on the design? Without sign-off, you don't know if the rest seat / guide plane geometry is correct for the planned crowns.
Survey crown Rx submitted to lab — and you have a return date confirmed.
Provisional matrix made on the diagnostic cast, ready to go.
Shade selected BEFORE you dry the tooth at the appointment.
**Custom tray delivered.** Try it on the cast dry — does it seat without rocking? Are the tissue stops present?
**Practice tooth preparations done on the duplicate cast.** You should have rehearsed exactly where each guide plane and rest seat goes.
**Plenty of impression material on the cart.** PVS light + medium/heavy + adhesive + spray. Running out mid-impression is a disaster.
**3 hours scheduled.** Don't try to compress this.
**Inspect the framework on the master cast BEFORE the patient arrives.** Rests seating fully? Guide planes flat against the proximal plates? No porosity in the casting?
If Kennedy I/II: altered cast materials ready (Vaseline, baseplate wax, Triad, Pattern resin backup, green stick, PVS adhesive, light/medium PVS).
Vita shade guide + Trubyte mold catalog available for chairside mold/shade selection.
**Wax try-in delivered + inspected on cast.** Teeth set per the planned arrangement?
**Hand mirror** for the patient to evaluate esthetics.
**Mentally run through the standard 7 parameters** before the patient arrives — know what you're going to evaluate.
**RPD inspected on master cast.** Does the partial match the design? Clasps engaging the right undercuts?
**POI handout printed.**
**PIP + Mizzy + AccuFilm + acrylic burs + polishing wheels all at chairside.**
**Time for adjustments scheduled.** Don't book this back-to-back with another patient — delivery + first adjustment takes time.

blocks.text: The four mistakes that cost the most time

items: Designing without mounting. You can't survey a cast that isn't mounted on the articulator. Mount first, always.
Skipping the practice prep on the duplicate cast. Discovering at appointment 3 that the planned rest seat location won't work in the patient's actual tooth = re-doing the design.
Letting the lab make the framework without verifying the wax-up of the survey crown(s) first. If the survey crown is wrong, the framework is wrong.
Pressing on the distal extension areas during altered cast impression. This single error invalidates the entire altered cast technique.

blocks.text: Flexible (Valplast / nylon thermoplastic) RPDs — what to know

**Not taught at undergrad** but commonly seen in private practice and asked about by patients. Flexible RPDs are made from injection-molded nylon thermoplastic (Valplast is the dominant brand; Duraflex and others are similar). They differ fundamentally from cast Co-Cr framework RPDs in indication, design, and durability.

items: **Indication:** esthetic anterior partials where metal clasps would show; patients allergic to monomer (PMMA) or metals; transitional / interim partials while planning a definitive solution; patients who reject metal clasps.
**Material:** nylon thermoplastic — flexible, tooth-colored, no metal. Clasps are tooth-colored extensions of the base material.

**Design differences from cast Co-Cr:** no rigid metal framework, no rest seats (the material flexes under load), no precision-machined parts. Retention comes from flexibility of the clasps engaging undercuts. No major connector in the traditional sense — the whole base flexes.
**Pros:** esthetic (no visible metal), comfortable initially (flexes with tissue), no metal allergy concern, lower lab cost than cast Co-Cr.

**Cons:** **no occlusal rest support** — load goes entirely onto the soft tissue → faster ridge resorption. Cannot be adjusted as easily (the material doesn't respond to acrylic burs the way PMMA does — trim with scissors or specific tools). Can't be relined chairside. Tooth-colored clasps stain over time and may lose engagement. Considered a temporary / interim solution by most academic prosthodontists.
**Standard position:** Co-Cr is the curriculum standard. If a patient specifically requests flexible, refer to faculty or consider as a transitional option only.

blocks.text: **If a patient comes in with an existing flexible partial:** don't try to repair it with cold-cure PMMA — the nylon doesn't bond chemically to PMMA. Replacement or lab-side repair (the lab can re-injection-mold the same material) is the correct path.

chapters.title: Wax types used in RPD work

blocks.text: RPD work uses several waxes for different purposes — surveying block-out, custom-tray spacer, framework wax-up, occlusion rims, tray edges. This page is the decoder. The bench stocks the same waxes as CD work; the differences are which ones get used heavily (block-out is a much bigger deal for RPD than CD).

items: **Block out an unwanted undercut on the cast for surveying** → **pink hard baseplate wax**, shaved flush with the surveyor's wax-knife tip (parallel to path of insertion).
**Block out a tooth's undercut for custom-tray fabrication** → **pink hard baseplate wax**, pressed into the undercut on the cast, trimmed flush by hand.
**Custom tray spacer over teeth + edentulous areas** → **pink hard baseplate wax** (1 sheet over distal extensions, 2 sheets over tooth-supported areas).
**Build a record base or occlusion rim** → **pink hard baseplate wax** (stacked sheets, contoured to ridge form).
**Build up a short stock tray border for the diagnostic impression** → **red utility wax (rope)**.
**Box the master impression before pouring** → **red utility wax** built up, OR **red/pink boxing-wax strip**.
**Seal a tray edge that's leaking impression material** → **red utility wax**.
**Tack the bite fork to the maxillary wax rim before face-bow** → **yellow sticky wax** (small dabs).
**Stabilize a cast on a mounting plate while stone sets** → **yellow sticky wax**.
**Add wax to a metal framework before bite registration** → **pink hard baseplate wax** melted onto the framework.

blocks.title: Why "block-out wax" and "spacer wax" sound different but aren't
blocks.body: Both jobs are done with the same pink hard baseplate wax. "Block-out" describes the *function* — filling an undercut so the framework / tray / impression can release. "Spacer" describes a different function — creating uniform standoff between cast and tray. The reason you'll hear both terms in custom-tray chapters is that the tray uses baseplate wax for both purposes simultaneously: first as block-out in each tooth's undercuts, then as a layered spacer over the whole cast. One material, two roles.

blocks.title: Surveying block-out vs. custom-tray block-out
blocks.body: **Surveying block-out** is the precise version: wax pressed into undercuts on the master cast and shaved flush by the wax-knife tip mounted in the surveyor's vertical spindle. The shave is parallel to the path of insertion — critical for the framework to seat passively. **Custom-tray block-out** is the less-precise version: wax pressed into undercuts and trimmed flush by hand. Good enough for the tray to release; the framework design hasn't been finalized at this stage anyway.

blocks.text: The four waxes you'll actually use

items: **Baseplate wax (pink wax sheets):** stiff, pink/red, 0.05–0.10 inch sheets. **Used for:** wax rims on RPD record bases, the wax setup of denture teeth on the framework at wax try-in. Melting point ~50–55 °C. Same material as CD baseplate wax.

**Inlay wax (blue lab wax):** rigid, high-melting (~63 °C), holds carved detail. **Used for:** the wax pattern of the cast Co-Cr framework before investing and casting. The framework as designed is carved in this wax on the refractory cast, then invested in a casting ring and burned out for casting.

**Utility / modeling wax (sticky rope wax):** soft, pliable, sticky. **Used for:** beading the master cast before duplicating to refractory, sealing the duplicating ring during impression of the master cast for refractory pour, sealing impression undercuts before pour.

**Sticky wax:** dark amber, brittle when cold but adheres when warmed. **Used for:** chairside spot fixes (broken clasp positioning during repair, holding wax-up parts together temporarily, tacking framework sections during a lab repair). Not structural.
**Setting denture teeth on the framework:** baseplate wax (pink) on the framework + retentive grid.
**Carving the framework pattern for casting:** inlay wax (blue) on the refractory cast.
**Sealing the duplicating impression to ring:** utility wax.
**Chairside clasp repair holding wires in position:** sticky wax, then embed in cold-cure PMMA.

blocks.text: Patient walks in with a fractured clasp, broken acrylic, missing tooth, or fractured framework. Five repair scenarios with the decision-tree for chairside vs lab vs remake.
Repair 1 — Fractured clasp (most common RPD repair)

items: **Examine the broken clasp.** Is the arm fractured at the body (rare) or at the tip (more common)? Is the rest seat intact on the abutment? Is the proximal plate / minor connector intact?

**If the clasp arm is broken at the tip and the rest seat is intact:** chairside replacement with a **wrought-wire clasp** is possible. Bend 18-gauge stainless steel wrought wire to match the new clasp path, embed in cold-cure acrylic added to the acrylic base, engage 0.02" undercut at gingival 1/3.

**If the clasp arm is broken at the body or the framework metal itself is broken:** chairside repair won't hold. Send the framework + master cast (if available) to the lab. Co-Cr fractures are joined by laser welding (conventional dental solder doesn't bond reliably to Co-Cr); gold/Pd frameworks can be soldered conventionally. The lab may re-cast the broken segment if the geometry is distorted.
**If the rest seat is damaged or the abutment tooth has changed:** the survey was on the old state. New survey + framework design may be needed. Investigate before repairing.
Remove the broken cast clasp arm with a separating disc, leaving the body + minor connector intact.
Cut 18-gauge wrought wire to ~20mm. Bend with #139 pliers to match the path of the original clasp (suprabulge approach → engage gingival 1/3 undercut).
Roughen the end of the wire that will embed in the acrylic with diamond bur (mechanical retention for the acrylic).
Mix cold-cure PMMA. Apply to the acrylic base where the wire will embed.
Insert the wire into the acrylic; hold in correct position until set (~10 min).
Trim + polish the acrylic around the wire.
Test seat on the patient — wire engages undercut, framework still seats passively, occlusion unchanged.

blocks.text: Repair 2 — Broken denture tooth on the RPD

items: **Same procedure as CD "tooth-out-of-denture" repair** (see CD Repair 2).
Verify the new tooth shade matches the surrounding RPD teeth + remaining natural teeth.
Re-attach with cold-cure PMMA in matching shade; finger pressure during set; trim + polish.
Verify occlusion + interproximal contact on the patient.

**If the fracture is along the major connector (acrylic portion):** same midline-fracture protocol as CD Repair 1. Reapproximate with sticky wax, stone matrix, bevel V-groove, cold-cure PMMA, finish + polish.

**If the fracture is in the lattice / saddle area:** chairside repair is harder because the lattice has metal embedded. Adhesion of cold-cure to metal is poor without surface preparation. Send to lab if reinforced repair is needed.
**Recurrent acrylic fractures:** investigate the cause. Usually the framework is bending due to inadequate cross-arch stabilization (Class IV problem). May need framework redesign.

blocks.text: Same indications and contraindications as CD reline (see CD). For RPD, the reline is **only on the acrylic base portions** (distal extensions, edentulous areas under the saddle). The framework metal + clasps + rest seats are NOT modified during reline.

items: **Chairside soft reline (Coe-Soft, Visco-gel):** appropriate for tissue conditioning over 2–4 weeks before definitive reline.
**Lab hard reline:** remove existing acrylic from intaglio, take wash impression with PVS through the cleaned base, send to lab for processed reline. 2-week turnaround.

**Critical:** verify the framework still seats passively on the abutments before relining. If the framework has distorted (e.g., dropped patient onto the floor), reline doesn't fix the framework problem — new framework needed.

blocks.text: **Almost always remake or lab repair.** Chairside techniques can't restore a broken Co-Cr framework. Two options:

items: **Lab welding (laser) at the fracture line:** Co-Cr does not solder reliably with conventional dental solder — the surface oxide layer interferes with the bond. Modern labs laser-weld Co-Cr at the fracture site (segments fit to the master cast, then joined). Cost is ~half a new framework but only viable when the break is at a connector with clean, undistorted geometry. Gold/Pd frameworks are still solderable in the conventional sense.

**Remake the framework:** for fractures at clasps, rest seats, multiple sites, or any case where the framework geometry has distorted, the lab repair won't survive. New impression + new framework. Patient may need an interim Valplast or flipper during the 2–3 wk turnaround.

**Investigate the cause:** framework fractures suggest excessive flex (Class I/II distal extensions can stress the major connector), trauma (drop), or metal fatigue from years of use. Adjust the design when remaking if a design flaw is identified.

blocks.title: When repair WON'T work — refer for remake
blocks.body: Multiple clasps fractured · framework distorted (no longer seats passively) · abutment tooth lost · major connector cracked · rest seat damaged on abutment tooth · acrylic base extensively worn or thin · patient's arch has changed (additional teeth lost) requiring a different Kennedy class design.

blocks.title: Materials for chairside RPD repair
blocks.body: **Cold-cure PMMA** in matching pink + tooth shades (Caulk Repair Acrylic; Lucitone). **18-gauge stainless steel wrought wire** for replacement clasp arms (Dentsply / Hu-Friedy stock). **#139 pliers** for wire bending. **Diamond bur** for roughening wire + acrylic surfaces. **Sticky wax** for reapproximating fragments. **Type III stone** for cast matrix. **Acrylic burs** (cross-cut carbide) + **pumice / rag wheel / high-shine compound** for finish + polish.

blocks.text: Mirror of the CD guide's. Identify which pathway your patient is on, then read the linked chapters in the listed order. Cross-guide references in italics with the guide name spell out where to go in the CD / Indirect / Implants guides.
Pathway 1 — Kennedy III gap (anterior or posterior tooth-bounded)
Tooth-bounded edentulous span. Most straightforward RPD case — tooth-supported, no altered cast technique needed.

**Reading order:** face-bow record → mount diagnostic casts → survey the cast → custom tray → single-step impression (19-step) → design form → lab Rx → PIP + Mizzy at delivery → occlusal adjustment → POI delivery script.
Pathway 2 — Kennedy I or II (distal extension)
Tissue-supported distal extension at one or both posterior segments. Altered cast technique is mandatory to capture the distal tissue under functional load.
**Reading order:** – (same diagnostic + impression flow as Kennedy III) → altered cast impression (12-step) → design form → lab Rx → – delivery flow.
Pathway 3 — Abutment tooth needs a crown first (survey crown sequence)

Diagnostic survey shows the planned abutment lacks usable undercut, or has a non-parallel guide plane, or has a defective restoration. Crown the abutment with surveyed contours before fabricating the RPD framework.

**Reading order:** – (mount + survey diagnostic cast first to determine the planned crown contours) → survey crown prep specifics — the crown appointment is sequenced before the rest of the RPD workflow → *Indirect guide * survey crown (the chairside crown prep + cementation workflow) → back to RPD: custom tray (now using the cemented survey crown as one of the surveyed abutments) → standard sequence from there.
Patient brings in an RPD with one clasp fractured. Quick chairside repair is feasible for single fractured cast clasps; multi-clasp failures suggest the framework itself is failing.

**Reading order:** RPD repair procedures — Repair 1 covers the chairside wrought-wire replacement protocol. If repair won't work, the same chapter's "when repair won't work" callout walks you through the remake decision.
Pathway 5 — Existing RPD loose / sore tissue
Patient complains of looseness, soreness, food impacting under saddle, or framework instability in a previously-fitting RPD.

**Reading order:** PIP + Mizzy diagnostic → pressure-point map (common sites for over-extension) → occlusal adjustment → reline section (Repair 4) if the diagnosis points to ridge resorption under the saddle.

Patient missing a maxillary central or lateral incisor; doesn't want a bridge or implant. Esthetic RPD with anterior pontic is the workflow. Class IV bound by tooth on each side → tooth-supported, no altered cast needed.

**Reading order:** – standard sequence → design form — pay particular attention to esthetic clasp choices (Reverse Akers, T-bar, or tooth-colored alternative) → lab Rx with explicit esthetic notes → the 7 esthetic parameters — the anterior tooth shade + mold + position decisions live here → – delivery flow.
Pathway 7 — Patient with implants wants a partial

Patient has some implants (placed for missing single teeth or as planned overdenture support) and now wants an RPD that incorporates the implants for retention. Implant-RPD hybrid — non-standard at undergrad but seen in practice.

**Reading order:** *Implants guide* sections on implant-level impression + attachment loading (mirrors the implant-overdenture workflow but with an RPD instead of a complete denture) → – standard impression flow modified to capture implant positions → design form with implants treated as additional "abutments" (locator or precision attachment retention rather than clasps).
Pathway 8 — Existing flexible (Valplast) RPD needs repair
Patient brings in a nylon thermoplastic partial (Valplast / Duraflex). These differ fundamentally from cast Co-Cr frameworks and require lab-side repair via re-injection.

**Reading order:** flexible RPD section (under common pitfalls). Cold-cure PMMA does NOT bond to nylon — chairside repair won't hold. Send to lab. Discuss with patient whether to remake as Co-Cr or continue with flexible.
Pathway 9 — New patient never had an RPD
First-time partial wearer. Expectation management + delivery education are as important as the technical workflow.

**Reading order:** standard pathway 1 or 2 depending on Kennedy class → POI script in full (the delivery counseling details) → schedule extra follow-up visits at 24-hour, 1-week, and 1-month intervals to support adaptation.
Pathway 10 — TMD patient considering an RPD
Patient with chronic temporomandibular dysfunction or bruxism wants a partial. Treatment planning has to accommodate the TMJ + neuromuscular constraints.

**Reading order:** *CD guide * edge-case patient management (TMD section — the same principles apply for RPD as for CD) → standard pathway 1 or 2, with explicit attention to: resolving TMD before final framework fabrication when possible, mounting in patient's functional position (MIP rather than CR if TMD prevents stable CR), avoiding VDO changes, building a nightguard concurrently with the RPD.

blocks.title: How to use this page
blocks.body: Identify which pathway matches your patient. Read the linked chapters in order — each click jumps to the chapter (chapter references are hyperlinked in oxblood). Cross-guide refs (italic) require opening the named guide separately. The pathways list is a *study order*, not a procedural script — faculty checkpoints and clinical judgment always supersede the listed sequence.

blocks.text: *This document is a working clinical reference, compiled from source materials. Verify all material with your faculty before applying chairside. Source PDFs cited per chapter.*

The cast metal framework comes back from the lab and you verify it fits the patient's mouth passively — every rest seated, every clasp engaged, no rocking — before the lab adds tooth setup. This is the last reversible moment in the RPD workflow. A framework that doesn't fit cannot be made to fit by adjustment; it's either remade or accepted as compromised. The visit is short (45 min) but every step has to be done in sequence.

items: **Framework** (returned from the lab on its working cast).
**The working cast** itself (verify the framework seats on the cast first — if it doesn't seat on the cast, it won't seat on the patient).
**PIP (pressure-indicating paste)** for spot pressure detection.
**Mizzy spray** or fit-checker spray as an alternative.
**Articulating paper** (red and blue, thin).
**Slow-speed handpiece** + finishing burs (carbide for metal, green stone for polishing).
**Floss** (to verify clasp tip clearance from contact areas).
**Mirror + explorer** + intraoral camera if available.
Verify the framework on its working cast first. If it rocks on the cast, the lab made an error — call before the patient arrives.
Re-survey the framework against the design form: are all the rest seats present? Are clasps engaging the planned undercuts? Is the major connector the form you specified?
Inspect for casting defects: pits, nodules, sharp edges. Polish any rough spots with a green stone before chairside.
Confirm the design form, Rx, and grading sheet are at hand.
**1a.** Anesthesia is rarely needed for framework try-in. Seat the patient upright.

**1b.** Insert the framework along the path of insertion documented on the design form. Apply gentle finger pressure — if the framework resists seating, do NOT force it. Stop and identify the obstruction.
**1c.** Check for fully-seated rests: each rest should touch its rest seat with no rock when pressed.
**1d.** Check clasp engagement: each clasp tip should be in its planned undercut. Run an explorer along the clasp tip — it should click into the undercut as it engages.
**1e.** Verify the framework doesn't blanch tissue anywhere. Blanching = excessive pressure = adjustment needed.
**2a.** Remove the framework. Dry the tissue side and the rest seats.

**2b.** Apply a thin even coat of PIP to the intaglio of the metal framework — major connector tissue surface, retentive lattice/mesh areas, and the underside of each rest. Denture bases aren't on the framework yet at this stage; the acrylic saddle goes on after the wax try-in.
**2c.** Seat the framework with light finger pressure. Hold for 5 seconds.
**2d.** Remove the framework and inspect: areas where PIP is rubbed through to the metal = pressure spots requiring adjustment. Areas with intact PIP = passive contact (acceptable).
**2e.** Adjust pressure spots with a slow-speed carbide bur (acrylic-cutting burs work on Co-Cr too). Take off only the minimum needed — over-adjustment loses passivity and forces re-fabrication.
**2f.** Re-PIP, re-seat, re-adjust. Repeat until no through-spots remain. Typically 2–3 iterations.
**3a.** Place thin articulating paper (red) between the framework rests/occlusal rests and the opposing teeth.
**3b.** Have the patient close into MIP. Look for marks on the rests indicating occlusal contact.
**3c.** Rest seats should be in light contact in MIP (shimstock holds). Excessive contact pushes the framework apically and can cause abutment pain.
**3d.** Adjust any heavy occlusal contacts on the rests with a fine carbide. The cast Co-Cr framework can be reshaped slightly — but be conservative.
**3e.** Check excursions: working and balancing — the framework should not be the dominant contact in any excursion.

blocks.text: Step 4 — Document + return to lab (5 min)

items: **4a.** Take an intraoral photograph of the seated framework. Document for the chart.
**4b.** Mark the working cast with any adjustments made — the lab needs to know if a rest was reshaped.
**4c.** If the framework is acceptable, send it back to the lab with the working cast for tooth setup (next visit = wax try-in).
**4d.** Write the lab Rx for the next step: shade for denture teeth, mold (anterior/posterior), occlusal scheme.
**4e.** Schedule the patient for wax try-in 1–2 weeks out (depends on lab turnaround).

blocks.title: When the framework doesn't fit — escalation criteria
blocks.body: **Mild misfit (single rest off):** chairside adjustment or lab adjustment with framework + cast returned. Acceptable. **Moderate misfit (multiple rests off, but the major connector seats):** lab adjustment with extensive notes. May require partial re-pour. **Severe misfit (major connector won't seat, framework rocks, multiple clasps fail to engage):** remake. Do not attempt to force a misfitting framework into service; abutment teeth will be torqued and the patient's tissue will be traumatized. Faculty sign-off is required before declaring remake — never make this decision unilaterally as a student.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Framework rocks on the cast:** lab error. Return to lab; do not adjust chairside. **Framework rocks in the mouth but seats on the cast:** the impression captured the ridge wrong or the cast distorted in transit. Take a new altered cast impression with the framework (Kennedy I/II) or accept the framework's footprint and process with awareness. **Clasp won't engage the undercut:** the undercut may be too deep (clasp catches and stops) or the survey was off. Bend the clasp tip gently toward the tooth, or accept and document. **PIP shows pressure on tissue but not rests:** the major connector is the contact, not the rests — re-survey on the cast, the framework is sitting tissue-borne instead of tooth-borne. Likely remake. **Patient reports clasp pain:** clasp tip is in an undercut deeper than 0.01 inches and engaging too aggressively. Polish the clasp tip mesially or have the lab re-design with a shallower undercut.

blocks.text: *Where this fits in the workflow:* You arrived at framework try-in after the impression, design form, and lab Rx (, 6, 7, 9). Next is wax try-in, where you evaluate the lab's tooth setup before processing. After wax try-in approval, the prosthesis is processed and you see the patient again at delivery (, 11, 12, 13).*

The framework returned from the lab now carries the tooth setup in wax — denture teeth positioned on the framework and the saddle areas waxed to final contour. You evaluate esthetics, occlusion, phonetics, and patient acceptance BEFORE the lab processes the prosthesis. Once processed, no major changes are possible. Plan 45 minutes; spend more time if the patient is anxious or this is their first prosthesis.

items: **Wax-set RPD** (framework + denture teeth set in wax) on its working cast.
**Mirror** + intraoral camera (photograph the smile, lateral views).
**Articulating paper** (red + blue, thin).
**Shade guide** to verify the lab matched the prescribed shade.
**Hot water bath** (for wax adjustments) + spatula + #7 wax instrument.
**Phonetic prompts** (sample sentences for /s/, /f/, /v/, /th/ sounds).
**Hand mirror for the patient.**
Inspect the wax setup on the cast. Are all teeth set in the planned positions? Is the occlusal scheme correct (Class I bilateral cuspid-protected, group function, balanced)?
Check shade against the prescription — labs occasionally substitute.
Verify the wax around the framework is smooth and contoured. Patient will see this in the mirror.
Have the patient's diagnostic photos / smile photos ready for esthetic comparison.

blocks.text: Step 1 — Intraoral seating + tissue check (5 min)

items: **1a.** Seat the patient upright. Insert the wax try-in along the planned path of insertion.
**1b.** Verify all rests seat and clasps engage — the framework should fit identically to last visit (it does — same metal framework, just with wax + teeth added).

**1c.** Check tissue adaptation under the wax saddles. If any wax is impinging on tissue, mark it with PIP or just by visual blanching — adjust the wax with a spatula intraorally if minor; return to lab if extensive.
**1d.** Verify the patient is comfortable seated with the wax try-in — they should report no pain, no acute pressure points.

**2a.** Articulating paper (red) — patient closes into MIP. Check that posterior teeth contact bilaterally and evenly. No anterior heavy contact (anterior contact in MIP indicates VDO problem or anterior occlusal interference).
**2b.** Verify wear facets on the wax align with the patient's existing wear pattern (look for matched cusp-fossa relationships).
**2c.** Excursions: have the patient move laterally — check working-side guidance is canine or group function as planned; balancing side should not have interferences.
**2d.** Protrusion: anterior teeth should contact in protrusion; posterior teeth should disocclude smoothly.
**2e.** Adjust occlusal contacts in wax (faster + reversible) with a hot spatula or by warming the cusp and gently moving the tooth.

blocks.text: Step 3 — Esthetic evaluation with patient (15 min)

items: **3a.** Patient holds a hand mirror at arm's length and looks at their smile.
**3b.** Ask: 'Are the teeth the right shape?' 'Are they the right size?' 'Is the color what you wanted?' 'Are they in the right position relative to your lip line when you smile?'
**3c.** Verify the midline of the denture matches the midline of the patient's face.
**3d.** Check the incisal plane parallels the interpupillary line (canted plane = unhappy patient).
**3e.** Verify smile line — the maxillary central incisors should be 1–2 mm below the upper lip at rest, more visible in active smile.
**3f.** For Kennedy IV cases, compare pontic shape and shade to the contralateral natural tooth using the smile photographs.
**3g.** If the patient brings a family member or friend for second opinion, include them.
**3h.** Take photos at this visit — full smile, lateral, retracted view. Compare with diagnostic photos.
**4a.** /s/ sounds — have the patient say 'sixty-six', 'Mississippi'. Listen for whistling (anterior teeth too far forward/wide spacing) or lisping (anterior teeth too close to palate).
**4b.** /f/ and /v/ — have the patient say 'fifty', 'video'. Lower lip should contact maxillary incisal edges. If contact is on the inside of the labial surface, anteriors are too palatal.
**4c.** /th/ — have the patient say 'thirty-three', 'thirsty'. Tongue should protrude slightly between anterior teeth.
**4d.** Vowels — read a sample sentence ('How now brown cow'). Listen for muffled or distorted vowels (often VDO too high) or open-mouth speech (VDO too low).
**4e.** Adjust by moving teeth in the wax if phonetic problems are identified.

blocks.text: Step 5 — Patient sign-off + lab return (5 min)

items: **5a.** Explicitly ask the patient: 'Are you happy with how this looks?' 'Are you happy with how this fits?' 'Is there anything you want changed before we process this?'
**5b.** Document the answer in the chart. If yes, sign-off; if no, identify the change and either adjust at this visit or schedule a second wax try-in.
**5c.** Re-mount the wax try-in on the working cast carefully (the wax is fragile).
**5d.** Write the lab Rx for processing: confirm acrylic shade, confirm gingival contour, confirm any modifications made chairside.
**5e.** Schedule delivery 1–2 weeks out.

blocks.title: When the patient rejects the wax try-in
blocks.body: Patient rejection of the wax try-in is common (10–20% of cases) and not a failure — it's exactly why the visit exists. Most rejections are about anterior tooth shape, size, or shade. The protocol: (1) acknowledge the concern, (2) identify the specific issue (color too dark? teeth too small? teeth in the wrong position?), (3) re-set the affected teeth in wax at this visit if the change is modest, (4) schedule a second wax try-in if the change is substantial. Never proceed to processing over a patient's objection — the finished denture will be rejected and you'll be remaking from scratch with no recoverable cost.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Anterior contact in MIP:** VDO is too high OR anterior teeth set too forward. Reduce VDO at this visit if minor; remount and re-set if substantial. **Whistling on /s/:** anterior teeth set too far forward or too widely spaced. Move teeth lingually or close spacing. **Lisping on /s/:** anterior teeth set too far palatal. Move teeth labially. **Canted incisal plane:** ask if the patient holds their head straight when smiling — sometimes the appearance is positional, not actual. If actual, re-orient the occlusal plane in wax and re-mount. **Pontic shade mismatch on Kennedy IV:** the lab used a denture-tooth shade that doesn't match the natural anterior. Re-order with the correct shade (or layer porcelain stain on the lab side). **Patient reports clicking on closure:** posterior teeth out of contact in MIP, causing the framework to flex. Add wax/raise posterior tooth contacts.

blocks.text: *Where this fits in the workflow:* Framework try-in preceded this visit. Next is processing at the lab — the wax setup is replaced with heat-cured PMMA and the prosthesis is finished. Delivery follows (PIP, pressure points, occlusal adjustment, POI). If the wax try-in was carefully evaluated, delivery is short. If rushed, delivery becomes a series of corrective adjustments.*

The diagnostic casts are where the entire RPD is planned — surveyed, designed on the preliminary form, and duplicated so you can rehearse the tooth modifications before you ever touch the patient. They are taken at the comprehensive oral exam, in alginate, and poured in micro-stone.

items: **Taken at the COE** — alginate impressions of both arches are made during the comprehensive oral examination for any Phase 3 (prosthetic) treatment.
**Both arches, every time** — you need the opposing arch to evaluate the occlusion and plan the occlusal scheme, not just the arch receiving the RPD.

**The cast is the design substrate** — it gets surveyed, the design is drawn on it, and it is duplicated so you can practice guide planes, rest seats, and undercut modifications. Capture every remaining tooth and the edentulous ridges + sulci accurately.
**Stock dentate trays** (metal or plastic), several sizes — try in dry first; the tray must cover all teeth and the edentulous ridges with ~2-3 mm of clearance.
**Alginate** + rubber bowl + flat spatula + room-temperature water.
**Alginate tray adhesive** — applied inside the tray so the set impression does not pull away from the tray on removal.
**Cheek retractor, mouth mirror, gauze.**
**Select + try in the tray dry.** It should seat fully over the teeth and ridges, borders in the vestibule, clearance for material on all surfaces. Extend short borders with utility wax.
**Apply adhesive** to the tray and let it dry.
**Mix the alginate** to a smooth, lump-free consistency and load the tray from posterior to anterior.
**Seat posterior-first, then rotate the anterior in.** On the maxillary, wipe a little material into the occlusal surfaces and palatal vault with a finger first to prevent voids.
**Hold still until set** (confirm by the residue left in the bowl), then remove with a single firm pull along the path of the teeth — do not rock.
**Inspect immediately:** every tooth captured, no voids or drags, ridges and sulci recorded, borders intact. Remake rather than accept a flawed impression.
**Pour in micro-stone** and trim to clean diagnostic casts.
**Hand-articulate + mount if there are stable tripod contacts** — enough opposing tooth pairs to seat the casts into one repeatable position.
**If you cannot hand-articulate** (too few occluding pairs), fabricate upper + lower record bases and wax rims so a bite registration can be taken at the next visit.
**The maxillary cast is mounted with a facebow record; the mandibular cast is mounted to it with the bite registration.**

blocks.title: Why accuracy matters here
blocks.body: The diagnostic cast is surveyed and the RPD is designed on it. A void over a cusp tip or a distorted tooth means an undercut or guide plane that is not really there — and a design that will not fit. The cast is only as good as this impression.

blocks.text: The facebow registers the maxillary arch in three planes of space and transfers it to the articulator, so the upper cast is mounted at the same distance and angle from the hinge axis as the maxilla sits from the condyles. The bite registration then mounts the lower cast to it. UIC uses the Denar facebow and a Denar semi-adjustable articulator.

items: **Measuring bow** — the U-shaped frame that seats in the ears and carries the orbital pointer.
**Bite fork** — carries the registration of the maxillary occlusal surfaces.
**Shaft assembly** — connects the bite fork to the bow and locks the relationship.
**Seat the patient upright and relaxed** with the upper and lower wax rims in.

**Load the bite fork** with bite-registration material and place it against the maxillary teeth/rim, centered, with the rod perpendicular on the patient's right side. Practice the placement a few times before recording.
**Have the patient hold the bite fork up** against the upper arch with both thumbs.
**Mark a point 42 mm from the patient's right lateral incisor** — the orbital pointer references this point.
**Assemble the measuring bow** over the bite fork, snug in both external auditory meatuses (ears).
**Level the bow:** the anterior cross-bar parallel to the bi-pupillary line and the floor; the side rods parallel to the Ala-Tragus line; the shaft parallel to the long axis of the face.
**Tighten the three screws** and remove the assembly without distorting it; transfer to the articulator to mount the maxillary cast.
**Condylar inclination:** 25-30°.
**Bennett angle:** 15°.
**Incisal pin:** 0 mm — the pin holds the vertical dimension and must read zero when the casts are mounted.

blocks.title: Why the facebow
blocks.body: The articulator can only reproduce the patient's protrusive and excursive movements if the upper cast sits the same distance and angle from the hinge axis as the maxilla does from the condyles. Skip it and the eccentric contacts you build on the articulator will not match the mouth.

blocks.title: Common errors
blocks.body: A bow that is not level (cross-bar tilted, side rods off the Ala-Tragus line) transfers a canted occlusal plane to the articulator. A bite fork that is not fully seated or not centered shifts the cast. Re-seat and re-level rather than mounting off a bad record.

chapters.title: Wax rims + bite registration for mounting

blocks.text: When the casts cannot be hand-articulated, you make record bases and wax rims so a bite registration can capture the patient's maximum intercuspation (MI) and mount the lower cast. UIC builds the record bases from light-cured Triad and the rims from hard baseplate wax.

items: **Vaseline the cast** so the cured base releases cleanly.
**Adapt a sheet of Triad to the cast with your fingers**, then trim the excess with a buffalo knife to form a base that fully covers the sulcus where it will carry the rim.

**Cure formula:** cure the cast + Triad in the curing unit for 1 min; remove, lift the base off, reposition it on the cast and cure another 1 min; if still not fully set, cure the intaglio for 20 sec. Do **not** over-cure the intaglio — it shrinks and the base will no longer adapt to the cast or the mouth.
**Smooth the borders** of the cured record base.
**Roughen the base** with a straight lab bur where the rim will sit, and apply yellow sticky wax to that area to lock the rim onto the base.
**Melt and shape hard baseplate wax** into a rim and attach it to the base over the edentulous area.
**Smooth the rim** — it carries the occlusal record.
**Adjust the rims in the mouth** so the patient closes into the *same MI with the rims in as without them* — the rims cannot prop the bite open or shift it.
**Create 1-2 mm of clearance** between the rims and the opposing dentition for the registration material, and cut index grooves in the rim's occlusal surface so the material keys in.
**Place bite-registration material (Aluwax or PVS)** on the occlusal surface of the rim and have the patient close into MI.
**Trim the set record with a scalpel** for a clean, accurate seat before mounting.

blocks.title: The MI test is the whole game
blocks.body: A record that holds the jaw even slightly off MI mounts the lower cast wrong, and every downstream occlusal step inherits the error. Confirm same-MI-with-and-without before you place any material.

chapters.title: Surveying + RPD design + preliminary design form

blocks.text: This is the intellectual core of the case. You survey the cast, propose the framework, and record the whole design on the preliminary design form before a single tooth is touched. The cast is surveyed twice — the diagnostic cast to plan, and the master cast before the framework is fabricated. (The component-by-component design logic — which clasp, which connector, by Kennedy class — lives in the RPD design tab; this chapter is the surveying + design workflow that wraps around it.)

items: A **paralleling instrument** used to determine the path of insertion, the heights of contour, and the retentive points on the abutments.
**Analyzing rod** — finds the path of insertion and tests proximal-surface parallelism.
**Undercut gauges — 0.01, 0.02, 0.03 inch** — measure the depth of a retentive undercut for the clasp tip.
**Carbon marker + sheath** — draws the height of contour.
**Wax trimmer** — trims block-out wax parallel to the path of insertion.
Determine the **path of insertion** for placement and removal of the framework.
Identify the **guide planes** — the parallel proximal surfaces that create a single path.
Find the **height of contour** (the greatest circumference of the tooth at a chosen orientation).
Locate and measure the **retentive undercuts** (0.01 / 0.02 / 0.03 inch).
Identify **tissue undercuts** that must be blocked out.
**Tripod the cast** so the path of insertion can be reproduced at any time.
**Start with a proposed framework design** and identify the direct retainers — the teeth adjacent to each edentulous area.

**Propose a path of insertion** (cast orientation) with minimal tilt and minimal guide-plane reduction; keep it roughly perpendicular to the occlusal plane (within ~10°), occlusal plane parallel to the floor if possible. The analyzing rod should contact the occlusal ½–2/3 of the proximal surfaces — meaning those surfaces are parallel to one another.
**Identify the height of contour and the undercuts, then reconfirm the path of insertion.** If you tilt the table to improve parallelism, the HOC and undercuts move and must be re-checked.

**Mark the height of contour in black** (carbon marker + sheath) on the abutments, and **mark the retentive undercuts in red** — choosing a 0.01, 0.02, or 0.03 inch undercut with the gauge (shaft on the HOC, disk to the tooth).
**Tripod the cast** (tissue-surface indexing with the 0.03 gauge circled in red, or art-portion indexing on the outer cast) so the exact path of insertion can be reproduced later.

blocks.title: Longer guide planes = more retention and stability
blocks.body: The guide planes are the parallel proximal surfaces that force the framework in along one path and resist its dislodgement. Longer, well-prepared guide planes give more retention and stability — worth the minimal enamel reduction.

blocks.text: Filled out before the tooth-prep appointment, the form is your checklist of every component so nothing is forgotten. During the prep visit you check items off as you complete them.

items: **Guide planes** and their references.
**Rest seats** and reduction amounts, including any indirect retainers.
**Undercuts to be created** — their location and depth.
**Height-of-contour adjustments** — especially for suprabulge clasps and reciprocal arms.
**Clasp types**, **minor connectors**, and the **major connector**.
**Duplicate the diagnostic cast** and practice the proposed modifications on the duplicate — guide planes, HOC reduction, undercut creation, and rest seats.
**Note exactly what you will prepare** at the chair, in sequence.
**Faculty approves the design** before the abutment-prep appointment — the prep removes only what the approved design dictates.

blocks.title: Undercuts hard to find?
blocks.body: Tilt the cast to redistribute the undercuts — but the path of insertion changes and you must re-mark the HOC for the new orientation. If a usable undercut still is not there, plan enameloplasty (re-contour the enamel) or, if recontouring enamel will not get you an adequate guide plane / undercut / rest seat, a survey crown.

chapters.title: Abutment preparation — guide planes, undercuts, rest seats

blocks.text: At this visit you prepare the abutments to receive the framework — guide planes, axial-contour adjustments, retentive undercuts, and rest seats — exactly as the faculty-approved design dictates. Everything is in enamel, so no anesthesia is needed; use water spray to avoid overheating.

items: **Guide planes** — cut on the proximal surfaces, parallel to the path of insertion.
**Axial contours** — reduce if the height of contour is not favorable for the selected clasp.
**Undercuts** — create where the design calls for them.
**Verify before the rest seats:** take an alginate, pour a fast-set (Snap) cast, and check the axial modifications on the surveyor. Repeat steps 1–4 if needed.
**Rest seats** — once the modifications are correct, cut the rest seats.
**Occlusal clearance** — confirm there is room for the framework where it crosses the occlusal surfaces.
Ask the dispensing window for the **RPD high-speed bur block**.
**Guide planes:** cylindrical diamond bur.
**Cingulum rest seat:** inverted cone.
**Occlusal rest seat — premolars:** #4 + #6 round burs.
**Occlusal rest seat — molars:** #4, #6, #8 round burs.
**No anesthesia** in clinical sessions — the prep stays in enamel. **Use water** to avoid overheating.
**Parallel to the path of insertion**, flat occluso-gingivally, curved to follow the original bucco-lingual contour, no sharp line angles, entirely in enamel, smooth.
**Posterior teeth:** 1/3 the bucco-lingual width (2/3 of the way cusp-tip to cusp-tip), and 2/3 of the tooth length from the marginal ridge toward the cervical.
**Anterior teeth:** usually placed on the linguo-proximal aspect to preserve esthetics; may involve the proximal surface if you need space for a replacement tooth.
Hold the handpiece **perpendicular to the occlusal plane** as you cut.
**1.5 mm marginal-ridge reduction.**
**1/3 the bucco-lingual width** (2/3 cusp-tip to cusp-tip).
As long as it is wide: **premolar ≈ 1/3 the mesio-distal dimension; molar ≥ 1/4 the mesio-distal dimension.**
**Rounded triangular outline**, base at the marginal ridge; **spoon-shaped, no undercuts**, smooth floor without steps.
Outline with a **#6 round bur (premolar)** or **#8 round bur (molar)**; establish the axial inclination / positive seat with a **#4 round bur (premolar)** or **#6 (molar)**.
Entire preparation in enamel, smooth surface.
**V or U shape** facio-lingually (viewed from proximal); V or U **peaked toward the incisal** mesio-distally.
Located occluso-gingivally at the **junction of the middle and gingival thirds** of the clinical crown (clear of the occlusion).
**Extends mesio-distally from one marginal ridge to the other.**
**Floor inclined toward the center of the tooth** to establish a positive seat; no significant undercut above the rest prep.
Entirely in enamel, smooth; the prep-to-long-axis angle is **less than 90°**.

blocks.title: A positive seat means no slide
blocks.body: Every rest-seat floor inclines toward the center / long axis of the tooth so the rest transmits load down the tooth rather than off it. A flat or outward-sloping floor lets the framework slide and torque the abutment — the opposite of what the rest is for.

blocks.title: Common errors
blocks.body: No occlusal clearance after the prep means the framework will prop the bite open — deepen the rest (still a smooth enamel floor) or re-check the design. Cutting through enamel into dentin means you over-prepared: the goal is a positive enamel seat, not a deep box.

chapters.title: Border molding + final impression (single-step / altered cast)

blocks.text: The definitive impression captures the functional form of the residual ridge so the denture base carries load without torquing the abutments. Tooth-bounded cases take a single-step impression in a full-arch custom tray; distal-extension (Kennedy I / II) cases use the altered-cast technique — an anatomic impression now for framework fabrication, then a functional impression at the framework try-in.

items: **Vaseline** the diagnostic cast.

**Spacer:** two sheets of soft baseplate wax over the teeth and tooth-supported edentulous areas; **one** sheet over the distal-extension area(s) — the lighter relief there is deliberate so the tray records the extension under slight pressure. Cut small openings through the wax for **three tissue stops**.
**Cover the spacer with Triad**, trim the vestibular borders 2 mm short of full depth, and add finger rests (1st-molar region) plus an anterior handle that does not interfere.
**Cure** (~2 min, then the intaglio ~1 min) — the wax must not melt.
**Remove 2 mm of wax from the intaglio borders** to leave space for the border-molding compound.
**Check the tray extension** in the mouth.
**Border mold** the distal-extension areas and the entire lingual vestibule (mandibular cases) with green-stick compound, then **reduce its thickness by 0.5 mm**.
**Remove the baseplate-wax spacer.**
**Place a relief hole in the maxillary tray — NOT the mandibular tray.**
**Apply PVS adhesive** and let it dry 10 min.
**Take the PVS wash:** light body on the teeth, medium or heavy body loaded in the rest of the tray.
**Box and pour** in Type III stone (Coe-Cal / Microstone); after 24–48 h soak in warm water to soften the compound, separate gently, and trim the cast preserving the vestibule.

blocks.title: Why border-mold an RPD?
blocks.body: The denture borders extend into the vestibule just like a complete denture — the more ridge they cover, the more the load is spread and the less the abutments are torqued. Distal-extension cases especially need a functional, border-molded extension.

items: **Two impressions instead of one.** First, an anatomic / non-pressure impression now, which the lab uses to fabricate the framework.

**Then a functional impression at the framework try-in:** a partial-arch tray attached to the seated framework, with little or no relief and a viscous material, so the distal-extension base contacts tissue under load.
More clinical and lab steps than single-step, so it is reserved for distal extensions where recording the ridge under function actually matters.
Send the **master cast + design cast** with the lab prescription, surveyed but marked differently.
**Master cast:** tripoded, exact undercut points in red, height of contour of the abutments.
**Design cast:** the full design drawn out.
**Marking convention:** metal framework components in **blue** — except the **tissue stop in red**; wrought-wire clasps and acrylic base extensions in **red**.

blocks.title: Common errors
blocks.body: Abutment torque on a distal extension traces to a base that does not record the ridge under function — use the selective spacer (single-step) or the altered-cast technique, and border-mold the extension. A relief hole in the mandibular tray (it should have none) bleeds pressure and under-extends the lingual.

blocks.text: The cast-metal framework is back from the lab, and this is the highest-stakes fit checkpoint of the case. It must seat fully and passively — first on the master cast, then in the mouth — before anything else proceeds. For distal-extension cases, the functional altered-cast impression is taken now.
Check the fit on the master cast first

items: **Is the design correct** — does the casting match what you prescribed?
**Is the framework rocking?**
**Are the rest seats flush and fully seated?**
**Are the clasps engaging the correct undercut?**
**Are the major connectors well-adapted to the tissue surface?**
**Note the distance between the lattice (mesh) and the tissue surface** — it carries the acrylic base.
**Melt a thin layer of disclosing wax** on every framework area that will contact the teeth.
**Insert gently — never force the framework in.**
**Adjust binding points** (pressure show-through) with a high-speed coarse diamond bur; leave broad, even contacts and remove only the small metal show-throughs; repeat until it is fully seated.
**Confirm it fits intraorally as it does on the cast.** If it does not seat the same way, decide: a new impression, or the altered-cast impression.
Check occlusion and articulation; the occlusion must be the **same with and without the framework**.
If RPDs are made for both arches, check **one arch at a time, then both together**.
With the framework verified, attach the **partial-arch tray to the framework** over the distal extension.
Take a **functional impression** (little/no relief, viscous material) so the extension base records the ridge under load.
The lab **alters the master cast** to this functional record before setting the teeth.

blocks.title: Passivity is non-negotiable
blocks.body: A framework that rocks, will not seat, or only seats under force is remade — you cannot bend a casting into fit. Adjust the binding points, but if it will not seat passively, it is a remake, not an adjustment.

blocks.title: Common errors
blocks.body: Framework seats on the cast but not in the mouth → the master cast was inaccurate; take a new impression or proceed with the altered-cast technique. Occlusion changes with the framework in → a rest or connector is high; mark with disclosing wax and relieve until the bite is identical with and without it.

chapters.title: Tooth selection + bite registration on the framework

blocks.text: With the framework verified in the mouth, you take the records the lab needs to set the teeth — a facebow and an occlusal (bite) registration with wax rims attached to the framework — and you select the mould and shade of the denture teeth.

items: **Attach wax rims to the verified framework.**
**Take a facebow record** to mount the maxillary, and an **occlusal / bite registration in MI** with the rims on the framework to mount the mandibular (see the Facebow chapter for technique).
These records mount the *framework + rims* so the lab sets the teeth to the same metal you just verified — not to a separate base that might seat differently.

**Mould** (size + shape) to fill the edentulous space and harmonize with the remaining teeth — the number of teeth set is **not necessarily the number missing**, and mandibular posteriors stop anterior to the ascending ramus.
**Shade** to match the adjacent natural teeth (use the mould + shade selector above).
**Decide the occlusal scheme before the teeth are set** — against an opposing complete denture, strive for **bilateral balanced articulation**.
**What the lab does next:** sets the denture teeth on the framework with the mandibular central grooves over the crest of the residual ridge, to the occlusal scheme you chose, then festoons the wax.

blocks.title: The framework is the reference now
blocks.body: The facebow and bite registration are taken with the rims on the metal, so the case is mounted on the exact framework you verified at try-in. Take the record on a separate base and the teeth may be set to a seat that does not match the framework.

blocks.text: The teeth are set in wax on the framework. This is the last checkpoint before processing locks everything in — evaluate the esthetics and the occlusion and get the patient's explicit approval, because nothing can change after the acrylic is processed.

items: **Esthetics** — mould, shade, midline, tooth position, lip support, and the gingival contours / festooning. Does it look right to you *and* the patient?
**Occlusion** — the set teeth contact in MI simultaneously with the remaining natural teeth at the correct VDO; verify the eccentric scheme (bilateral balance against an opposing complete denture).
**Fit** — the framework still seats fully and passively with the teeth on it.
**Phonetics** — speech is comfortable; have the patient read aloud.

blocks.title: Last stop before processing
blocks.body: Every esthetic and occlusal change is cheap now and permanent after the acrylic is processed. Get the patient to approve the look explicitly — color, shape, position, midline — before the case goes to the lab.

blocks.title: Common errors
blocks.body: Patient unsure about the look → adjust the wax set-up now and re-try; do not process to 'see how it looks in acrylic.' Occlusion off → correct it on the articulated case before processing, not at delivery.

chapters.title: Delivery — insertion, PIP, occlusal adjustment, POI

blocks.text: The processed RPD is back. Seat it, relieve the pressure spots, adjust the occlusion so the natural and denture teeth meet together, confirm the clasps retain without traumatizing the abutments, then deliver the post-op and home-care instructions. Most of what could go wrong here should already have been caught at the framework try-in.

items: Have the **diagnostic cast with the drawn design** and the **lab prescription** in front of you — did the lab build the RPD per the design you requested?
Ask whether the patient has worn a removable prosthesis before, and explain the **adaptation period** up front.

**Feel the RPD all around** and remove sharp acrylic, undercuts, and bony-spicule show-throughs. Remember it has one path of insertion — any binding of the teeth against the metal should have been found at try-in.

**Insert along the path of insertion**; confirm every rest is seated in its prepared seat and the clasp arms engage the proposed undercuts; check the acrylic for over-extensions that dislodge the RPD.

**PIP paste + Mizzy spray:** deposit PIP in one direction all around the intaglio, then spray Mizzy over it — the Mizzy keeps the PIP on the RPD instead of spreading around the lips and cheeks. Relieve the show-through spots.

**Maxillary pressure areas** (most common): buccal posterior border parallel to the tuberosities, hamular notches bilaterally, over-extension over the zygomatic arch / rugae / mid-palatal suture or torus.

**Mandibular pressure areas:** lingual ridge (especially the premolar region), mylohyoid ridge, buccal shelf, retromylohyoid space, retromolar pad, the disto-buccal border at the ascending ramus / external oblique ridge, and any tori or bony irregularities.
If there is no acrylic interference, re-check the metal seating: the **rests must seat in their seats and the proximal plates contact the guide planes**.
Use **disclosing wax** on the proximal plates and rest seats; the **show-through areas are the interferences** — relieve them (again, should have been caught at try-in).
**Without the RPD in**, see how the remaining natural teeth occlude — identify the teeth contacting in MI.
**Adjust one RPD at a time.** Seat it and verify the patient's own teeth still contact.

If the inter-occlusal contacts are high but the framework seats without interference, the **prematurities are on the denture teeth** — reduce them gradually until the natural teeth occlude and the VDO is re-established.
**Goal: simultaneous MI contact on the natural and denture teeth.** (A clinical remount with a pick-up impression + new records is an option if chairside adjustment is not enough.)
Adjust retention **only after** the RPD is fully seated and the occlusion verified. Use ortho pliers (Bird-Beak or three-prong).

**Wrought-wire** clasps adjust easily in three dimensions; **cast** clasps are harder. An **I-bar** adjusts only toward or away from the tooth; a **cast Akers** adjusts only in the horizontal plane (toward or away).
**Verify esthetics** match what the patient approved at the wax try-in, and **phonetics** (advise reading aloud daily).
**Show insertion and removal** and have the patient practice in the clinic before trying at home.
**Wear for the first 24 hours**, then remove at night and keep in water. Clean daily with soap or denture cleaner — **not toothpaste**.
Counsel on adaptation (eating, salivary flow), and book the 24-hour and 1-week follow-ups.
Polish the **metal** with a metal polishing kit and the **acrylic** with pumice on a rag wheel.
**Do not polish the intaglio surface** (it would lose its fit), and **do not polish the denture teeth** — you would change the occlusion you just adjusted.

blocks.title: Occlusion: natural first, then denture teeth
blocks.body: Always establish how the natural teeth meet with the RPD out, then add the RPD and reduce only the denture-tooth prematurities. If you grind the framework or the natural teeth to 'fix' the bite, you have moved the problem, not solved it.

blocks.text: See the patient at 24 hours and 1 week. Interview, examine, relieve the new sore spots, re-check the occlusion, and reinforce care. Most early complaints are routine intaglio or occlusal adjustments — and for distal-extension cases, this is where you decide whether a reline is coming.

items: **Interview:** ask specifically about soreness, retention, stability, speech, and problems eating — patients say 'fine' to generic questions.
**Examine** the soft and hard tissues, the support, the rest seats, and the retention.
**Relieve sore spots** on the intaglio with PIP paste + Mizzy spray, the same way as at delivery.
**Re-check the occlusion** and adjust the denture-tooth contacts if needed.
**Reinforce care:** remove at night and keep in a moist environment; clean daily with soap or denture cleaner, never toothpaste.

**Caries risk around the retainers.** Many of these patients are on xerostomia-inducing medications; dry mouth drives root caries around the clasped abutments. Advise more frequent recall (POE), better oral hygiene, and high-fluoride toothpaste (Prevident 5000) or varnish.
**Reline evaluation for Kennedy Class I / II.** If a distal-extension RPD rotates around the fulcrum line as the ridge resorbs, plan a reline — see the Reline / rebase / repair track.

blocks.title: Adaptation takes weeks
blocks.body: Set the expectation that soreness and an odd feeling for the first days are normal, and that eating and speech improve with practice. Build the rapport — and avoid delivering the day before a holiday when you cannot see the patient back.

blocks.text: When an abutment cannot give an adequate guide plane, retentive undercut, and rest seat from enamel recontouring alone, it gets a PFM survey crown — a crown built with the exact contours the partial needs. The defining rule: the RPD is designed first, so the lab knows what contours to build into the crown.

items: A **survey crown** is fabricated to be fitted with the RPD when acceptable contours cannot be achieved by modifying the surfaces of the residual tooth.
**Finalize the RPD design before crown prep** — the **path of RPD insertion determines the crown's guide planes**.
The crown is **delivered first**, then you proceed with the RPD impression.
**2.5–3.0 mm of occlusal reduction** in the area of the RPD rest seat, plus **0.5–1 mm** for the metal thickness of the crown.
**Final impression** in light + medium, or heavy body PVS.
Two clinic options: prep, temp, and cement the crowns **one at a time, re-surveying each**; or temp them all and take **one final impression of all preps at once**, then send for pouring + Pindexing.

blocks.text: Surveyed contours the lab builds in

items: A **positive rest seat**.
A defined **survey line / ideal height of contour**.
A **retentive undercut** for the clasp tip.
A flat **guide plane** on the proximal toward the edentulous span.
Ask the lab to return the **full-contour wax-up** so you can verify the guide planes and undercuts with the surveyor, and verify the finished crowns on the master cast **before cementation**.

Design the RPD → prep the survey crowns → Pindexed master cast → wax rims + bite registration → mount → lab fabricates the crowns → **survey the returned crowns on the master cast** → cement → take the RPD alginate, then **rejoin the main pathway at abutment prep / final impression**.

blocks.title: Design before you prep
blocks.body: This is one of the few crown cases where the partial design must be final before the crown is prepared — the crown's guide plane, undercut, and rest seat are all dictated by the RPD's path of insertion. Prep first and the crown will not serve the partial.

blocks.text: Over time the ridge resorbs and the RPD loses tissue support — most importantly on distal-extension (Kennedy I / II) cases, where the base rotates around its fulcrum line and starts to torque the abutments. Relining re-adapts the intaglio; rebasing replaces the whole base; repairs handle broken teeth, bases, clasps, connectors, and rests.

items: **Complaints:** loose prosthesis, reduced chewing, food packing under the base, soft-tissue irritation/ulceration, lost anterior esthetics.
**Rotation test:** apply a seating force at the distal end of the base and watch whether an anterior indirect retainer lifts off its rest seat as the RPD rotates around its fulcrum line.
**Alginate test:** a thin alginate mix on the intaglio, seat the RPD and apply force *only* on the rests and major connector, hold until set, then measure the alginate thickness to judge the gap.
**Materials:** tissue conditioner (temporary, sometimes medicated — antifungals for candidiasis), soft reline (temporary, changed periodically), or hard reline (permanent).
**Closed-mouth** (tooth-supported RPDs): the positive rest seats at each end support the base — just have the patient bite after you seat it.

**Open-mouth** (distal-extension RPDs): the dentist holds the prosthesis down at the rests and major connector while fully seated, to prevent overseating and rotation. A closed-mouth technique here would just reproduce the rotation in the relined base.

**Chairside:** less costly, no need to take the prosthesis from the patient — but you cannot border-mold, and auto-polymerizing resin has more free monomer, more porosity, less color stability, and more patient sensitivity.

**Lab:** border molding is possible and heat-cured resin is more stable (less monomer + porosity) — but the patient gives up the prosthesis and it costs more. For a distal extension, use the altered-cast technique, keep impression material from under the framework, and expect a clinical remount.
**Reline** when the base is adequate to support the reline impression (extension is acceptable, can be border-molded; slight occlusal wear is re-established by the reline).

**Rebase** (lab removes *all* base acrylic and replaces it) when the base is inadequate — too short for border molding, or the acrylic base is fractured. The occlusal relationship is maintained throughout.
**Reconstruction:** if the base is poorly adapted *and* the teeth no longer occlude, remove both and rebuild on the existing framework — which must still fit well to accept new teeth and base.

**Lost / broken tooth:** select mould + shade, pick-up alginate impression, block out undercuts and pour in plaster, lute the replacement tooth with auto-polymerizing acrylic (cure in the pressure pot), trim + polish.

**Fractured acrylic base:** index the parts intraorally, pick-up impression, pour quick-set plaster, remove 1–2 mm of acrylic from each fragment and cut retention grooves, reseat and process with auto-polymerizing acrylic.
**Fractured clasp / connector / rest:** index intraorally, pick-up impression, then solder / electro-solder (re-cast a cast clasp arm; re-shape and re-embed a wrought-wire arm).
**Fractured framework:** repair is extensive and unpredictable — **remake the RPD** if at all possible.

blocks.title: Hold a distal extension open-mouth
blocks.body: On a Kennedy I / II reline, seat the RPD and load only the rests and major connector while the impression sets — not the patient's bite. Let the patient bite down and you record the very rotation you are trying to eliminate.

parts.label: All chapters
parts.tagline: Generated from build_indirect_guide.py on the indirect restorations build script.

chapters.title: Conventional crown workflow appointment-by-appointment

blocks.text: Appointment 1 · Tooth preparation, final impression, bite reg, provisional

Clean tooth prep meeting reduction targets; accurate final impression of the prep + adjacent + opposing teeth; accurate bite registration; well-fitting provisional that won't shift abutments or under-develop the pontic site. Plan 2–2.5 hours for a single unit; 3 hours for an FPD.

items: **Anesthesia:** 2–3 carpules 2% lidocaine 1:100,000 epi (PSA + buccal infiltration for maxillary molars; IA block + buccal infiltration for mandibular molars; infiltration alone for premolars + anteriors).
**Isolation:** Isodry (Isodry for crown preps), or cotton roll + Dri-Angle + saliva ejector.
**Diagnostic wax-up** + **silicone putty matrix** (Lab Putty or Coltene; fabricated in pre-clinic from the wax-up).
**Shade guide** (Vita Classical or 3D Master) + patient mirror + camera.
**Burs:** #8856 chamfer (coarse + fine), 845KR series for occlusal reduction, football diamond, round-end taper, fine finish chamfer. Bur block organized.
**Retraction cord:** #00, #0, #1 sizes (Ultrapak or equivalent) + hemostatic agent (Hemodent or ViscoStat).
**Impression:** PVS heavy body + light body (Aquasil Ultra is standard) + automix gun + intraoral tips + tray adhesive.
**Tray:** triple tray (single unit, hand-articulable) or full-arch impression tray (multi-unit or hand-articulation impossible).
**Bite registration:** Regisil PB or fast-set PVS bite material in cartridge (only if hand-articulation isn't reliable).
**Provisional:** Bis-acryl (Protemp Plus or Integrity) in matching shade + automix gun.
**Provisional finishing:** Tempbond NE (non-eugenol), scalpel, finishing burs, polishing rubber points.
**Shimstock** + articulating paper for pre-/post-provisional occlusal check.
Diagnostic wax-up faculty-approved and signed off (pre-clinical lab).
Silicone putty matrix fabricated from the wax-up — sectioned for reduction guide use (vertical cut through the planned tooth) and intact for provisional fabrication.
Shade guide cleaned, patient pre-informed to remove lipstick.
Bur block set up at chairside, organized prep-to-finish.
Provisional + impression materials at room temperature (cartridges set out 30 min before use).
Lab Rx form printed, ready to fill in.
Camera ready for shade documentation.

blocks.text: Step 1 — Shade selection BEFORE anesthesia (5 min)

items: **1a.** Patient sits upright in natural light or with the operatory light off. Avoid pure incandescent or pure fluorescent (color shifts).
**1b.** Wet the patient's teeth with water or saliva. **Critical:** teeth increase in value (brightness) when dry — selecting shade after isolation makes the final crown look too dark.
**1c.** Use Vita Classical or 3D Master. Hold the tab next to the tooth at the same height. Glance at the tab + tooth for 5 sec, look away, repeat.
**1d.** Verify shade with a second person (assistant or faculty) if possible — tooth color perception is highly subjective.
**1e.** Photograph with the shade tab adjacent to the tooth, ideally with a polarizing filter (cuts surface reflections).
**1f.** Record on lab Rx: tab number, any modifiers (more chroma at cervical, more translucency at incisal, characterizations like white spots / craze lines).
**2a.** Administer anesthesia per the planned prep tooth (PSA, IA block, or infiltration as appropriate).
**2b.** Verify anesthesia depth: tooth feels nothing to firm explorer probing on the occlusal + axial surfaces.
**2c.** Place Isodry with the prep tooth isolated and adjacent teeth visible for matrix/reference. Use clamp #00 / #2 / #2A as appropriate.
**2d.** If no Isodry: cotton rolls + Dri-Angle + saliva ejector. Verify dry field before proceeding.

blocks.text: Step 3 — Pre-prep reduction check with putty matrix (3 min)

items: **3a.** Seat the silicone putty matrix over the tooth + adjacent teeth.
**3b.** Verify the matrix fits cleanly (made from the original tooth or wax-up; should rock onto the cast cleanly).
**3c.** Remove the matrix — this is your reference for occlusal + axial reduction targets.
**4a. Depth-cut grooves:** use the 845KR diamond to cut depth grooves on the occlusal surface (1.5 mm functional cusp slope, 1.0 mm non-functional, or 2.0 mm for full-zirconia / PFM occlusal table).
**4b. Reduce occlusal surface** to the depth-groove level. Use the 845KR or football diamond.
**4c. Axial depth-cut grooves:** use the chamfer diamond. Cut 1.0 mm axial grooves on B + L + M + D.
**4d. Reduce axial walls** to the depth-groove level. Maintain a 6–10° total occlusal convergence (a tapered, parallel-to-itself axial wall).

**4e. Path of draw:** for multiple-unit FPDs, the most-misaligned abutment determines the path. Taper non-parallel abutments inward to achieve common path (e.g., for #3–5 FPD with #5 distally inclined: taper the distal axial wall of #5 more aggressively; taper the mesial axial wall of #3 to match).

**4f. Finish the margin** with a fine-grit chamfer diamond. Chamfer width: 0.5–1.0 mm. Margin location: 0.5–1.0 mm subgingival for esthetic zones (single cord retraction will pull tissue away), supragingival or equigingival otherwise.
**4g. Smooth all line angles + surfaces.** Sharp angles concentrate stress; smooth flowing surfaces give the crown material adequate bulk + clean seating.

**4h. Verify reduction with the putty matrix:** seat the matrix, look for 1.5–2.0 mm clearance throughout the prep tooth. **Use a 1.0 mm probe (or condenser) inside the putty** — classic error is acceptable-looking putty clearance from the outside that's actually under-reduced when measured inside.
**4i.** Verify with explorer that no exposed dentin shows undercuts (undercuts trap impression material + interfere with crown seating).
**5a.** Select cord size: #00 for subgingival margins on healthy thin tissue; #0 for moderately subgingival on average tissue; #1 for deeper subgingival on thick tissue.
**5b. Single cord** works for most cases. **Dual cord** (smaller #00 placed first, larger #0/#1 on top) for deeper margins or sensitive bleeding tissue.
**5c.** Soak cord in hemostatic agent if active bleeding (ViscoStat 25%).
**5d.** Pack cord into the sulcus with a Fischer or thin cord packer. Work circumferentially. Cord should sit at the margin level, not over the margin.
**5e. Wait 5–10 min** for hemostasis + tissue retraction.
**6a.** Load the impression tray with heavy body PVS.
**6b.** Have an assistant remove the top retraction cord (leave deeper cord in place if dual-cord) just before impression.
**6c.** Dry the prep area with air syringe.
**6d.** Syringe light body PVS around the prep margin first, then over the prep, then over adjacent teeth.
**6e.** Immediately seat the loaded tray. Patient bites into MI (if triple tray) or you stabilize (if full-arch).
**6f. Hold for 4–5 min** set time (per material specs).
**6g.** Remove with a quick straight pull along the path of draw.

**6h. Inspect:** margin captured circumferentially (no missing areas), no voids, no tears, no folding, opposing arch captured clearly. Re-impress immediately if any issue (the cord is still in place, the tissue is still retracted).
**7a. Decide if needed:** if casts will hand-articulate (cleanly) and remain stable when articulated, **skip the bite registration** — Freilich 1992 principle, standard.
**7b. If bite reg is needed:** dispense fast-set PVS bite material on the occlusal of the prep + adjacent teeth only (not full arch).
**7c.** Patient closes into MI. Hold 1–2 min set.
**7d.** Remove. Trim excess flash with scissors. Verify the bite reg fits cleanly on both casts at the lab.
**8a.** Load the silicone putty matrix with bis-acryl provisional material (Protemp Plus or Integrity). Fill to ~80% of the tooth volume.
**8b.** Apply petroleum jelly to the prep tooth (release agent — otherwise bis-acryl sticks to the prep).
**8c.** Seat the matrix on the prepped + adjacent teeth.
**8d.** Hold 1.5–2 min through the doughy stage, then 3 min through full set. *Don't remove during the exotherm* — bis-acryl flashes briefly to >60°C; let it pass before pulling the matrix.
**8e.** Remove the matrix. The provisional should pull off the prep tooth with the matrix (or be removable as a unit).
**8f.** Separate the provisional from the matrix.
**8g.** Trim flash with a scalpel #15 blade or fine-grit acrylic bur.
**9a. Interproximal contacts:** floss should pass with snug resistance, not break. Adjust contacts first with fine diamond.
**9b. Margins:** trim flush to the prep margin. Use a scalpel #15 or a fine bur. *Slightly underextended is OK* (tissue heals); overextended causes inflammation + plaque.
**9c. For FPD: trim pontic to modified ridge lap.** Buccal contact with tissue, slopes lingual, convex M-D, no concavities. **No ridge lap (saddle) pontic** — uncleansable.
**9d.** Polish the provisional with rubber points + pumice + high-shine.
**9e.** Try in. Verify: passive seat, no rocking, marginal closure, contour matches the wax-up + adjacent teeth.
**9f. Occlusal check:** articulating paper. Compare to pre-prep reference contacts (which you recorded with shimstock at the start of the appt). Provisional should match the pre-prep contact pattern.
**9g.** Adjust occlusion with fine diamond + polish.
**10a.** Dry the prep with air syringe.
**10b.** Mix Tempbond NE per manufacturer (auto-mix tip or hand-mix).
