**10c.** Apply a thin layer to the intaglio of the provisional.
**10d.** Seat the provisional with firm finger pressure or have the patient bite into MI.
**10e. Gel-stage cleanup (3–4 min after seating):** remove excess cement with explorer + floss before it fully sets. Easier than chipping set cement.
**10f.** Wait full set (typically 6–8 min from mix).
**10g.** Final cleanup with explorer + floss. Polish any rough cement with polishing rubber points.
**10h.** Discharge instructions: avoid sticky/chewy foods on that side, brush + floss gently, call if provisional loosens.

blocks.title: Issues (Appt 1)
blocks.body: **Shade selected after isolation:** color reads too light. Use the photograph (with shade tab adjacent) as the reference for the lab; re-do shade selection at the next appointment if the photo isn't clear. **Outer putty clearance looks fine but inner reduction is under-prepared:** use a 1.0 mm probe / condenser *inside* the putty to verify the actual reduction. Re-prep before impression. **Impression has a void at the margin:** retraction tissue collapsed before the light body set. Re-pack cord, re-impress. **Bleeding contaminated the impression:** hemostatic-soaked cord for 5 min, re-impress. If bleeding persists, ViscoStat applied directly + cord re-pack. **Provisional won't release from prep:** insufficient release agent. Carefully pry with explorer; reseparate with retreat / fresh provisional if it fractures. Apply more petroleum jelly next time. **Ridge-lap pontic created accidentally (saddle):** trim to modified ridge lap chairside. The pontic must allow floss to pass under cleanly. **Provisional occlusion doesn't match pre-prep reference:** adjust with fine diamond + verify with shimstock. Don't deliver a provisional that alters the bite — the abutment teeth shift, the final crown won't fit.

blocks.text: Lab between Appt 1 and Appt 2 · Pour impression, fabricate working cast

items: Type IV die stone (high strength, low expansion) for working cast.
Vacuum mix per package W:P ratio (lower W:P = higher compressive strength).
Wait 45–60 min before separating from impression (~30 min after second pour).
Trim cast to horseshoe.
Pindex: holes drilled into bottom, rigid metal pins glued in with one path of insertion only.
Stone base poured around pins.
Section with saw cuts parallel to pin and long axis of prep (not parallel = die cannot remove cleanly).

blocks.text: Appointment 2 · Trim dies + mount casts (the lab session)

Cleanly trimmed dies with ideal root form emergence profile 3 mm below margin, independently removable, smooth surfaces, access to margin without touching the finish line. Mounting that reproduces patient MIP within shimstock accuracy.

items: Large excess off with lab bur, slow speed.
Detail with scalpel under magnification (loupes / microscope required by).
Aim for ~3 mm of root form below the margin (emergence profile).
**Never touch the finish line** — over-trimming creates poor emergence profile and overcontoured bulky final restoration that promotes plaque and gingival inflammation.
**Faculty grades on this:** "marking the margin with a graphite pencil" — discouraged because it abrades the margin and contaminates the surface.
Evaluate and clean casts — remove blebs and bubbles on occlusal surfaces.
Attach mounting plate to upper articulator member.
Set incisal pin to "0", confirm centric latch locked.
Hand-articulate upper working cast against lower diagnostic cast — confirm fit before mounting.
Trim base of working cast as needed for mounting plate clearance.
Cut deep retention grooves into base. Tape / rope wax over pin access holes.
Stabilize casts in MIP with sticky wax (casts must be dry).
Mix mounting stone; apply small amount to base plate and to center of cast.
Close articulator slowly — make sure stone doesn't cover pins.

After set, evaluate with shimstock — contacts should match what's seen in mouth. If they don't: distorted models, centric latch not locked, casts shifted, casts not seated in bite record, or bite record incorrect.

blocks.text: Lab between Appt 2 and Appt 3 · Framework wax-up and casting (outsourced)

items: Die spacer painted onto dies for cement space (ISO max film thickness: 25 µm water-based cement, 50 µm resin-based).
Full-contour wax-up to ideal.
Cut-back ensuring metal thickness ≥0.2–0.3 mm and porcelain space 0.8–2.0 mm.
Connectors designed: minimum 2 mm height anterior and posterior FPD.
Sprue attached, invested.
Wax eliminated in high-heat furnace.
Molten alloy cast (centrifuge).
Divestment, sprue removal, finishing.

blocks.text: Confirm framework fit BEFORE porcelain application; verify mounting accuracy.

items: Evaluate margins with explorer — closed simultaneously on both abutments without rocking.

If margins not closed: **Fit Checker** to find binding spots. Light lubricant on prep surfaces. Inject Fit Checker into intaglio, seat in mouth, remove after ~1 min. Adjust metal where Fit Checker appears extremely thin. Repeat until thin uniform layer with no perforations.
Periapical radiograph confirms complete seating.
Interocclusal clearance ≥0.5–0.8 mm.
Caliper-measure framework thickness while adjusting — minimum 0.2–0.3 mm metal (don't perforate).
Adjust with **aluminum oxide stone**, working one direction. **Never diamond** — particles embed, bubble during firing.
Check bite if mounting accuracy in doubt — PVS or resin index.
**Rocks / non-passive** → **section and solder**. Section ~business-card thickness, try in each segment, rejoin intraorally with pattern resin, lab solders.
**Seats but margins open** → check margins on cast. If margins closed on cast: redo impression. If margins not closed on cast: remake framework.

blocks.text: Lab between Appt 3 and Appt 4 · Porcelain application (outsourced)

items: Sandblast framework — uniform roughness for micromechanical retention, increased wettability.
**Oxidation firing** — tin, indium, gallium migrate to surface to form oxide layer for chemical bond.
**Opaque** layer — blocks dark metal color.
**Dentin** porcelain — forms bulk, contributes most color.
**Enamel** porcelain — translucency, contour.
Fired under vacuum to reduce voids; slow cooling to prevent cracking. Restoration over-contoured initially because porcelain shrinks 27–45% during firing.
Final contouring with diamond burs after last firing.
Adjustment sequence on mounted working cast: interproximal contacts → margins → pontic → occlusion → contours.

blocks.text: **Return options:** bisque bake try-in (unglazed, unstained — preferred for 4+ unit cases) OR fully glazed/stained ready for cementation.

**Bisque bake** = unglazed, unstained porcelain. Indicated for 4+ unit cases — contour adjustments can be made without breaking the glazed surface, and refiring is minimized (excess refiring causes **devitrification** — vitreous to crystalline lattice change, increases CTE and opacity).

items: **Interproximal contacts:** visually closed, identical firmness to contralateral, floss passes without shredding. If too tight, mark with articulating paper, adjust with fine diamond. Too open: porcelain needs to be added (back to lab).
**Margins:** explorer checks for closed; ideal margin is undetectable. Slightly underextended (tooth ledge <0.25 mm) acceptable; open (explorer fits between margin and finish line) = remake.
**Pontic:** modified ridge lap, tissue surface convex and cleansable, contact with minimal blanching, floss passes under.
**Occlusion:** MIP even and widely distributed matching contralateral; protrusive shared between #8 and #9; left lateral excursive smooth on #11 with posterior disclusion.
**Contours / esthetics:** symmetry to contralateral, line angles, developmental grooves, incisal edge length.

blocks.text: Lab between Bisque Bake and Final · Glaze and stain

items: **Autoglaze (natural glaze):** bisque bake raised to fusion temperature, sharp angles round slightly, occlusal contacts slightly altered.
**Overglaze:** glaze paste applied to surface and fired. Wears more rapidly than natural; may erode leaving rough porous surface in mouth.
**Stains:** extrinsic basic color guide — **yellow cervical**, **blue incisal**, **brown interproximal**, **white facial**. Use sparingly; blend in clear glaze liquid.
**Final restoration** (lab-returned, in case), **working cast** for reference.
**Isolation:** cotton rolls, Dri-Angle, saliva ejector. (Isodry if margins are subgingival or any moisture risk.)
**Try-in materials:** articulating paper (AccuFilm II + Surgident), shimstock, floss, explorer.

**Intaglio prep:** sandblaster (at lab or in clinic), or roughening for chairside; alcohol wipe / disinfectant. For e.max: 5% HF acid + silane primer. For zirconia: alumina sandblast + zirconia primer (10-MDP, e.g., Z-Prime).

**Cement:** RMGI (RelyX Luting Plus, RelyX Luting 2) for standard PFM / zirconia; resin cement (RelyX Unicem 2, Multilink Automix) for e.max / esthetic anterior; conventional GIC (Fuji I) for full-cast or in cases of poor isolation. Match cement to crown material.
**Polishing kit:** rubber points (blue / pink / gray) for porcelain; brown + green points for metal collar.
**Fine red-stripe diamond** for occlusal adjustments to glazed porcelain (if needed).
Final restoration inspected on the cast: marginal seal, no porcelain chips, polish acceptable.
Patient anesthetized if needed (typically not necessary if provisional was on a vital tooth; consider for sensitive patients).
Provisional removed gently with a Backhaus or by patient-bite-and-twist. Remove all temporary cement residue from the prep with hand instruments + air-water spray.
Verify prep is intact (no fractures, no caries discovered since impression).
**Step 1. Try in glazed restoration.** Re-verify all 5 items from bisque bake (interproximal contacts, margins, pontic, occlusion, contours/esthetics).
**Step 2. Isolation:** cotton rolls + Dri-Angle + saliva ejector. Isodry if margins are subgingival.

**Step 3. Clean intaglio:** sandblast at lab or in clinic if available; alcohol / disinfect on prep side. For e.max: 5% HF etch 20 sec + silane primer 60 sec air-dry. For zirconia: alumina sandblast + 10-MDP primer.
**Step 4. Mix cement** per manufacturer (auto-mix gun for paste-paste; hand-mix for powder-liquid).
**Step 5. Apply** a thin layer to the entire intaglio.
**Step 6. Seat** with firm pressure. For FPD: alternate finger pressure on both retainers to seat evenly.
**Step 7. Gel-stage cleanup** of excess (2–3 min after seating, before fully set — **much** easier than chipping set cement). Use explorer + floss interproximally.
**Step 8.** Light cure margins if RMGI dual-cure (20 sec each margin) or wait per manufacturer for GIC final set.
**Step 9. Final excess cleanup:** explorer interproximally, floss through contacts (curve the floss around the lingual to release from the contact — don't snap up vertically).

**Step 10. Check occlusion** with articulating paper. Adjust with fine red-stripe diamond on glazed porcelain if needed. Re-polish to high luster with rubber points: blue (coarse) → pink (medium) → gray (fine).
**Step 11. Metal collar polishing** (PFM only) with brown + green polishing points after final glazing — removes oxide layer, enhances esthetics, improves corrosion resistance.
**Step 12. Post-op:** recommend OTC analgesics for 24–48 hours, normal hygiene, normal eating, call if any pain / loose feeling / high spot.

blocks.title: Issues (full workflow)
blocks.body: **Crown doesn't seat fully (Appt 4):** remove provisional cement residue from prep (most common cause). If still won't seat: Fit Checker the intaglio (Appt 3 protocol) to find binding spots. Adjust intaglio with red-stripe diamond. **Margin is open after cementation:** stop. Document with PA, discuss with faculty. Open margins lead to recurrent caries and pulp issues — usually requires remake. **Occlusion is high after cementation:** patient bites lightly, you mark with AccuFilm, adjust with red-stripe diamond, re-polish. *Don't* aggressively grind — small adjustments preserve the glaze. **Cement sets before you finish cleanup:** chip excess with the explorer + use a fine-grit interproximal saw (Brasseler). Floss may snap; thread fresh floss using a floss threader. Polish any rough cement spots. **Sensitivity post-cementation:** usually from desiccated dentin during isolation; should resolve in 1–2 weeks. If persistent > 2 weeks or worsening: vitality test, consider RCT if non-vital. **Provisional fell off between Appt 1 and Appt 4:** patient calls — bring in for re-cementation with Tempbond NE. Verify the prep + adjacent teeth haven't shifted. **Crown fractures at Appt 4 try-in:** remake. Document, photograph, send back to lab. Common cause: thin porcelain in a stress area or bisque-bake adjustment thinned the porcelain.

chapters.verdict: The traditional PFM (or all-ceramic conventional) workflow. Four clinical sessions with lab steps between. Even for a digital chairside e.max single crown, knowing this workflow tells you what the lab is doing when you outsource.

chapters.title: Digital (CAD/CAM) workflow appointment-by-appointment

items: **Anesthesia + prep materials** (same as conventional): anesthesia, isolation, bur block, retraction cord, hemostatic agent.
**3Shape TRIOS scanner** in its cradle, with the tip warmed (button on the handle, ~3 min warm-up to avoid fogging).
**Design Studio software** open on the operatory computer, patient case created.
**e.max block** (lithium disilicate) in the patient's shade, or zirconia block. stocks B1, A2, A3, A3.5 commonly. Verify shade before milling.
**CEREC MC X or Roland mill** with fresh diamonds (replace at the diamond-tip indicator).

**Crystallization is sent to the lab tech** (Dan) for the ~25 min Programat firing — students don't operate the furnace themselves. Hand off the milled, sprue-removed restoration in a labeled cup; pick up crystallized.

**Stain + glaze kit** (Ivoclar IPS Empress Esthetic or e.max Ceram): brown / yellow / blue / white stains, glaze paste, firing liquid, firing paste, firing pins — applied to the milled restoration before lab firing, or after as a correction fire.
**Polishing kit:** rubber points (blue / pink / gray) + diamond polishing paste + felt wheel.
**Cementation kit:** Ivoclean, IPS Ceramic Etching Gel (5% HF), Clearfil Ceramic Primer, ED Primer II A&B (self-etch primer), Panavia F2.0 dual-cure resin cement (A&B pastes), OxyGard II.
**Articulating paper** (AccuFilm II + Surgident) + shimstock.
**Fine red-stripe diamond** for post-cementation occlusal adjustment.
Scanner tip warmed and ready (insert into the cradle warmer 3+ min before).
Design Studio open, patient case created with: tooth #, restoration type, material, shade.
Mill prepped: dummy block in place, diamonds inspected, water reservoir full.
Hand-off plan to the lab tech (Dan) confirmed — restoration will be brought over after milling for crystallization (~25 min round trip). Brief Dan if the case is rushed.
Stain + glaze kit on the lab bench, brushes clean.
Cementation kit ready in the cementation drawer.
Faculty checkpoint plan: scan approval (after Step 5), design approval (after Step 6), final restoration (before Step 11).

blocks.text: A pre-preparation scan captures the patient's existing contours, used by the designer to mimic those contours when restoring. Used for: cases where preserving the original tooth shape is important, anterior crowns where the patient liked the original, restorations copied from a dentist-designed provisional (scan the provisional, copy contours to the final restoration).
Visualization slider in Design Studio toggles between pre-prep and prep scans. Pre-prep scan should stay under ~1,000 images; preparation scans under 2,000 (zoom into specific areas once exceeded).

Same as conventional — but Standard practice emphasizes that for the scanner to capture the finish line, **tissue retraction must clearly expose the finish line**. Supragingival margins are ideal; if subgingival, cord retraction is mandatory. Lack of retraction = "Standard Not Met" on the IOS rubric.

Occlusal first (most distal molar — multiple reliable data points), then roll lingual scanning mesial-to-distal, then roll buccal scanning distal-to-mesial. Upper: occlusal → buccal → palatal. Lower: occlusal → lingual → buccal. **"Tissue is the issue"** — gingival tissue tends to bounce data; aim for clean teeth with 2–3 mm gingival capture buccal and lingual.
Goals per the IOS PPC rubric

items: 100% of tooth preparation, interproximal contacts, and adjacent teeth.
2–3 mm of gingival tissue buccal and lingual on involved teeth.
Clearly visible, well-defined finish line.
Mark the prepared tooth in software.
HD photo (press scanner button to capture image).
Texture scan (verify finish line definition).
Virtual sectioning (slice the data).
Lock tool (prevent re-scanning of a finalized area).

blocks.text: **Critical:** under 2,000 images per scan or the software degrades — clear and restart if exceeded.

100% of occlusal and buccal surfaces; 90% of lingual; 2–3 mm of buccal gingiva. Lingual is non-critical. **Proper number of teeth for stable bite registration:** for posterior single units, at least 2 adjacent teeth on each side; for anterior single units, 2nd premolar to 2nd premolar.

Insert scanner supporting on cheeks, ask patient to bite firmly, verify bite. Scan from most distal max teeth captured previously to most mesial, then from mesial-to-distal on lower — creating a "C" pattern. Minimum 4 teeth per arch in the bite scan plus 2–3 mm gingiva. **For full-arch, right and left side occlusion scans both required.**
Step 5 · Analyze + margin select (in Design Studio)

items: **Alignment tool** — verify maxillary and mandibular scans align reproducing clinical scenario.
**Undercuts tool** — visualize undercut areas highlighted; if path of insertion cannot be modified to eliminate undercut, refine prep and rescan.
**Clearance** — verify interocclusal space.
**Finish line** — autodetection click-and-go for crowns; click-and-go for partial coverage; HD photos to verify position. Drag to edit position as needed.
**Select anatomy:** AI-proposed, Smile Library, Copy tool from contralateral or pre-prep scan, Other Library.
Activate antagonist visualization and Transform tool to place the restoration in proper position and size.
**Connect Margin tool** (Automatic tools) — connects restoration margin to selected finish line.
**Minimum Thickness tool** — areas thinner than material's minimum highlight on the restoration; use Wax Knife / Morph to add or Minimum Thickness automatic tool to adjust.
**Contacts tool** — apply automatic interproximal contacts; verify size and position; use Paint and Erase tools or Wax Knife to broaden / narrow.
**Occlusal contacts** — automatic setting verified manually.
Refine anatomy: Morph tool (large areas), Wax Knife (smaller adjustments), Cross Section tool (measure thickness / contour).
Address all notifications — accept or adjust.
Save case.

blocks.text: Step 7 · Mill (chairside) or send to lab
the chairside default is the in-clinic mill. Place sprue away from margins, interproximal contacts, occlusal contacts, and esthetic areas. For lab-milled, the file goes to the digital lab.

For e.max: hand off to the lab tech (Dan) for the ~25 min Programat fire. Strength jumps from 130 ± 30 MPa (blue block) to 360 ± 60 MPa (crystallized). Students don't operate the furnace; the firing happens behind the scenes while you re-fixture the operatory and brief the patient. For zirconia: sintering parameters per material; the lab handles this if sent out.
Two options per: characterize and stain **in the pre-crystallized stage** (built into the crystallization fire), or **after crystallization** (requires a second correction fire).

**Stain placement:** brown / yellow cervical, blue incisal (for translucency), white incisal (halo), white facial (decalcifications). Endo files for fine details; thin brushes for blending. **Firing paste** fixes restoration to firing pin, applied to intaglio extending to margins but not over-filling (so glaze doesn't seal the intaglio).

items: **Remove sprue** holding the restoration. Short motions, light pressure (overheating risks crystallized e.max).
**Proximal contacts:** articulating paper, slow-speed adjustment.
**Internal surfaces (intaglio) — but first adjust the prep:** look for sharp angles on the prep; only adjust the restoration intaglio if the prep is already smooth. Measure thickness with calipers.
**Radiograph (bitewing)** to verify seating in clinic.
**Occlusion:** always measure restoration thickness before adjustment.
**Anatomy:** facial characterization with diamond burs to create texture.

blocks.text: Resin cement, dual-cured, two shades available at. Self-etch primer.

items: Ivoclean intaglio 20 sec; rinse, air dry.
5% HF etching gel (IPS Ceramic Etching Gel) 20 sec; rinse, air dry.
Clearfil Ceramic Primer 60 sec (no rinse); air dry.
Mix ED Primer II A & B equal parts (self-etch primer).
Apply to tooth 30 sec; air dry excess (no rinse).
Dispense Panavia F2.0 pastes A & B equal parts; mix 20 sec.
Apply to intaglio with microbrush.
Seat restoration with firm finger pressure; remove gross excess.
Either light-cure margins 20 sec per surface, OR apply OxyGard II at margins for self-cure (wait 1 min, wash).

blocks.title: Intraoral scanner — what to verify
blocks.body: The Intraoral Scanning Pre-Patient-Care rubric scores 19 criteria across Excellent / Clinically Acceptable / Standard Not Met. **Critical deficiency triggers:** case selection, professionalism, patient management / infection control, time management, preparedness / cleanliness. **Two or more "Standard Not Met" = fail.** **Key rubric items:** dry field with cotton rolls + dry angle + saliva ejector; teeth in scan clean from debris; proper cord selection and placement; scanner ergonomic and properly positioned on holder when not scanning; scan pattern followed (occ → lingual → buccal as above); 100% of prep + interproximal contacts + adjacent teeth + 2–3 mm gingiva; tooth marked; ≥2 adjacent teeth posterior single / 2P–2P for anterior single; finish line clearly visible; opposing 100% occlusal+buccal, 90% lingual, 2–3 mm gingival buccal; max-mand alignment reproduces clinical scenario; ≤2,000 images per scan; undercuts and clearance evaluated; margin properly selected; scans properly trimmed; lab script complete (tooth #, restoration type, material, shade, scan date, additional details); local anesthesia achieved.

blocks.title: Issues (digital workflow)
blocks.body: **Scanner can't capture the finish line:** tissue isn't retracted enough. Re-pack cord, wait 5 min, re-scan only the affected region. Don't blame the scanner — tissue is almost always the issue. **Scan has >2,000 images and is degrading:** stop, clear, restart with a more efficient pattern. Don't add to a corrupt scan. **Bite scan doesn't align with the prep + opposing scans:** the bite was scanned with the patient not in MI, or there's a tooth shift. Re-scan with the patient firmly biting MI, verify alignment in software before proceeding. **Mill fails or fractures during milling:** check the diamond burs (replace if dull or chipped) and the block (verify it's seated correctly). Re-mill with a fresh block. **Crystallized e.max comes back from the lab with cracks or distortion:** firing-pin placement was wrong, or the firing paste was inadequate. Document, remake from the same case file; consult with the lab tech about the firing parameters used. **Crystallized e.max is too dark / wrong shade:** block was wrong shade. Verify the block lot # against the order; remill from the correct block. **Try-in shows the crown won't seat:** check for prep undercut you missed (Design Studio undercuts tool should have caught it). Adjust the intaglio with the red-stripe diamond at the binding spot. Verify with Fit Checker. **Occlusion is too high at try-in:** articulating paper marks, adjust with red-stripe diamond. Polish to high luster with rubber points after adjustment. **Margin gap visible after cementation:** intaglio wasn't fully seated — cement set with the crown in suspension. Remake. Common cause: missed sprue removal, or undercut prep.

chapters.verdict: the digital track uses 3Shape TRIOS + Design Studio chairside design software, plus lab-milled paths through the same software when needed. Same-day chairside is the lithium disilicate single-unit standard. Plan 3–4 hours for same-day chairside e.max single units.

chapters.title: Metal framework try-in evaluation

items: Framework received from lab. Verify patient name + tooth #.
**Inspect on working cast first:** rests seat, no rocking, marginal closure with explorer, no porosity or defects in casting.
Verify cast accuracy: any chips at the prep margins? Are the dies independently removable?
Have **Fit Checker** (silicone disclosing medium) + lubricant ready.
Have **aluminum oxide adjustment stones** (NOT diamond) on the slow-speed.
PA radiograph supplies ready for seating verification.
Calipers for framework thickness measurement.
Schedule 1 hour for routine try-in; 1.5 hr if anticipating misfit.
Remove provisional. Clean abutment teeth of any temporary cement (scaler + prophy cup).
Seat framework with finger pressure. Should drop into place with light resistance.
**Run explorer along the margins on both abutments simultaneously** — should be undetectable closure with no catch.
Test for rocking: alternating digital pressure on opposite sides. Any rock = not passive.
If margins look closed and no rocking → proceed to PA radiograph (Step 3).
If margins open OR rocking → proceed to Fit Checker (Step 2).
**Lubricate the abutment prep surfaces** lightly (Vaseline or supplied lubricant). Don't over-apply; thin film only.
Inject **Fit Checker into the framework intaglio** — thin uniform layer over all internal surfaces.
Seat the framework with even finger pressure.
**Hold for ~1 minute** while Fit Checker sets.
Remove the framework carefully (don't smear).
**Inspect the intaglio:** areas where Fit Checker is **extremely thin or absent** are binding spots — the metal is contacting prep too tightly there.
Adjust those areas with **aluminum oxide stone** at slow speed.
Clean off old Fit Checker, re-apply, re-seat, re-inspect.
Repeat until you see a **thin, uniform Fit Checker layer with NO perforations** — that's complete seating.
Take a periapical radiograph with the framework seated.
**Confirm complete seating:** no visible gap between framework margin and prep finish line.
Check connector area for any radiopaque shadow indicating incomplete seating elsewhere on the bridge.
If gap visible: Fit Checker again to find the offending area.
**Interocclusal clearance:** ≥0.5–0.8 mm between framework occlusal and opposing. Verify with AccuFilm + shimstock.
If too little clearance: reduce framework occlusal with aluminum oxide stone.
**Framework thickness:** minimum 0.2–0.3 mm metal. Measure with calipers as you adjust.
**Don't perforate.** If you start seeing pinpoint shine through, stop adjusting that area.
**Aluminum oxide stone only.** NEVER diamond — diamond particles embed in alloy and bubble during porcelain firing, ruining the restoration.

blocks.text: Step 5 — Bite verification (if mounting in doubt)

items: If you suspect the lab's mounting was off (centric contact discrepancies, weird interocclusal clearance): take a new bite reg with the framework in place.
PVS or pattern-resin index between max and mand frameworks/teeth.
Lab can remount and verify.
**Rocks / non-passive seating:** framework is geometrically wrong on multi-unit bridge.

→ **Section and solder**: with a separating disc, cut the framework at a connector to the thickness of a business card. Try in each segment separately — they should each seat passively. Rejoin intraorally with pattern resin (Duralay) at the section line. Send to lab for soldering.
**Seats but margins are open**: check margins on cast.
If margins closed on cast: redo the impression (cast-to-mouth mismatch).
If margins not closed on cast: remake the framework (lab error in fabrication).
**Framework perforates during adjustment**: remake. Don't send a perforated framework for porcelain.
**Interocclusal clearance grossly inadequate:** either reduce more (if metal thickness allows) or remake.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Fit Checker shows uniform thin but framework still rocks:** the seating is fine but the prep itself has a problem — faculty assist. **Patient says "feels weird" on bite:** the framework may be impinging the lateral wall of an adjacent tooth. Test by removing the adjacent contact with a thin disc on the framework proximal — does it fit then? If yes, the contact was the issue. **Connector area is too thin:** remake. Below 2 mm height for PFM FPD = fracture risk. Don't accept the framework. **You over-adjust:** small adjustments only. If you've reduced >0.5 mm and still don't have closure, faculty assist or remake.

blocks.title: Faculty checkpoints — framework try-in
blocks.body: **Why Fit Checker?** Identify binding areas. **Minimum metal thickness?** 0.2–0.3 mm. **Minimum connector height (PFM FPD)?** 2 mm. **Misfit management?** Section + solder, or remake based on cast inspection. **Where should occlusal contacts be?** Entirely porcelain OR entirely metal; never at the junction (1.0–2.0 mm minimum from junction). **Porcelain thickness range?** 0.8–2.0 mm. 2.0 mm = insufficient support, fracture risk.

chapters.verdict: The last reversible step before porcelain. If the framework rocks or has marginal openings, you fix it here — sectioning and soldering is cheap, remaking after porcelain is applied is expensive.

chapters.title: Cementation comprehensive

blocks.text: **Luted** (mechanical retention from prep + cement film holding it):

items: GIC (RelyX Luting, FujiCEM, Fuji PLUS, Riva Luting Plus, RelyX Plus).
RMGI (improved GIC with resin + acid-base + polymerization, fluoride release).
Resin cements (conventional with separate primer / adhesive; or self-adhesive with primer / acid built in).
Weaker restorative material (lithium disilicate, feldspathic, leucite-reinforced).
Non-retentive preparation (overly tapered, <4 mm crown length, inlays / onlays).
Translucent restorations (veneers — light-cured veneer cement).
Less than ideal occlusal thickness.
**Self-cured:** chemical reaction only (base + catalyst, tertiary amine + benzoyl peroxide). C&B MetaBond, Comspan, Superbond, C&B Cement. Tend to **darken with time**.
**Dual-cured:** chemical + light. Used when ceramic is too thick for light penetration. NX3 Nexus, RelyX ARC, Variolink II, RelyX Ultimate, Panavia. Most clinic restorations.
**Light-cured:** photoinitiator (camphoroquinone) — color stable but requires thin/translucent restoration. RelyX Veneer, Variolink Veneer, Choice 2. For veneers.

blocks.text: **the digital clinic default:** self-etch dual-cured resin cement.

What you do to the intaglio (fitting surface) of the restoration before cement contact. The protocol depends on material + cement system. For digitally-designed e.max at the clinic, the IPS Ceramic Etching Gel + Clearfil Ceramic Primer + Panavia sequence is the documented protocol.
Before any cementation — soak the restoration

items: **Cavicide 5 minutes** → rinse with water.
**Chlorhexidine 5 minutes** → rinse with water.
Air dry 100%. This is the clinic's documented pre-cementation crown disinfection step.

**PFM (metal substrate at intaglio):** verify fit + clean. No HF or silane needed (no glass phase exposed). For adhesive cementation, air-abrade metal with 50 µm Al₂O₃ and use a metal primer per cement manufacturer; for conventional cementation (RelyX or FujiCem) no primer needed.

**e.max (digital, Panavia system per clinic documentation):** Ivoclean 20 sec → rinse + dry → IPS Ceramic Etching Gel (5% HF) 20 sec → rinse + dry → Clearfil Ceramic Primer 60 sec (no rinse) → dry. Clearfil Ceramic Primer contains MDP + silane in one bottle.
**e.max (lab-fabricated, RelyX Ultimate system):** intaglio treated per RelyX manufacturer instructions — HF etch + silane + RelyX Ultimate adhesive cementation.

**Feldspathic porcelain (veneers, layered ceramics):** HF etch the porcelain surface (manufacturer time, longer than e.max because the glass phase is denser) → rinse → silane → bond. Cement choice typically light-cure resin for translucent veneers; clinic may specify a particular brand.

**Zirconia (3Y / 4Y / 5Y monolithic):** HF does NOT etch zirconia (no glass phase). Air-abrasion with 50 µm Al₂O₃ + MDP-containing primer (Clearfil Ceramic Primer is the clinic primer of choice from the digital protocol; clinic may use a different MDP primer for zirconia-specific cases) + dual-cure resin cement.
**Cast gold / noble alloy:** air-abrasion to roughen intaglio if using adhesive cementation. For conventional cementation (RelyX or FujiCem), no surface treatment beyond cleaning is needed.
**Fiber post (ParaPost):** clean canal + sandblast or air-abrade post per manufacturer; silanate the post surface; dual-cure resin cement (Panavia or RelyX Ultimate).

blocks.title: Try-in contamination cleanup
blocks.body: Saliva contamination at try-in destroys adhesive bond strength. The clinic's digital protocol uses **Ivoclean 20 sec → rinse → dry** as the documented post-try-in cleanup before bonding. This is part of the documented e.max cementation sequence.

blocks.text: Per the clinic Materials reference, the cement choices are: **FujiCem (RMGI)**, **RelyX Ultimate (resin for ceramic restorations)**, and **Panavia (resin, dual-cure, for digitally-designed ceramic crowns)**. The choice by case:

items: **PFM crown (single unit or bridge):** **RelyX or FujiCem** — the clinic default. RelyX is the resin option; FujiCem is the RMGI option. Either acceptable for PFM with adequate prep retention.

**e.max (digital chairside, inlay/onlay/crown):** **Panavia (dual-cure resin)** per the digital workflow protocol — Ivoclean cleanup → 5% HF etch → Clearfil Ceramic Primer → Panavia A+B paste mix → seat → light cure 20 sec each surface.
**e.max (lab-fabricated):** **RelyX Ultimate** (the resin for ceramic restorations per the Materials reference).

**Zirconia:** **Panavia + Clearfil Ceramic Primer** by analogy with the e.max digital protocol; HF does NOT etch zirconia — air-abrasion + MDP primer is the only effective surface treatment. Clinic may use a different MDP primer system for zirconia.
**Cast gold:** **RelyX or FujiCem**. Either works; cement choice is less critical because cast gold's marginal accuracy is high and retention form is good.
**Bridge / FPD (PFM):** same as PFM single crown — **RelyX or FujiCem**.
**Veneer:** light-cure resin cement (Variolink-style products); the clinic Materials reference does not list a veneer-specific cement. Verify with the lab + faculty for the current clinic default.
**Fiber post (ParaPost):** dual-cure resin cement (**Panavia** or **RelyX Ultimate**) — light cannot reach the apical 1/3, so the cement must self-cure under the post.

blocks.title: Common cement-choice errors
blocks.body: **Using FujiCem on a digitally-designed e.max crown:** the protocol is Panavia for digital ceramic crowns. RMGI lacks the adhesive bond e.max needs for long-term success. **Skipping Ivoclean after try-in:** saliva contamination drops the bond to ~30% of design strength. **HF etch on zirconia:** wastes a minute and provides false confidence — the bond is from sandblast + MDP primer, not HF. **Light-cure only under a fiber post:** cement at the apical 1/3 never polymerizes; post loosens within months.

items: Restoration verified at try-in: margins close, contacts pass floss, occlusion correct, contours match design.
Patient seen + approved the shade + shape in mirror.
Faculty present (cement-retained crowns require oversight).
Throat pack placed (if posterior).
Cement chosen per the cement-material matrix above.

**Materials staged:** HF gel (if needed), silane, primer (per cement system), tooth-side etch + primer + adhesive (per cement system), mix pad, cement dispenser, microbrush, plastic instrument, floss with knot, explorer.
Curing light tested for output.

blocks.text: Step 1 — Try-in verification (last chance to catch fit issues)

items: Seat the restoration with finger pressure.
Run explorer along all margins — should be undetectable.
Pass floss through both contacts — should snap through with light resistance (not loose, not stuck).
Bite check: AccuFilm (40 micron) marks centric contacts; shimstock (8 micron) holds in MIP.
Visual contour check: symmetric to contralateral, embrasures correct, no high spots.
**If anything fails:** stop. Adjust or send back to lab. Don't cement a compromised restoration.
**Cotton rolls** buccal and lingual of the working area.
**Dry angle** on the buccal cheek covering parotid duct.
**Saliva ejector** in the floor of the mouth.
For **bonded ceramic** (e.max, feldspathic, veneers): Isodry if feasible. Moisture during bonding = failure within months.
Dry the prep with mild air. No saliva film.
Look up the material in the intaglio-treatment matrix above.
**e.max:** Ivoclean 20 sec → rinse, dry → **HF 5% × 20 sec** (NOT 60) → rinse 30 sec, dry → silane (Clearfil) 1 min → dry.
**Zirconia:** ≤50 µm Al₂O₃ air abrasion → MDP primer (per cement system).
**Feldspathic:** HF 10% × 60 sec → rinse → silane.
**PFM:** lab sandblasted already; nothing more needed for GIC/RMGI.
After surface treatment, do NOT contaminate the intaglio with saliva or oils.
Per chosen cement system:
**Self-etch:** apply primer per manufacturer (typically 20–30 sec). Air dry to thin film.
**Total-etch:** 36–37% phosphoric acid (etch enamel ≥15 sec, dentin ≤10 sec). Rinse 30 sec. Leave dentin moist. Apply primer, then adhesive.
**Self-adhesive cement:** no tooth-side treatment (etch + primer built into cement).
For **the clinic default (self-etch dual-cured resin)**: mix ED Primer II A&B, apply to tooth 30 sec, air dry excess.
Mix cement per manufacturer (typically 20-sec mix for dual-cured resin; GIC follows powder/liquid ratio).
Apply cement to **intaglio of the restoration**, NOT the prep (this ensures cement is captured inside the crown).
Thin layer; no globs.
Seat the restoration on the prep with **firm fingertip pressure**.
Hold steady; don't rock.
**Within 30–60 sec**: cement enters gel stage.
**Tack-cure 1–2 sec at each margin** (resin cement) — accelerates gel stage so cleanup is much easier.
Peel away gross excess with an explorer at the margins.
**Floss with a knot tied**: pull through interproximal contact and pull out the buccal side, dragging cement out.
Inspect: any remaining excess?
Use plastic instrument or explorer to remove anything visible.
**This is the moment that determines whether you'll have residual cement or not.** Take your time.
**Light cure:** 40 sec per surface (buccal, lingual, occlusal).
OR self-cure wait time (5–10 min per manufacturer).
For GIC: wait full set per manufacturer; don't disturb during set.
Once fully cured, use explorer to confirm no residual cement at margins.
Floss through all contacts again.
**For posterior crowns (implants especially): post-cement PA radiograph to verify NO subgingival residual cement.**
If residual cement seen: scaler removal. Plastic scaler for ceramics; metal explorer / curette only for non-ceramic.
AccuFilm + shimstock check in MIP. Adjust high spots with fine red-stripe diamond (yes, diamond OK on ceramic; just re-polish after).
Excursion check: working + balancing + protrusive. Adjust per BULL principle.
**Re-polish any adjusted area** with rubber points (blue coarse → pink medium → gray fine) to high luster. Unpolished ceramic abrades opposing.
Final visual + tactile inspection.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Restoration doesn't seat fully:** high interproximal contact or high spot in intaglio. Don't force. Adjust interproximal first (least likely to compromise fit), then re-check. **Cement set faster than expected:** faculty assist. Plastic scaler for ceramic; metal explorer for non-bonded. Pick excess off slowly. **You see residual cement on the post-cement radiograph:** mandatory removal. Explorer + scaler. Re-radiograph after. **Crown rocks after cement set:** incomplete seating during cementation, or marginal mismatch. May need to remove + redo. Faculty. **Patient gags during impression-tray try-in cementation:** raise the chair, redirect breathing, work fast. **Color looks wrong after cementation:** cement shade mismatched to block + stump. For e.max, color = block + cement + remaining tooth + stains. Live with it or remake (rare).

blocks.text: Re-cementing a debonded crown or bridge

Patient calls or walks in with a crown / bridge in hand, says it "just fell off." Two-part evaluation: **(1) is the crown still good?** and **(2) is the prep still good?** If both yes — re-cement chairside today. If either is compromised — remake.

items: **Visual inspection:** any fractures? Chipped porcelain? Worn-through occlusal? → remake.
**Intaglio inspection:** residual cement still bonded inside (debonded from prep side) or cement adherent to prep (debonded from crown side)? Both happen; both are fine for re-cementing.
**Margin integrity:** margins still crisp? → OK to re-cement. Worn / scalloped margins? → remake.
**Try in on the prep dry** (after cleaning, see Step 2). If it seats fully + contacts feel correct + the crown rocks (rocks = no), the crown is still usable.

**Crown intaglio:** remove all residual cement. Sandblast (lab) is ideal; chairside alternative is a tungsten-carbide bur on slow speed, working circumferentially. For glass-ceramic (e.max): re-etch with 5% HF for 20 sec, rinse, silanize before re-cement. For zirconia: sandblast + re-prime with 10-MDP primer.
**Prep surface:** remove all residual cement from the prep with an ultrasonic scaler or hand instruments. Don't damage the prep margins. Pumice + prophy cup to clean the dentin.
**Dry both surfaces.** Air syringe both intaglio + prep until clean and dry.

**Cement is intact on the crown but not the prep:** the bond failed at the tooth interface. Common cause: moisture contamination at original cementation, or the prep was too short for retention (height <3 mm). If retention is short, consider prep adjustment or post + core.

**Cement is intact on the prep but not the crown:** bond failed at the crown intaglio. Common cause: inadequate intaglio prep at original cementation (didn't sandblast, didn't HF-etch). Re-prep the intaglio thoroughly this time.

**Cement is degraded on both sides (crumbly or absent):** material failure of the original cement. Choose a different cement this time — if the original was a conventional GIC and the crown debonded under load, escalate to a resin-modified GIC or a self-adhesive resin.

**Crown rocks or doesn't fully seat now:** something has changed. Prep may have been damaged (caries, fracture), or adjacent teeth have shifted closing the contact, or the crown itself is distorted. Investigate before re-cementing — might be a remake.
**If the original cement is known and worked initially:** use the same cement (with whatever surface prep is appropriate for it).

**If the original failed under load:** escalate. RMGI (RelyX Luting Plus) is a safe upgrade from conventional GIC. Self-adhesive resin (RelyX Unicem 2) is a further upgrade. Dual-cure resin (Multilink Automix) is the strongest but most technique-sensitive.
**For e.max:** always resin cement after HF-etch + silane. Don't downgrade to RMGI on e.max — the glass-ceramic chemistry needs the resin bond.
**For zirconia:** RMGI or self-adhesive resin both work. Sandblast intaglio + 10-MDP primer regardless.
**For PFM or cast-metal:** RMGI is the standard choice. No special surface prep beyond intaglio cleaning.

blocks.text: Follow the standard cementation steps from earlier in this chapter (intaglio prep per material → isolation → mix cement → apply to intaglio → seat with firm pressure → gel-stage cleanup → final cleanup → verify margins + interproximal + occlusion + polish). The only difference from a first cementation is that you may need to take a post-cementation PA radiograph to verify no excess cement subgingivally if the prep margin is deep.

blocks.title: When NOT to re-cement
blocks.body: **Recurrent debonding** (this is the 2nd or 3rd time the same crown has fallen off) → the prep is the problem, not the cement. Investigate retention form, prep height, taper. May need post + core or remake. **New caries detected** at the margin during the re-cement evaluation → remake (excavate caries, build up, re-prep, re-impress). **Crown has visible damage** (chip, crack, worn cusp) → remake. **Adjacent teeth have shifted** creating tight contacts the crown no longer fits through → either adjust the adjacent teeth (orthodontic / interproximal reduction) or remake the crown with new impression.

blocks.text: Bridges debond in three patterns, each with a different evaluation:

items: **(1) Both retainers debonded simultaneously** — like a single-unit crown failure but for both abutments. Most common in conventional bridges where cement fatigue or trauma dislodges the entire prosthesis. Re-cement protocol same as single-unit, but on BOTH abutments. Seat with even pressure on both retainers simultaneously (excess cement should escape evenly from both).

**(2) One retainer debonded, the other still cemented** — **the most dangerous pattern.** The cemented abutment continues to load the still-cemented retainer while the debonded retainer creates a hidden gap subgingivally — **caries develops underneath** while the patient may not realize anything is wrong. The patient feels "it's loose" or "food gets stuck" without realizing one retainer is no longer holding. Action: **immediately section + remove the bridge**, assess the debonded abutment for caries, restore as needed, refabricate the bridge. *Do NOT try to re-cement only the loose end — you cannot create a proper seal at that retainer without removing the bridge to clean the prep.*

**(3) Bridge fractured at a connector** — the two halves come apart cleanly. Connector failure usually means inadequate connector dimensions (<2 mm height for a posterior FPD) or excess pontic span. Remake with adequate connector design. Soldering broken connectors chairside is not viable for clinical use.
Bridge in hand — visually inspect. Both retainers intact? Connector intact? Pontic intact?

If **both retainers detached and the bridge is intact**: same protocol as single-unit re-cement (evaluate crown + prep, clean both surfaces, escalate cement choice, re-cement). Standard PA radiograph post-cementation to verify no subgingival cement.

If **only one retainer is detached** (so the bridge has been functioning partially-debonded): section + remove the bridge, evaluate both abutments for caries, assume the debonded abutment likely has caries given the time it's been leaking, restore + remake bridge.

If **connector fractured**: don't try chairside repair. Remake the bridge with adequate connector dimensions (anterior ≥3 mm height, posterior ≥2 mm). May need to redesign for a shorter span or reinforced connector.

If **porcelain chipped off a retainer or pontic but framework intact**: depends on size. Small chip in non-stress area → intraoral composite repair feasible (HF etch + silane + composite). Large chip or stress-bearing location → remake.

blocks.title: The hidden-caries trap of partial bridge debonding
blocks.body: Patients sometimes report a bridge has been "a little loose for a few months" before presenting. In that time, a partially-debonded retainer has been leaking saliva + bacteria into the prep margin. Take a periapical radiograph BEFORE attempting re-cementation — if you see any radiolucency at the margin of the debonded retainer, you're looking at recurrent caries and a remake. Cementing over caries traps the disease and accelerates pulpal involvement.

blocks.text: *Now that you know what a crown workflow looks like, here's how each material modifies it. PFM is the default (built into Part 1). The others show what changes when you pick a different material — different reduction, different cement, different lab steps.*

chapters.verdict: Cement choice is driven by restoration material and prep retention. Bonded resin for weak ceramics and non-retentive preps; conventional luting cements for retentive metal-containing preps. Get the material-cement matrix right and most cementation failures disappear.

chapters.title: PFM (porcelain-fused-to-metal)

items: Posterior FPD — **the default for posterior bridges**.
RCT posterior single crown where esthetic demand is moderate.
Cases needing metal strength + porcelain esthetics together.
Patients allergic to all-ceramic options or wanting maximum bridge longevity.
Anterior esthetic zone where the metal collar shows a gray hue at the gingival.
Patients with metal allergy — particularly Ni (5–8% of female population per Dental Alloys lecture).
Cases where bond-strength advantages of resin-cemented all-ceramics are desired.
**Axial reduction:** 1.25–1.5 mm.
**Occlusal reduction:** 1.5–2.0 mm (Review & Overview lists 1.75–2.0 mm).

**Finish line:** facial deep chamfer or shoulder 1.0–1.25 mm extended into interproximal; lingual chamfer 0.5–0.8 mm if metal collar used (more conservative); or deep chamfer / shoulder 1.0–1.25 mm all around if buccal porcelain butt margin all the way around.
**Taper:** 4–10° between opposing axial walls.
**Preparation height:** anteriors + premolars 3 mm minimum, molars 4 mm minimum.
**H:B ratio:** minimum 0.4.
Open with what you want done and for what: "complete full-contour wax-up and cut-back" + "fabricate metal framework for PFM FPD #9-x-11."
Alloy: base / noble / high noble.
Margin design: disappearing margin / metal collar / porcelain butt margin — and specify which design for which surface.
Pontic: modified ridge lap / ovate / sanitary; tissue side in porcelain or metal.
Occlusal contacts and interproximal contacts: porcelain or metal.
What was sent: pindexed master cast, solid cast, opposing cast, cross-mounted provisional cast, articulator name, custom incisal guide table (and why).
Return for what step: framework try-in, or ready for cementation; "please check framework on solid cast before returning."

blocks.text: Marginal closure with explorer, passive fit without rocking, **Fit Checker** to identify binding areas, radiograph for complete seating, interocclusal clearance ≥0.5–0.8 mm, framework thickness 0.2–0.3 mm minimum (calipers). Adjust with aluminum oxide stone, **never with diamond** (diamond particles embed in alloy and bubble during porcelain firing).

RMGI (RelyX Luting, FujiCem) for retentive PFM preps. Conventional GIC (Fuji I) only in cases of poor isolation. Resin cement (Panavia F2.0) if the prep is non-retentive or has minimal axial wall height.

blocks.title: Common pitfalls
blocks.body: **Occlusal contact at the porcelain-metal junction** — fracture risk. Contacts should be entirely on porcelain or entirely on metal, located **1.0–2.0 mm from the junction**. **Inadequate connector dimensions for FPD** — minimum 2 mm height anterior and posterior. Height matters more than width because bending varies inversely with the cube of pontic occlusogingival thickness. **Porcelain layer thinner than 0.8 mm** (opaquer shows through) or **thicker than 2.0 mm** (insufficient support, fractures).

chapters.verdict: The posterior bridge default at. Cobalt-chromium or noble alloy framework fused to dentin / opaque / enamel porcelain layers.

chapters.title: Lithium disilicate (e.max)

items: **The chairside workhorse.** Single units especially.
Anterior crown, posterior crown, inlay, onlay, veneer, ¾ crown.
Cases where bondable cementation is achievable.
Patient values single-visit chairside delivery (CEREC same-day path).
Cannot achieve bonded cementation (no isolation possible).
Prep with <1 mm thickness of restorative material plus cement space — ultra-minimal-reduction cases.
**Crown:** 1.0 mm minimum restorative thickness + cement space + overmill of uneven surfaces = **1.2–1.5 mm of tooth reduction**.

**Inlay:** pulpal floor 1.5–2.0 mm, axial wall 1.25–1.5 mm, isthmus ½ to ⅓ intercuspal width, B/L walls divergent 6–10° toward occlusal, butt joint margin (no bevel for ceramic). Interproximal flare 100–120°.
**Margin position:** 0.5 mm above gingival crest (supragingival ideal for IOS).
**Internal:** no sharp internal line angles, no feather-edge margins, no parallel walls or undercuts.

blocks.text: Pre-crystallized blue block milled in the chairside mill or sent to lab, then **15–25 minutes firing** to crystallize. Pre-crystallized strength is **130 ± 30 MPa**; crystallized strength is **360 ± 60 MPa** — a 2.5× strength jump from the firing.

items: Try in pre-crystallized restoration (rough fit check before firing).
After crystallization: **Ivoclean** intaglio for 20 sec, rinse, air dry.
**HF acid (4–5%) etch intaglio for 20 seconds** (lithium disilicate timing; feldspathic gets 60 sec at 10%, leucite-reinforced gets 60 sec at 5%).
Rinse and air dry.
**Clearfil Ceramic Primer** (contains MDP + silane in one bottle) — apply 60 sec (no rinse), air dry.
Tooth: total/selective etch + primer, OR self-etch primer (the clinic default). **The clinic default is dual-cure resin cement (Panavia F2.0) with self-etch primer (ED Primer II A&B).**
Mix and apply ED Primer II to tooth (30 sec, air dry excess).
Mix resin cement pastes A & B for 20 sec, apply to intaglio.
Seat, remove gross excess, either light-cure margins or apply OxyGard II for self-cure (wait 1 min, wash).

blocks.title: Common pitfalls
blocks.body: **Insufficient prep thickness** → overmilled thin areas in the restoration. **Sharp internal line angles** in the prep → overmill, restoration fractures. **Wrong HF time** — e.max is 20 sec, not 60. Over-etching weakens the surface. **Cement shade choice mismatched to block + stump shade** — final color = block + cement + remaining tooth + stains.

chapters.verdict: The the chairside default. Anterior, posterior, inlay, onlay, veneer — e.max does all of them. Milled in pre-crystallized blue blocks, then crystallized to full strength.

chapters.title: Zirconia (monolithic 3Y / 4Y / 5Y + layered)

blocks.text: Yttria-stabilized zirconia comes in three concentrations + a layered (core + porcelain) format. The yttria percentage directly trades off strength for translucency — the choice is dictated by tooth position + esthetic demand + occlusal load.

items: **3Y-TZP (3 mol% yttria, tetragonal zirconia polycrystal):** flexural strength ~1100–1200 MPa (highest). Most opaque of the zirconias. **Use for:** posterior molars in bruxers, multi-unit posterior bridges (3+ units), screw-retained implant crowns where the access hole limits material thickness, any case where strength dominates esthetics. The default for posterior monolithic zirconia.

**4Y-PSZ (4 mol% yttria, partially stabilized zirconia):** flexural strength ~700–900 MPa (medium). Better translucency than 3Y. **Use for:** posterior premolars + first molars in non-bruxers, premolar bridges, anterior single-unit crowns where a balance of strength + esthetic is needed. The 'all-arounder.'

**5Y-PSZ (5 mol% yttria):** flexural strength ~500–700 MPa (lower). Highest translucency of the monolithic zirconias — approaches e.max territory. **Use for:** anterior single-unit crowns where esthetics dominates, anterior 3-unit bridges (#7-x-#9 etc.). Avoid in bruxers + posterior load areas.

**Layered zirconia (3Y core + feldspathic porcelain veneer):** core has 3Y strength; the visible surface is feldspathic with its esthetic + chipping characteristics. **Use for:** anterior crowns or bridges where 3Y opacity is unacceptable but 5Y strength is inadequate. **Risk:** porcelain veneer chips under occlusal load (the well-documented 'porcelain chipping on layered zirconia' problem). Avoid in bruxers.

blocks.title: Choosing zirconia for a specific case — quick chooser
blocks.body: **Posterior bruxer:** 3Y monolithic. **Posterior non-bruxer single crown:** 4Y monolithic. **Posterior 3-unit bridge:** 3Y monolithic. **Anterior single crown + esthetic priority:** 5Y monolithic OR layered (3Y core + feldspathic). **Anterior 3-unit bridge:** 5Y monolithic. **Screw-retained implant crown:** 3Y (access hole reduces material thickness; need the strength margin). **Cementation for any zirconia:** air-abrade the intaglio with ≤50 µm aluminum oxide first (mandatory — no air-abrasion means the bond drops dramatically), then Clearfil Ceramic Primer (MDP + silane), then Panavia F2.0 resin cement. NO HF etch — zirconia has no glass phase to etch.

items: **3Y in anterior esthetic zone** — too opaque.
**5Y in long-span all-ceramic FPDs** in high-stress posteriors.
**Layered zirconia in heavy bruxers** — porcelain chipping on the veneered surface (same failure mode as PFM).
Trying to chairside-mill in clinics without a sintering oven — zirconia must be sintered after milling.

blocks.text: Prep (3Y — the most conservative ceramic prep)
0.5 mm minimum occlusal thickness. Same axial and finish-line requirements as other crown ceramics — chamfer or rounded shoulder, 6–10° taper, no sharp internal angles.
Cementation (oxide ceramic, no glass phase — HF does NOT work)

items: **Air abrasion** of the intaglio with ≤50 µm aluminum oxide.
**MDP primer** application (10-MDP monomer is the key for chemical bonding to zirconia).
**Resin cement** (dual-cure), **OR self-adhesive resin** (RelyX Unicem) for retentive preps, **OR RMGI** for highly retentive preps.
Cementation sequence follows standard universal protocol (try-in → isolate → clean → treat tooth → mix → seat → gel-stage cleanup → cure → final cleanup → occlusal check).

blocks.title: Common pitfalls
blocks.body: **Trying to HF-etch zirconia** — does nothing. There is no glass phase to attack. Must use air abrasion + MDP primer instead. **Not air-abrading the intaglio before cementation** — bond strength drops dramatically. **Insufficient sintering shrinkage compensation by the lab** — marginal opening. **Layered zirconia in bruxers** — porcelain veneer chipping is the classic failure.

chapters.verdict: The strongest ceramic. Oxide ceramic with no glass phase, so it cannot be HF-etched — bonded only via air abrasion + MDP primer. Different yttria contents trade strength for translucency.

chapters.title: Cast gold (full gold crown)

items: Posterior single units in heavy bruxers.
Minimal interocclusal space cases.
Second molars where esthetics matter least.
Patient is metal-tolerant; no Ni/Cr/Co allergy.
Anterior esthetic zone.
Anywhere the patient declines visible metal.
Metal allergy (uncommon but real).
**Occlusal:** 1.25–1.5 mm.
**Finish line:** chamfer 0.5–0.8 mm all around — **the most conservative finish line of any restoration**.

blocks.text: **Lost-wax casting.** Alloy choice: **high-noble** (≥40 wt% Au, ≥60 wt% noble metals), **noble** (≥25 wt% noble metals), or **base** metal alloy. High-noble preferred for gold restorations — easier to cast accurately, easier to finish, less allergenic, but more expensive. Base alloys (Co, Ni, Cr) have superior mechanical properties but worse castability, higher corrosion in acidic environments, allergy risk.

**RMGI (RelyX Luting) or conventional GIC (Fuji I).** No bonding needed because retention / resistance is mechanical — the prep does the work. Zinc phosphate is largely historical and is no longer the clinic default.

chapters.verdict: The most conservative crown prep in the curriculum. Posterior, heavy bruxers, second molars where esthetics matter least. Common question: "Is a FGC prep more conservative than a PFM prep? Justify." Yes — less axial, less occlusal, smaller finish line.

chapters.title: Feldspathic porcelain

items: Veneers (the classic indication).
Low-stress anterior inlays.
Anterior crowns where exceptional translucency and natural optics matter and the patient understands the fragility trade-off.
Any high-stress posterior location.
Any non-bonded scenario.
Patients who can't accept the higher fracture risk for the esthetic gain.

blocks.text: **Prep:** 1.5 mm minimum, butt joint margin. **Cementation:** predominantly glass — adhesive bonding mandatory. **HF (10%) etch for 60 sec + silane + resin cement**. Without adhesive cementation, feldspathic does not survive.
Veneer workflow (the dominant feldspathic use case)

Veneers are facial-only partial coverage restorations primarily for esthetic improvement of anterior teeth. Workflow has unique steps not shared with full crowns: minimal prep, isolated bonding protocol, and seating sequence that differs from cementation.

items: **Discoloration** (tetracycline staining, fluorosis, age-related darkening) not amenable to bleaching.
**Diastema closure** (most common cosmetic indication).
**Mild malposition / crowding** where orthodontics is declined.
**Worn / chipped incisal edges.**
**Enamel defects** (developmental, hypoplasia).
**Patient values esthetics + accepts fragility trade-off vs full crown.**
Heavy bruxism (porcelain veneers fracture).
Inadequate enamel (bonding to dentin alone is less reliable than to enamel).
End-to-end or Class III occlusion (mechanical disadvantage).
Active periodontal disease (margin will eventually be exposed).
Poor oral hygiene (porcelain margins demand impeccable hygiene).
Take pre-op photos + alginate impressions of both arches.
Lab fabricates a diagnostic wax-up showing planned veneer shape, length, position.
Make a silicone matrix of the wax-up.
**Mock-up at the patient appointment:** inject bis-acryl provisional material into the matrix, seat over patient's teeth without etching/bonding. Patient sees the planned esthetics in the mouth.
Patient + clinician approve before any irreversible prep.
**Depth-cut grooves** with a 0.5mm or 0.8mm depth-cutting bur (specifically marketed for veneer prep). Place grooves on the facial.
**Reduce facial surface** to the depth-groove level using a tapered diamond. Goal: 0.5mm cervical, 0.7–0.8mm middle, 1.0mm incisal (more reduction needed at incisal for translucency layering).

**Incisal reduction:** 1.0–1.5mm depending on incisal coverage decision. Pre-decide whether the veneer wraps the incisal edge (more durable but more prep) or stops short (more conservative but worn incisal stays visible).
**Proximal extension:** just into the proximal contact, not through. Preserve the contact with the adjacent tooth where possible.

**Margin:** chamfer (preferred for ceramic) or feather (more conservative but risks crack propagation). Place subgingival in esthetic zone (cervical 1/3) so the patient doesn't see the porcelain-tooth interface.
**Verify reduction** with the silicone matrix from the wax-up — should have 0.5–1.0mm clearance throughout.
**Stay in enamel.** Veneers bond reliably to enamel. Dentin exposure compromises long-term retention.
Single-cord retraction if margins are subgingival.
PVS impression (light body around veneers + heavy body in tray). For multi-tooth veneer cases, full-arch impression.
Opposing alginate.
Bite registration in MIP.

**Provisional:** bis-acryl provisional fabricated in the silicone matrix. Spot-etch each tooth (10% phosphoric acid on a small dot at the cervical, rinse, dry) to retain the provisional. Don't do a full etch — the provisional must come off easily at delivery.
Send to lab for feldspathic veneer fabrication (or e.max if preferred).

blocks.text: **Step 4 — Try-in + bond (the appointment veneers are made or broken at)**

items: **Try-in dry first.** Each veneer seated individually, verify margin fit + interproximal contact with explorer. Don't etch / bond yet.
**Try-in with try-in paste** (water-soluble paste in cement shades; Ivoclar Variolink Esthetic Try-In, RelyX Try-In). Lets you preview the final cemented color. Patient confirms shade.
**Rinse try-in paste thoroughly**; dry both teeth and veneer intaglios.
**Isolation is non-negotiable:** Isodry. Moisture contamination during bonding = veneer failure within months.
**Etch the tooth:** 35% phosphoric acid on enamel for 20 sec (15 sec on dentin if exposed). Rinse 10 sec, dry but leave moist (not desiccated).
**Apply bonding agent** (Optibond, Scotchbond Universal) to the etched tooth. Air-thin. Light-cure 10 sec.
**Prep the veneer intaglio:** 10% HF etch for 60 sec (feldspathic) or 5% HF etch for 20 sec (e.max). Rinse 30 sec, dry. Apply silane primer; air-dry 60 sec.
**Mix light-cure resin veneer cement** in the shade selected from try-in paste. Apply to the veneer intaglio.
**Seat the veneer.** Light pressure, slow firm seat (allow excess cement to flow out at the margins).
**Tack-cure** from the facial for 3 sec just to spot-fix the position.
**Remove gross excess** with explorer + microbrush at the margins before full cure.
**Full cure** 40 sec facial + 40 sec lingual.
**Floss** the interproximal immediately after cure to break any cement bridges.
**Verify margins** with explorer; no rough cement, no overhangs.

**Repeat for each additional veneer.** Seat in pairs or one-at-a-time depending on case complexity. For 6 anterior veneers, recommended order: cementing the two centrals first (anchor the smile midline), then laterals, then canines.
Remove Isodry.
Check occlusion with articulating paper. Adjust any high spots with fine red-stripe diamond. Polish adjustments with ceramic polishing wheels (blue → pink → gray) until smooth and high-shine.
Final shade verification with patient + mirror.
Post-op: avoid sticky/chewy foods for 24 hours; nightguard if any bruxism risk (strongly recommended for posterior teeth wear, less critical for anterior veneers).

blocks.title: Issues (veneer workflow)
blocks.body: **Veneer doesn't seat at try-in:** interproximal contact too tight. Adjust by trimming the adjacent veneer's proximal surface, not the current one. **Color mismatch at try-in:** try a different shade of try-in paste; the cement shade affects final color significantly. If no try-in paste matches, the lab needs to remake. **Veneer cracks during try-in:** remake. Feldspathic is fragile; don't try to repair. **Saliva contamination during bonding:** re-etch, re-prime, re-bond. Any moisture compromises the bond. Worth the extra 5 min over a failed veneer. **Cement haze at the margin after full cure:** cement extended past the porcelain margin. Remove with a #12 scalpel blade or polish with rubber points if minor. **Veneer debonds days/weeks after delivery:** bond was contaminated, or veneer flexed under load. Re-bond with renewed etch + silane on the intaglio. If debonds again, the prep is wrong (too much dentin exposed, no enamel band) — consider remake with prep modification or escalate to full crown.

chapters.verdict: The most translucent and natural-looking ceramic. The most fragile. Bonded cementation is mandatory or it fractures under load. covers it conceptually; fabrication is lab-side.

chapters.title: Hybrid ceramic / resin-ceramic blocks (chairside)

blocks.text: common chairside materials in this category

items: **Cerasmart** (GC) — pure resin-based.
**Tetric CAD** (Ivoclar) — resin-based.
**Lava Ultimate** (3M ESPE) — resin nano-ceramic.
**VITA Enamic** — PICN (polymer-infiltrated ceramic network); distinct from pure resin-based.
**Inlays** where opposing wear concern is high (resin-based wears less aggressively than ceramic against opposing enamel).
Cases where intraoral repair flexibility is desired.
Single-visit chairside restoration where lithium disilicate's 25-minute crystallization is impractical (resin blocks need no crystallization fire).
**Onlays** per — the Inlays/Onlays lecture states the weaker resin-based blocks "could not be used for onlays."
Long-term esthetic-stability cases (color drift over time).
High-load posterior single units.

blocks.text: **Prep:** 1.0–1.5 mm minimum depending on material (resin-based 1.5 mm; PICN 1.0 mm).

items: **PICN only (VITA Enamic):** HF 9.5% × 60 sec + ≤50 µm Al₂O₃ abrasion + silane + resin cement.
**Pure resin-based (Cerasmart, Lava Ultimate, Tetric CAD):** ≤50 µm Al₂O₃ abrasion + silane + resin cement (**no HF — no glass phase to etch**).

chapters.verdict: Resin-matrix CAD/CAM blocks that occupy the middle ground between pure ceramic and composite. Less aggressive on opposing wear, easier to repair intraorally, but lower long-term color stability and lower flexural strength than e.max.

chapters.title: Survey crowns for RPD abutments

blocks.text: What's different vs. a conventional crown

items: **Pre-prep planning is mandatory.** Mount diagnostic casts, survey, design the RPD framework, wax up the planned crown to ideal contour, then survey the wax-up to verify rest seat / undercut / HOC / guide plane BEFORE doing the prep.
**Occlusal reduction is deeper at the rest seat location.** 2.5–3.0 mm (vs. 1.5 mm standard) so the crown has bulk for the rest seat to be carved without exposing underlying material.
**Proximal reduction is parallel to the RPD path of insertion** on the guide-plane surface (not perpendicular to the tooth's long axis). The crown will be built flat in this region.
**The lab Rx is much more detailed** — rest seat dimensions + location, guide plane orientation, undercut location + depth, HOC location, RPD path of insertion (with tripod marks on the cast).
**You verify the wax-up on the surveyor BEFORE final firing.** Cheaper to re-wax than to refire / recast.
**All materials from the conventional crown workflow**.
**Surveyor** + analyzing rod + undercut gauges (for pre-prep planning + wax-up verification).
**Mounted diagnostic casts** (Denar 320, face-bow + Regisil bite reg).
**Duplicate diagnostic cast** for practice prep + diagnostic wax-up.
**Inlay wax + PKT instruments** for the diagnostic wax-up.
**Lab Rx** with full RPD design specifics.

blocks.text: Pre-prep planning (in the lab, before the patient appointment)

items: Mount diagnostic casts (mounting protocol in).
Survey the diagnostic cast: set path of insertion, mark HOC, identify usable undercuts. (Full surveying protocol in the RPD Deep Dives guide,.)
**Design the RPD framework** on the diagnostic cast. Identify which abutments need crowns + where each rest seat / guide plane / undercut goes.
Duplicate the diagnostic cast with an alginate impression + pour a second cast.
Practice the abutment prep on the duplicate (or wax it up on the cast).
**Wax up the planned survey crown to ideal contour.**

**Survey the waxed crown:** verify positive rest seat (with floor angled toward the long axis), ideal HOC at the gingival 1/3, 0.01" usable undercut at the planned surface, guide plane parallel to RPD path of insertion.
Photograph or sketch the planned contours for the lab Rx.

blocks.text: Steps 1, 3, 4, 5 (anesthesia / margin finish / retraction + impression / opposing + bite reg) follow the conventional crown workflow in. The differences below are concentrated in **Step 2 (reduction)**, **Step 6 (provisional)**, **Step 7 (lab Rx)**, and a new **Step 8 (wax-up verification before final firing)**.

items: **2a. Occlusal reduction at the planned rest seat location:** 2.5–3.0 mm (deeper than standard 1.5 mm). The extra 1–1.5 mm gives the crown bulk for the rest seat to be carved in the crown material without exposing underlying alloy or substrate.
**2b. Occlusal reduction elsewhere:** 1.5 mm (standard).
**2c. Axial reduction:** 1.0 mm chamfer all around (standard chamfer margin).

**2d. Guide plane region:** reduce the planned guide plane proximal surface so it is *parallel to the RPD path of insertion*. The crown will be built up flat in this region. Different from a conventional crown, where the proximal surface follows the tooth's natural taper.
**2e. Undercut region:** over-reduce the planned undercut surface slightly so the lab can build the crown out with adequate bulk for the 0.01" undercut at the gingival 1/3.
**2f.** Verify reduction with the putty matrix made from the diagnostic wax-up — 2.5–3.0 mm at the rest seat location, 1.5 mm elsewhere occlusally, 1.0 mm axially.
**2g.** Finalize the margin with the fine-grit chamfer diamond.

blocks.text: Make a quick provisional from the pre-prep matrix as with a conventional crown, but **the provisional must include the planned rest seat**. The patient may need to wear an existing or interim RPD during the 2–3 weeks until cementation, and the rest seat in the provisional supports the framework. Adjust occlusion to light contact (not high). Cement with Tempbond NE.
Step 7 — Lab Rx (much more detailed than a vanilla crown Rx)

items: **Crown material:** PFM or full-cast metal (default: PFM for anterior + premolar; full-cast metal for molars).
**Metal thickness:** 0.5–1.0 mm in the rest seat area.
**Rest seat:** location (M / D / cingulum), dimensions (1.5 mm deep, 2.5 mm wide, floor angled toward the long axis of the tooth).
**Guide plane:** proximal surface flat + parallel to the RPD path of insertion. Provide the cast tripod marks to the lab.
**Undercut:** location (mid-B, MB, DB, ML, DL), depth (0.01" for cast clasp; 0.02" for wrought wire), at the gingival 1/3.
**HOC:** at the junction of the middle 1/3 and the gingival 1/3 of the clinical crown.
**RPD path of insertion:** marked on the working cast with tripod dots.
**Enclosed:** final impression, opposing impression, bite reg, surveyed + tripod-marked diagnostic cast (as reference for the lab).

blocks.text: Step 8 — Verify wax-up before final firing (NEW step, critical)

items: **8a.** Ask the lab to send back the **full-contour wax-up** of the survey crown before they apply porcelain (PFM) or invest (full-cast).
**8b.** Tripod the lab's working cast using the tripod dots you marked at planning.

**8c.** Lower the analyzing rod on the surveyor. **Check:** positive rest seat (clear depression with floor angled toward the long axis), ideal HOC at the planned location, 0.01" usable undercut at the gingival 1/3 on the planned surface, guide plane parallel to the path of insertion.
**8d.** If anything is wrong, mark on the wax-up with a pencil and send back for correction. Cheaper to re-wax than to refire / recast.
**8e.** Approve in writing on the lab Rx. Lab proceeds to final firing.

blocks.text: Conventional — follow. After cementation, the new crown becomes the abutment for the RPD design that was planned around it. **Try in the existing or interim RPD against the cemented crown** to verify framework seating before the patient is discharged.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Reduction was too shallow at the rest seat:** the lab will report inadequate metal thickness. Re-prep at the next visit (deepen the rest seat area by 0.5 mm). **Provisional fractures at the rest seat:** patient was biting hard. Re-make with more occlusal clearance + wire mesh reinforcement. **Wax-up arrives without the rest seat:** lab missed it on the Rx. Send back; do not approve. **HOC is in the wrong location on the wax-up:** survey on your surveyor, mark the desired HOC location in pencil on the cast, send back. **Final crown returns with no usable undercut:** you can add composite to the cemented crown at the gingival 1/3 chairside (etch the porcelain or roughen the metal, bond, composite). Discuss with faculty before adjusting. **Patient's existing RPD doesn't fit on the new crown:** the framework was designed for the natural tooth contour, not the crown contour. If you didn't plan an RPD-design-first workflow, you may need to refabricate the RPD. This is why pre-prep planning is mandatory.

chapters.verdict: When an RPD abutment tooth lacks adequate natural contour for the framework — rest seat too shallow, no usable undercut, guide plane non-parallel to the path of insertion — the abutment gets crowned. The crown is built to provide all the design features the framework needs. Workflow and cementation are conventional (,); the prep + lab Rx + wax-up verification have extra requirements.

chapters.title: ¾ crown

items: Posterior tooth with intact buccal cusp (typically mandibular molar).
No esthetic conflict — partial metal display is acceptable.
Patient accepts the partial-metal appearance.

blocks.text: Full-coverage axial walls except for the buccal (or facial in anterior) surface preserved as unprepared enamel, with a butt margin or shoulder on the prepared interproximal / lingual. source material doesn't give specific reduction amounts — treat as full-coverage spec on the prepared surfaces.
The ¾ crown is the most conservative full-coverage option short of an inlay/onlay — it covers three axial walls + the occlusal but leaves the facial enamel intact. Niche but specific:

items: Posterior tooth needing coverage but esthetics dominate (facial enamel preserved).
Adjacent-tooth FPD abutment where you want to engage proximal contacts without sacrificing the facial.
Maximally conservative case where the facial wall is intact and disease-free.
Material restriction to cast gold (modern all-ceramic ¾ crowns exist but are rare).
Facial caries or decalcification (no enamel to preserve).
Heavy bruxer (the cast gold can be deformed under heavy occlusal load over years).
Anterior tooth — the facial-preservation logic doesn't apply, esthetics demands full coverage.
Severely tipped or rotated teeth where the path of draw is impossible to establish without facial coverage.
**Occlusal reduction:** 1.5 mm functional cusp, 1.0 mm non-functional cusp. Same as conventional crown.
**Lingual axial reduction:** 1.0 mm chamfer (cast gold) or 1.5 mm shoulder (PFM ¾, rare).
**Proximal axial reduction:** 1.0 mm chamfer extending from the lingual line angle around the proximal toward the facial line angle but stopping ~1 mm short of the facial midline.
**Proximal grooves:** 1.0 mm deep grooves carved into the mesiolingual and distolingual line angles. These are the retentive features that replace the missing facial wall.
**Occlusal isthmus:** 1.0 mm deep connecting groove on the occlusal surface between the proximal grooves, running lingual to the marginal ridges.
**Facial reduction:** NONE on the intact facial enamel.
**Path of draw:** restrictive — tapered toward the lingual since the missing facial wall means the prep must allow seating from above.

blocks.text: Cast gold (Type III or Type IV) is the canonical material — high castability, ductility for marginal adaptation, no porcelain to chip on the facial line. Modern alternatives:

items: **Cast gold (Type III / IV):** the standard. Best castability, best marginal adaptation, longest track record. Esthetic limitation: gold color visible at the proximal line angles in the smile zone.

**Pressed e.max ¾:** rare; requires careful prep to give the ceramic adequate bulk (the proximal grooves work in metal but are too thin for ceramic). Mostly used for partial coverage of posterior teeth where esthetics matters.
**Milled zirconia ¾:** even rarer; same bulk concerns as e.max.
**Cast gold:** zinc phosphate, RMGI (Fuji Plus), or self-adhesive resin (RelyX Unicem) all work. Retention form is good if the prep was correct, so cement choice is less critical.
**Ceramic ¾:** adhesive cementation mandatory (HF etch + silane + bond + resin cement). The bond is what compensates for the reduced retention form.

blocks.title: Why this is rare in modern practice
blocks.body: Most patients who would accept a ¾ crown (conservative, partial coverage) end up accepting a full crown because the cost is similar, the lab work is similar, and the full crown is more forgiving of prep variation. The ¾ remains useful because it teaches conservative thinking + path of draw + retention form via grooves — concepts that transfer to inlay/onlay design. Don't expect to do many of these in clinic; expect to be encountered with the principles.
