items: **1.** Pack #00 (bottom, no tail) cord soaked in **Hemodent** below the margin to control seepage + bleeding.

**2.** Pack a second (larger, size #1 or #2, leave a tail) cord above the first to expand the sulcus. Half the thickness of the top cord should sit in the sulcus; the other half visible around the tooth circumference.
**3.** Scan the prep arch first WITH both cords in place.
**4.** Use the 'Lock' + 'Trim' tools to erase the prep area from the scan.
**5.** Remove the top cord. The bottom cord stays for bleeding control.
**6.** Immediately scan the prepped tooth again. The scanner fills in the margin data.
**Hole in the scan (missing data):** insufficient capture coverage of that area. Rescan only the affected region — don't restart the whole scan.
**Double-scanned area shows blur or ghost margin:** the software couldn't reconcile two overlapping captures. Delete the overlap section and re-capture from a cleaner angle.

**Scan tracking is lost (scanner can't orient itself):** common when sweeping into an empty area (extraction socket, no opposing landmarks). Pull back to a known stable area and re-establish tracking.
**Margin renders as a blob, not a sharp line:** moisture or saliva on the prep at scan time. Dry thoroughly; re-pack cord if subgingival; rescan.
**Adjacent contact is open in the digital articulation but tight in the patient:** scan capture was off on the contact area. Rescan the contact specifically.

blocks.title: Scan quality checklist before sending to lab
blocks.body: Before clicking 'send to lab' or 'design', verify on screen: (1) margin is a continuous sharp line, no gaps or blurs; (2) no holes in the prep surface itself; (3) adjacent contact areas are smooth and complete; (4) opposing scan covers the occlusal table of the opposing tooth; (5) bite registration articulates cleanly with no obvious gaps in MIP. If any criterion fails, rescan THAT region before committing. The 60 seconds spent rescanning saves the remake. **Before cementing the provisional:** always apply **Gluma** to the crown prep — many patients are super sensitive after crown preps. Exception: RCT teeth don't need Gluma.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Scanner overheating mid-scan (TRIOS particularly):** common in long full-arch scans. Pause, let the scanner rest 60 seconds, resume. **Software won't stitch the three scans (prep + opposing + bite):** the bite scan didn't capture enough buccal overlap. Retake the bite scan with the scanner more lateral, ensuring the buccal surfaces of both arches are visible. **Patient gags during posterior scanning:** use a smaller scanner tip (TRIOS has multiple tip sizes), warm the tip first if available, and brief breaks between captures. **Crown won't seat from the digital scan:** verify the scan didn't miss the contact area or have a smeared margin. Rescan if margin was suspect; conventional impression if multiple scan attempts fail.

blocks.text: *Where this fits in the workflow:* The scanner is the front end of every digital pathway (ind-digital-emax, ind-cad-cam-inlay-onlay, ind-zirconia-posterior, cross-anterior-implant-esthetic). After scanning, the workflow continues to design (chairside or lab) and mill or print. The scan replaces conventional impressioning entirely; if the scan fails, fall back to PVS for that case.*

chapters.title: Intraoral repair protocols — PFM porcelain chip + zirconia chip

blocks.text: Porcelain chips on existing crowns are a common walk-in complaint — patient bit on something hard, a small piece of porcelain flaked off. Decision: composite repair intraorally (chairside, 30 min) vs lab remake. Composite repairs are durable 1–3 years and acceptable for small chips. Larger fractures or occlusal-bearing chips usually need remake.

items: **1.** Examine the chip: location, size, is metal exposed (PFM substructure visible) or is the chip in the body porcelain only?
**2.** Determine repair candidacy: chip is supragingival, <4 mm diameter, not in primary occlusal contact, patient accepts the composite-color match limitation.
**3.** Isolate the area (Isodry, cotton rolls + DriAngle otherwise).
**4.** Roughen the porcelain surface with a fine diamond bur — creates micromechanical retention.
**5.** Etch the porcelain with **5% HF (IPS Ceramic Etching Gel per clinic protocol)** for 60 seconds (longer than for e.max because feldspathic PFM porcelain is denser). Rinse 30 seconds.
**6.** Apply **silane** to the etched porcelain. Let air-dry 60 seconds.
**7.** If metal is exposed at the chip area: apply **metal primer** (Alloy Primer, Z-Prime Plus) to the metal area. Air-dry.
**8.** If metal is exposed and showing through: apply a thin layer of **opaque composite** (Opaquer shade in your composite kit) over the metal to hide it.
**9.** Apply **bonding agent** (single-bottle or two-step adhesive). Cure 20 seconds.
**10.** Layer **composite** in the chip space — start with a shade-match for the body porcelain, finish with a translucent enamel shade if matching incisal. Cure each increment.
**11.** Contour to match the original tooth shape. Finishing burs + polishing rubber points.
**12.** Verify occlusion — composite repair should NOT be a primary contact in MIP or in excursions.

blocks.title: Color match — managing expectations
blocks.body: Composite will not perfectly match porcelain. Composite has slightly different translucency, slightly different surface gloss, and ages differently (composite picks up stain over 1–2 years; porcelain doesn't). Counsel the patient before starting: 'This will be very close but may be slightly visible up close. The alternative is remaking the entire crown, which is more invasive and more expensive.' Document the conversation.

blocks.text: Zirconia behaves differently from porcelain. Hydrofluoric acid does NOT etch zirconia (zirconia is not silica-based). Composite bonding to zirconia requires a different surface treatment. The chip is also less common — zirconia rarely chips because it's much stronger than porcelain, but when it does, it's usually a delamination at the porcelain-veneer-on-zirconia interface (layered zirconia) rather than a chip in monolithic zirconia.

items: **1.** Identify whether the chip is on layered porcelain over zirconia (treat as PFM porcelain repair using HF + silane) OR monolithic zirconia (different protocol below).
**2.** For monolithic zirconia chip: isolate the area.

**3.** **Air-abrasion** the zirconia surface with 50 µm aluminum oxide at 2 bar pressure for 5–10 seconds. This is the critical step — chairside air-abrasion units (Microetcher, Danville) are needed; standard prophy polish does not roughen zirconia adequately.
**4.** Clean with alcohol; air-dry.
**5.** Apply **MDP-containing primer** (Z-Prime Plus, Monobond Plus, Clearfil Ceramic Primer). The MDP molecule bonds chemically to zirconia.
**6.** Air-dry the primer (no curing needed; MDP doesn't light-cure).
**7.** Apply **bonding agent**. Light-cure.
**8.** Layer composite. Same approach as PFM repair from this point.

blocks.title: When to remake instead of repair
blocks.body: Repair is acceptable for: supragingival chips <4 mm, single-incident damage, patient accepts color/longevity compromise. Repair is NOT acceptable for: chips at the occlusal contact area (will re-fracture), chips revealing the metal substructure subgingivally (cement seal is compromised), recurrent chipping on the same crown (the original design or material was wrong — fix the cause, not the symptom), patient who wants perfect esthetic match (composite won't deliver this). For these cases, remake.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Composite repair debonds at 1 week:** HF etch was inadequate (rinse contamination, insufficient etch time) OR silane was contaminated before application. Re-do the protocol completely; do not re-bond on top of failed bond. **Color match looks fine when wet but yellows after polishing:** composite picks up surface stain from the air-dry step. Polish more aggressively (finer grit, longer); use a glaze-like sealant (Permaseal). **Repair feels rough to the patient's tongue at follow-up:** under-polished. Re-polish with a porcelain polishing kit. **Zirconia chip recurs after composite repair:** the composite is harder than the zirconia substrate it's bonded to — the bond failed under load. Likely remake; consider going to monolithic zirconia next time (no porcelain veneer layer to delaminate).

blocks.text: *Where this fits in the workflow:* Used in repair-fractured-porcelain-pfm and repair-veneer-debond (veneer cementation includes similar HF etch + silane protocol). Patient typically presents acute; this is a same-visit chairside fix when candidacy criteria are met. Always confirm the repair holds at the 1-month recall — early debond predicts later failure.*

A resin-bonded fixed partial denture (RBFPD), known historically as the Maryland bridge, replaces a single missing tooth (typically an anterior incisor) by bonding metal or zirconia wings to the lingual surfaces of the abutment teeth. The abutments are largely conserved — minimal preparation, often no preparation at all. Indications are narrow but for the right case the RBFPD is the most tooth-conservative fixed option short of an implant.

items: **Anterior single missing tooth, typically lateral incisor:** the classic indication. Pontic supported by lingual wings bonded to the central and canine.

**Trauma case, young patient:** patient lost an anterior tooth but is too young for an implant (skeletal growth incomplete until 18–25). RBFPD as an interim solution that doesn't damage the adjacent teeth.
**Patient who declines implant (cost, anxiety, medical contraindication) and rejects RPD (esthetic, removable):** RBFPD as the conservative fixed alternative to a conventional 3-unit FPD.
**Sound abutment teeth, intact enamel, no caries or large restorations on the abutments:** the wing bonds to enamel; needs uncompromised enamel for retention.
**Heavy bruxer or known parafunctional habits:** the shear forces on the bonded wing exceed the adhesive bond strength; debond risk high.
**Posterior site:** the masticatory forces are too high for the adhesive bond; reserved for anterior cases.
**Restorations or caries on the abutment lingual surfaces:** the wing needs enamel substrate.
**Severe gingival recession or short crown height on the abutments:** insufficient surface area for adequate bond strength.
**Deep overbite where the wing would interfere with opposing teeth in MIP:** the wing must seat without occlusal interference.
**Metal-wing (Co-Cr or noble metal):** the traditional design. Cast wings approximately 0.5–0.7 mm thick. Strong, durable; metal show-through at the wing is the main esthetic limitation.

**Zirconia-wing:** more recent design. Wings milled in zirconia, often integrated with a zirconia pontic. Esthetic advantage over metal (no metal show-through); newer bonding protocols required (sandblast + MDP primer).
**Etched-metal (Maryland classic):** the original design used base metal with internal acid-etch retention features. Largely superseded by modern adhesive bonding.

**Visit 1: Records + design.** Take a conventional or digital impression of the area. Diagnostic wax-up with the planned pontic shape and wing extension. Patient approves the design. Communicate prep requirements to the lab.

**Visit 2: Prep + impression (if prep is needed).** Minimal lingual reduction of the abutments (~0.5 mm) to create a chamfer for the wing. Some designs require no prep (no-prep Maryland). Final impression after prep.
**Visit 3: Try-in.** Lab returns the bridge. Try in dry. Verify the wings seat passively, the pontic contacts the soft tissue correctly (modified ridge lap), and esthetics match.

**Visit 4: Bond.** Isolation is critical (Isodry). Etch the enamel of the abutment lingual surfaces (37% phosphoric acid 30 sec, rinse, dry). Apply bonding agent. Surface treatment of the wing intaglio: for metal — sandblast, apply Alloy Primer; for zirconia — sandblast, apply MDP primer. Resin cement (dual-cured), seat the bridge, hold under finger pressure during cure, clean excess at gel stage.

blocks.title: Bond strength is everything
blocks.body: RBFPD success rates vary widely in the literature (60–90% at 5 years) and the variability comes almost entirely from the bonding step. Sloppy isolation, inadequate etch, contaminated wing surface, or wrong primer choice — any of these turns a 5-year prosthesis into a 6-month debond. Isolation must be Isodry; etch must be timed; primer must match the wing material; cement must be light-cured at every accessible angle. This is not a step to rush.

blocks.text: Set expectations honestly. The RBFPD lasts 5–15 years typically. Some patients get 20+ years; some get 2 years and debond. The most common failure mode is debond (not fracture), and a debond is rebondable — patient comes in with the bridge in hand, you re-prep the bonding surfaces, re-bond. Plan for this possibility. If the patient wants 'forever' durability, recommend an implant or conventional FPD instead.

blocks.title: Tips
blocks.body: **Bridge debonds within months:** isolation was inadequate during bonding OR the abutment lingual had a restoration the wing couldn't bond to. Re-bond with stricter isolation; if a restoration is present at the wing area, the case may not be a good Maryland candidate. **One wing debonds, other still bonded:** unequal load distribution. Re-bond the loose side; counsel patient about reducing lateral force on that wing (e.g., avoid biting hard objects with the front teeth). **Wing shows through at the gingival margin (metal show-through):** unavoidable cosmetic limitation of metal wing. Consider remaking in zirconia if patient finds it unacceptable. **Pontic looks too long compared to adjacent teeth:** ridge resorption has occurred since the diagnostic wax-up; modified ridge-lap may not contact tissue. Re-design with a shorter pontic or add ovate-pontic site development with provisional first.

blocks.text: *Where this fits in the workflow:* Used in cross-anterior-trauma as a less-invasive alternative to a 3-unit FPD. Also a viable consideration in any anterior single-tooth replacement decision (e.g., as alternative to an implant during growth, or in cross-anterior-implant-esthetic if the patient declines the implant route).*

chapters.title: Crown removal — sectioning, spreader, airway protection
chapters.verdict: When an existing crown needs to come off — fractured tooth underneath, recurrent caries that's exhausted the margin, failed endo through the crown, or planning a remake — you section the crown with a crown-removing bur, split it with a crown spreader, and pull. The case turns on airway protection (Isodry over gauze), accurate sectioning that goes through to dentin, and not fracturing porcelain into the patient's airway. Source: Swade p. 70.

blocks.text: Crown removal is one of those procedures every dentist does eventually and one nobody is comfortable with the first time. The work is mechanical — section the crown buccal-to-occlusal with a crown-removing bur, place a crown spreader in the cut, rotate to split the halves apart. Most of the failure modes are about preparation: not making a putty for the provisional before starting, not protecting the airway against porcelain fragments, not sectioning deep enough to reach tooth structure (so the spreader can't catch and leverage).

items: **From sterilization:** fixed prosth kit, handpieces, radiograph kit, fixed prosth burs, **crown-removing burs (anterior OR posterior — pick before starting)**, **crown remover (spreader)**, Isodry, patient goggles, blood glucose kit.
**From locker:** blood pressure cuff, loupes / eye protection.
**In clinic:** retraction cords + Hemodent + dappen dish.

**Make a putty.** Before bringing the patient in, take a putty impression of the cast (or the existing crown if you have it) so you can use it as a provisional matrix later. Without a putty you'll be improvising a provisional chairside, which adds 20-30 minutes and looks worse.
**Get a start check.**
**Deliver local anesthetic.**

**Select a shade.** Determine the shade of the existing crown AND record it for future reference. Choose a shade for the final crown — use overhead light, ambient light, AND natural light for the comparison (different lights reveal different mismatches).
**Place cord.** Use a retraction cord as needed to better visualize the subgingival margins of the crown — this is what tells you where to terminate the section.

**Protect the throat.** Isodry is ideal — it captures fragments and protects the airway. Gauze is acceptable but more difficult to manipulate and less reliable. Do NOT skip this — porcelain fragments aspirated into the airway are a real complication of crown removal.

**Section the crown with a bur.** Use a crown-removing bur to cut THROUGH the buccal and occlusal of the crown — down to tooth structure. The cut needs to go all the way through; an incomplete section means the spreader can't get purchase later.

**Remove the crown.** Place the crown spreader in the cut and gently rotate to force the halves apart. If the buccal-occlusal cut alone doesn't release the crown, section part of the lingual surface to facilitate. If still unsuccessful, section the lingual fully. **Be careful that porcelain does not fracture into the patient's mouth** — aspiration risk.
**Take a radiograph.** Bitewing of the tooth to visualize the underlying tooth structure now that the crown is off. Decay, fracture lines, post status — all visible only after removal.
**Continue treatment.** Whatever the case demanded: excavate decay, complete core buildup, provisionalize from the putty you made in step 1, plan RCT, plan extraction, etc.

blocks.title: Airway protection is non-negotiable
blocks.body: Porcelain fragments are sharp and radiopaque, and aspiration is a documented complication of crown removal. Isodry over the working area + active high-volume suction during sectioning is the standard. If Isodry isn't available, fold gauze into a posterior throat pack and verify it's secure before starting. If the patient is supine and the crown is in the maxillary arch, the risk is highest — consider semi-upright positioning.

blocks.title: Anterior vs posterior crown-removing burs
blocks.body: Pick the right bur before starting. Anterior crown-removing burs are smaller, more delicate, and angled for incisal access. Posterior burs are stouter and cut through the occlusal of a molar crown faster. Using a posterior bur on an anterior wastes tooth structure; using an anterior bur on a posterior takes forever and burns out the bur.

blocks.title: Crown removal pitfalls
blocks.body: **Section is partial but the spreader won't catch:** the cut didn't reach tooth structure. Extend the cut deeper — the spreader needs leverage against the dentin on either side. **Porcelain fragments break loose during sectioning:** Isodry wasn't well-seated or suction wasn't aggressive enough. Pause, secure the airway, retrieve fragments, then continue. **Crown won't release even after lingual section:** check that the cement type is conventional (which releases) vs resin (which holds firmer). Resin-cemented crowns sometimes need multi-axis sectioning. **No putty made before starting:** improvise a provisional from a previous cast or use a quick-set bis-acryl over the prepared stump. Note for future: putty BEFORE patient seated.

blocks.text: **Where this fits in the workflow:** Triggered by failed endo through an existing crown (often the precursor to ind-ch32 Crown endo access fill OR a full remake via ind-ch1), recurrent caries that extends beyond a repairable margin, or a fractured underlying tooth that needs assessment before deciding remake vs extraction.

chapters.title: Crown endo access fill — composite over Vitrebond after RCT through a crown
chapters.verdict: When endodontic treatment was done through an existing crown (rather than removing the crown to do the RCT and then placing a new crown), the access cavity needs to be filled. Vitrebond goes over the gutta-percha as a base; composite fills the access. Code D2950 (core buildup including pins). Source: Swade p. 71.

blocks.text: Sometimes RCT is done through an existing crown — the crown is in good shape, the patient doesn't want a remake, the tooth needed endo, and the path of least resistance is access-through-crown rather than crown removal first. After the obturation, the access cavity is restored with Vitrebond over the gutta-percha and a composite fill on top.

items: **From sterilization:** fixed prosth kit, handpieces, radiograph kit, fixed prosth burs, crown-removing burs (anterior or posterior — kept on the tray in case the crown is failing), crown remover, Isodry, patient goggles, blood glucose kit.
**From locker:** blood pressure cuff, loupes / eye protection.
**In clinic:** retraction cords + Hemodent + dappen dish.
**Get a start check.**
**Select a shade.** Match composite to the existing crown shade. Tip: open the "Labs" tab in the EHR to see the original shade the crown was fabricated to. Use overhead, ambient, and natural light.
**Isolate** with Isodry.

**Remove temp filling.** Use a diamond tapered bur to remove the temporary that was placed at the end of the RCT visit. Remove sponge / cotton pellet completely. The gutta-percha should be exposed and clean.
**Check in** — instructor checks the access prep; refine until approved.
**Clean access with Consepsis.** Apply Consepsis, scrub 10 sec, rinse 5 sec, gently air-dry leaving dentin moist.
**Place Vitrebond over RMGI base.** Dispense Vitrebond (RMGI) onto a mixing pad, mix with a spatula, apply a thin layer (~0.5 mm) to cover the gutta-percha. Light cure 20 sec.

**Etch and bond.** Etch the access cavity walls: etch enamel + dentin, wait 15 sec, rinse 5 sec, lightly dry leaving dentin moist (use high-volume suction over the tooth + light quick air dry; dentin should be glossy without pooling). Apply ScotchBond in a vigorous scrubbing motion for 20 sec, air thin for 5 sec, light cure 10 sec. If contaminated: re-etch for 5 sec only, then proceed (rinse, dry, bond, dry, cure).
**Place composite.** Place in increments and cure 20-40 sec after each increment (depending on increment size). Cure the final increment for 60 sec.
**Check occlusion.** Remove Isodry. Check with articulating paper.
**Finish and polish.** Composite finishing burs, Shofu, Jiffy Brush.
**Check in** — instructor checks the restoration.
**Complete codes and note.** D2950 (Core buildup — including pins).

blocks.title: Why Vitrebond over the gutta-percha
blocks.body: Vitrebond is an RMGI — it bonds chemically to dentin and seals the access opening of the canal system. The composite alone wouldn't reliably seal the GP-tooth interface (composite doesn't bond to gutta-percha). The Vitrebond layer acts as the seal; the composite acts as the structural fill. Skip the Vitrebond and you get coronal leakage that re-infects the obturated canal within months.

blocks.title: Endo access fill pitfalls
blocks.body: **Composite debonds from the access cavity floor within months:** Vitrebond layer wasn't well-adapted or wasn't there. Re-do — replace with full Vitrebond + composite. **Patient returns with sensitivity:** the access wasn't fully sealed, or the GP exposed during access work has bacterial contamination. Re-treat. **Composite stains around the access opening:** under-polished. Re-polish; if recurrent, may indicate microleakage at the margin. **Existing crown porcelain chips during access prep:** diamond bur pressure was too high or the access opening too wide. Document, evaluate the chip — if small and supragingival, polish; if large or in occlusion, the crown may need remake.

blocks.text: A PFM crown needs room for **both** the metal substructure and the porcelain veneer, so it is the most reduction-heavy crown prep. At UIC you prepare a deep chamfer / shoulder all around.

items: **Occlusal / incisal:** 1.5–2.0 mm.
**Finish line:** facial **deep chamfer / shoulder 1.2–1.5 mm**; lingual chamfer 0.5–0.8 mm (or deep chamfer/shoulder). *For UIC, prepare a deep chamfer / shoulder all around.*
**Taper:** 2–5° per wall. Place the finish line **0.5 mm coronal to the free gingival margin** — do not prep more than 0.5 mm into the sulcus (biologic width).

Make a **PVS reduction matrix** — a silicone index of the *un-prepped* tooth that you can section and seat during the prep to gauge how much you have reduced. (This is a separate index from the provisional putty, which is made off the waxed-up diagnostic cast.)

Know the **margin design** — how much metal shows dictates how much you reduce: metal collar (hidden subgingivally, unesthetic) vs disappearing margin (common) vs porcelain butt margin (best esthetics, lab-demanding, can chip).

**Select the final-crown shade** in overhead, ambient, and natural light before you cut — it reads more accurately against the unprepped tooth, and it is the baseline you reconfirm at the impression and delivery visits.
**Record the pre-op occlusion** before anesthetic — mark the contacts on the tooth and the two adjacent teeth so you can confirm they are unchanged after the prep; a quick sketch helps you compare.
**Initial interproximal reduction** — flame diamond (850-012) held parallel to the long axis to break the contacts; do not nick the adjacent teeth.

**Axial reduction** — round/flat-end tapered diamond (856-016 or 847KR-016); place depth cuts ~1–1.25 mm buccal + lingual first, then reduce; verify 1.25–1.5 mm with a perio probe; place the deep chamfer/shoulder finish line ~0.5 mm coronal to the free gingival margin — **keep it supragingival to start; you can always lower it later** if esthetics demand.

**Occlusal reduction** — depth cuts ~1.25–1.5 mm following the cuspal inclines; maintain the occlusal topography. (Occlusal and axial reduction are interchangeable — some operators reduce the occlusal first.)
**Functional cusp bevel** — for contour, durability, and conservation on the functional cusp.
**Final refinements** — round the axial-occlusal line angles with a fine diamond (8856-014); evaluate taper + path of insertion.
Tooth prep balances four goals: **preserve tooth structure, retention & resistance form, structural durability, marginal integrity.**

**Retention** resists the crown lifting along its path of insertion; **resistance** resists it tipping under chewing/oblique forces. Both come mostly from near-parallel axial walls — the feature most under your control.
**Taper:** aim for a total occlusal convergence of **6–10°** (≈2–5° per wall); up to ~20° is clinically acceptable. Every extra 5° of convergence can cut retention/resistance **up to 50%**.
**Height:** every extra 1 mm of wall height adds up to **10%** retention/resistance — so on a short molar, keep the taper tight and add boxes/grooves rather than chasing height you do not have.

blocks.text: The final restoration is only as good as the prep and the impression. You expose the margin with retraction cord, control bleeding and moisture, and capture every prepared surface plus enough unprepared tooth beyond the margin for the lab to read the finish line and trim the die.

items: Healthy tissue first — bleeding and crevicular fluid displace the material → voids and indistinct margins. A clean, well-contoured provisional and OHI beforehand pay off here.
**Biologic width** ≈ 1 mm connective-tissue attachment + 1 mm junctional epithelium: **do not prep > 0.5 mm into the sulcus** and avoid aggressive retraction (→ recession, inflammation, pain).

**Agents:** aluminum chloride (Hemodent, ViscoStat Clear — least irritating, OK 10–15 min) or ferric sulfate (ViscoStat — syringe/brush for hemostasis, acidic, don't use under ceramics). Epinephrine cords raise HR/BP — avoid in cardiovascular disease.
Keep a dry field; local anesthesia reduces salivary flow (± an anticholinergic such as atropine for heavy flow on lower arches — caution in heart disease and glaucoma).
The standard at UIC is the **two-cord technique** for a subgingival margin. A single cord is enough only for a supragingival/equigingival margin or a sulcus too shallow for two.

Soak the cords in Hemodent. Pack a **smaller bottom cord under the prepared margin** to control seepage and bleeding — **no tail**, fully below the margin. (#00 is a common size, but pick the cord to the sulcus — they run #000 up to #3.)

Pack a **larger top cord above the first** (commonly #0); about half of it sits in the sulcus, the cord visible all the way around the tooth — **leave a tail**. (A cord submerged below the gingiva collapses the tissue when removed and hides the margin.)
Start at the interproximal papilla (thickest, most resilient tissue) and pack in small segments, each fully tucked before moving on.
**Remove only the top cord** just before the impression (creates access); leave the bottom cord in during the impression.

Size the tray and apply a thin layer of PVS adhesive with a clean brush (never double-dip). Apply it **early — before you pack cord** — so it is dry and just tacky (not wet or pooling) by the time you impress. (Triple / dual-arch trays are popular for single units, taken at MI.)
Thoroughly dry the prep + adjacent teeth, then quickly remove the top cord.

Inject **light body** into the sulcus and all around the prep — micro-tip pointed down and material flowing forcefully into the sulcus to avoid bubbles — while your assistant loads the tray with **heavy body**; seat the tray, and its hydraulic pressure drives the light body into the sulcus. (You want a second pair of hands for this — warn the patient it is unpleasant and ready suction + paper towels.)
Hold the full set (~6 min, set a timer), remove the impression, then remove the bottom cord.
Recement the provisional and send the impression + lab script.

blocks.text: Evaluate before you send — retake or accept?

items: **Finish line:** the entire margin reproduced crisp and continuous. This is the one zone that must be perfect.
**Cuff beyond the margin:** an intact, uninterrupted collar of material extends past every margin — that unprepped tooth is what lets the lab read the finish line and trim the die.
**Complete + clean:** the whole prep, the adjacent teeth, and the soft tissue captured, one homogeneous color, free of blood and saliva.
**Stable:** rigid tray, no pulls or distortion, material not separated from the tray.
**Not a retake:** a small bubble on an axial wall, or a void/tear on the sulcus cuff *away from* the finish line. A void, tear, or bubble **at or across the finish line** → retake.

blocks.text: The provisional protects the prep, holds the tooth's position and occlusion, and keeps the tissue healthy for the impression. At UIC you fabricate it from Integrity (bis-acryl) in a **putty matrix made off the diagnostic cast** — formed *before* the impression, cemented *after*.

items: Load the **pre-op putty matrix** (made earlier from the diagnostic cast) with Integrity — discard a pea-size of base + catalyst before seating the mix tip, then bleed a little onto the tray; keep the tip immersed to avoid bubbles, filling the deepest part first.

Seat the filled putty within ~45 s with gentle pressure; let it reach the firm-elastic stage, then tease it off with gauze or an explorer (not pliers — they distort it); reseat and have the patient close into MI.
Repair any voids with flowable composite (roughen the surface, re-vaseline the prep, feather the margins, light-cure).

**Trim + shape (out of the mouth, with extra-oral provisional burs):** open the gingival embrasures with a bur held parallel to the emergence profile, refine the proximal contacts to convex contours *without* touching the contact area, and blend the facial/lingual margins.
**Polish:** remove the tacky air-inhibited layer (alcohol gauze), then pumice + rag wheel + composite polishing points (e.g., Shofu); an optional light-cure glaze adds gloss.
**Apply Gluma to the prep first** — a thin scrubbing layer 15 s, wait 30 s, air dry. Patients are very sensitive after crown preps. **Exception: RCT teeth don't need Gluma.**
Clean + dry the prep (remove all old temp cement if recementing); vaseline the *outside* of the provisional for easy clean-up.

Load **TempBond or UltraTemp** into the provisional (80–90 %, cover the margins) and seat it; when the cement is set halfway (goopy/crumbly) floss off the excess (hold the crown, floss down then up); let it fully set — hold an anterior, or have the patient bite a cotton roll ~2 min for a posterior.
Verify + adjust the interproximal contacts (floss) and occlusion (articulating paper) to ideal.

blocks.title: Why it's made before the impression
blocks.body: You fabricate the provisional while the prep is fresh, set it aside uncemented, take the final impression on a clean dry prep, then cement the provisional — so you never run out of time and the impression isn't compromised.

blocks.text: The PFM crown is back from the lab. Verify the fit on the cast, try it in, adjust contacts → intaglio → occlusion, confirm full seating on a bitewing, then cement and polish.

items: **Check the crown on the cast** — margins and interproximal contacts — before the patient is in the chair.
**Disinfect:** Cavicide 5 min → rinse, then chlorhexidine 5 min → rinse.

**Record the pre-op occlusion with the provisional still in place** (you remove it next), before anesthetic — mark the contacts on the provisional and the two adjacent teeth. The neighbors are your reference: afterward you confirm the final crown reproduces the same contacts and does not throw the bite off. A quick sketch helps you compare.
**Pack a cord if needed** — for a subgingival margin or any bleeding, to keep the field dry for cementation.

**Remove the provisional** — fingers first, then a hemostat (gentle, so you do not distort it in case the crown does not fit), then a sickle eased into the margins if needed — **do not damage the margins or the crown will not seat**. Clean off all temp cement.

**Try the crown in** behind a **gauze throat-pack**. Evaluate interproximal contacts (floss, holding the crown so it does not dislodge), margins (explorer), and seating — the crown's marginal ridges should sit level with the neighbors'.
**Adjust the interproximal contacts first** — too-tight contacts are the #1 reason a crown won't seat. Mark with articulating paper, relieve, re-try.

**Then the intaglio** — spray **Occlude** or mix **Fit-Checker** (equal paste-paste) into the intaglio, seat, and read the show-through / binding marks; relieve those spots with burs and re-try until it seats fully.
**Bitewing** to confirm full seating and closed margins (no radiograph needed for anteriors).
**Adjust the occlusion extraorally, before cementing** — articulating paper for centric, a second color for excursions; relieve with ceramic burs.
**Confirm esthetics** with the patient — shade, shape, size — with a hand mirror.

**Clean the prep** — remove all temp cement with a **scaler, not a bur** (a bur changes the fit), then pumice with a prophy cup. (Check the RelyX instructions for any recommended prep/crown conditioning.)
**Rinse and lightly dry**, leaving the dentin moist; isolate with dry-angles, cotton rolls, and suction.
**Load the intaglio** ½–⅔ full, covering the margins, and **seat the crown**.
**Wait 1–2 min** until the excess is tacky / semi-set, then flick it off with a scaler or explorer; **floss** down and up several times (holding the crown) and floss-pull-through.
Have the patient **bite on a cotton roll ~3 min** for the final set.
**Verify** margins (explorer), interproximal contacts (floss), and occlusion — centric + excursive with articulating paper, adjusted to ideal with burs.
**Polish** any ceramic you adjusted with intraoral ceramic polishing burs in sequence.
**Post-op:** no sticky foods for 24 h; call if the bite feels high or there is pain.
**Codes:** D2750C (cementation, PFM high noble) — with D2750A (prep) + D2750B (final impression) from the prep visit; plus **D0275** (bitewing) and **I9002** (lab quality review).

blocks.text: A core build-up replaces missing coronal tooth structure with composite or core material (Bisco Blue / White) — ± a post on an endo-treated tooth — so the crown prep has sound walls to cut and the finished crown has something to grip. Build it in a shade that contrasts sharply with dentin — deliberately too light or too dark — so weeks later, at the prep, you can tell core from tooth. The single biggest predictor of the crowned tooth's survival is a sound **ferrule**: a continuous collar of intact tooth, ideally ~2 mm all the way around, that the crown margin encircles *below* the core.

**How broken down is ‘enough’?** You need a build-up when removing all the caries and the failing restoration would leave too little **sound coronal tooth** to retain and support the crown — and to keep an adequate **ferrule**. Remaining sound tooth structure is the key variable in the restorability literature. If essentially **no sound supragingival tooth** is left and there is no ferrule to grab, that is no longer a build-up question — the tooth may be non-restorable (→ extraction).

items: **Confirm you have a diagnostic cast** — you make the provisional putty off it at the prep visit. If the tooth's shape is far off ideal, plan to re-impress after the build-up or wax it up on the cast.

**Pre-op occlusion + shades (before anesthetic)** — mark the occlusion on this tooth and the two neighbors with articulating paper (a quick sketch helps you compare afterward), so you can confirm it is unchanged at the end. Pick the *final-crown* shade now as a baseline to reconfirm later, and pick a build-up composite a **drastically lighter or darker** shade.
**Anesthetize, isolate, pre-wedge** — Isodry or rubber dam (tie floss to the clamp). Pre-wedge mesial and distal (both for an MOD) to expand the PDL so the contact is easier to restore.

**Remove the decay / failing restoration** — a slow-speed round bur is more conservative than a spoon excavator. Infected dentin drills out wet, cheesy, and clumpy; healthy dentin dry, chalky, and dusty. Old composite scratches under an explorer and tooth does not, which helps you find the junction. Have the instructor confirm all decay is out.

**Matrix + disinfect** — sectional band (smiling toward the occlusal) + plastic wedge + Garrison ring, burnished, floss tied to the ring (two rings for an MOD); or a Tofflemire (closed end toward the occlusal, band narrower gingivally). Scrub **Consepsis** 10 s, rinse 5 s, leave the dentin moist.

**Liner / base if indicated** — Vitrebond (RMGI, ~0.5 mm, cure 20 s) where the remaining dentin is too thin to protect the pulp; place Dycal *under* it only over a point of pulpal proximity or a small mechanical exposure.

**Etch → Gluma → bond** — etch enamel then dentin 15 s, rinse 5 s, leave moist; Gluma in a thin scrubbing layer 45 s, wait 15 s, air-dry, rinse, leave moist; ScotchBond scrubbed 20 s, air-thin 5 s, cure 10 s. (If contaminated, re-etch just 5 s and continue.)
**Build up** — composite (or Bisco Blue / White) in increments, each cured 20–40 s, in the contrasting shade; cure the **final increment 60 s**.

**Finish + check** — remove isolation, finish with composite finishing burs + Shofu, then verify the interproximal contact with floss and the occlusion with articulating paper against your pre-op sketch.

blocks.text: When it also needs a post

A post seats in the **root-canal space**, so it goes only in an **endo-treated tooth** — a vital tooth still has pulp in the canal, with nowhere to put one. Even then, add a post only when **too little coronal tooth remains to retain the core on its own**. Its job is purely to **retain the core** — it does *not* strengthen the root (Pathways of the Pulp). Preserve as much radicular dentin as possible and leave the canal retreatable.

items: **Re-check the ferrule first.** With an inadequate ferrule, longevity is compromised no matter which post you use — confirm there is a sound collar of tooth before committing.

**Choose the post.** Default to a **prefabricated fiber post** (UIC's is **ParaPost**): custom-fit to the canal, cost-effective, and close to dentin in stiffness, so it distributes stress and lowers root-fracture risk versus a rigid metal post. Pick one that **fits passively** — reserve active (threaded) posts for short teeth, since they raise fracture risk and are harder to retrieve.

**Size the post space.** Post length ≈ **at least the crown height**, extending apically past the crest of bone, while you **retain ≥4 mm of gutta-percha apically** (leave ~5 mm radiographically to allow for angulation). Remove as little dentin as possible — root curvature and cervical dentin are the limits; over-widening invites root fracture or perforation.

**Bond it under a rubber dam.** Contamination is *enemy number one* for an endo-treated tooth — isolate, then bond the post with adhesive resin at **both the radicular and coronal levels**, choosing post + core materials that behave like dentin.
**Build the core over the seated post** using the build-up steps above, preserve the ferrule, and proceed to the crown prep.
**One post is enough.** In a multirooted tooth use the straightest, largest canal — distal in mandibular molars, palatal in maxillary molars.
**Cast post + core** is the older alternative (high success, retrievable) but needs more chair time and a temporary, and is largely replaced by prefab fiber posts.
**Codes:** **D2950** core build-up (incl. pins); add **D2954** when you place a prefabricated post + core. NV: crown prep.

blocks.text: A patient presents with a crown that came off, in hand. Decide re-cement vs remake by checking three things before any cement opens: is the crown intact, is the prep intact (no recurrent caries, adequate ferrule), and can the intaglio be cleaned.

items: **Three-way check:** crown intact? prep intact (no recurrent decay, enough tooth / ferrule)? cement + intaglio cleanable? If any answer is no → remake, not re-cement.
**Clean the intaglio thoroughly** — the most-skipped step and the #1 cause of a second debond. Remove all old cement; cementing onto a contaminated intaglio fails.
Re-cement with RelyX (if there's any retention doubt) or FujiCem; remove excess, verify margins, contacts, and occlusion.
**Code D2920** (re-cement or re-bond crown). Document that a re-cement may not last.

blocks.title: A repeat debond is a retention problem
blocks.body: A crown that keeps coming off usually has short or over-tapered prep walls or a lost cement seal — re-cementing buys time, but the real fix is often a remake with better retention form.

chapters.title: Crown removal / endo-access through a crown

blocks.text: Sometimes you have to get through or take off an existing crown — to access for a root canal, or to remove a failed crown.

items: When an RCT is needed on a crowned tooth and the crown is otherwise sound (good margins + fit), **access through the crown** and keep it.
Use burs appropriate to the crown material (ceramic / metal), find the access conservatively, and restore the access over the orifices (composite / build-up) after obturation.
Ask **sterilization** for the **crown-removing burs (anterior or posterior)** and the **crown remover (crown spreader)** — both come from sterilization, along with the radiograph kit and Isodry.
Record the existing crown's shade, anesthetize, and **protect the airway with Isodry** — porcelain fragments are an aspiration risk.

**Section** the crown buccally and occlusally down to tooth structure with a crown-removing bur, then seat the **crown spreader** in the cut and rotate to force the halves apart (section the lingual too if needed). Take care the porcelain doesn't fracture into the mouth.
Take a **radiograph** to see the tooth structure under the crown, then continue (excavate decay, core build-up, provisionalize, RCT, or extract as indicated).

blocks.title: Access-through vs remove
blocks.body: It's a restorability + crown-quality call: access through a sound crown to preserve it; section off a crown that has failed at the margin or has decay under it.

chapters.title: Core build-up on an abutment (when needed)

blocks.text: When a bridge abutment is broken down or endo-treated, it needs a core build-up before the prep — the same procedure as a single-crown build-up, just done on the abutment tooth (and on both abutments if both need it). Build it in a shade that contrasts with dentin so you can tell core from tooth at the prep, and preserve a **ferrule**.

items: Indicated when caries or a failing restoration leaves too little sound coronal tooth on an abutment to retain its retainer crown.

Same steps as the crown build-up: isolate, remove the decay, matrix + Consepsis, liner/base if indicated, etch → Gluma → bond, build up in increments (composite or Bisco Blue/White) in a contrasting shade, finish, then check contact + occlusion. It can be its own visit or the start of the prep appointment.
A post goes only in an **endo-treated** abutment with too little coronal tooth to hold the core — it retains the core, it does not strengthen the root; preserve at least 4 mm of apical gutta-percha.

blocks.title: When an abutment lacks a ferrule
blocks.body: If an abutment can't give a ferrule, that isn't a build-up case — it's an abutment-selection / restorability problem. Reconsider the abutment or replace the tooth another way (implant, RPD). Codes: D2950 build-up (± D2954 post) on the abutment, in addition to the bridge codes.

blocks.text: An FPD replaces a missing tooth by crowning the two adjacent **abutments** and suspending a **pontic** between them. The case lives or dies on abutment selection — the abutments carry the load of the missing tooth too, so they must be periodontally sound, favorably shaped, and collectively strong enough, judged before you pick up a bur.

items: **Full work-up per abutment** before prepping: intraoral, periodontal, radiographic, and pulpal exam. A compromised pulp gets endo *before* fixed pros; an RCT abutment must still have sound structure for a ferrule. Sometimes extraction beats trying to save a severely damaged abutment.
**Crown-to-root ratio:** 2:3 is ideal; **1:1 is the minimum** acceptable for an FPD abutment.

**Root form:** roots broad facio-lingually beat round ones; widely separated multi-rooted teeth beat fused, converging, or conical roots. Perio bone loss shrinks support faster than bone height suggests — the center of rotation moves apically and the lever arm grows.
**Ante's Law:** don't make an FPD when the abutments' combined root surface area is **less than** the root surface area of the teeth being replaced.

**Span deflection (cube law):** deflection rises with the cube of span length — 1 pontic = D, 2 pontics = 8D, 3 pontics = 27D — and inversely with the cube of the pontic's occlusogingival thickness. **Replacing three posterior teeth has a poor prognosis** → recommend an implant or RPD. Long spans need bulky, rigid connectors and pontics.

**Pier abutment** (a lone tooth between two edentulous spaces): acts as a fulcrum that can unseat a retainer → use a **nonrigid connector** (T-key + dovetail keyway, broken-stress) or a cantilever; needs very retentive retainers.

**Cantilever FPD** (pontic attached on one side only): induces tipping/rotation/drifting forces → needs multiple/double abutments (a premolar cantilever needs double abutments; a lateral-incisor cantilever can use the canine alone). **Every cantilever case needs an implant consult first.**

**Tilted molar abutment:** a mesially tilted second molar makes a common path of insertion impossible, and a third molar intrudes on the path → upright orthodontically and/or remove the third molar before the FPD.

blocks.title: When NOT to bridge
blocks.body: If the abutments would need endo + posts + perio surgery to carry a long, complex span — or you'd be replacing three posterior teeth — an **implant** or **RPD** is the better call. An FPD wins when the abutments are perio-sound, the span is short and straight, and the retainers are well designed.

chapters.title: FPD prep — the common path of insertion

blocks.text: An FPD prep is two PFM crown preps that must share **one path of insertion**. Each abutment gets the same reduction-heavy PFM prep as a single crown, but their axial walls have to be near-parallel to *each other* so the finished bridge seats as one rigid unit.

items: Prep **both abutments toward a shared path** — taper their long axes toward each other as needed; check the draw by sighting both preps with one eye, and against the diagnostic cast / putty matrix.
A mesially **tilted molar** or an interfering **third molar** can make a common path impossible — solve that orthodontically / by extraction first (see Abutment selection).

A little more taper than a single crown is acceptable to achieve the common path, but stay as parallel as you can — aim for **4–10° between the opposing axial walls**; retention and resistance still fall up to ~50% per extra 5° of convergence.
**Axial** 1.25–1.5 mm; **occlusal** 1.5–2.0 mm; **finish line** deep chamfer / shoulder **1–1.25 mm all around** (lingual chamfer 0.8–1 mm); keep it supragingival to start.
Make a **diagnostic cast + putty matrix** first (waxed up, *including the pontic site*), plus a **PVS reduction matrix** to gauge depth — same indices as the crown.
**Initial interproximal reduction** (flame diamond) — open the proximals toward the edentulous space and the far neighbors without nicking them.
**Axial reduction toward the common path** — depth cuts, then reduce; place the deep chamfer/shoulder finish line ~0.5 mm coronal to the free gingival margin.
**Occlusal reduction** following the cuspal inclines, then the **functional cusp bevel**.
**Final refinements** — round the line angles and **verify the shared path of draw** across both preps.

blocks.text: The pontic is the artificial tooth slung between the retainers. Its design is a tug-of-war between **esthetics** and **cleansability** — the tissue surface has to be convex enough to clean under, yet contact the ridge so the tooth looks like it emerges from the gingiva.

items: **Ovate** — most esthetic; a convex tissue surface sits in a small concavity in the ridge so the pontic looks like it erupts from the gingiva. Needs ridge volume / surgical site development.

**Modified ridge lap** — the UIC default. An all-convex, cleansable tissue surface that touches **only the buccal attached keratinized gingiva** (hides the buccal, stays cleansable on the lingual). Best balance of esthetics + hygiene.
**Sanitary (hygienic)** — the tissue surface stays clear of the ridge entirely (easiest to clean); non-esthetic posterior spans only.
**Conical** — a rounded, near-point tissue contact for thin / knife-edge ridges; less esthetic.
Tissue surface **convex and cleansable**, contacting the ridge with **minimal blanching**, with floss able to pass underneath.
**Tissue side in porcelain or metal** — both are biocompatible if well polished or glazed; porcelain is easier to adjust and add to, metal is for limited interocclusal space + maximum strength.
Keep it convex — a concave or ridge-lapping tissue surface traps plaque and inflames the ridge.

blocks.text: The provisional bridge protects both preps, holds the abutments and the space, and develops the pontic-site tissue. Make it as **one connected unit** — provisionalizing a 3-unit FPD as two separate crowns lets the teeth shift, leaves the pontic site under-developed, and gives no trial of the final form. At UIC the material is **bis-acryl (Integrity)**, preferred over acrylic for a span because it's stronger (also low-shrinkage, low-exotherm, easy to mix and polish; the trade-off is it's brittle and less forgiving of under-reduction or undercuts).

items: **Shade first.** Then take the **putty matrix on the *un*-prepared teeth** — if the pontic tooth is already missing, wax it up first so the matrix carries a pontic.

Lightly **Vaseline** the preps + tissue and place a **retraction cord** to see the subgingival margins. Load the bis-acryl cartridge, **bleed a pea-size** of base + catalyst, then seat the mixing tip.
**Inject into the matrix** — deepest part first, then the gingival areas, tip kept immersed to avoid bubbles — and **seat on the preps within 45 s**, holding firmly.

**Leave it ~2–3 min** from the start of the mix, then remove at the **firm-elastic** stage: tease it off with gauze or an explorer (not pliers — they distort/scratch it). Bulk-trim the gross excess, then **fine-trim and contour at ~6–7 min**.

**Shape:** open the gingival embrasures to a correct emergence profile, keep the proximal contacts convex *without* touching the contact area, and form a **modified ridge-lap pontic** (touches only the buccal, slopes up toward the lingual, convex mesiodistally — no concavities). Remove the tacky air-inhibited layer with alcohol gauze.

**Repair/reline** any margin gap with flowable composite (roughen, re-Vaseline the prep, reseat, apply flowable, feather past the margin, light-cure ~20 s in + 20–40 s out), then polish (FlexiDiscs + OneGloss/Shofu points, or rag wheel + pumice).

**Check occlusion against the unprepped teeth:** first mark where the *unprepared* teeth touch in MI (Shimstock 8 µm / Accufilm) as your reference, then re-check with the provisional seated — those reference teeth must still contact in MI, or the provisional is high and needs adjusting.

**Gluma** the preps first (except RCT abutments), clean + dry, Vaseline the outside. Load TempBond / UltraTemp, seat, clear the excess when half-set, floss every embrasure (holding it), and confirm it seats on **both** abutments with **no rocking**.
It has to come off in one piece at the impression visit — seat it well, but don't lock it on.

blocks.title: Bis-acryl vs acrylic
blocks.body: Bis-acryl (Integrity) is the preference for a span — stronger, low-shrinkage, easy to handle, dispensed from a cartridge gun. The alternative is **acrylic (Jet Set-4)**, mixed powder-and-liquid through **soupy → doughy → rubbery → set** stages; it's cheaper and easier to repair but weaker, which is why bis-acryl wins for a bridge.

blocks.text: Same soft-tissue management and PVS technique as the single crown, but you have to capture **both prepared abutments, every margin, and the edentulous pontic site** in one impression — so retraction and a dry field matter even more.

items: Pack **two cords around each abutment** — a smaller bottom cord under the margin (no tail), a larger top cord above (leave a tail); size them to the sulcus (#00 / #0 are common). Healthy tissue + hemostasis first.
Biologic width still rules — don't prep or retract more than 0.5 mm into the sulcus.
Apply tray adhesive early so it's dry; thoroughly dry both preps and the ridge.
Remove the top cords, inject **light body** into both sulci and over the pontic site, then seat the **heavy-body** tray (you'll want an assistant) and hold the full ~6-min set.
**Read it before sending:** both finish lines crisp, an intact cuff of material beyond every margin, the pontic ridge captured, no voids/tears at any margin — otherwise retake.
Recement the provisional; send the impression with the **framework lab script**.

blocks.text: The lab returns the cast metal framework **before any porcelain** so you can confirm it fits — there's no point baking porcelain onto a framework that doesn't seat. The bar is high: both retainers must seat fully and simultaneously, with a passive, rock-free fit, and verified mounting.

items: Seat the framework. **Both margins must close simultaneously** (check with an explorer) with a **passive fit and no rocking**.

If the margins aren't closed or it rocks, find the binding spots with **Fit Checker** — lubricate, fill the intaglio, seat, remove at ~1 min, relieve wherever the silicone is thin or perforated, and repeat until you get a thin uniform layer with no perforations.
**Radiograph** to confirm complete seating and closed margins.

Check **interocclusal clearance** — you need **0.5–0.8 mm** of space for the porcelain. Adjust the metal with an aluminum-oxide stone (preferred) or a fine diamond; use calipers to keep at least 0.2–0.3 mm of metal, work in one direction, and use **no diamond-coated burs** on the alloy (particles embed and bubble the ceramic during firing).
Verify the mounting with a check-bite (PVS or resin) if needed.

blocks.title: If it still rocks — section and solder
blocks.body: A framework that rocks or won't seat passively after adjustment gets **sectioned** (about the thickness of a business card), the segments tried in individually, and — if each abutment fits — re-joined intraorally with a **resin index**, then returned to the lab to **solder** (joining the segments below 450 °C with added metal). Decision tree: if the margins are closed on the cast but not in the mouth → start over with a new impression; if they're open on the cast too → remake the framework.

items: Once it fits, send it back with the **porcelain lab script** — shade (guide, shade, where, translucency, photos), the occlusal scheme, and your choice of **"return for bisque try-in" vs "return glazed / ready for cementation."**

blocks.text: Optional — you only have this visit if you asked the lab to **"return for bisque try-in"** (unglazed, unstained porcelain). It's the chance to fix contour, contacts, occlusion, and shade *before* the glaze is fired on; worth it for big cases (4+ units), and often skipped on a clean 3-unit (delivered glazed instead).
Adjust in sequence (the order the lab used too)

items: **Interproximal contacts** — visually closed and the same firmness as the contralateral side; floss passes without shredding. Too tight → mark and adjust with a fine diamond; too light or open → porcelain has to be added (back to the lab).

**Margins** — explorer; ideal margins are virtually undetectable. Trim bulky/bulbous porcelain off the collar; a tooth-ledge underextension < 0.25 mm is acceptable; an **open** margin (the explorer tip drops between margin and finish line) → remake.
**Pontic** — modified ridge lap, convex and cleansable, minimal blanching, floss passes under. Too tight → Fit-Checker + fine diamond; not convex / too light → add porcelain.
**Occlusion** — even, widely distributed MI contacts matching the contralateral side; check excursions.
**Contours + esthetics + shade** — symmetry to the contralateral tooth (buccal contour, line angles, developmental grooves); confirm the shade in good light.

blocks.title: Then glaze
blocks.body: Approve it and send it back to **stain + glaze**. Minimize refirings — over-firing causes **devitrification** (the porcelain turns crystalline, raising opacity and thermal expansion). If you had instead ordered it glazed, you skip this visit and go straight to delivery.

blocks.text: The glazed bridge is back. Delivery is the crown's delivery with one extra demand: **all retainers must seat fully and simultaneously with no rocking** — a bridge that rocks on one abutment is not seated.

items: Check the bridge on the cast, then disinfect (Cavicide 5 min → rinse, chlorhexidine 5 min → rinse). Remove the provisional + all temp cement.

Seat behind a **gauze throat-pack**. Verify **both retainers seat fully with no rocking**, margins (explorer), interproximal contacts to the neighbors (floss), and that the pontic contacts the ridge with minimal blanching.
If it won't seat → adjust the interproximal contacts to the neighbors first, then the intaglios (Fit-Checker). If it **rocks**, that's a framework problem (section + solder), not a cement problem.
**Adjust the occlusion extraorally** — even across all units in MI plus excursions (two paper colors), ceramic burs.

Clean the preps (scaler, not a bur; pumice), rinse + lightly dry, isolate. **Cement** with RelyX or FujiCem — fill all retainer intaglios ½–⅔, seat the **whole bridge at once**, clear excess when tacky, and floss through every embrasure (and under the pontic).
**Polish the metal collar** (brown then green points) to remove the oxide layer. Verify margins, contacts, and occlusion.
Post-op: no sticky foods for 24 h; clean **under the pontic** daily with a floss threader or proxabrush.

Per unit, phased prep / impression / cementation: **D6750A/B/C** PFM retainer crown (high noble) on **each abutment** (×2) + **D6240A/B/C** PFM pontic (high noble). The framework-try-in visit carries that phase on the unit codes.

blocks.text: A patient presents with a bridge that has come loose or off. Same logic as a crown, with a catch: if **one** retainer debonds the whole bridge loosens — and a partially-debonded bridge can hide recurrent caries under the retainer that stayed on.

items: **Three-way check before re-cementing:** is the bridge intact, are **both** preps intact (no recurrent caries — check carefully under the still-attached retainer; adequate ferrule), and can the intaglios be cleaned. Any "no" → remake.
A bridge that debonded on one side has often been leaking and decaying under that retainer — **radiograph and probe** before deciding.
Clean **all** intaglios thoroughly (the most-skipped step), then re-cement with RelyX (if any retention doubt) or FujiCem; clear excess, verify it seats with no rocking, contacts, and occlusion.
**Code D6930** (re-cement / re-bond fixed partial denture). Document that a re-cement may not last.

blocks.text: Sometimes a failed bridge has to come off — recurrent caries under a retainer, a failed abutment, or endo access. Unlike a single crown you usually **section the connectors** first, then remove each retainer.

items: Ask **sterilization** for the **crown-removing burs (anterior or posterior)** and the **crown remover / spreader** — same kit as a crown. Protect the airway with **Isodry** (porcelain and metal fragments are an aspiration risk).
Record the existing shade and anesthetize.
**Cut the connectors first** to separate the units, then section and remove each retainer like a single crown — cut buccal and occlusal to tooth structure, seat the spreader in the cut, and rotate.
If you only need **endo access** on an otherwise sound abutment, access **through the retainer** and restore the access afterward — don't take the whole bridge off.
Take a radiograph, then continue (excavate, build-up, re-prep, or treatment-plan a replacement).

blocks.text: When the tooth is broken down or endo-treated it needs a core build-up before the digital prep — the same build-up as any crown. It can be its own visit or the start of the prep/scan appointment.

items: Build up with composite or Bisco Blue/White in a contrasting shade so you can tell core from tooth at the prep; ± a post on an endo-treated tooth with too little coronal structure (preserve at least 4 mm of apical gutta-percha).
Preserve a **ferrule** — a ferrule-less tooth is a restorability problem (reconsider the tooth), not a build-up one.
Code D2950 (± D2954 post), in addition to the crown codes.

blocks.text: Digital e.max is the **esthetic** route — most anterior crowns and any case where you want a tooth-colored, metal-free restoration. PFM stays the conventional choice when strength is the priority. The material is **lithium disilicate (e.max CAD)**, milled from a block and bonded to the tooth.

items: Indicated when esthetics drive the case, when contours are hard to control with a direct restoration, or for any prep that allows **1.2–1.5 mm** of material; maxillary anterior crowns (the blocks are monochromatic).
Subgingival margins are workable — the scanner reaches ~17 mm depth as long as you can retract the tissue.
**Contraindications:** surveyed crowns, rest seats, or anywhere you can't control moisture.

**e.max CAD** (lithium disilicate) is milled in a **pre-crystallized 'blue' block** and crystallized later to full strength and final shade. **Crowns use low-translucency** blocks; high-translucency is for inlays/onlays.
Select the shade from the digital e.max shade guide (with the assistant in the Chicago office) in overhead, ambient, and natural light.

blocks.text: An e.max crown prep is reduction-similar to a PFM, but one rule overrides everything: it must be **scannable and millable**. No undercuts, no feather edges, no sharp angles — anything the scanner's light can't reach or the mill can't cut will wreck the crown.

items: **Axial** 1.25–1.5 mm · **occlusal** 1.5–2.0 mm · **chamfer** 1.0–1.25 mm · **finish line 0.5 mm supragingival** (supragingival captures best on the scan — retract for a subgingival margin).
**Taper 6–10°** per wall; **very rounded and smooth** — round every line angle.
~1 mm of ceramic + cement space + a little for over-milling of uneven surfaces = the 1.2–1.5 mm reduction. (Material minimums: zirconia 0.5 mm, lithium disilicate 1 mm, PFM 1.5 mm.)

blocks.text: Why digital is particular about shape

items: **No undercuts** — the scanner can't see them and the mill can't cut them; a gross undercut means the crown can't be fabricated (and undercuts can't be blocked out the way they are for an impression).
**No feather-edge margins** — milling burs can't form them and they're hard to capture; use a rounded shoulder / chamfer.
**No sharp angles** — they get over-milled, leaving the ceramic too thin.
Make **two putties** off the diagnostic cast — one for the provisional, one as a reduction guide.
Record the pre-op occlusion (tooth + two neighbors) before anesthetic; a quick sketch helps you compare after. Rubber dam isolation is preferred (Isodry in some situations).

blocks.text: Instead of a PVS impression you capture an **intraoral scan** with the TRIOS. The provisional is made *first* (before scanning); then you scan the prep, the opposing arch, and the bite, and send the case straight to the UIC Dental Lab.

items: **Fabricate the Integrity provisional before scanning** (set it aside — you cement it after the scan), then **pack two cords**: a smaller bottom cord under the margin (no tail) and a larger top cord above (at least size 1, ideally size 2; half in the sulcus, leave a tail).
Open **TRIOS**, pick the patient (or New Patient), select **UIC Dental Lab**, select the tooth, set restoration = crown, material = **e.max CAD**, and the shade; pick any delivery date; Next.
**Scan the opposing arch first** (maxillary: occlusal → buccal → lingual; mandibular: occlusal → lingual → buccal), staying under ~1000 images (2000 absolute max — erase + re-scan if over).

**Scan the prep arch.** Then the cord trick: **Lock** everything except the prep, **Trim** to erase the prep, remove the **top** cord intraorally, and **immediately re-scan the prep** — the scanner fills in the finish line the cord was hiding.
Turn off colors to check the margin; confirm you captured the **interproximal contacts and enough occlusal clearance**; **mark the tooth**; then **scan the occlusion** (the software auto-occludes).
Instructor checks the scans → **Next → Send** to the UIC Dental Lab.
**Cement the provisional** — Gluma the prep first (except RCT), seat, clear excess, and **remove the cords**. Codes: DD2740A (prep) + DD2740B (scan & design).

blocks.text: Same as the PFM crown's provisional — **Integrity (bis-acryl)** formed in the putty matrix off the diagnostic cast, made **before** the scan and cemented **after**. Gluma the prep first (except RCT teeth), seat with TempBond / UltraTemp, clear the excess, and remove the retraction cords. It just has to protect the prep and hold the space until delivery — and come off cleanly then.

The crown comes back **milled but pre-crystallized** — soft, on its block, sprue attached. You adjust it in that soft state, crystallize it in-house, then **bond** it with Panavia. The digital tell at try-in: if it won't seat, adjust the **prep**, not the crown margins.

items: Pick up the restoration and cavicide it. **Remove the sprue** — hold the *restoration*, not the block (so it doesn't fly off) — segment-cut with the diamond disk, then smooth with the pink/red disc; don't damage the restoration.
Place **Isodry** (keeps it dry and catches the crown — aspiration risk). Remove the provisional (fingers → hemostat → sickle at the margins) and clean the tooth with pumice (fluoride-free).

**Try in:** floss the contacts (hold the crown), explorer the margins. If it won't seat → adjust the **interproximal contacts first**; then mark the intaglio (articulating paper / Fit-Checker) and **round the sharp spots on the PREP** — not the crown margins.

**Bitewing** (PA for anterior) to confirm seating + closed margins. Adjust occlusion extraorally (centric + excursive, ceramic burs), adjust contours, then **polish with the e.max polishing burs** in sequence — all *before* crystallizing.

**Crystallize:** remove all paper marks, soak in Cavicide 1 min, rinse, into water, and **take it to Dan for crystallization (~25 min)**. Re-try the crystallized crown (re-confirm contacts, margins, occlusion).

**Prep the crown:** air-dry → **Ivoclean 20 s** (cleans off the try-in saliva) → rinse + dry → **IPS Ceramic Etching Gel (5% HF) 20 s** (etches the glass for micromechanical retention) → rinse + dry → **Clearfil Ceramic Primer (MDP + silane) 60 s, no rinse**, dry.
**Prep the tooth:** isolate, pumice (fluoride-free) + Consepsis, then **mix Panavia A + B Primer, apply 30 s, no rinse**, dry.
**Cement:** mix **Panavia Paste A + B**, apply to the intaglio, seat, remove excess, **light-cure 20 s per surface.**
Verify margins, interproximal contacts (floss), and occlusion — centric + excursive — and re-polish anything you adjust.
Post-op: no sticky foods for 24 h; call if the bite feels high or there's pain.
**Code DD2740C** (digital crown — delivery), with DD2740A (prep) + DD2740B (scan & design) from Visit 2.

blocks.text: An e.max crown that comes off gets a clean re-bond — but because it's adhesively cemented, a debond often means the bonding surface was contaminated or the prep lacks retention, so check before you re-bond.

items: **Three-way check:** crown intact? prep intact (no recurrent caries, adequate retention/ferrule)? can both bonding surfaces be cleaned? Any "no" → remake.
Re-prepare the bonding surfaces the same way (Ivoclean / re-etch + Clearfil primer on the crown; pumice + primer on the tooth) and **re-bond with Panavia** — adhesive cementation, not a quick lute.
Code **D2920** (re-cement / re-bond a crown). Document that a re-bond may not last if retention is marginal.

blocks.text: Getting through or off a bonded e.max crown — for endo access or a failed crown.

items: Ask **sterilization** for the **crown-removing burs** and the **crown remover / spreader**; protect the airway with **Isodry** (ceramic fragments aspirate).

For **endo access** through a sound, well-bonded e.max crown, cut the access with diamond (ceramic) burs, find the orifices, and restore the access over them after obturation — keep the crown if the margins are sound.
To **remove** a failed crown, section it buccally and occlusally to tooth structure with crown-removing burs and lift it off — the bonded interface makes it more tenacious than a luted crown.
Radiograph, then continue (excavate, build-up, re-prep / re-scan, or plan a replacement).

parts.label: All chapters
parts.tagline: Generated content for the direct-restoration workflow and per-class chapters.

chapters.title: Direct restoration workflow — the universal sequence
chapters.verdict: Every direct restoration follows the same eight-step arc — anesthesia, isolation, caries removal, prep refinement, etch and bond, composite placement, light cure, finishing and polishing. The case-specific chapters in Part 2 modify only a few of these steps; the rest stays constant.

blocks.text: A direct restoration is completed in a single visit, chairside, without lab involvement. The substrate is the prepared tooth; the restoration is built up incrementally and cured in place. This sequence applies to every case type — what changes between a Class I and a Class IV is which matrix is used, which composite shades are layered, and how aggressively the margin is bevelled. The procedural spine stays the same.

items: **Anesthesia.** Block or infiltration appropriate to the tooth. Many cervical and shallow Class I cases need no anesthesia; ask the patient.
**Isolation.** Isodry is the default. Cotton roll isolation is acceptable for supragingival Class I and emergency interim restorations only.
**Caries removal.** Selective caries removal — remove infected dentin (soft, wet, stainable), preserve affected dentin (hard but discoloured). Stop short of pulp exposure.
**Prep refinement.** Smooth cavity walls, no undercuts that the composite can't flow into, bevel the enamel margin where esthetics matter (Class III, IV, V on the facial).
**Etch and bond.** Selective enamel etch with 35% phosphoric acid (15 sec), rinse, dry without desiccating, then a universal adhesive. Cure 10 sec.
**Composite placement.** Increment in layers (1.5–2 mm) or bulk-fill if the material is rated for it (up to 4 mm). Adapt to cavity walls with a small condenser.
**Light cure.** 20 sec per increment with a calibrated light. Verify the curing tip is within 2 mm of the composite surface.
**Finishing and polishing.** Coarse to fine to superfine in stages. Check occlusion before final polish — a high spot you polish into the anatomy is hard to find again.
**Anesthetic:** 2% lidocaine 1:100,000 epi; 4% articaine for difficult infiltrations.
**Isodry kit:** dam material, hole punch, clamp set (W2A, W8A, 212 for cervical), forceps, frame, dental floss.
**Caries indicator dye** (Caries Detector, Seek) — confirms infected vs affected dentin endpoint.
**Bur block:** round bur for caries access (#2, #4, #6), pear-shaped diamond for prep refinement, finishing bur for margin bevel.
**Adhesive system:** universal adhesive (Scotchbond Universal, OptiBond Universal) + 35% phosphoric etch gel.
**Composite:** universal nanohybrid (Filtek Supreme, Tetric EvoCeram) in A1–A4 shades; bulk-fill option (SonicFill, Tetric Bulk Fill) for posterior boxes ≥4 mm deep.
**Curing light:** the operatory LED. Tip clean, glass intact, no composite contamination.
**Finishing:** Soflex discs (medium, fine, superfine), composite finishing burs (ET series), polishing paste (Enhance, Astropol).

blocks.title: Time budget
blocks.body: Class I: 30–45 min. Class II: 60–75 min. Class III: 45–60 min. Class IV: 75–90 min (often a second visit for polish). Class V: 45 min. These assume Isodry is up in 5 min and caries access is straightforward.

blocks.title: Workflow pitfalls
blocks.body: **Anesthesia inadequate during caries removal:** stop, supplement with intraligamentary or a fresh infiltration, do not push through a patient flinching at every touch. **Isodry leaks:** check clamp wings for proper engagement; consider an Oraseal sealant under the gingival margin of the dam. **Caries removal goes too deep and approaches pulp:** stop at caries-affected dentin. Place a calcium-silicate liner (TheraCal LC, Biodentine) over the deepest area only — do not line the entire cavity floor. **Etch contamination after rinse:** if any saliva touches the etched surface, re-etch for 5 sec, rinse, dry, and proceed. **Composite voids at the cavity wall:** under-condensed; use a small condenser to adapt each increment before cure.

chapters.title: Isodry isolation for direct restorations
chapters.verdict: Isodry is the UIC default for direct-restoration isolation — a single-use bite-block device with built-in suction that retracts the tongue and cheek, captures aerosols, and keeps the field dry. Faster to place than a rubber dam, no clamps, no extra anesthesia for clamp placement. (Rubber dam is reserved for endo at UIC.)

blocks.text: Bond strength to enamel drops by 30–40% with even trace saliva contamination, and dentin bond strength drops more steeply. The clinical consequence is post-op sensitivity, marginal staining, and recurrent caries within 1–3 years. Isodry closes this gap reliably and is far faster to place than the rubber-dam workflow students may have learned in pre-clinical lab. UIC uses Isodry for every direct-restoration scenario; rubber dam is reserved for endodontic procedures.

items: **Size S (small):** adult patients with narrow arches, or larger pediatric patients.
**Size M (medium):** the default adult size — fits most patients.
**Size L (large):** adult patients with wider arches.
**Size XS / pediatric:** pediatric patients (also used in the peds clinic with Isodry-compatible attachments).
Pick by trial — Isodry should fit snugly between the arches with the patient relaxed, without forcing the bite open beyond their comfortable resting position.
**Connect Isodry** to the operatory's high-volume suction line. Verify suction is on and pulling air before placing.
**Pick the size** (S / M / L per the patient's arch width).
**Lubricate the inner bite-block surface** with water or a small dab of lubricant — makes it easier to seat without binding on the gingiva.
**Have the patient open**, slide Isodry into the mouth with the bite-block flange on the working side, suction tip pointing posteriorly.

**Patient bites down** to seat the bite block. The mouthpiece sits between the upper and lower posterior teeth on the working side; the tongue retractor sits lingual to the lower teeth; the cheek retractor sits buccal to the upper teeth.

**Verify position:** the working tooth is exposed, tongue is retracted clear of the field, cheek is held back. The suction tip should be inferior to the working tooth (or posterior to it) so saliva flowing from the floor of the mouth is captured continuously.
**Adjust if needed** — if the suction is sucking the patient's tongue or cheek (rare but uncomfortable), reposition slightly. If the patient gags, swap to a smaller size.
**Begin the procedure** with a confirmed dry field. Plan to break and verify dryness every few minutes — Isodry holds dryness well but a sudden movement or a swallow can briefly compromise the field.

blocks.text: For most direct restorations, Isodry alone is sufficient. For a few specific cases you may need supplemental isolation: **subgingival cervical lesions** where the gingival margin needs additional retraction (use retraction cord under Isodry), or **patients with significant salivary flow** where you may need cotton roll + Dri-Angle adjuncts on the buccal side. A leaking gingival margin during the bonding step is the moment to pause and add supplemental isolation; don't push through and contaminate the bond.

blocks.title: Why not rubber dam at UIC?
blocks.body: Rubber dam isolation is the gold standard for moisture control but the placement workflow is slow (5-10 minutes), requires clamp placement (often with supplemental anesthesia), and the clamps + frame can be uncomfortable for patients. Isodry provides 90%+ of the moisture-control benefit in 30-60 seconds of placement, with built-in airway protection from its continuous suction. UIC's standard is Isodry for direct work; rubber dam stays in the endo clinic where the procedure justifies the extra setup time.

blocks.title: Common Isodry issues
blocks.body: **Suction is weak:** check that the line is fully connected and the operatory's HVE valve is open. **Patient gagging:** swap to a smaller size, or place an adjunct cotton roll in the soft palate region. Some patients gag on the cheek retractor specifically — pre-warn them and consider topical anesthetic spray. **Tongue keeps slipping past the retractor:** Isodry isn't sized right — go up one size. **Field is dry but the patient feels overwhelmed:** Isodry can feel claustrophobic to anxious patients.
