Pre-procedure communication ("you'll feel a suction in your mouth, it's loud but it keeps the area dry") helps. **Leak at the gingival margin during bonding:** Isodry doesn't seal at the gingival like a well-inverted dam does. Re-isolate with retraction cord or supplemental cotton roll before continuing. **Patient bites the suction tube:** reposition; the suction tube should sit clear of the occlusal table.

chapters.title: Caries removal — endpoint and instrumentation
chapters.verdict: The endpoint is not 'all soft tissue removed' but 'all infected dentin removed, affected dentin preserved'. Stopping at the right depth preserves pulp vitality and tooth structure; over-removing risks pulp exposure for no clinical benefit.

blocks.text: Caries removal endpoint is a clinical judgment, not a textbook rule. Three signs together indicate adequate removal: the dentin is hard to a probe (does not yield to firm pressure), the dentin is dry (not soft or wet), and the dentin does not stain with caries indicator dye applied for 10 seconds. If any one of these is positive in a localised area at the pulpal floor, leave that area alone — the deepest caries can be left in place under a calcium-silicate liner.

items: **Slow-speed round bur** (#2, #4, #6 steel) — the workhorse for caries removal. Use with a feather-light touch and copious water spray.
**High-speed round diamond** for access through enamel only — switch to slow speed once into dentin.
**Hand instruments:** spoon excavator (Hu-Friedy Black 31/32 or similar), large for bulk removal, small for fine excavation near the pulpal floor.
**Caries detector dye** (Caries Detector, Seek) — applied with a microbrush, dwell 10 sec, rinse. Pink-staining tissue is infected (remove); pale or unstained is affected or sound (leave).
**No 1/2 round diamond** for refining the prep walls and beveling the enamel margin.

blocks.text: Selective caries removal — the modern protocol

Selective caries removal preserves pulp vitality in deep lesions. The protocol is to remove all caries at the peripheral walls (where the bond will be) but leave caries-affected dentin at the pulpal floor under a calcium-silicate liner. Two-stage and stepwise excavation are no longer required in most cases; the seal of the bonded restoration over caries-affected dentin arrests further progression.

items: **Open the cavity** with a high-speed round diamond through enamel only, expose the caries.
**Remove peripheral caries fully** with a slow-speed round bur at the dentino-enamel junction and along all cavity walls — the seal of the restoration depends on a clean DEJ here.
**Assess the pulpal floor.** If the pulpal floor is intact and only superficially carious, remove with a spoon excavator until the dentin is hard.
**If the pulpal floor approaches the pulp** (within 0.5 mm radiographically), stop. Leave caries-affected dentin in place over the area closest to the pulp.
**Apply caries indicator dye** to verify clean walls and acceptable pulpal floor.
**Place a calcium-silicate liner** (TheraCal LC or Biodentine) over the deepest area only — not the entire cavity floor.

blocks.title: Indirect pulp cap vs direct pulp cap
blocks.body: An **indirect pulp cap** leaves affected dentin over the pulp under a liner — no pulp exposure. Predictable, 90%+ success at 5 years. A **direct pulp cap** is placed over a frank pulp exposure — the bleeding stops, the area is sterilised, and a calcium-silicate liner is placed directly on pulpal tissue. Less predictable (60–80% at 5 years); the patient must be informed that endodontic treatment may follow.

blocks.title: Caries removal pitfalls
blocks.body: **Pulp exposure during caries removal:** if bleeding can be controlled with pressure and saline irrigation within 5 minutes, attempt a direct pulp cap with a calcium-silicate liner. If bleeding is persistent or the exposure is wide (>1 mm), abandon the restoration and start endodontic treatment. **Over-prepared cavity:** common in students learning the endpoint. Once into dentin, switch to slow-speed and excavator only — high-speed in dentin removes too much, too fast. **Caries indicator dye over-stains:** caries detector dye stains affected dentin lightly even when it's structurally sound. Don't remove every pink area — judge with the probe as well. **Stained but hard dentin at the DEJ:** this is sclerotic dentin and is sound. Do not remove.

chapters.title: Etch and adhesive — total-etch vs selective-etch vs self-etch
chapters.verdict: Selective-etch (enamel-only phosphoric acid, then a universal adhesive on enamel and dentin) is the default for most direct restorations — strong enamel bond, low risk of dentin-related post-op sensitivity. Total-etch on dentin is reserved for cases where every drop of bond strength matters.

blocks.text: The three adhesive strategies differ in how dentin is conditioned. Total-etch (etch-and-rinse) uses 35% phosphoric acid on both enamel and dentin — strongest bond but high technique sensitivity and post-op sensitivity risk if dentin is over-dried. Self-etch uses acidic monomers in the adhesive itself — gentler on dentin, weakest enamel bond. Selective-etch combines them — phosphoric on enamel only, universal adhesive on both surfaces — and is the modern default.

items: **Apply 35% phosphoric acid gel** to enamel margins only — use a small microbrush to keep it off dentin. Dwell 15 seconds.
**Rinse for 10 seconds** with copious water spray.
**Air dry the enamel until frosty** — chalky white appearance confirms the etch. Do not desiccate dentin; leave it moist (visible glisten).
**Apply universal adhesive** to both enamel and dentin with a microbrush. Rub for 20 seconds — agitation is what infiltrates the smear layer.
**Air-thin** with gentle air for 5 seconds to evaporate solvent; the surface should look glossy, not pooled.
**Light cure 10 seconds** with the curing tip held within 2 mm of the surface.

blocks.text: Total-etch (phosphoric acid on enamel and dentin) gives slightly higher bond strength on dentin in carefully controlled conditions. It's worth the technique cost in two situations: Class IV restorations where every MPa of bond matters for fracture resistance, and direct veneer cases where esthetic longevity is paramount. The risk is over-drying the etched dentin and collapsing the collagen mesh — keep dentin moist (wet bonding) by blotting with a damp cotton pellet rather than air-drying.

Self-etch (acidic monomer in the adhesive, no separate etch step) is gentler on dentin and reduces post-op sensitivity risk. It's the right choice for shallow cervical lesions (Class V), for patients with a history of post-op sensitivity, and for emergency interim restorations where you want minimum technique complexity. The trade-off is a weaker bond to unetched enamel — bevel and lightly roughen the enamel margin if you're going self-etch and the case has significant enamel area.

blocks.title: Universal adhesives
blocks.body: A universal adhesive (Scotchbond Universal, OptiBond Universal, Adhese Universal) can be used in all three modes — apply over phosphoric-etched enamel for selective-etch, over phosphoric-etched enamel and dentin for total-etch, or over unetched enamel and dentin for self-etch. One bottle covers all cases, simplifies inventory.

blocks.title: Adhesion pitfalls
blocks.body: **Etch on dentin in selective-etch mode:** the dentin is over-etched, collagen will collapse on drying. Salvage by keeping the dentin moist and applying adhesive immediately. **Adhesive pools at the cavity floor:** under-evaporated solvent; air-thin again and re-cure. **Post-op sensitivity at 24 hours:** likely a dry-dentin total-etch case. Counsel patient; usually resolves in 2 weeks. If persistent, may need to revisit with a sedative liner. **Bond fails at delivery (composite separates from cavity wall):** moisture contamination between adhesive cure and composite placement. Re-etch the affected area, re-bond, re-place.

chapters.title: Composite placement — incremental versus bulk-fill
chapters.verdict: Incremental placement (1.5–2 mm layers, each cured separately) is the traditional approach and works in every case. Bulk-fill (up to 4 mm in a single cure) is faster and acceptable when using a bulk-fill-rated composite; mixing standard composite at bulk-fill depths will leave the bottom uncured.

blocks.text: Composite shrinks toward the light during cure (1.5–3% volumetric shrinkage). In a bulk increment, this shrinkage pulls the composite away from the cavity walls and creates a gap at the bond interface — the cause of marginal staining and recurrent caries within years. Incremental placement breaks the bulk shrinkage into smaller, less-stressed layers; bulk-fill composites are formulated with modifier monomers that reduce shrinkage stress to acceptable levels at greater depths.

items: **First increment: gingival floor** (Class II/III) or **pulpal floor** (Class I), 1.5–2 mm thick. Place with a small condenser, adapt to the cavity walls. Light cure 20 sec.

**Second increment: oblique layer** filling one half of the cavity from gingival floor to occlusal/incisal margin. The oblique orientation reduces C-factor (cavity configuration factor) and limits shrinkage stress at any one interface. Cure 20 sec.
**Third increment: contralateral oblique** filling the other half. Cure 20 sec.
**Final increment: occlusal/incisal layer.** Shape with a small instrument while still soft — anatomy is easier to carve in uncured composite than to grind in cured composite.
**Final cure** for 40 sec on the occlusal surface to ensure thorough cure of the bulk.

blocks.text: Use only a composite rated by the manufacturer for bulk-fill at the depth you're placing it (typical depth: 4 mm). Materials: SonicFill 3, Tetric Bulk Fill, Filtek Bulk Fill Posterior. Place a single 4 mm increment, adapt to walls, cure 20–40 seconds (manufacturer-specified). For Class II cases, finish with a 1–2 mm occlusal layer of standard composite for better wear and stain resistance — bulk-fill composites are slightly softer and stain more than standard nanohybrids.

blocks.title: C-factor — why oblique layering matters
blocks.body: The cavity configuration factor (C-factor) is the ratio of bonded surfaces to unbonded surfaces in a cavity. A Class I has the highest C-factor (5 bonded walls, 1 unbonded surface = 5) and the highest shrinkage stress. A Class V has the lowest (1 bonded wall, 1 unbonded = 1). High C-factor cases stress the bond more during cure; oblique layering creates a free surface for each increment to shrink toward, reducing the stress at the bond interface.

blocks.title: Placement pitfalls
blocks.body: **Composite voids at the cavity wall:** under-condensed increment, or the previous increment had an oxygen-inhibited surface that wasn't bonded — composite-to-composite bonding through the oxygen-inhibited layer is reliable, do not air-dry between increments. **Bulk-fill bottom uncured (soft to a probe at the gingival floor):** light energy didn't reach the depth. Use a smaller bulk-fill increment (3 mm), cure longer (40 sec), or switch to incremental layering. **Composite over-flows the cavity:** small increments, condense before adding more; do not try to shape a large excess with finishing burs after cure.

chapters.title: Light cure protocol — depth, energy, oxygen-inhibited layer
chapters.verdict: Adequate cure depends on two things you actually control at the chair — composite-increment depth and the tip's distance from the surface. Get either wrong and the bottom of the increment stays soft, the bond fails, and the restoration debonds within months.

blocks.text: Curing-light failure is one of the most under-recognised causes of restoration debonds. The operatory LED delivers enough energy in 20 seconds to cure 2 mm of standard composite through enamel-thickness translucency — but only when the tip is right on top of the surface. Drop the tip 2 mm farther from the surface and energy drops by half (inverse-square law). Block the tip with a tongue or cheek for a second, and the cure is uneven. The two variables that matter to you: how thick is the increment, and how close is the tip.
What to watch at the chair

items: **Tip distance: within 2 mm of the surface.** Energy delivered drops with the square of distance — the single biggest variable under your control.
**No tip contact with composite** — composite sticks to the tip and pulls during cure.
**Tip clean, glass intact** — composite contamination or scratches on the glass scatter the beam.
**Cover with a clear plastic barrier** to prevent composite contamination of the tip.
**Stable hand position** — block-out from tongue, cheek, or saliva ejector mid-cure is the same as not curing at all.

blocks.text: Standard nanohybrid composite (Filtek Supreme, Tetric EvoCeram): 20 seconds per 2 mm increment. Bulk-fill composite at 4 mm: 20–40 seconds (manufacturer-specified). RMGI: 20 seconds per surface. Adhesive (universal): 10 seconds. A flowable liner over a calcium-silicate liner: 20 seconds. The final occlusal layer: 40 seconds to ensure thorough through-cure.

The 50-µm surface layer of composite remains uncured after light exposure because oxygen quenches the free-radical polymerisation. This is by design — the uncured surface lets the next composite increment bond chemically to the previous one. Do not wipe, air-dry, or otherwise remove the oxygen-inhibited layer between increments. After the final cure, the oxygen-inhibited layer is removed by finishing burs.

blocks.title: Wide preparations need overlapping cures
blocks.body: A standard curing-light tip is 8–10 mm diameter. A wide Class IV restoration may be wider than the tip — cure the mesial half (20 sec), then move the tip to the distal half (20 sec). Do not assume a single central cure reaches the margins; the energy drops with distance from the centre of the tip.

blocks.title: Cure pitfalls
blocks.body: **Composite soft at the bottom of a 4 mm increment:** too little energy delivered. Check tip distance (within 2 mm of surface) and cure time. Re-cure with the tip closer. **Restoration debonds at 24 hours:** undercured at the bond interface. The adhesive layer probably wasn't cured before composite placement. **Pulpal sensitivity during cure:** the curing light heats the tooth — for thin cervical preps or deep preps near the pulp, cure with air-coolant on the tip or in two 10-sec increments separated by a brief air-cool pause. **Tip contaminated with composite:** clean with alcohol and a finishing bur; replace if the surface is scratched.

chapters.title: Finishing and polishing
chapters.verdict: Finishing shapes the contour and removes excess; polishing reduces surface roughness to the point where plaque can't adhere. Skip the polish step and the restoration accumulates stain and biofilm within weeks.

blocks.text: A finished but unpolished composite has surface roughness around 1 µm — well above the 0.2 µm threshold below which plaque cannot mechanically adhere. The patient leaves with a restoration that looks acceptable but will stain and accumulate calculus within months. Polishing reduces roughness to 0.1 µm or better and is the final step that separates a good restoration from one that lasts.

items: **Bulk excess removal** with a coarse-grit composite finishing bur (ET6, ET9). Slow speed, light touch, water spray. The goal is to bring the contour to within 0.5 mm of final shape.
**Contour refinement** with a fine-grit finishing bur. Define embrasures, marginal ridges, cusp tips, and developmental grooves. Anatomy at this stage, not after polishing.
**Margin finishing** with a fine-grit flame bur or 12-fluted carbide. Sweep the bur across the margin parallel to the cavosurface, never perpendicular — perpendicular strokes create ledges.
**Coarse polish:** medium-grit Soflex disc on a slow handpiece. Sweep across the surface in one direction; do not back-and-forth.
**Fine polish:** fine-grit Soflex disc. Same sweeping motion.
**Superfine polish:** superfine Soflex disc, or silicone polisher (Astropol, Enhance) with a slow rotary motion.
**Final polish:** felt cup with diamond polishing paste, 30 sec per surface. The composite should reflect light at this point.

blocks.text: Interproximal areas can't be reached with discs or rotary instruments — polish them with finishing strips. Use a metal strip (coarse) for bulk excess, then composite finishing strips (fine, superfine) for the final polish. Saw back-and-forth gently; pulling too hard cuts a step in the contact area. End with a polishing strip dipped in polishing paste.

blocks.title: Check occlusion before final polish
blocks.body: Place articulating paper (40 µm) and have the patient close in MI, then perform protrusive and lateral excursions. Heavy contacts on the new restoration will show as concentrated marks; adjust with a fine football diamond, then re-check. Do not skip this step — a high spot the patient feels for hours after the appointment is the most common reason for callbacks.

blocks.title: Finishing pitfalls
blocks.body: **Margin ledge at the cavosurface:** the finishing bur was held perpendicular to the margin instead of parallel. Re-finish with a flame bur held parallel to the cavosurface, sweeping across the margin. **Composite stains within weeks:** under-polished. The patient's diet (coffee, red wine, turmeric) accumulates in the surface roughness. Re-polish with superfine disc and felt + paste. **Dull/cloudy surface after polishing:** likely the oxygen-inhibited layer wasn't removed. Re-finish with a fine-grit bur to expose fresh composite, then re-polish. **Bur skipped across the surface and left a scratch:** finish with a fine-grit bur, then re-polish through the stages.

chapters.title: Class I composite — occlusal pit and fissure
chapters.verdict: The simplest case in operative dentistry — and the one most often over-prepared. Modern adhesion lets you preserve every millimetre of sound tooth; the prep is whatever the caries demands.

blocks.text: A Class I restoration sits in the occlusal pits, fissures, or grooves of a posterior tooth. The classic G.V. Black outline form — extension for prevention — is obsolete; modern protocols remove only the caries and leave sound tooth structure alone. The restoration is fully retained by the adhesive bond, not by mechanical undercuts. The most common error is over-preparation, where a 1 mm Class I lesion becomes a 3 mm cavity 'to remove all the dark areas' — most of which are stained but sound dentin.

items: **Anesthesia:** infiltration only; many shallow Class I lesions need no anesthesia at all.
**Isolation:** cotton roll or Isodry, depending on depth and patient. Isodry is preferred for any case deeper than 2 mm.
**Access:** small pear-shaped diamond through enamel only at the carious pit. Stop the high-speed at the DEJ.
**Caries removal:** slow-speed round bur and excavator. Stop at hard, dry dentin — do not chase staining.
**No bevel** on a Class I occlusal margin; the bevel would compromise the marginal ridge.
**Adhesion:** selective-etch enamel (15 sec), universal adhesive (20 sec), light cure 10 sec.
**Composite:** bulk-fill (up to 4 mm) or 2 mm incremental for standard composite. Carve anatomy with an IPC instrument before final cure.
**Cure:** 20 sec, then a final 40 sec after carving.
**Finish and polish** in stages.

blocks.title: Stained pits — fluoride treatment, not restoration
blocks.body: A pit that's discoloured but probes hard, with no radiographic translucency, is not caries. It's pigmented enamel — likely from coffee, tea, or wine — and the right treatment is a fluoride varnish or a small composite resin sealant placed without any tooth reduction. Do not drill out pigmented sound enamel.

blocks.title: Class I pitfalls
blocks.body: **Caries extends further laterally than expected once you enter:** stop and reassess — the case may need a Class II (proximal extension) approach instead. **Pulp exposure on a deep occlusal lesion:** stop, attempt direct pulp cap with calcium-silicate liner if bleeding controls. **Composite over-flows the small cavity:** less is more — place smaller increments and carve excess away before cure. **High contact in MI:** adjust with a fine football diamond. A Class I composite should feel passive at MI.

chapters.title: Class II composite — proximal contact and matrix technique
chapters.verdict: The case turns on the proximal contact. A sectional matrix with a separator ring delivers an anatomically contoured tight contact; a flat Tofflemire band gives a flat, often loose, contact that becomes a food trap and recurrent-caries site.

blocks.text: Class II restorations replace proximal tooth structure on a posterior tooth — typically caused by interproximal caries. The case has three distinct challenges: the gingival floor needs a clean seal (no overhang, no open margin), the proximal contact must be tight and anatomically contoured, and the occlusal surface must reproduce cuspal anatomy. Sectional matrix systems (Palodent, Composi-Tight) address the first two; careful layering and carving address the third.

items: **Pick a sectional band sized to the box.** Bands come in narrow (premolar), medium (small molar), and wide (large molar) sizes. The band should extend slightly above the marginal ridge of the adjacent tooth.
**Place a wedge interproximally.** Insert from the lingual or buccal side — whichever has less crowding. The wedge separates the teeth and seals the gingival floor.
**Pre-burnish the band** against the adjacent tooth with a ball burnisher before placing the separator ring. This creates the contoured proximal shape.
**Place the separator ring** over the band, engaging the buccal and lingual aspects of both teeth. The ring's tension applies separation force and holds the band in place.
**Verify the seal at the gingival floor** with an explorer. No light visible between band and tooth; no air leaks when blown with the air syringe.
**Verify the band contour** is convex, not flat. A flat band creates a flat contact.

blocks.text: The first increment of composite goes at the gingival floor — 1.5–2 mm thick, well-adapted to the box walls with a small condenser. Cure 20 seconds. The second increment fills the lingual half of the box obliquely; the third fills the buccal half. Build up to the marginal ridge level, then place the final occlusal layer over the prepared occlusal surface. Each layer cures for 20 seconds; the final cure is 40 seconds over the occlusal surface.

items: **Final cure of the occlusal layer** (40 sec).
**Remove the separator ring** with the ring forceps. Ring tension is high — be controlled.
**Remove the wedge** by sliding it back out the way it came in.
**Lift the band** straight occlusally with cotton pliers or sectional band forceps. Pulling lingually or buccally can fracture the new contact.
**Check the contact** with floss — should snap through with resistance and have a clean snap on the way back.
**Finish the gingival margin** with a curved finishing bur (no flat burs in the box — they create ledges).

blocks.title: When you must use a Tofflemire
blocks.body: A Tofflemire is acceptable for an MOD restoration where two boxes need matrix support simultaneously, or for a single-box case in an area where a sectional ring won't seat (e.g., tight interproximal contacts that won't accept a wedge). Pre-burnish the Tofflemire band aggressively to give it some proximal contour, but accept that the contact will be less anatomic than a sectional.

blocks.title: Class II pitfalls
blocks.body: **Open gingival contact (floss falls through):** matrix wasn't well-seated at the gingival floor, or the first increment didn't fully adapt. Re-do — there's no salvaging a Class II with an open gingival margin. **Overhang at the gingival margin:** composite extruded past the cavosurface during placement. Trim with a curved finishing bur or a No 12 surgical blade. **Flat proximal contact:** matrix wasn't burnished against the adjacent tooth before the separator ring went on. Re-prep and re-do if the contact is loose; some flat-but-tight contacts are clinically acceptable. **Marginal ridge fracture during finishing:** placed too thin or under-cured. Re-place the marginal ridge area and re-cure.

chapters.title: Class III composite — Mylar strip and lingual access
chapters.verdict: Approach interproximal anterior caries through the lingual surface whenever possible to preserve the facial enamel and its shade-matching contribution. A Mylar strip held with finger pressure or a small wedge shapes the proximal contour.

blocks.text: Class III restorations sit interproximally on an anterior tooth — typically distal of a central incisor or mesial of a lateral, often caused by inadequate flossing technique. The case is shorter and simpler than a Class IV but more demanding than a Class I because the contact has esthetic implications. Shade selection must happen before isolation. Mylar strip technique preserves the proximal anatomy without the bulk of a sectional matrix.

items: **Shade selection first**, before any isolation. Anterior teeth desiccate within minutes of isolation and shift toward whiter value.
**Anesthesia:** infiltration in the facial vestibule of the tooth, or no anesthesia if the lesion is small and superficial.
**Isolation:** Isodry, isolating canine-to-canine. Inverted dam, ligated if necessary at the cervical of the working tooth.
**Access through the lingual** with a small pear-shaped diamond. Open the lesion just enough to allow caries access; preserve the facial enamel.
**Caries removal:** slow-speed round bur and small spoon excavator. Take care at the gingival floor — the lesion often extends subgingivally.
**Bevel the lingual margin** (0.5 mm long bevel) to disguise the composite-enamel transition. Facial margin (if accessed facially) needs a longer 1.0 mm bevel.
**Mylar strip placement** — see below.
**Selective-etch enamel** (especially the bevels), universal adhesive.
**Layer dentin shade first**, then enamel shade over it. A monochromatic restoration looks flat against natural enamel translucency.
**Cure**, **remove Mylar**, **finish and polish.**
**Pre-curve the strip** over the round handle of a mirror to give it a natural proximal convexity.
**Place the strip** between the teeth, lingual end protruding for finger control.
**Place a small wedge** at the gingival of the strip — supports the strip and seals the gingival floor.
**Pull the strip taut against the proximal** with finger pressure or pliers as you place the composite increment.
**Cure through the strip** — the strip is clear and the light penetrates without issue.
**Remove the strip** by pulling lingually after final cure.

blocks.title: Two-shade vs single-shade
blocks.body: A simple Class III in a young patient with a high-value tooth can often be done with a single nanohybrid composite shade. A Class III in an older patient with significant value variation across the tooth needs at least two shades — a higher-chroma dentin shade at the cervical and body, a higher-translucency enamel shade at the incisal and facial. Photograph and check before final cure.

blocks.title: Class III pitfalls
blocks.body: **Shade selected after isolation:** restoration looks too light against the rehydrated tooth at follow-up. Make a note for next time; counsel patient that the shade will settle in 2 weeks as the tooth rehydrates. **Composite extends past the lingual margin:** trim with a sharp curved finishing bur, then re-polish. **Open gingival margin under the strip:** wedge wasn't well-seated. Re-do the gingival floor with proper wedge support. **Contact too tight (floss won't pass):** over-bulged proximal. Re-shape with a fine-grit finishing strip until floss passes with resistance.

chapters.title: Class IV composite — incisal corner build-up
chapters.verdict: The highest-stakes case in operative dentistry — sits in the smile line, must reproduce translucency and opalescence, and must withstand incisal-edge loading. A diagnostic wax-up and putty palatal index turn an artistic challenge into a reproducible technique.

blocks.text: A Class IV restoration replaces a missing incisal corner — usually from trauma or a long-standing Class III that broke through. The esthetic challenges are layered: the cervical and body have higher chroma than the incisal; the enamel has translucency at the incisal edge that lets you see through to an underlying halo of dentin; the incisal edge often has subtle opalescence visible at certain lighting angles. A monochromatic Class IV looks 'too perfect' and obviously restored. The diagnostic wax-up + putty palatal index is the technique that turns this from artistry to procedure.

items: **Diagnostic wax-up** of the missing corner on a study cast. Wax to ideal incisal-edge length matching the contralateral.
**Putty palatal index** — heavy-bodied PVS or lab putty pressed against the lingual of the waxed-up tooth. Trim to expose only the incisal edge.
**Shade selection** — at this visit, before any prep. Pick a body/dentin shade, an enamel shade, and a translucent incisal shade.
**Photograph** with shade tab adjacent.
**Anesthesia:** facial infiltration of the involved tooth.
**Isolation:** Isodry, isolating canine-to-canine.
**Bevel the facial enamel margin** with a fine flame diamond, 0.5–1.0 mm long, scalloped to disguise the margin.
**Selective-etch enamel, universal adhesive, cure.**
**Seat the putty palatal index** against the lingual of the tooth.
**Place the lingual enamel shell** — thin layer of enamel-shade composite against the putty index, defining the lingual contour. Cure 20 sec.

**Build dentin shade layers** behind the enamel shell, working from cervical to incisal. The dentin layer should stop short of the incisal edge by 1 mm to leave room for the incisal translucent shade.
**Add a thin translucent incisal layer** for opalescence — sweeps from one corner of the incisal edge to the other.
**Cap with facial enamel shade** matching the surrounding tooth.
**Cure thoroughly** with overlapping cures across the surface.
**Remove the putty index** by pulling lingually.
**Finishing and polish** in extended stages — discs, silicone polishers, felt + paste.

blocks.title: When to refer to porcelain
blocks.body: A direct composite Class IV is the right choice for trauma cases in young patients (the tooth may still be growing or remodelling), for incremental corner fractures (1–2 mm missing), and for patients who can't afford or don't want a porcelain veneer. For an adult patient missing >50% of the crown, with high esthetic demands, and willing to invest in porcelain — refer for a feldspathic or lithium disilicate veneer. The direct will look acceptable; the porcelain will look indistinguishable from the contralateral.

blocks.title: Class IV pitfalls
blocks.body: **Restoration looks too opaque, no translucency at the incisal:** dentin layer extended too far incisally. Trim back the incisal 1 mm of dentin shade and re-layer with translucent enamel. **Shade match looks off at delivery, looks worse at 1 week:** desiccation effect — tooth value rises during isolation, drops back to baseline over 1–2 weeks. The restoration that looked too dark at delivery will look correct at 1 week. **Incisal edge chips at 6 months:** under-thickness of the incisal enamel layer. Repair by bonding a thicker enamel shade to the chipped area. **Margin staining at 1 year:** under-polished bevel area. Re-polish; if recurs, may need to re-bevel and re-bond the facial margin.

chapters.title: Class V — composite versus RMGI decision
chapters.verdict: If the gingival margin still has enamel, choose composite. If the lesion is entirely on root surface (no enamel margin), choose RMGI. Mixing them — composite over a root-surface margin — predictably fails at the apical seal within 2 years.

blocks.text: Class V lesions sit at the cervical of the tooth — at the gum line or just apical to it. The clinical decision turns on one question: is there enamel at the gingival margin? Caries that have advanced apically past the CEJ, or non-carious cervical lesions (NCCLs) caused by abrasion or erosion, often sit entirely on root surface. Composite needs enamel to anchor the bond; RMGI bonds chemically to dentin and continuously releases fluoride.

items: **Enamel margin coronal AND apical:** composite. Selective-etch protocol; universal adhesive.

**Enamel margin coronal, no enamel apical (lesion extends below CEJ):** sandwich technique — RMGI at the gingival floor (chemical bond to root dentin), composite over the coronal portion (bond to remaining enamel).
**No enamel margin (lesion entirely on root surface, often an NCCL):** RMGI. No phosphoric etch — use RMGI conditioner.
**Active root caries with cavitation:** RMGI alone, no composite overlay. The fluoride release from RMGI is the active anti-caries agent.

blocks.text: Sandwich technique (mixed enamel and root margin)

items: **Isolation** with Isodry and cervical clamp (212 or butterfly).
**Caries removal** with slow-speed round bur and excavator.
**Condition the gingival floor** with RMGI conditioner (polyacrylic acid, 15 sec). Rinse, dry without desiccating.
**Place RMGI at the gingival floor** (0.5–1 mm thick). Cure 20 sec.
**Etch the coronal enamel** (selective-etch, 15 sec). Rinse, dry.
**Apply universal adhesive** to enamel and the RMGI surface. Cure 10 sec.
**Place composite** over the RMGI in the coronal portion. Build to contour, cure incrementally.
**Finish and polish** gently — RMGI is softer than composite and roughens easily.

blocks.title: Cervical clamps for isolation
blocks.body: A standard W2A clamp won't reach a subgingival Class V margin. Use a 212 clamp (butterfly) for posterior cervical access, or a B6 clamp for anterior cervical access. These clamps engage just apical to the lesion and retract the gingival tissue for visualisation.

blocks.title: Class V pitfalls
blocks.body: **Composite over root-surface margin debonds at 18 months:** the bond was always going to fail. Replace with RMGI or sandwich technique. **RMGI surface roughens within months:** RMGI is softer than composite and stains from coffee/tea/wine. Counsel patient; can be re-polished. **Persistent post-op sensitivity:** likely a deep dentin lesion. Place a calcium-silicate liner under the RMGI for a future case; for this case, manage symptoms with desensitising toothpaste. **Open margin at the gingival floor:** isolation was inadequate during placement. Re-isolate and re-place. There is no minor repair for a leaking Class V.

chapters.title: Direct composite veneer — single tooth shape correction
chapters.verdict: A no-prep or minimal-prep additive restoration for shape correction on a single anterior tooth — peg lateral, diastema closure, or small fracture. Single-visit, fully repairable, but technique-sensitive and shorter-lasting than porcelain.

blocks.text: Direct composite veneers add to existing tooth structure rather than replacing it. The clinical indication is a single tooth that's too small (peg lateral), the wrong shape, or has a small fracture that wouldn't justify a full porcelain veneer. The advantages are speed (one visit), reversibility (no enamel removed), repairability (chipping is fixable chairside), and cost (1/3 to 1/2 of porcelain). The trade-offs are esthetic longevity (5–7 years before stain or wear becomes noticeable) and the higher technique demand for a multi-shade layered build-up.

items: **Good indications:** peg lateral, single-tooth diastema closure, single small fracture, additive shape correction on a young patient.
**Acceptable but porcelain may be better:** multiple anterior teeth needing shape changes, significant value mismatch with adjacent teeth.
**Poor indications:** full smile makeover (porcelain is better), heavy bruxer with parafunction, deep bite where the veneer would take excursive load.
**Contraindicated:** patient with poor oral hygiene (composite stains heavily without maintenance), unrealistic patient expectations.
**Diagnostic wax-up** of the planned veneer on a study cast. **Putty palatal index** of the wax-up.
**Shade selection** before isolation — body, enamel, and translucent incisal shades.
**Air-abrade or lightly roughen the enamel** with a fine diamond. No bulk reduction — the goal is additive.
**Selective-etch the prepared enamel** (35% phosphoric acid, 15 sec). Rinse, dry.
**Universal adhesive,** cure 10 sec.
**Putty index in place,** build the lingual enamel shell against it.
**Build dentin layer**, then enamel layer over it, then translucent incisal layer at the edge.
**Cure thoroughly** with overlapping cures.
**Remove putty,** check shape with patient sitting up.
**Finish and polish** in extended stages — discs (medium, fine, superfine), silicone polishers, felt + diamond paste.
**Photograph** at delivery; schedule 1-week post-op for touch-ups.

blocks.title: Microfill vs nanohybrid for veneers
blocks.body: Microfill composites (Renamel Microfill, Estelite Sigma Quick) polish to a higher gloss and resist surface staining better than nanohybrids — important advantages for the smile zone. The trade-off is slightly lower mechanical strength, which matters for posterior load-bearing restorations but rarely for anterior veneers. Use microfill for the surface layer; nanohybrid is fine for dentin layers underneath.

blocks.title: Direct veneer pitfalls
blocks.body: **Veneer looks too thick / bulky:** the wax-up wasn't to ideal contour, or extra composite was added during build-up. Trim with finishing burs, re-shape, re-polish. **Shade match looks worse over time:** composite stains over years; counsel patient about maintenance polish at 12-month intervals. **Veneer chips at the incisal:** thin incisal enamel layer. Repair by bonding additional translucent shade to the chipped area. **Marginal stain at the cervical:** the cervical margin was under-polished, or the patient's brushing technique abrades the bond line. Re-polish; consider relining the cervical area at 2–3 years if recurrent.

chapters.title: Amalgam restoration — Class I and Class II
chapters.verdict: Amalgam is the legacy posterior restorative — non-adhesive, mechanical retention, slowly disappearing from clinical practice but still on the indication list for Class II preps with subgingival margins where bonding is unreliable, for patients with cost barriers to composite, and for any case where a clean dry field can't be reliably maintained. The case turns on prep geometry (this is one of the few restorations where Black's prep parameters still rule), proper matrix + wedging for Class II, condensation that overfills then carves back to anatomy, and a Gluma sealer step that is mandatory under amalgam to prevent post-op sensitivity. Source: Swade pp. 35-36.

blocks.text: Amalgam is mechanically retained, not bonded. That single fact drives most of the procedural differences from composite — the prep must have geometry that holds the restoration in place against occlusal forces, the matrix system stays simple (Tofflemire band + wedge), and there is no etch-and-bond step. The Gluma sealer step that replaces the bonding step is mandatory: it desensitizes the prepared dentin and reduces post-op sensitivity that is otherwise common under fresh amalgam.

items: **From sterilization:** amalgam kit, Isodry, handpieces, restorative burs, composite finishing burs, Isodry, septocaine, patient goggles, blood glucose kit.
**From locker:** blood pressure cuff, loupes / eye protection.
**In clinic:** articulating paper, matrix band (Tofflemire universal), wooden wedges, wedget, amalgamator, amalgam capsules, **Gluma sealer**, Isodry.
**In unit:** anesthetic needle / carpule / shield / topical, floss.
**Get a start check.**

**Check occlusion before anesthesia.** Use articulating paper to mark contacts on the tooth and the two adjacent teeth. Draw a simple diagram of the marks so post-op occlusion can be compared to pre-op (especially adjacent-tooth contacts, which should be unchanged).
**Deliver local anesthetic.**
**Isolate** with Isodry. Tie floss to the clamp.

**Complete prep.** Pre-wedge with a wooden wedge for Class II (expands the PDL and makes restoring the contact easier). Slow-speed round bur for caries removal — infected dentin reads wet, cheesy, clumpy; healthy dentin reads dry, chalky, dusty. If you're removing an existing composite, you can distinguish composite from tooth by drying thoroughly and scratching with an explorer (composite scratches, tooth does not).
**Check in** — have instructor check the prep.

**Place matrix and wedge.** Tofflemire retainer + universal matrix band + wooden wedge, then **burnish** the band against the adjacent tooth. The closed end of the Tofflemire retainer goes toward the occlusal. The matrix band should be narrower gingivally and wider occlusally (anatomic shape).
**Clean prep with Consepsis.** Apply Consepsis and scrub for 10 seconds, rinse 5 seconds, gently air-dry leaving dentin moist (not desiccated).

**Place liner or base if indicated.** Liner (Vitrebond / RMGI, ~0.5 mm) when remaining dentin thickness is too thin to prevent bacterial insult to the pulp. Base (Dycal — calcium hydroxide) ONLY over an indirect pulp cap area or a small mechanical (non-carious) pulpal exposure — then cover with Vitrebond.

**Place Gluma sealer.** Gluma is ALWAYS indicated under amalgam restorations. Apply a very thin layer in a scrubbing motion for 45 sec → wait 15 sec → air dry → rinse 15 sec → lightly dry, leaving dentin moist (high-evacuation suction over the tooth + light quick air dry; dentin should be glossy without pooling).

**Deliver and condense the amalgam.** Amalgamate the capsule for 9-12 sec at 3600 cpm (medium speed). Evaluate the mix — should look consistent, shiny, and smooth (a poor mix looks dull, crumbly, or dry). Empty the capsule into the amalgam well, use the amalgam carrier to deliver into the prep. Overfill — the cavosurface margins should be well covered.

**Continue condensing.** Use the small condenser first with adequate pressure over the pulpal wall and into preparation line angles. Then switch to the large condenser for the bulk of the preparation. Use high-speed suction to remove excess amalgam. Use a large ball burnisher to sweep across the restoration and establish the right contour (fossae and ridges) — generally while continuing to condense.

**Carve.** 5T for initial carving (keep the carving surface on both the tooth and the restoration to expose margins). Then Cleoid-Discoid, ½ Hollenback, and anatomic burnisher to carve anatomy. Smooth the surface with a wet cotton roll once the amalgam is mostly set.

**Check occlusion and contact.** Remove Isodry. Check occlusion with articulating paper — **be careful**: if the patient bites really hard amalgam can fracture before it fully sets. If occlusion is high, adjust gently with a high-speed bur (don't be aggressive — you don't want to fracture the amalgam). Check interproximal contact with floss.
**Check in** — have instructor check the restoration.
**Complete codes and note.** D2140 (1 surface), D2150 (2 surfaces), D2160 (3 surfaces), D2161 (4 or more surfaces).

blocks.title: Amalgam prep parameters (Swade — these are non-negotiable)
blocks.body:
Amalgam needs specific geometry because it retains by mechanical undercut, not bonding. The 5 specs:

**1. Convergent buccal and lingual walls** (occlusally) — creates the mechanical undercut.
**2. Parallel or slightly divergent proximal walls** — prevents over-thinning and gives the restoration draw.
**3. At least 1.5 mm deep** — deeper if decay extends deeper. Less than 1.5 mm risks fracture.
**4. Flat pulpal floor with rounded line angles + beveled axiopulpal walls** — distributes occlusal force evenly; rounded angles avoid stress concentrations.
**5. Does not undermine marginal ridges** — unsupported enamel will fracture later.

blocks.title: Why Gluma is mandatory under amalgam
blocks.body: Amalgam doesn't seal the dentinal tubules (composite does, via the bond layer). Without a sealer step, dentinal-fluid movement causes post-op sensitivity that can last weeks. Gluma (glutaraldehyde + HEMA) coagulates protein in the tubule fluid and seals the surface chemically. **Apply Gluma to the prep before placing the amalgam, EVERY time.** Skip the Gluma step and the patient returns with a sensitivity complaint.

blocks.title: When to choose amalgam vs composite
blocks.body: Amalgam is still indicated for: (1) Class II preps with deep subgingival margins where bonding can't be kept dry, (2) patients with significant cost barriers to composite (amalgam is faster, no etch/bond materials), (3) cases requiring high compressive strength under heavy occlusal load where bonded composite has a track record of marginal failure (rare), (4) any case where a clean dry field can't be reliably maintained throughout the bonding sequence. Composite is preferred everywhere else — esthetics, conservation of tooth structure, and the bonded seal.

blocks.title: Amalgam pitfalls
blocks.body: **Amalgam fractures within days of delivery:** prep was less than 1.5 mm deep, OR the patient bit hard before set was complete. Always counsel the patient to avoid chewing on the new amalgam for 24 hours. **Post-op sensitivity at 1 week:** Gluma step was skipped or under-applied. Counsel; usually resolves over 2-3 weeks. If persistent, may need to replace. **Marginal ridge fractures:** restoration was placed with the marginal ridge thin or unsupported. The prep undermined the natural marginal ridge or the carving over-thinned the amalgam ridge. **Open contact at delivery:** matrix band wasn't well-burnished against the adjacent tooth before placing the amalgam. Floss should snap through with resistance. Re-do — there is no salvaging an open contact. **Mix looks dull or crumbly:** amalgamator setting wrong, or capsule expired. Replace with a fresh capsule. **Carving wears through to dentin:** carved too aggressively while amalgam was still soft. Wait until amalgam is mostly set (use the wet-cotton-roll smoothing step) before final carving.

chapters.title: Sealants — caries prevention + incipient-lesion arrest
chapters.verdict: Sealants are placed on caries-free teeth to prevent pit-and-fissure caries, OR on teeth with non-cavitated enamel lesions to arrest them before they progress. Quick, single-visit, code D1351 per tooth. The case turns on absolutely dry isolation (Isodry — saliva contamination kills the bond), pumice + dry the surface clean, etch + optional bond + sealant material, light cure. Material is thick; over-application causes occlusal interference, so check with articulating paper before dismissing. Source: Swade p. 51.

blocks.text: Sealants prevent caries by mechanically sealing the deep pits and fissures of posterior teeth where biofilm accumulates. Two indications: (1) caries-free teeth where prevention is the goal — typical for first and second molars in young patients shortly after eruption, and (2) teeth with non-cavitated carious lesions limited to enamel, where the sealant arrests the lesion by cutting off substrate. If the lesion has cavitated through enamel into dentin, sealant is no longer indicated — that's a Class I composite case (see dir-ch10).

items: **From sterilization:** composite kit, handpieces, prophy handpiece, composite finishing burs, curing light, Isodry, patient goggles, blood glucose kit.
**From locker:** blood pressure cuff, loupes / eye protection.

**In clinic:** articulating paper, etch, ScotchBond, **sealant material** (note: at UIC the sealant material lives in the peds clinic supply — pick it up there if it's not on your operatory cart). Sealant material per Swade: Ultradent UltraSeal XT Plus.
**In unit:** prophy pumice (fluoride-free), prophy angle.
**Get a start check.**

**Isolate.** Achieve adequate isolation. Sealant placement requires a *totally* dry field — saliva contamination during the etch or bond step means the sealant won't adhere. Isodry is the ideal isolation here because it doesn't require numbing for Isodry placement (sealants are usually placed without anesthesia).
**Clean the tooth.** Use a prophy cup with **fluoride-free** pumice (fluoride-containing pumice inhibits the etch and the bond). Thoroughly dry the tooth after cleaning.

**Etch and bond.** Etch enamel (35% phosphoric) for 30 sec → rinse → lightly dry. Apply ScotchBond in a vigorous scrubbing motion for 20 sec → air thin for 5 sec → light cure 10 sec. Bonding before the sealant is technically optional, but faculty typically want it done — confirm with your instructor before skipping.

**Apply sealant.** Use the included sealant brush, a microbrush, or an explorer tip to manipulate the material into the pits and fissures. The material is THICK — apply too much and it interferes with occlusion. Better to under-fill and add a thin top layer than to over-fill and adjust afterward. Light cure 20 sec.
**Check for excess.** The most common excess-sealant location is distal of mandibular molars, near or under the gingival margin. Remove excess with a finishing bur before dismissing the patient.

**Check occlusion.** Articulating paper at MI; sealant should not be high on the occlusion. If it is, adjust gently with a fine finishing bur. Patient should not feel any change in bite from a properly placed sealant.
**Complete codes and note.** D1351 (Sealant — per tooth). Note records each tooth sealed individually.
**Check in** — instructor checks the work + swipes EPR/codes/notes.

blocks.title: When sealants are NOT indicated
blocks.body: If the lesion has progressed into dentin (cavitated, radiolucency on bitewing), a sealant won't stop it — that's a Class I composite case. Don't seal over frank dentinal caries; you'll trap bacteria under the seal and the lesion will progress under the restoration. Also do not seal teeth that are too newly erupted (gingival tissue covering occlusal surface — wait until the tooth is fully erupted).

blocks.title: Sealants on multiple teeth — batch the workflow
blocks.body: Sealants are routinely done as batches: all four first molars (#3, #14, #19, #30) in a single visit, or all 8 molars + premolars in some cases. Set up Isodry once, etch all teeth in sequence, bond all teeth, then place sealant on each. Saves time + the patient is anesthesia-free so chair time is fast.

blocks.title: Sealant pitfalls
blocks.body: **Sealant debonds within weeks:** moisture contamination during the etch or bond step. Re-isolate, re-etch (5 sec only this time), re-bond, re-place. **Patient reports bite feels uneven:** sealant over-filled the occlusal — adjust with a fine finishing bur. **Sealant doesn't reach into a deep fissure:** material was too viscous or didn't flow. Use a microbrush or explorer tip to actively push material into the fissure before curing. **Tooth has both a sealable area and a small cavitation:** the cavitation is a Class I composite case; the rest of the occlusal can be sealed at the same visit. This is technically a PRR (Preventive Resin Restoration) — single code, different from D1351 sealant alone. **Patient asks if it'll "wear off":** modern sealants last years if well-placed; degradation is at the margins typically. Recheck at recall visits and reapply if needed.

chapters.title: Local anesthesia reference — maxillary + mandibular blocks
chapters.verdict: Comprehensive reference for every block technique used in restorative dentistry — maxillary infiltrations + 6 named max blocks, the IAN + long buccal + mental for the mandible, plus the innervation lookup table and the maximum-dose table for every anesthetic-vasoconstrictor combination. Use as a chairside reference when planning anesthesia for a procedure that's outside your routine. Source: Swade pp. 175-180.

blocks.text: Most restorative procedures use infiltration (maxillary) or IAN block (mandibular) and never need anything else. The other blocks come up when infiltration won't reach (e.g., dense maxillary bone in older patients), when a single tooth needs deep anesthesia and the infiltration didn't take, or when the procedure crosses an anatomic boundary (e.g., a maxillary midline frenectomy needs both ASA and nasopalatine for bilateral coverage). The first table below maps every tooth + soft-tissue area to the relevant block. The second table is the maximum-dose table for safety calculations.

items: Do not insert the needle to the full length at the hub — if it breaks, retrieval is difficult.
Do not change needle direction while it's deep in tissue.
**Always aspirate before injection** to avoid intravascular delivery.
Inject slowly to minimize pain.
Do not inject into or through an infected area.
Use needles no smaller than 27-gauge — higher gauge (smaller bore) makes aspiration unreliable.

blocks.text: **Anesthetizes:** the individual maxillary tooth + its associated buccal gingiva (sometimes palatal gingiva, depending on volume). The injection works by depositing anesthetic near the bone supporting the tooth; the solution penetrates the cortex to reach the nerve.

items: Apply topical (20% benzocaine on a cotton-tipped applicator; have the patient or the cheek hold it in place 2-3 min). Dry the area thoroughly with gauze first.
Pull the mucous membrane outward and downward to extend the mucosa and delineate the mucobuccal fold.
Puncture the mucobuccal fold with the bevel facing bone, aligned with the center of the tooth, aimed toward the maxilla.
Contact maxilla, then withdraw the needle 1 mm.
Aspirate.
Deposit anesthetic.
**Failure modes:** solution deposited too far from the periosteum (needle too high above or too low below the root), or unusually dense bone — the infiltration may not take.

blocks.text: **Anesthetizes:** palatal gingiva immediately around a single tooth.

items: Apply topical.
Apply pressure to the injection site with a cotton-tipped applicator to blanch the tissue (left hand).
Puncture palatal gingiva within the blanched area, close to the applicator.
Deposit a small volume while simultaneously advancing the needle until bone is contacted.

blocks.text: **Anesthetizes:** maxillary molars + associated buccal gingiva. **Caveat:** the MB root of the maxillary 1st molar is usually innervated by the MSA (not PSA) and may require an additional MSA injection or buccal infiltration around the 1st molar.

items: Apply topical.
Retract the cheek laterally.
Puncture the mucobuccal fold just distal to the distobuccal root of the maxillary 2nd molar.

Direct the needle toward the maxillary tuberosity (upward, backward, inward), then along the curvature of the maxilla to a depth of 2.0-2.5 cm. **Don't advance too far posteriorly** — risks a CN V division II block. **If the needle contacts bone, withdraw and redirect more laterally.**
Aspirate (critical — risk of injection into the pterygoid plexus, which causes hematoma).
Deposit anesthetic.

blocks.text: **Anesthetizes:** maxillary premolars + MB root of maxillary 1st molar + associated buccal gingiva.

items: Apply topical.
Retract the cheek laterally.
Puncture the mucobuccal fold between the maxillary 2nd premolar and 1st molar.
Direct the needle posteriorly at a 45° angle, into the tissue at 45°.
Advance to a depth of 1-1.5 cm.
Aspirate. Deposit anesthetic.

blocks.text: **Anesthetizes:** maxillary incisors + canine + associated buccal gingiva. **Landmark:** the apex of the canine tooth.

items: Apply topical. Retract the lip anteriorly and locate the mucobuccal fold.
Puncture the mucobuccal fold at the apex of the canine tooth.
Insert the needle at the intersection of the mucobuccal fold and the apex / center of the canine at a 45° angle.
Advance to a depth of 1-1.5 cm.
Aspirate. Deposit anesthetic.

blocks.text: **Anesthetizes:** posterior 2/3 of the palatal gingiva, unilateral, from premolars distally.

items: Locate the greater palatine foramen via palpation — 1 cm medial from the distal of the 2nd molar; a cotton-tipped applicator will fall into the depression.
Apply topical.
Apply pressure with the applicator to blanch the tissue for 30 sec.
Puncture within the blanched area, close to the applicator, at a 90° angle to the curvature of the hard palate. **Do not advance the needle into the foramen.**
Aspirate.
**Deposit ¼ carpule only** — more risks separating the mucosa from the palate (necrosis).

blocks.text: **Anesthetizes:** anterior 1/3 of the palatal gingiva — canine to canine, bilaterally (single injection covers both sides).

items: Locate the incisive foramen via palpation — underneath the incisive papilla, ~0.5 cm posterior to the central incisors at the midline.
Apply topical.
Apply pressure with the applicator to blanch the tissue for 30 sec.
Puncture the palatal mucosa just lateral to the incisive papilla.
Advance 3-4 mm medially or until the needle contacts bone.
Aspirate.
**Deposit ¼ carpule only** — same necrosis caveat as greater palatine.

blocks.text: **Anesthetizes:** incisors + premolars + buccal gingiva + lower eyelid + upper cheek + part of nose + upper lip (extraoral coverage). Used for procedures involving the upper lip or for cases where ASA + MSA combined aren't covering well.

items: Locate the infraorbital foramen via palpation — just medial to the intersection of a vertical line from the pupil and the inferior border of the infraorbital ridge.
Apply topical.
Place the index finger of the left hand above the infraorbital foramen, retract the cheek with the thumb.
Puncture the mucobuccal fold at the junction of the premolars; direct the needle parallel to the long axis of the 2nd premolar, palpating its location as the needle advances.
Advance until adjacent to the infraorbital foramen, about 1.5-2 cm. **If the angle is too acute the needle hits the maxillary eminence; if too superior the needle enters the orbit.**
Aspirate.
Deposit anesthetic while holding firm pressure over the infraorbital ridge — prevents ballooning of the lower eyelid.

blocks.text: **Anesthetizes:** ALL mandibular teeth + buccal gingiva from premolars to midline + lingual gingiva + floor of mouth + tongue + lower lip + skin of chin.

items: Palpate the retromolar fossa with index finger or thumb. Identify the coronoid notch (greatest depth of the anterior border of the ramus).
With thumb in the mouth and index finger placed externally behind the ramus, retract the tissues toward the buccal side and visualize the pterygomandibular triangle.
Hold the syringe parallel to the occlusal surfaces of the teeth and angled so the barrel lies between the premolars on the opposite side of the mandible.

Puncture the pterygomandibular triangle 1 cm above the occlusal surface of the molars. **If the needle enters too low** (at the level of the teeth), anesthetic is deposited over the bony canal and prominence (lingula) that house the mandibular nerve — not over the nerve.
There may be slight resistance as the needle passes through the ligaments and muscles covering the internal surface of the mandible.

Stop when the needle has reached bone — this signifies contact with the posterior wall of the mandibular sulcus. **Failure to contact bone usually means the needle was directed too posteriorly toward the parotid gland**, which anesthetizes the facial nerve instead.
After reaching bone, withdraw slightly. Aspirate. Deposit anesthetic.
**Deposit anesthetic while withdrawing the needle** to ensure lingual nerve anesthesia (the lingual nerve sits anterior to the IAN in the same compartment).

blocks.text: **Anesthetizes:** buccal gingiva of mandibular molars. Required IN ADDITION to the IAN block for procedures involving molar buccal soft tissue (e.g., extractions).

items: Apply topical.
Pull the cheek laterally.
Puncture the mucosa 1 mm lateral to the molars at the anterior border of the ramus, in the occlusal plane.
Advance 3-4 mm.
Aspirate. Deposit anesthetic.

blocks.text: **Anesthetizes:** premolars + canine + (sometimes incisors) + buccal gingiva from premolars to midline + lower lip + skin of chin. Useful as a less-invasive alternative to IAN for procedures involving only the anterior mandible.

items: Locate the mental foramen via palpation — halfway between the upper and lower edges of the mandible, underneath the 2nd premolar. **Alternative:** locate the foramen on the patient's panoramic radiograph.
Apply topical.
Retract the lower lip.
Puncture the mucosa at the depth of the buccal vestibule at the 2nd premolar, parallel to its long axis.
Insert the needle ~1 cm.
Aspirate. Deposit anesthetic.

blocks.title: Oral cavity innervation — tooth/soft tissue → block lookup
blocks.body:
**Maxillary (CN V2):**
- Incisors + buccal gingiva: ASA n. — buccal infiltration OR ASA block.
- Canine + buccal gingiva: ASA n. — buccal infiltration OR MSA block.
- Premolars + buccal gingiva: MSA n. — buccal infiltration OR MSA block.
- Molars + buccal gingiva: PSA n. (MB root of 1st molar 75% MSA) — buccal infiltration OR PSA block.
- Palatal gingiva, anterior (canine to canine): nasopalatine n. — palatal infiltration OR nasopalatine block.
- Palatal gingiva, posterior: greater palatine n. — palatal infiltration OR greater palatine block.
- Upper lip: infraorbital n. — infraorbital block.

**Mandibular (CN V3):**
- Incisors / canine / premolars: inferior alveolar n. (some incisor innervation from mylohyoid n.) — IAN block OR mental block.
- Molars: inferior alveolar n. (mesial root of 1st molar sometimes from mylohyoid) — IAN block.
- Buccal gingiva, incisors to premolars: mental n. — mental block OR buccal block.
- Buccal gingiva, molars: long buccal n. — long buccal block.
- Lingual gingiva / floor of mouth / tongue (anterior 2/3): lingual n. — lingual block (usually on withdrawal of IAN block).
- Lower lip: mental n. — mental block.

blocks.title: Maximum dose reference (Swade p. 180)
blocks.body:
**Per carpule (1.8 mL):**
- 2% lidocaine: 36 mg
- 3% mepivacaine: 54 mg
- 4% prilocaine or 4% articaine: 72 mg

**Maximum total dose (typical adult):**
- 2% lidocaine + 1:100k epi: 500 mg max, ≈13 carpules. With 1:50k epi: 5 carpules (epi limits before lidocaine does).
- 3% mepivacaine (no vasoconstrictor): 400 mg max, ≈7 carpules.
- 2% mepivacaine + 1:200k levonordefrin: 400 mg max, ≈11 carpules.
- 4% prilocaine + 1:200k epi: 600 mg max, ≈8 carpules.
- 4% articaine + 1:200k epi: 500 mg max, ≈6 carpules. With 1:100k epi: ≈6 carpules.
- 0.5% bupivacaine + 1:200k epi: 90 mg max, ≈10 carpules.

**By weight:** lidocaine 7 mg/kg, mepivacaine 6.6 mg/kg, prilocaine 8 mg/kg, articaine 7 mg/kg, bupivacaine 1.3 mg/kg.

**Vasoconstrictor limits (typical adult):** 0.2 mg epi total. With cardiac patients, 0.04 mg epi total. 1:100k = 0.018 mg per carpule; 1:50k = 0.036 mg per carpule; 1:200k = 0.009 mg per carpule.

blocks.title: Anesthesia pitfalls
blocks.body: **IAN block didn't take (patient still feels the tooth):** common causes — needle was too low (deposited over bone canal, not nerve), accessory innervation from mylohyoid (give a buccal infiltration of the affected tooth as a supplement), or patient anatomy with accessory canals. Re-block from a different angle, OR supplement with intraligamentary or intraosseous. **PSA block caused hematoma:** needle entered the pterygoid plexus — apply pressure for several minutes, ice on the cheek. Bruising lasts 7-10 days; counsel the patient. **Patient develops Bell's-palsy-like symptoms after IAN:** facial nerve (CN VII) was anesthetized because the needle was directed too posteriorly into the parotid gland. Effect is temporary; reassure the patient, cover the eye to protect from drying out, anesthesia wears off in 1-3 hours. **Patient feels electric shock during IAN:** the needle hit the IAN directly — withdraw 1-2 mm before depositing. Persistent paresthesia is rare but documented; refer if it doesn't resolve. **Palatal injection caused necrosis:** too much volume deposited — limit to ¼ carpule on greater palatine and nasopalatine. Necrotic area heals in 2-3 weeks; topical analgesic + reassurance.

chapters.title: PRR (Preventive Resin Restoration) — small cavitation in a sealable surface
chapters.verdict: PRR is the case in between a sealant and a Class I composite — there's a small cavitation that needs restoration, but the surrounding occlusal anatomy is sealable. You restore the cavitation with composite AND seal the rest of the occlusal at the same visit. Single code D2391 (cannot bill sealant separately on the same tooth on the same day). The case turns on minimal-prep philosophy (the smallest cavity prep that addresses the caries), uniform etch + bond across the whole occlusal surface, composite in the cavitation, sealant material as the top layer covering both composite and remaining pits. Source: Swade pp. 136-137.

blocks.text: PRR comes up when you do a sealant exam and find one tooth with a small cavitation that has progressed past the enamel — a sealant alone is now insufficient, but the tooth isn't a full Class I composite case either. The minimal-prep restoration + sealant-over-the-rest combination is faster than a full Class I composite, less invasive, and codes as D2391 (resin-based composite, 1 surface, posterior) rather than as a sealant + separate restoration. Note: DHS will not cover both a composite AND a sealant on the same tooth on the same day, so PRR is the right single code.

items: **From sterilization:** peds composite kit, handpieces, peds burs, composite finishing burs (not peds-specific), restorative burs (not peds), composite gun, curing light, peds Isodry, nitrous nose, peds goggles.
**From locker:** loupes / eye protection.
**In clinic:** anesthetic needle/carpule/shield/topical, Isodry, wedget, Isodry, etch, ScotchBond, Shofu, articulating paper.

**Present patient to instructor BEFORE bringing them to the chair.** Cover: patient (age, gender, MH, sig dental history, behavior), restorative plan (procedures for today, sequencing if multiple teeth), anesthesia plan (anesthetic + injection type + how much + nitrous), isolation plan. Other useful mentions: how recent the treatment plan is, date of last radiographs.

**Call patient in.** Find patient + guardian in waiting room. Ask the guardian if anything has changed (med history, medications, allergies, etc.). Explain what you're doing today and how long they should expect to wait. Leave guardian in waiting room, bring patient back. **Weigh the patient** (peds dosing depends on it).
**Administer nitrous oxide** if planned.
**Give local anesthetic.** **1 carpule is the limit** — check with instructor before delivering more than 1.

**Isolate.** Isodry is acceptable. If using Isodry: tie floss to the clamp; instead of punching several holes, punch 2 holes ½ inch apart and use scissors to cut between them — allows quicker placement on a peds patient.

**Complete prep.** Remove decay with high-speed then slow-speed burs. With slow-speed round bur: infected dentin reads wet, cheesy, clumpy; healthy dentin reads dry, chalky, dusty. **Only remove carious enamel + dentin** — the point of a PRR is to make the SMALLEST possible preparation. Depth is usually 1.0-1.5 mm and typically only involves pits (no need to extend into grooves unless cariously involved). If caries is larger than expected or you're close to the pulp, check with instructor.
**Check in** — instructor checks the cavity preparation.

**Restore, polish, and adjust.** Etch enamel first (the ENTIRE occlusal surface to allow sealant placement later), then dentin → wait 15 sec → rinse → lightly dry leaving dentin moist (high-evac suction + light quick air dry; dentin should be glossy without pooling). Apply ScotchBond in a vigorous scrubbing motion for 20 sec → air thin for 5 sec → light cure 10 sec. If contaminated: re-etch for 5 sec only, then proceed. Place composite in increments and cure 20-40 sec each (often 1 increment due to small prep size); cure final increment 60 sec. **Place a sealant over the composite restoration** and cure 20 sec (10 sec on high). Remove Isodry, check occlusion with articulating paper, finish and polish.

**Complete codes.** D2391 (Resin-based composite — 1 surface, posterior). **Cannot complete codes for composite AND sealant on the same tooth in the same day** because DHS will not cover both. The PRR is one code that covers both.
**Check in** — instructor checks final restoration, swipes EPR/codes/notes.

blocks.title: PRR vs sealant vs Class I composite — the decision
blocks.body:
**Sealant only (D1351):** caries-free pits/fissures OR non-cavitated enamel lesion. No drilling.

**PRR (D2391):** small cavitation in one or two pits, surrounding pits/fissures are sealable. Minimal prep + composite + sealant overlay, all in one tooth, one visit, one code.

**Class I composite (D2391):** larger cavitation that requires a more substantial prep. No sealant overlay needed — the composite IS the restoration.

The code D2391 covers both PRR and a simple Class I composite — they bill the same. The PRR distinction is procedural (sealant overlay) not billing.

blocks.title: Peds patient prep notes
blocks.body: Most PRRs are placed on pediatric patients. Specific peds considerations: weigh the patient before dosing anesthesia (1 carpule is the routine limit), use Isodry over Isodry when possible to avoid clamp-related complaints, give 100% oxygen at 4 L/min for 5 minutes at the end if nitrous was used, counsel the guardian about post-op numbness + lip/tongue biting watching. Sarah Swade / Dr. signature on the note.

blocks.title: PRR pitfalls
blocks.body: **Prep extended too far into the grooves:** the point of PRR is minimal prep. If you find yourself extending into surrounding grooves to follow caries, the case has become a Class I composite — not a PRR. Don't try to keep it minimal at the expense of leaving caries. **Sealant overlay debonded within weeks:** moisture contamination during the etch — re-isolate, re-etch (5 sec), re-bond, re-place. **Composite + sealant high on occlusion:** the sealant material over the composite added bulk. Adjust gently with a fine finishing bur. **Billing wrong code (D2391 + D1351):** DHS doesn't cover both on the same tooth same day. Just the D2391. Educate yourself before the codes step.

chapters.title: SDF (Silver Diamine Fluoride) — non-surgical caries arrest
chapters.verdict: SDF is a topical liquid that arrests caries in place — no drilling, no anesthesia, no restoration required. Used for cases where definitive treatment isn't possible: very young / very anxious patients, patients with significant medical or behavioral barriers to operative work, palliative cases in the elderly, root caries in patients who can't tolerate restorative procedures. The patient (or guardian) MUST be informed that SDF stains carious lesions black permanently — show pictures during consent. Source: Swade p. 133.

blocks.text: SDF is a 38% silver diamine fluoride solution that's painted onto carious lesions. The silver kills cariogenic bacteria + forms a precipitate that mechanically blocks dentinal tubules; the fluoride remineralizes adjacent tooth structure. The result is an arrested carious lesion — the surface stays soft initially but hardens over weeks, and the bacterial activity stops. The lesion turns BLACK permanently, which is the trade-off the patient/guardian must understand before consenting.

items: **In clinic:** SDF (38% solution, single-use vial or capsule), dappen dish, microbrush.
**In unit:** cotton rolls, gauze.

**OBTAIN INFORMED CONSENT FROM PARENT, INCLUDING SHOWING PICTURES OF SDF-STAINED LESIONS.** This is the single most important step. The carious lesion will turn permanently black. If the parent is not OK with that — even if you've explained that it's only the carious area, not the whole tooth — do NOT proceed. Have the conversation BEFORE you set up.
**Put 1 drop of SDF in a dappen dish.** That's enough for several lesions; SDF goes a long way.
**Apply a thick layer of Vaseline to the lips.** SDF will stain skin temporarily — Vaseline prevents this. Don't skip on a child patient.
**Isolate the tooth with cotton rolls**, or retract with your hand if possible. Isodry is overkill; cotton rolls suffice.
**Dry the tooth completely using gauze, then air.** SDF needs a dry surface to penetrate.
**Apply SDF with a microbrush** — you don't need a lot, just a very thin coating.
**Wait 60 seconds** for SDF to dry/be absorbed.
**Dry the tooth with cotton roll** and wipe away remaining wet SDF.
**Rinse the tooth and suction.**

blocks.title: Informed consent talking points
blocks.body:
Before doing SDF, walk the parent through:

**Pros:** No drilling, no anesthesia, no nitrous, no chair time beyond ~5 minutes per tooth. Arrests the caries — buys time. Can be repeated every 6-12 months.

**Cons:** Carious areas will turn permanently BLACK (show pictures from a reference). Soft tissue can develop transient staining that resolves in 1-2 weeks. The treatment doesn't restore the tooth — if a definitive restoration is eventually needed, SDF doesn't prevent it.

**When SDF is the right call:** patient is too young / too anxious for operative work, the carious lesion is not symptomatic, the parent is informed about and OK with the staining, and the goal is buying time until definitive treatment is feasible.

blocks.title: After SDF — what happens to the tooth
blocks.body: Over 2-4 weeks, the carious surface hardens. The tooth still LOOKS carious (black + cavitated) but the bacterial activity has stopped. The lesion can still progress mechanically (the patient can chip more tooth off) but the caries itself isn't actively destroying tooth structure. Re-application every 6-12 months maintains the arrest. If a definitive restoration is later placed, the stained dentin can be excavated normally OR left in place under the restoration (since it's arrested).

blocks.title: SDF pitfalls
blocks.body: **Skin staining on the patient's face or lips:** insufficient Vaseline. Wipe immediately with a damp gauze — staining is transient (1-2 weeks) on skin. Counsel the parent. **Soft tissue staining adjacent to the tooth:** transient, resolves in 1-2 weeks. Counsel the parent before they panic. **Parent unhappy with the black staining at follow-up:** this is the informed consent failure. Document the consent conversation carefully BEFORE applying. If the parent already consented and is now unhappy, document, explore restoration options. **SDF dripped onto operatory countertops:** SDF stains EVERYTHING. Wipe immediately; permanent staining is the rule. Keep SDF over the dappen dish and microbrush only. **Lesion didn't arrest:** very deep lesions or those with pulpal involvement won't arrest with SDF alone. Re-evaluate with bitewing; may need definitive treatment.

chapters.title: Sedative restoration (D2940) — emergency IRM placement
chapters.verdict: A short-term palliative restoration for patients in pain whose definitive treatment must wait. IRM seals the dentin and quiets the symptoms; the patient must return for definitive treatment within 2–6 weeks.

blocks.text: Sedative restorations belong to emergency dentistry. The patient is in pain — a fractured cusp exposed dentin, a temporary lost off a prepped tooth, a deep caries that's too painful to address with definitive treatment. The goal is to seal the dentin, palliate the symptoms, and buy time for the patient to return for definitive treatment. IRM (intermediate restorative material) — a zinc-oxide-eugenol-based cement — is the material of choice because eugenol has a known pulpal sedative effect. The restoration is not intended to last; the patient must return.
When to place a sedative versus a definitive restoration

items: **Patient in acute pain, time-limited visit:** sedative. Manage the emergency; schedule definitive.
**Tooth fractured but asymptomatic, awaiting indirect treatment:** sedative is acceptable; the indirect restoration is the definitive treatment.
**Deep caries that approaches the pulp, patient unsure about endo vs extraction:** sedative buys 2–6 weeks to discuss treatment options. Vitality testing at the next visit to inform the decision.
**Asymptomatic caries discovered at routine exam:** do not place a sedative. Schedule definitive treatment at the next visit.
**Patient unable to afford definitive treatment now:** sedative is an option, but the patient must return — counsel honestly about the temporary nature.
**IRM** (Dentsply) in powder + liquid form, or pre-dosed capsules.
**Spatula and mixing pad** (if powder/liquid).
**Plastic instrument** for placement.
**Articulating paper** for occlusion check.
**Anesthesia** if the tooth is symptomatic; not needed for asymptomatic placement.
**Isolation** with cotton rolls and high-volume suction. Isodry is overkill for a temporary.
**Caries removal** — bulk removal only, with a spoon excavator. Do not aggressively chase deep caries; the IRM will seal the affected area.
**Mix IRM** to a thick, putty-like consistency. Powder dropped onto the liquid, spatulated until it no longer sticks to the spatula.
**Roll IRM into a small ball**, place in the cavity, condense with a plastic instrument.
**Shape the surface** to match the original anatomy approximately — exact occlusion is not the goal.
**Check the bite** with articulating paper, adjust if high.
**Schedule the patient** for definitive treatment within 2–6 weeks.
**Counsel the patient:** the IRM may chip; the tooth may still be slightly tender for 24–48 hours; if pain worsens, call.

blocks.title: Why not composite for a sedative
blocks.body: Composite is a definitive restoration material, not a sedative. The acidic etch and adhesive components irritate inflamed pulp; the bond is unreliable on partially excavated caries. More importantly, the eugenol in IRM provides a known pulpal sedative effect that composite lacks. If you then place IRM after a composite for a future definitive treatment, the eugenol contaminates the bond. Use IRM for sedatives; reserve composite for definitive cases.

blocks.title: Sedative pitfalls
blocks.body: **IRM fractures or falls out within days:** under-condensed, or the cavity didn't have mechanical retention. For a fractured IRM, re-place; for a tooth without retention, may need a custom temporary crown (orthodontic-band-based temp or pre-formed crown). **Patient still in pain at 48 hours:** the pulp may be irreversibly inflamed. Vitality test (cold, percussion) and consider scheduling endodontic treatment. **Patient doesn't return for definitive treatment:** the IRM will leak within 2–3 months and the caries will progress. The patient is now lost-to-follow-up but the tooth is at risk. Document attempts to recall.

chapters.title: Core buildup (D2950) — foundation for a crown
chapters.verdict: The core replaces missing tooth structure to create the bulk that the crown prep will be cut into. The case turns on whether 1.5–2 mm of sound tooth structure remains for the crown ferrule after prep; without ferrule, consider extraction instead.

blocks.text: A core buildup is the bridge between operative dentistry and indirect restorative dentistry. The tooth is too damaged to support a crown directly — usually post-endodontic, with significant coronal tooth structure missing — and needs a foundation that will then be prepared as the crown abutment. Modern cores are dual-cure resin composite bonded with a dual-cure adhesive. The critical question before placing a core is whether the case is restorable at all: 1.5–2 mm of sound tooth structure remaining coronal to the crown margin (the ferrule) is needed for predictable crown longevity.

Before committing to a core-and-crown plan, evaluate the ferrule. Walk the periodontal probe around the gingival margin and identify where the tooth structure ends.
