Ferrule should be at least 1.5–2 mm of sound dentin or enamel circumferentially, and at least 1 mm of dentin axial thickness. If the ferrule is questionable (<1 mm in any one spot), discuss with the patient: a no-ferrule crown debonds within 2–5 years, an extraction with implant or bridge has 10–15 year longevity. Heroic core-and-crown on a non-restorable tooth wastes the patient's time and money.

items: **Dual-cure resin composite core material** (Bisco Light-Core, Build-It FR, FluoroCore) — auto-mix in dual-syringe cartridge.
**Dual-cure bonding agent** (matched to the core composite) — important. A light-cure-only adhesive may not fully cure under the bulk of the core.
**Etch gel** (35% phosphoric acid).
**Microbrush** for adhesive application.
**Plastic instrument** for shaping.
**Tofflemire or other matrix** if the cavity has missing walls — the matrix shapes the core during placement.
**Confirm restorability** — ferrule check. If marginal, discuss alternatives with the patient before proceeding.
**Anesthesia** if the tooth is vital; not needed post-endo.
**Isolation** with Isodry — moisture control is critical for the bond.
**Remove any remaining caries** or failing restoration. Probe the cavity floor; if any dentin is soft, remove it.
**Selective-etch enamel** (15 sec), rinse, dry.
**Apply dual-cure bonding agent** to enamel and dentin. Light cure 10 sec; the chemical-cure component will continue to set under the core.
**Place matrix** if needed for missing walls.
**Inject dual-cure composite** from the auto-mix cartridge into the cavity. Fill to slightly over the planned core height.
**Shape with a plastic instrument** to approximate the original tooth contour.
**Light cure 40 seconds** on each accessible surface; the chemical-cure component cures the bulk over the next 4–5 minutes.
**Remove matrix**, refine the contour with finishing burs.
**Prep the crown** within the same visit, or schedule the crown prep visit within 2 weeks.

blocks.title: Post-and-core cases
blocks.body: If the tooth is post-endo and lacks enough coronal dentin to retain a core, a post may be needed for retention. Use a fiber-reinforced composite post (ParaPost Fiber Lux) bonded into the canal — the fiber elasticity matches dentin and reduces root-fracture risk versus metal posts. Place the post first (etch the canal, apply adhesive, cement with dual-cure resin cement, light cure), then place the core composite around it. Pin retention is rarely needed in modern dentistry; adhesive bonding is more reliable.

blocks.title: Core buildup pitfalls
blocks.body: **Core fractures off during crown prep:** under-cured at the bond interface, or the case lacked ferrule from the start. Re-evaluate restorability; may need to extract. **Voids in the core after cure:** under-condensed during injection — air pockets where the auto-mix tip wasn't fully embedded. Re-prep into the void and re-build. **Core too soft at 24 hours:** dual-cure didn't activate. Likely the auto-mix tip was used past its working time, or the cartridge had stagnated. Replace and re-place. **Crown prep cuts into composite, not tooth:** the prep is too aggressive given the available tooth structure. Stop, reassess; the case may need a different approach (smaller prep, taller core, post).

chapters.title: Composite material reference — Renamel, Filtek, Tetric
chapters.verdict: Material choice affects polish, wear, stain resistance, and esthetics — but not bond strength. Within nanohybrid composites, all major brands are clinically equivalent for typical posterior work; the differences matter most in the smile zone.

blocks.text: The composite market is crowded with brand names, but most cases come down to choosing among three categories: nanohybrid (the workhorse, fine for everywhere), microfill (for esthetics in the smile zone), and bulk-fill (for deep posterior boxes). Within each category, the major brands perform similarly in clinical studies. The decision turns more on availability in your clinic and what you're comfortable handling.

items: **Filtek Supreme Ultra (3M)** — broad shade range, predictable handling, well-studied. The default for general posterior and anterior work.
**Tetric EvoCeram (Ivoclar)** — slightly higher polish than Filtek, slightly softer in handling. Good in the smile zone where polish matters.
**Empress Direct (Ivoclar)** — premium anterior nanohybrid with broad esthetic shade options. Worth the cost for esthetic anteriors.
**Estelite Sigma Quick (Tokuyama)** — highest polish in the nanohybrid category, very chameleon effect (blends with surrounding tooth). Good for chairside-restoration single-shade cases.

blocks.text: Microfill composites have smaller filler particle sizes (~0.04 µm) than nanohybrids (~0.4 µm). The clinical implication is a higher initial polish, better stain resistance, and a glossier surface that better mimics enamel optical properties. The trade-off is slightly lower wear resistance — not an issue for anterior smile-zone restorations but disqualifies microfill for posterior load-bearing cases. **Renamel Microfill** (Cosmedent) is the long-standing reference brand.
Bulk-fill composites — for deep posterior boxes

items: **SonicFill 3 (Kerr)** — sonic-activated handling thins the composite during placement, then it stiffens after sonic cessation. Excellent adaptation to box walls. Cure 20 sec at 4 mm.
**Tetric Bulk Fill (Ivoclar)** — works with conventional placement; relies on modifier monomers for stress reduction. Cure 20 sec at 4 mm.
**Filtek Bulk Fill Posterior (3M)** — opaque shade only; capped with a 1–2 mm standard composite layer for esthetics. Cure 20 sec at 4 mm.
**SureFil SDR Flow+ (Dentsply)** — flowable bulk-fill, self-leveling. Use for the proximal box first 4 mm, then cap with standard composite. Cure 20 sec at 4 mm.
**Ketac Nano (3M)** — light-cure RMGI in capsule form (auto-mix). Bonds chemically to dentin, releases fluoride.
**Fuji II LC (GC)** — light-cure RMGI in powder/liquid or capsule form. Long-established reference brand for cervical and root-surface restorations.
**Fuji Triage (GC)** — high-viscosity glass ionomer for atypical-tooth or pediatric cases. Not the same as RMGI; lower polish but excellent fluoride release.

blocks.title: Shade selection systems
blocks.body: **Vita Classical** (A1, A2, A3, A3.5, A4, B1, B2...) is the original; intuitive for general use. **Vita 3D Master** (1M1, 2L2.5, 3R2.5...) separates value, chroma, and hue into independent axes; more precise for esthetic cases. Most composite manufacturers map their shades to both systems on the same shade guide. For anterior smile-zone work, use 3D Master; for typical posterior work, Vita Classical is sufficient.

blocks.text: Single-shade composites (Omnichroma, Essential Universal) use structural color or chameleon-effect optics to match a wide range of tooth shades from a single composite. Convenient for the inventory and useful in clinical contexts where shade-matching is challenging; trade-off is reduced precision for cases needing layered multi-shade build-up.

chapters.title: Common pitfalls — post-op sensitivity, marginal staining, open contacts
chapters.verdict: Three failure modes account for most direct-restoration callbacks — post-op sensitivity (often from over-etched dentin), marginal staining (under-polished or moisture contamination), and open proximal contacts (matrix or band issues). Knowing the cause for each makes the fix straightforward.

blocks.text: Most post-op sensitivity from direct restorations resolves within 1–2 weeks without intervention. The mechanism is usually fluid movement through dentinal tubules — either because the bond seal is incomplete, or because the dentin was over-etched and the smear layer was over-modified. Counsel the patient that mild sensitivity to cold and biting pressure is expected for up to two weeks. Sensitivity that worsens, that's spontaneous, or that lasts more than 3 weeks is a different problem and may need re-evaluation.

items: **Cold sensitivity lasting <30 sec after stimulus:** likely smear-layer modification with universal adhesive; will resolve in 2 weeks.
**Sensitivity to biting pressure:** check occlusion. A high spot, even one too small to see clinically, will cause biting pain. Adjust with articulating paper guidance.
**Cold sensitivity lasting >30 sec, or worsening over days:** consider pulpitis. Vitality test, take a periapical radiograph, may need to revisit with a sedative or proceed to endo if irreversible.
**Spontaneous pain at night:** irreversible pulpitis. The restoration was too close to the pulp; endodontic treatment is likely needed.

blocks.text: A line of brown stain at the cavosurface margin appears within 1–3 years on many composite restorations. The most common causes are under-polishing (residual oxygen-inhibited layer attracts stain), moisture contamination during bonding (microleakage at the margin), and patient diet (coffee, red wine, turmeric pigment). The clinical decision is whether the stain is cosmetic only or whether the margin is leaking — re-polish for cosmetic, replace for leaking.

items: **Stain line is superficial, doesn't catch a probe, doesn't extend internally on a periapical:** cosmetic. Re-polish with superfine disc + felt and paste. Counsel patient about diet and home polishing.
**Probe catches at the margin, or radiolucency extends along the margin radiographically:** the margin is leaking. Replace the restoration; do not just polish.
**Stain extends into the body of the restoration, not just the margin:** the composite itself stains (more common with bulk-fill composites). Re-polish the surface; if persistent, plan replacement.

blocks.text: An open or weak proximal contact is one of the few outright failures of a Class II that requires immediate revision. Floss should snap through the contact with resistance; if it falls through without contact, the restoration is wrong. The cause is almost always a matrix issue — flat Tofflemire band when a sectional was indicated, inadequate wedge placement, or premature ring removal before final cure. There is no salvaging an open contact; the case is a re-do.

items: **Floss snaps through with resistance:** contact is acceptable.
**Floss snaps but with a flat 'click' rather than resistance:** flat contact. Marginal; consider re-doing if the patient will be flossing in this area daily.
**Floss falls through with no resistance:** open contact. Re-do.
**Floss won't pass at all:** over-bulged proximal. Re-shape with a fine finishing strip until floss passes with resistance.
**Restoration debonds within months:** moisture contamination during bonding or under-cured adhesive. Re-bond with strict isolation.
**Composite chips at the marginal ridge:** under-cured at the surface or excessive load at the marginal ridge. Re-place the ridge area and verify occlusion.
**Color match looks off at delivery, looks better at 1 week:** desiccation effect from Isodry isolation. The tooth re-hydrates and the restoration blends. Counsel patient before delivery.
**Composite voids visible on a 6-month periapical:** under-condensed during placement. Cosmetic only if the voids are internal and not at the cavity wall; replace if voids extend to the cavity floor.

parts.label: Diagnosis & access
parts.tagline: The diagnostic foundation every endo case rests on, plus the rubber dam + access steps that come right after.

chapters.title: Pulp diagnosis — vital, reversible vs irreversible pulpitis, necrotic
chapters.verdict: Treatment for an endo case depends almost entirely on the pulp diagnosis. Cold-positive + non-lingering = reversible (no RCT); cold-positive + lingering or spontaneous = irreversible (RCT); cold-negative = necrotic (RCT, usually multi-visit). Get the diagnosis right first or every later step is built on sand.

blocks.text: Pulp diagnosis is a clinical exercise in differential pattern matching. You take five small data points - symptom history, cold test, EPT, percussion, palpation - and one larger one (radiograph), and you fit them into one of five named diagnoses. The diagnosis determines whether the tooth gets restorative treatment alone, vital pulp therapy, single-visit RCT, multi-visit RCT, or extraction.

items: **Normal pulp.** Asymptomatic. Cold response brief (1-2 sec), non-lingering. EPT positive. Percussion negative. Palpation negative. No periapical changes on radiograph. *No endo treatment indicated.*

**Reversible pulpitis.** Provoked sensitivity to cold or sweets that resolves within seconds of stimulus removal. No spontaneous pain. EPT positive. Percussion negative. No radiographic changes. *Treatment: remove the cause (caries, leaking restoration). Pulp will return to normal.*

**Symptomatic irreversible pulpitis.** Spontaneous pain (the patient wakes up at night), lingering response to cold (>10 sec after stimulus removal), often referred pain (patient can't localize). EPT positive but exaggerated. Percussion may be positive. Radiograph usually normal or shows minimal PDL widening. *Treatment: RCT or extraction.*

**Asymptomatic irreversible pulpitis.** Pulp is inflamed enough that it will not recover, but the patient feels nothing. Discovered on caries excavation (massive carious exposure with vital pulp) or radiographically (calcifications + caries to the pulp). *Treatment: RCT.*

**Pulp necrosis.** Pulp is dead. Cold-negative. EPT-negative. Often percussion-positive (periapical inflammation). May have a sinus tract, fistula, or fluctuant swelling. Radiograph shows periapical radiolucency in chronic cases. *Treatment: RCT (multi-visit if symptomatic, single-visit possible if asymptomatic and small lesion).*

blocks.text: Periapical diagnoses (the second half of the diagnosis)

Every pulp diagnosis is paired with a periapical diagnosis describing the state of the tissues at the apex. The pulp + periapical pair is the full diagnosis you document and that drives the treatment plan.

items: **Normal apical tissues.** Percussion negative, palpation negative, no radiographic changes. Pairs with normal pulp or reversible pulpitis.
**Symptomatic apical periodontitis.** Painful percussion or palpation. May or may not have radiographic changes. Pairs with irreversible pulpitis or necrosis.
**Asymptomatic apical periodontitis.** Radiographic periapical radiolucency, no symptoms. Pairs with pulp necrosis (chronic).

**Acute apical abscess.** Rapid onset of pain, swelling, possible pus drainage from sulcus or fistula. Tooth percussion-exquisitely-positive. Radiograph may not yet show changes (acute is too fast for bone resorption). Pairs with pulp necrosis.
**Chronic apical abscess.** Periapical radiolucency with an associated sinus tract or fistula draining pus. Patient often asymptomatic except for the fistula. Pairs with pulp necrosis.

**Condensing osteitis.** Radiographic radiopacity (not radiolucency) at the apex. Reactive bone formation from chronic low-grade pulpal inflammation. Pairs with chronic irreversible pulpitis or necrosis.

**Cold test (Endo Ice or refrigerant spray on a cotton pellet):** apply to mid-buccal of suspect tooth + a control tooth (same arch, opposite side). Time the response. Brief, non-lingering = normal/reversible; lingering >10 sec = irreversible; no response = necrotic or false-negative (recent restoration, full crown, calcified canal).

**Electric pulp test (EPT):** moisten the tip with toothpaste for conductivity, apply to enamel (NOT a restoration - false-positive from restoration alone). Patient holds the handle to close the circuit. Tingling = vital. No response at full scale = necrotic. EPT is binary (vital / not vital) - it doesn't grade inflammation.
**Percussion:** light tap with the mirror handle on occlusal/incisal surface. Compare to control tooth. Positive = inflammation in the PDL (apical or lateral).
**Palpation:** firm finger pressure on buccal mucosa over the apex. Compare to control. Positive = inflammation reached the buccal cortical plate.

**Bite stick test (Tooth Slooth):** patient bites on plastic cone over each cusp individually. Positive on release = cracked tooth syndrome. Differs from RCT-needing pain in that bite-test pain is positional (on release, on certain cusps) not constant.

**Radiograph (PA):** look for PDL widening, periapical radiolucency, internal/external resorption, calcifications, root anatomy. Two angulations (straight + 20 deg mesial shift) for premolars and molars to detect second canals.

**Referred pain.** Pain location often doesn't match the involved tooth, especially for maxillary molars (can refer to mandibular molars on the same side). Cold-test multiple teeth, not just the one the patient points to.

**False-negative EPT.** Calcified canals (older patients, previously traumatized teeth) can give cold-negative + EPT-negative responses even with vital pulp. Cross-check with the radiograph for canal calcification before concluding necrosis.
**False-positive EPT through metal.** Touching the probe to a metal restoration or crown gives a positive response from the metal, not the pulp. Place the probe on exposed enamel only.

**Recent trauma.** Teeth that were avulsed, luxated, or had trauma in the past 4-6 weeks can give transient false-negative responses while the nerve recovers. Re-test in 2-4 weeks before concluding necrosis.
**Multiple teeth, same quadrant.** Periodontal abscess, cracked tooth, and pulpal pathology can all coexist. The bite-stick test + isolated cold test on individual teeth narrows it down.

blocks.title: If the diagnosis still isn't clear
blocks.body: **All tests inconclusive but patient is in pain:** ask the patient to come back when the pain is active. Tests during a pain-free interval often miss the diagnosis. **Periapical lesion present but tooth tests vital:** the lesion isn't endo-origin. Consider periodontal lesion, lateral canal pathology, or non-endo lesions (cyst, tumor) - get a CBCT. **Tooth tests vital but patient has clearly endo-pattern pain:** consider a missed canal (already-treated tooth that's necrotic in a missed MB2), or referred pain from an adjacent tooth. **Cold-positive on a tooth with a deep restoration but lingering:** could be reversible pulpitis from recent placement (gives time for the pulp to settle, re-test in 4-6 weeks) OR irreversible (proceed to RCT if not improving). Document the timeline and re-evaluate.

chapters.title: Rubber dam - the one and only endo isolation method (no Isodry)
chapters.verdict: Rubber dam is mandatory for every endodontic procedure. Isodry does not provide the chemical seal needed for sodium hypochlorite irrigation, and a NaOCl leak onto mucosa is a chemical burn. The dam isolates the tooth, protects the patient's airway from instruments and irrigants, and keeps the field dry for adhesive procedures.

blocks.text: Rubber dam isolation is non-negotiable in endodontics. Three reasons drive this rule: (1) hypochlorite irrigation will burn oral mucosa on contact - severe enough to cause weeks of healing and rare cases of permanent paresthesia; (2) endodontic files are small and easily swallowed or aspirated without isolation; (3) the canal must be dry for obturation, and a single drop of saliva contaminates a sealer bond. Isodry, cotton rolls, and high-volume suction cannot replicate the chemical and airway seal of a properly placed rubber dam.

items: **Rubber dam sheet:** medium-gauge latex (Hygenic, Sanctuary Health) or latex-free (Hygenic Non-Latex) for allergic patients. 6" x 6" pre-punched or full sheet to punch chairside.
**Rubber dam clamp:** anterior (Brinker B4, W8A); premolar (W2, W2A); molar (#26, #56, W56). One winged clamp simplifies retraction; wingless clamps grip more tenaciously on broken-down teeth.
**Clamp forceps:** Brewer or Stokes. The 'beaks-down' position holds the clamp open; releasing the forceps closes it on the tooth.
**Rubber dam frame:** Young's frame (plastic, radiolucent - preferred for endo) or Nygaard-Ostby frame (metal).
**Hole punch:** dental hole punch with 5 hole sizes. Use the smallest that fits the tooth.
**Floss:** thin floss (Glide) to assist passing the dam over contacts.
**OraSeal / Cavit / wax:** caulking to seal any leaks at the dam-tooth interface.
**Confirm anesthesia.** Rubber dam clamping pressure can hurt - profound anesthesia first.

**Pre-check the tooth:** circumferentially probe the gingival margin to confirm there's enough sound tooth structure above the gum for the clamp to grip without slipping. If broken-down to the gum-line, plan for a buildup or orthodontic extrusion before endo.
**Select the clamp.** Try it in dry first to confirm it grips. Most-common mistake: clamp too small slips off; clamp too large pinches the gingiva.
**Floss the contacts** mesial and distal to the tooth - confirms they're open enough for the dam to pass without ripping.
**Punch the hole** in the dam sheet, sized to the tooth. Position the hole so the clamp wings won't tear the dam - usually 1/3 of the way down from the top edge for a maxillary tooth.

**Choose the technique:** (a) clamp-first - secure the clamp on the tooth, then stretch the dam over the clamp wings; (b) dam-first - pre-mount the clamp on the dam, then carry both as a unit; (c) split-dam - punch one hole, use a wingless clamp, drape the dam after clamping.
**Stretch the dam onto the frame.** The dam should be taut but not so tight it pulls off the clamp.
**Pass the dam through the contacts** with floss. Pull the dam interproximally on both sides; floss helps it tuck under contact points without ripping.

**Squirt water from a 3-way syringe** onto the dam surface around the clamped tooth. Look for water beading up on the dam (good seal) vs water running down under the dam at the gingival margin (leak).

**Common leak sites:** the gingival aspect of the clamp and the interproximal contacts. Caulk leaks with OraSeal applied with a microbrush. For stubborn leaks, place a small bead of Cavit at the leak point.
**Test again** after caulking. The seal must hold against the volume and pressure of hypochlorite irrigation.

blocks.text: Before drilling, scrub the isolated tooth surface with sodium hypochlorite or chlorhexidine for 30 sec. The dam-isolated surface is now sterile; bacteria from saliva that contacted the tooth before isolation are killed.

blocks.title: Common dam problems
blocks.body: **Dam keeps slipping off the clamp:** the clamp size is wrong (too small) or the clamp is broken. Replace. **Clamp keeps slipping off the tooth:** sound tooth structure is inadequate; build up the tooth with bonded composite before endo. **Dam tears at the punched hole:** hole was too small. Re-punch larger. **Patient feels the clamp pinching:** anesthesia hasn't reached the marginal gingiva - give buccal infiltration. **Bleeding from marginal gingiva under the clamp:** clamp jaws are biting tissue. Reposition or build the tooth up first. **NaOCl leak onto the patient's lip or cheek:** stop, irrigate with sterile saline immediately, apply vitamin E oil, document, recall at 24-48 hours. Most chemical burns heal in 7-10 days.

chapters.title: Access prep - finding the orifice, axis correction
chapters.verdict: Access prep is the single step that most determines RCT outcome. Get the access right and the rest of the procedure flows. Get it wrong (offset, under-extended, missed canal) and you're fighting the case for the next 90 minutes. The outline depends on the tooth: triangle on lingual of anteriors, oval on occlusal of premolars, rhomboid on occlusal of molars.

blocks.text: Access prep has two simultaneous goals: (1) remove the entire roof of the pulp chamber so all canal orifices are visible, and (2) create straight-line access from the access cavity to the apex of each canal so files can drop in without binding on residual coronal dentin. A common error is stopping when the first orifice is found and not deroofing the chamber fully - this hides additional canals (MB2 in maxillary molars) and forces files to curve around residual dentin (file separation risk).

items: **Anterior (#7-10 maxillary, #23-26 mandibular):** triangle on the lingual surface, base toward the incisal edge, apex at the cingulum. Single canal.
**Canine (#6, #11, #22, #27):** narrow triangle or oval, longer mesiodistally, on the lingual. Single canal but the chamber is deeper than incisors.
**Maxillary premolar (#4, #5, #12, #13):** oval, mesiodistally narrow + buccolingually long. Two canals 60-90% of the time (buccal + palatal).
**Mandibular premolar (#21, #28):** small oval or round, central groove. Usually one canal; #21 occasionally has a lingual bifurcation.
**Maxillary molar (#3, #14):** rhomboid - palatal corner, MB corner, DB corner. Hunt for MB2 mesially to MB orifice. 60-90% have MB2.
**Mandibular molar (#19, #30):** rounded triangle or rhomboid, mesial wider. MB + ML + (usually one) distal.

blocks.text: Remove all caries and any existing restoration BEFORE making the formal access cavity. Restorative material masks the pulp chamber location, and caries-affected dentin near the pulp can hide a perforation.

items: **Use a #4 round bur on slow speed** for initial penetration. Slow-speed gives tactile feedback when you drop into the chamber.
**Drill perpendicular to the surface first** (lingual on anteriors, occlusal on posteriors), then redirect to follow the long axis of the root.
**You'll feel a 'drop' or hear a change in pitch** when the bur penetrates the roof of the pulp chamber. Stop immediately.
**Confirm penetration with an endodontic explorer (DG-16).** The DG-16 tip should drop into the chamber without resistance.
**Switch to a Endo Z bur (safety-tip carbide) on high-speed** with copious water spray. Engage the chamber walls and lift the chamber roof off.
**Outline form expansion:** trace the chamber outline with the bur to remove all residual roof. Test with explorer.
**Critical check:** flood the chamber with NaOCl 5%. Look for bubbles from the orifices - that's pulp tissue dissolving.
**Identify orifices** with the DG-16. Each canal orifice has a distinct color and tactile catch - slightly darker than surrounding dentin, slightly sticky to the explorer.
**Set up magnification:** microscope or 3.5x loupes with a headlamp. MB2 is rarely visible without.
**Drop the DG-16 explorer 1-2 mm mesial to the MB orifice.** MB2 is usually under a calcified shelf in this position.
**Trough mesially with a #2 round bur or LN bur** on slow speed, removing 1-2 mm of dentin at a time. Re-explore with DG-16 after each pass.

**The orifice is found when the apex locator catches** when an apex locator-coupled file is inserted. If you've troughed 3 mm without finding it, MB2 may be absent (10-40% of maxillary first molars don't have MB2).

blocks.title: Common access problems
blocks.body: **Can't find the chamber:** the chamber is calcified. Switch to a #2 long-neck round bur and aim for the radiographic chamber location on the PA. If still no penetration after 5 minutes, get a CBCT. **Perforated the floor of the chamber:** stop immediately. Seal with MTA or Biodentine. Continue endo if the perforation is small (<2 mm). **Found the first orifice but the patient has 3+ canal anatomy:** keep deroofing and exploring. Missed canals are the #1 cause of RCT failure in molars. **Access cavity too small to allow file movement:** widen with the Endo Z bur.

chapters.title: Cleaning & shaping - hand files, rotaries (ProTaper / WaveOne), irrigation
chapters.verdict: Cleaning means killing the bacteria and removing the necrotic debris; shaping means creating the geometric form (apical 1/3 narrow, coronal 1/3 wide) that allows obturation to seal. Sodium hypochlorite is the irrigant that does the killing; the files create the path. Modern protocols pair hand-file scouting with rotary NiTi shaping plus copious irrigation.

blocks.text: Modern endodontics has three mainstream approaches to canal shaping: hand files (K-files, Hedstrom), rotary nickel-titanium systems (ProTaper Gold, WaveOne Gold), and reciprocating motion files. Pre-doctoral cases typically use a hybrid: hand files for early scouting + apex location, then rotaries for the shaping. The same irrigation protocol applies to all three: 6 mL sodium hypochlorite between every file size, 17% EDTA at the end of shaping for smear layer removal.
Step 1 - Scouting (hand files, 5-10 min per canal)

items: **Pre-curve a #10 K-file** with a sterile gauze pad. Slight apical curve replicates the canal's anatomy and prevents transportation.
**Insert the #10 until it binds.** Note depth on the file rubber stop. Confirm with apex locator: tip should be 0.5-1 mm short of the apical foramen.
**Take a working-length radiograph** with the file in place. Working length = apex locator reading - 0.5 mm (safety margin).
**Continue with #15, then #20 K-files** to working length. Each file should rotate ~90 degrees clockwise then withdraw - the watch-winding motion.
**If a file binds before working length is reached:** irrigate with 6 mL NaOCl, recapitulate with a smaller file (e.g., back to #10), then re-advance the original size.

blocks.text: Step 2 - Rotary shaping (ProTaper Gold sequence, 15-20 min per canal)

items: **Set rotary handpiece to 300 rpm + 5.2 N-cm torque** (ProTaper Gold manufacturer setting).
**S1 (shaper #1):** orange handle. Insert into the canal coronal-to-apical with light apical pressure. The file should advance with finger pressure alone - never push.
**S2 (shaper #2):** purple handle. Same insertion. S2 widens the coronal 1/3.
**F1 (finishing #1):** yellow handle. Take to working length. F1 sets the apical preparation to a #20/0.07 taper. Confirm WL with apex locator.
**F2 (finishing #2):** red handle. Take to working length. F2 expands the apex to #25/0.08 taper. Most pre-doctoral cases stop at F2.
**Between every file:** irrigate with 6 mL NaOCl using a side-vented needle inserted to 2 mm short of WL. Let the NaOCl sit in the canal for 30-60 seconds between irrigation cycles.
**After final shaping, irrigate with 17% EDTA** for 60 seconds. EDTA dissolves the smear layer.
**Final NaOCl rinse** (3 mL, 30 seconds).
**Dry the canal with paper points** sized to working length. Paper points should come out dry.
**Single drop of CHX 2% (optional but recommended for necrotic cases)** for final disinfection. Dry again with fresh paper points.

blocks.title: Cleaning & shaping problems
blocks.body: **File binds and rotates without advancing ('screwing in'):** apical pressure is too high. Withdraw, irrigate, advance with lighter pressure. **Working length keeps decreasing across instrumentation:** debris is packing apically. Recapitulate with the smallest file (#10), irrigate thoroughly, then resume. **Apex locator readings are erratic:** the canal isn't dry (NaOCl conducts) or the file is touching a metal restoration. Wipe the file dry, isolate from metallic contact, retest. **File separates in the canal:** see endo-ch20 chapter. **Persistent bleeding from a canal during instrumentation:** the file is past the apex (over-instrumented). Pull back 0.5 mm, irrigate, place Ca(OH)2 paste, close the visit and obturate at the next appointment.

chapters.title: Working length determination - apex locator + radiograph
chapters.verdict: Working length defines where the obturation ends - 0.5-1 mm short of the apical foramen, at the apical constriction. Get it right and the seal is biologically sound. Get it short (under-instrumented) and bacteria persist; get it long (over-instrumented) and you damage periapical tissues. Apex locator + PA radiograph together establish working length to within 0.5 mm of true position.

blocks.text: Working length (WL) is the distance from a coronal reference point (cusp tip or incisal edge) to the apical end of canal instrumentation. The target is the apical constriction - the narrowest point of the canal, typically 0.5-1 mm coronal to the radiographic apex. Modern apex locators (Root ZX, Apex ID) use the canal's electrical impedance to identify this point with 90-95% accuracy when used correctly.

items: **Apex locator:** Morita Root ZX II or similar. 9V battery (check before each procedure).
**K-files #08, #10, #15** for working length scouting.
**Rubber stops** for file length marking.
**PA radiograph capability** (sensor + computer at chairside).
**Measure the tooth on the pre-op PA radiograph** with a digital ruler (incisal/occlusal tip to radiographic apex). Record this as the 'estimated working length' (EWL).
**Subtract 1 mm** from EWL for safety. This is the file length you'll insert initially.
**Set the rubber stop on a #10 K-file** at the EWL minus 1 mm.
**Connect the lip clip** to the patient's lower lip. Apex locator needs a body-circuit reference.
**Insert the file holder clip** onto the metal shank of the K-file (not the handle).
**Insert the K-file** to the EWL minus 1 mm depth.

**Watch the apex locator display.** Most show a 'circles' or 'bar' display: as you advance the file, indicators move toward the apex. Display reaches '0.5' (or APEX, depending on device) when the file tip is at the apical foramen.
**Withdraw 0.5 mm** from the 'APEX' reading. This is your apex locator working length (ALWL).
**Document ALWL on the chart.** This is your reference for every subsequent file.
**Place a #15 or #20 K-file** at the ALWL.
**Take a PA radiograph** with the file in place.
**Measure on the radiograph:** the file tip should be 0.5-1 mm short of the radiographic apex.
**If file is too long (past the apex):** reduce ALWL by the over-extension amount and re-set the rubber stop on subsequent files.
**If file is too short (>2 mm from radiographic apex):** the apex locator may have read prematurely. Re-take with a slightly longer length and verify.

blocks.text: Working length can change during instrumentation. As coronal dentin is removed, the file 'shortens' relative to the patient's tooth - meaning the apical constriction is effectively further away. Re-verify ALWL after every 2-3 file size changes, especially in molars.

blocks.title: Working length problems
blocks.body: **Apex locator gives erratic readings:** likely causes: canal isn't dry enough (NaOCl conducts current); file is touching a metal restoration; lip clip not contacting moist mucosa; calcified canal hasn't been opened yet to allow electrical contact. **Apex locator reads 'APEX' at unexpectedly short depth:** the file is probably touching a lateral canal or accessory canal opening. Re-position the file along the long axis; try again. **PA confirms file is past the apex despite normal apex locator reading:** check that the lip clip is making good mucosal contact. Faulty contact gives premature 'APEX' readings. **Pediatric patient with open apex:** apex locators are unreliable in immature teeth. Rely on PA radiograph alone; use a periapical bleeding-zone technique for vital pulp therapy cases instead of full instrumentation.

chapters.title: Obturation - single cone vs warm vertical, sealer choice
chapters.verdict: Obturation is the one-shot final step. The goal: fill the canal three-dimensionally with no voids, sealed against bacterial re-entry from the coronal restoration. Single cone with bioceramic sealer is the modern default (simpler, fewer steps). Warm vertical compaction is the traditional gold standard (better adaptation to canal anatomy). Both work; technique matters more than which you choose.

blocks.text: Obturation seals the cleaned and shaped canal against bacterial recontamination. The seal has two components: the cone of solid gutta-percha (mass) and a sealer (the chemical bond between gutta-percha and dentin walls). Most pre-doctoral cases use single cone obturation with a bioceramic sealer like BC Sealer or AH Plus. Warm vertical compaction (System B + Obtura) is the traditional alternative, more technique-sensitive but with proven outcomes.

items: **Gutta-percha cones:** matching the rotary file size (e.g., #25/0.08 master cone if F2 was the final shaping file).
**Bioceramic sealer:** EndoSequence BC Sealer, BioRoot RCS, or AH Plus (epoxy-based, older but proven).
**Paper points** for canal drying (sized to working length).
**Lentulo spiral or syringe applicator** for sealer placement.
**Heat source** if doing warm vertical (System B touch & heat, or EndoActivator with heated tip).
**Obtura backfill gun** (if doing warm vertical with thermoplasticized gutta-percha).
**Pluggers** (Schilder, hand pluggers in various sizes) for vertical compaction.

blocks.text: Single cone with bioceramic sealer (most common pre-doctoral)

items: **Dry the canal thoroughly** with paper points. Last paper point should come out completely dry.

**Try-in the master cone.** Insert the matching-size GP cone to working length. Should feel slight tug-back resistance (apical seat). If too loose - go up a size; if won't reach WL - drag-down a size or re-instrument apically.
**Take a master cone radiograph** to confirm WL placement.

**Apply sealer** to the canal walls. Bioceramic sealer can be syringed in (BC Sealer syringe) or applied with a lentulo spiral at slow speed (rotate clockwise to push sealer apically, then withdraw while rotating slowly).
**Coat the master cone** in sealer.
**Insert master cone slowly** to working length. Don't push fast - it traps air and creates voids.
**Sear off the GP** 1-2 mm below the canal orifice with a heated instrument.
**Compact vertically** with a plugger - 2-3 firm seats.
**Dry canal, master cone try-in, sealer placement, master cone insertion** - same as above.
**Apical seal:** sear off the master cone 4 mm short of working length with the System B heat plugger. Heat-soften, compact apically with the matching cold plugger to set the apical 3-4 mm of GP.
**Backfill:** use the Obtura gun to inject thermoplasticized GP into the empty middle and coronal canal. Pause every 2-3 mm to compact with a cold plugger.
**Final compaction** of the coronal GP to ensure no voids.
**Take a final radiograph** to verify three-dimensional obturation.
**Clean the access cavity** of all GP and sealer remnants on the chamber walls.
**Place definitive coronal seal:** glass-ionomer (Vitrebond, Fuji II LC) over the canal orifice + composite or amalgam buildup, OR Cavit + amalgam if scheduled for crown in 1-2 weeks.
**Definitive restoration within 4 weeks** of obturation. Coronal leakage is the #1 cause of long-term RCT failure.
**Schedule recall** at 6 months and 1 year for PA + clinical exam. Look for periapical healing on radiograph.

blocks.title: Obturation problems
blocks.body: **Master cone won't reach working length:** debris is blocking the apex; the apical shape isn't smooth; or the cone is too large. Recapitulate, re-irrigate, try a smaller cone. **Master cone has no apical tug-back (slides too easily):** the apical shape is too wide. Drag-down a size or two; or trim the cone tip to fit. **Sealer extruded past apex on radiograph:** small extrusion is acceptable (bioceramic and AH Plus are biocompatible). Large extrusions may cause transient post-op discomfort but rarely cause persistent symptoms. **Air voids visible on PA:** insufficient compaction or sealer application. Don't re-treat for cosmetic voids; do re-treat if voids extend to a canal wall (allows bacterial re-entry).

chapters.title: Necrotic pulp - pus management, calcium hydroxide intracanal medicament
chapters.verdict: Necrotic pulp + periapical lesion = two-visit RCT with calcium hydroxide between. Drain the canal first (pus comes out during access), then thoroughly clean and shape, then place Ca(OH)2 paste for 2-4 weeks to reduce the bacterial load, then obturate at the second visit when symptoms have resolved. Antibiotics ONLY for systemic involvement.

blocks.text: Necrotic pulp cases differ from vital pulp RCTs in three ways: (1) the canal contains bacterial necrotic debris, not just inflamed but living pulp; (2) the periapical tissues are inflamed or infected, with potential acute swelling or fistula; (3) single-visit obturation has lower success rate because residual bacteria can survive the cleaning + shaping. The standard protocol is two-visit treatment with calcium hydroxide as intracanal medicament between visits.

items: **Cold test:** NEGATIVE. Necrotic pulp doesn't respond to cold. (Recently-traumatized teeth can be transiently cold-negative even when vital - re-test in 4-6 weeks if injury was recent.)
**EPT:** NEGATIVE. No electrical response.
**Percussion:** often POSITIVE (acute or chronic apical periodontitis). The periodontium is inflamed.
**Palpation:** may be positive if there's a buccal fluctuant area or sinus tract.
**Radiograph:** may show periapical radiolucency (chronic), PDL widening (acute), or condensing osteitis (rare reactive pattern).
**Sinus tract or fistula:** trace with a gutta-percha point inserted into the fistula and PA radiograph to confirm the origin tooth.

**Anesthetize** (necrotic pulps don't need anesthesia for the pulp itself, but soft tissues do - especially the marginal gingiva and apical mucosa for any subsequent percussion testing or rubber dam clamp).
**Place rubber dam.** Mandatory - NaOCl will be used.
**Access prep.** Be careful - the chamber may contain pus under pressure that releases when you penetrate. Have suction ready.
**If pus drains:** let it flow until the pressure releases. Don't aggressively curette or push debris apically. Irrigate with NaOCl 5% to flush the canal.
**Establish working length** with apex locator + PA. Standard technique (see endo-ch3).

**Clean & shape to F2 (or equivalent) with copious irrigation.** Necrotic cases need MORE irrigation than vital - 8-10 mL NaOCl between every file. The bacterial load is higher, and the file mechanically alone doesn't kill bacteria.
**Final irrigation:** 17% EDTA for 60 sec, NaOCl rinse, 2% CHX for 30 sec (extra disinfection step in necrotic cases).
**Dry the canal** with paper points.
**Place Ca(OH)2 paste** (UltraCal, Vitapex) with a syringe to working length. Compact with paper points. The paste fills the canal and sits against the dentin walls.
**Seal with Cavit + IRM or temporary glass-ionomer.** Cavit alone leaks; combine with IRM for a more robust seal.
**Schedule visit 2 at 2-4 weeks.**

blocks.text: Ca(OH)2 has high pH (~12.5) that creates an antibacterial environment in the canal. It dissolves residual necrotic tissue, neutralizes bacterial endotoxins, and promotes periapical healing. Some bacteria (Enterococcus faecalis, fungi) are relatively resistant to Ca(OH)2, but the overall bacterial load is significantly reduced over a 2-4 week dwell.

items: **Confirm symptoms have resolved** (no spontaneous pain, percussion-negative or much improved, no sinus tract). If symptoms persist, re-evaluate: missed canal? Vertical root fracture? Inadequate irrigation last visit? Consider extra cleaning + another 2 weeks of Ca(OH)2 before obturation.
**Re-place rubber dam, re-access.**
**Remove the Ca(OH)2** with copious NaOCl irrigation + ultrasonic activation if available. Important - residual Ca(OH)2 reduces obturation sealer adhesion.
**Re-instrument lightly** (one size up from the previous master file, e.g., F3 if you stopped at F2). This refreshes the canal walls and ensures no debris remains.
**Final irrigation sequence:** NaOCl - EDTA - NaOCl - CHX - dry.
**Obturate** with master cone + sealer (see endo-ch5).
**Coronal seal:** definitive restoration within 4 weeks.
**Antibiotics NOT indicated for:** localized periapical abscess that drains, asymptomatic chronic periapical lesion, sinus tract without spreading infection.
**Antibiotics ARE indicated for:** spreading cellulitis, fever >100.4F, lymphadenopathy, trismus, immunocompromised patients (diabetes, transplant, chemo).
**First-line:** Pen V K 500 mg QID x 7 days OR amoxicillin 500 mg TID x 7 days (BID dosing easier compliance).
**Penicillin-allergic:** clindamycin 300 mg QID x 7 days OR azithromycin 500 mg load + 250 mg QD x 4 days.
**Always document:** indication for antibiotics, drug + dose, duration. Antibiotic over-prescription contributes to resistance.

blocks.title: Necrotic pulp problems
blocks.body: **Pus drains continuously after access - won't stop:** leave the tooth open for 24-48 hours ONLY if the patient cannot tolerate having the tooth sealed (severe pain that doesn't respond to pressure on the pus pocket). Most modern protocols close at first visit. If left open, close at 24-48 hours regardless. **Symptoms recur after Ca(OH)2 placement:** missed canal (MB2 in maxillary molars, second canal in premolars), inadequate cleaning, or vertical root fracture. Re-evaluate at second visit. **No periapical healing at 6-month recall:** lesion should reduce in size by 6 months. If unchanged or larger, consider endo retreatment or apico referral. **Fistula doesn't close after first visit:** common - fistulas can take weeks to close. Recheck at second visit; should be smaller. If persistent at 3 months post-obturation, evaluate for retreatment.

parts.label: Workflow fundamentals
parts.tagline: The atraumatic technique that should be the default for every extraction in pre-doctoral clinic - slow, controlled, bone-preserving.

chapters.title: Atraumatic extraction technique
chapters.verdict: An atraumatic extraction protects the alveolar bone, minimizes flap reflection, and respects the periodontal ligament - preserves the socket for an implant 3-6 months down the line. The workflow is periotome first, elevator second, forceps last. Most pre-doctoral extraction errors come from skipping straight to forceps and rocking aggressively.

blocks.text: Atraumatic extraction is not a single technique - it's an attitude that prioritizes preserving alveolar bone for future restorative work (implant, RPD, bridge) over speed. Every step is designed to expand the socket gradually and sever the periodontal ligament progressively so the tooth releases under controlled force rather than fracturing the bone. The 3-step sequence - periotome, elevator, forceps - is the spine.

items: **Block + supplemental as needed.** Mandibular molars: IA block + buccal infiltration + long buccal (for soft tissue). Maxillary anteriors and premolars: buccal infiltration + palatal infiltration. Maxillary molars: PSA + buccal + palatal infiltration.

**Test profound anesthesia BEFORE starting.** Probe gingiva for sharp sensation, NOT just touch (touch returns first). Use a 17 explorer in the gingival sulcus - if the patient feels sharp, give more anesthetic and wait.
**Cold-test the tooth one final time.** Cold should produce no sensation. If the patient feels cold, the pulp isn't anesthetized; supplement intraligamentary or intraseptal injection.

blocks.text: Step 2 - Periotome use (5-10 min, the most underused step)

The periotome is a thin, sharp instrument that cuts the periodontal ligament fibers and expands the socket. It's the difference between a tooth that releases with finger pressure and one you have to muscle out. Use it BEFORE the elevator, on every extraction, no exceptions.

items: **Insert the periotome along the long axis of the root** at the mesial line angle, with the blade against the root surface (NOT against the bone). Apply gentle apical pressure with slight side-to-side wiggle.
**Walk it circumferentially** around the tooth - mesial, buccal, distal, palatal/lingual. Each insertion goes 2-3 mm deep, slowly expanding the socket.
**Re-insert deeper** with each pass. After 3-4 passes around the tooth, you should be 4-6 mm apical along the root. The tooth begins to feel slightly mobile.
**Avoid the lingual on lower posteriors** if the lingual plate is thin (mandibular molars often have paper-thin lingual bone - risk of plate fracture if instrumented aggressively).
**Time check:** if you've been periotoming for 10 minutes and the tooth isn't loose, switch to the elevator. Some teeth (ankylosed, dilacerated roots) won't yield to periotome alone.

**Use a small straight elevator** (Cogswell A or #301) for initial luxation. Insert at the mesial line angle, blade against the tooth's cervical area, fulcrum on the alveolar bone (NOT the adjacent tooth).
**Rotate the elevator slightly** to apply rotational force to the tooth. The PDL on the mesial side fails progressively under controlled torque. Repeat from the distal line angle.
**For multi-rooted teeth,** transition to a larger elevator (Apexo, Crane pick) once initial mobility is established. Engage the furcation and lift one root at a time.
**Avoid the adjacent tooth as a fulcrum.** Repeated elevator pressure against the next tooth can cause it to develop mobility or pulpal pain. Always fulcrum on bone.
**Stop and reassess if the tooth fractures.** Don't continue to elevate fragments - switch to surgical technique (flap, bone removal).

**Choose the right forceps:** maxillary anteriors = #150; maxillary premolars = #150 or #150A; maxillary molars = #53R / #53L (palatal grip) or #88R / #88L; mandibular anteriors = #151; mandibular premolars = #151 or #151A; mandibular molars = #23 cowhorn (engages furcation) or #17 universal molar.
**Seat the forceps as apically as possible** - engage the cervical area of the root, NOT just the crown. Crown engagement = fracture risk.

**Apply slow, sustained buccolingual rocking** for posterior teeth (or rotational for anterior teeth with conical roots). The goal is to continue expanding the socket the periotome started, not to lift the tooth out.

**Once the tooth moves freely, deliver in the direction of least resistance** - usually buccally for maxillary teeth, buccally or occlusally for mandibular teeth, palatally for maxillary anteriors with palatal-curved roots.
**Inspect the tooth after delivery** - confirm the apex is intact. A broken apex requires retrieval (small curette in the socket) or referral if deep.

**Inspect the socket** with good lighting and the suction tip. Look for retained granulation tissue (chronic infection - needs curettage), root fragments (retrieval needed), or sharp bone edges (need filing).
**Curette gently** with a small spoon curette if granulation tissue is present. Don't over-curette - that removes the periosteum and slows healing.
**Irrigate with sterile saline** (20 mL syringe with a blunt-tip cannula). This removes debris and confirms the socket is clean.
**Compress the buccal and lingual plates** firmly with finger pressure for 30 seconds. This restores socket morphology if expansion occurred during delivery.
**Place a gelatin sponge** (Gelfoam, Surgicel) ONLY if bleeding doesn't stop with pressure, or if the socket will be grafted for future implant. Otherwise, allow natural clot formation.
**Pressure pack with gauze** for 30 minutes - patient bites firmly, no spitting, no rinsing.

blocks.text: Cover the next 24-72 hours of expected experience. The detailed post-op chapter has dosing and timing; the key points at chairside are: bleeding is expected and stops with pressure, no spitting / rinsing / straws / smoking for 24 hours (clot disruption = dry socket), and ibuprofen scheduled (not PRN) for the first 48 hours. Schedule recall in 7-10 days unless the patient is symptomatic earlier.

blocks.title: When the technique runs into trouble
blocks.body: **Tooth fractures during luxation:** stop. Get a radiograph to see what's retained. If the fragment is small and in the apical third, consider conversion to surgical extraction (flap + bone removal). **Adjacent tooth becomes mobile after elevator force:** apologize, document, recall in 2 weeks for vitality testing. Most cases recover; persistent mobility may need a splint. **Lingual plate fractures during mandibular molar extraction:** common with paper-thin lingual plates. Remove the fractured fragment with the tooth (it's already mobilized), smooth the edges. **Buccal plate fractures during maxillary anterior extraction:** more concerning - can affect future implant placement. Save the fragment if loose, smooth edges, consider socket preservation graft. **Sinus communication noticed after maxillary molar extraction:** test with the Valsalva maneuver (patient closes nose, gently blows - air through socket = communication). Small (2-3 mm) = primary closure, antibiotics, sinus precautions; large (>5 mm) = referral to OMFS for buccal advancement flap.

chapters.title: Pre-op assessment - medical history, anticoagulants, bisphosphonate risk
chapters.verdict: The pre-op assessment is what separates a routine extraction from a hospital visit. Three medical-history red flags: anticoagulants (bleeding management), bisphosphonates (MRONJ risk), and cardiac/transplant patients (antibiotic prophylaxis). Get these wrong and the patient bleeds for hours, develops osteonecrosis, or comes back with endocarditis.

blocks.text: Every extraction starts with a structured medical-history review focused on three categories: bleeding risk, bone-healing risk, and infection-defense risk. A 10-minute pre-op interview prevents 90% of post-op emergencies.

items: **Warfarin (Coumadin):** INR target depends on indication. INR <=3.5 is generally safe for routine extractions with local hemostasis. INR >3.5 - consult physician. **Never** stop warfarin without physician consult.

**Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) - Eliquis, Xarelto, Pradaxa, Savaysa:** continue for routine single-tooth extractions; local hemostasis sufficient. For multiple extractions, consult physician about timing.
**Antiplatelets - aspirin, clopidogrel (Plavix), prasugrel (Effient), ticagrelor (Brilinta):** continue for routine extractions. Local hemostasis is usually adequate.
**Supplements that increase bleeding:** fish oil, ginkgo biloba, garlic, vitamin E, ginger. Ask explicitly.
**Bleeding disorders:** hemophilia, von Willebrand disease, ITP, liver disease. Require hospital-based extraction with hematology consult.

**Oral bisphosphonates** (alendronate/Fosamax, risedronate/Actonel, ibandronate/Boniva): MRONJ risk ~0.1-0.4%. Risk increases with: >4 years duration, concurrent corticosteroid use, diabetes, smoking.

**IV bisphosphonates** (pamidronate/Aredia, zoledronate/Reclast/Zometa): MRONJ risk 1-15% after dental surgery. Discuss alternatives to extraction (RCT, restoration, partial pulp therapy) when possible.
**Denosumab** (Prolia, Xgeva): RANKL inhibitor with similar MRONJ risk profile to IV bisphosphonates.

**Pre-op management:** consult oncologist/PCP. Surgical technique modifications: minimal trauma, primary closure of socket, antibiotic coverage (amoxicillin 7-day course), 1-week and 2-week follow-up.

blocks.text: AHA 2021 guidelines: prophylaxis indicated for: prosthetic cardiac valves, history of infective endocarditis, congenital heart disease (unrepaired cyanotic CHD; completely repaired CHD with prosthetic material within 6 months; repaired CHD with residual defects), cardiac transplant patients with valvulopathy. Orthopedic implants (knee, hip, shoulder replacements) DO NOT require prophylaxis except in specific high-risk situations per the patient's orthopedic surgeon.

items: **Standard regimen:** amoxicillin 2 g PO 30-60 min before procedure.
**Penicillin allergy (non-anaphylactic):** cephalexin 2 g, OR clindamycin 600 mg (clindamycin no longer first-line per 2021 update due to C. difficile risk).
**Penicillin allergy (anaphylactic):** azithromycin 500 mg or clarithromycin 500 mg.
**Diabetes (HbA1c):** poorly controlled (HbA1c >8) = delayed healing + infection risk. Schedule morning, post-meal, consider antibiotic coverage.
**Pregnancy:** elective procedures in second trimester. Lidocaine + epi safe (Category B). Avoid bupivacaine in third trimester.
**Immunosuppression** (transplant, chemo, biologics, high-dose steroids): consult treating physician. Often need prophylactic antibiotics.
**Recent MI or stroke (within 6 months):** defer elective procedures. Emergency only after cardiology/neurology consult.
**Hypertension (uncontrolled):** SBP >180 or DBP >110 = postpone elective; refer to PCP. SBP 160-179 = manage anxiety + epi-restricted anesthesia.

blocks.title: When to defer or refer
blocks.body: **Refer to OMFS when:** root tips overlap the IAN canal on PA; sinus communication anticipated for maxillary molars; third molars beyond simple eruption (deep impaction, distal angulation, IAN proximity); patient on IV bisphosphonates; severe bleeding disorder; multiple medical comorbidities. **Defer when:** uncontrolled HTN >180/110; recent MI/stroke within 6 months; INR >3.5 on warfarin; active acute infection (treat with antibiotics first, extract when controlled). **Document the consult in the chart:** name of physician, date, recommendation. The consult note is your medicolegal protection.

chapters.title: Post-op instructions - bleeding, swelling, dry-socket prevention
chapters.verdict: Post-op instructions are 80% of the long-term success of an extraction. Patients who follow the rules heal in 7-10 days; those who don't develop dry socket, prolonged bleeding, or wound dehiscence. Write instructions down, demonstrate technique, ask the patient to repeat the key three (no spitting, no smoking, no straws for 24 hr).

blocks.text: Effective post-op instructions are concrete, time-bounded, and action-focused. 'Take care of the area' is vague and easily forgotten; 'Bite firmly on gauze for 30 minutes; do not check the gauze every 5 minutes' is specific. Patients vary in compliance - studies suggest 30-40% don't follow even basic post-extraction directions. Set them up for success with written instructions, demonstrated technique, and a clear escalation path.

items: **Pressure pack with gauze for 30 minutes.** Patient bites firmly on a folded gauze pack. Do not allow them to check the gauze every few minutes - constant lifting disrupts clot formation.

**Inspect the socket at 30 minutes.** A formed clot should be visible. If active bleeding continues, replace gauze, continue pressure another 30 minutes. If still bleeding after 60 minutes, consider gelatin sponge + suture.
**Pre-emptive analgesia.** Give ibuprofen 600-800 mg with the patient in the chair before they leave.
**No spitting, no rinsing.** Active spitting creates negative pressure that dislodges the clot.
**No straws.** Same reason - sucking creates negative pressure.
**No smoking, no vaping.** Nicotine constricts blood vessels and suction motion dislodges the clot. Dry socket risk in smokers is 3-5x non-smokers.
**No alcohol.** Anticoagulant effect prolongs bleeding; ethanol delays healing.
**No carbonated drinks.** Same suction issue.
**Soft diet only.** Yogurt, smoothies (by spoon, not straw), mashed potatoes, scrambled eggs, soup.
**Sleep with head elevated.** 2-3 pillows or a recliner.
**Ibuprofen 600-800 mg scheduled every 6 hours for 48 hours.** Scheduled (clock-based) dosing is more effective than as-needed. Take with food.
**Add acetaminophen 500-650 mg every 6 hours** if ibuprofen alone isn't sufficient. Stagger the doses for round-the-clock coverage.
**Opioids only if absolutely necessary.** Hydrocodone 5/325 or oxycodone 5/325, 1-2 tablets every 6 hours PRN. Limit to 10-15 tablets max.
**Cold compress for first 24 hours.** 20 min on, 20 min off.
**Warm compress starting at 48 hours.** Promotes resolution of swelling.
**Begin gentle saltwater rinses at 24 hours** (1 tsp salt in 8 oz warm water). Tilt the head; let the water roll over the site. 4-6 times per day.
**Resume gentle brushing of all teeth EXCEPT the extraction area.** Day 3-4, gently brush adjacent teeth; day 5-7, all areas.
**Activity:** light activity (walking) day 2; avoid vigorous exercise 5-7 days.
**Diet:** progress soft to semi-soft to normal over 5-7 days.
**Bleeding that soaks a gauze pad in 20 minutes** (after the first hour). Call clinic.
**Severe throbbing pain that started 2-4 days after extraction** - dry socket. Call for same-day or next-day appointment.
**Swelling that worsens after day 3** - infection. Call for evaluation.
**Fever >100.4F.** Call clinic.
**Numbness of lip, tongue, or chin persisting >24 hours after anesthesia wore off** - possible nerve injury.
**Inability to open mouth (trismus) worsening over days 2-4** - possible infection or muscle hematoma.

blocks.title: Common post-op complaints
blocks.body: **'My socket is bleeding again after going home':** apply pressure with a damp folded tea bag (tannins cause clot formation) for 30 minutes. If unresolved, return. **'My pain peaked on day 2-3 with severe throbbing':** classic dry socket. Schedule same-day appointment for socket dressing. **'My swelling is worse on day 4 than day 1':** infection - examine, consider antibiotics. **'I can't fully open my mouth':** common day 2-3 from masseter hematoma. Warm compress + jaw exercises. **'There's a hard bump in the socket':** likely a sequestrum working its way out. Recall at 1 week.

chapters.title: Anesthesia for extractions - block selection, supplemental infiltration
chapters.verdict: Profound anesthesia is the difference between an atraumatic extraction and a wrestling match. Block injections (IA, PSA) anesthetize a quadrant; supplemental infiltrations cover the gaps (long buccal, palatal). Test with sharp probe BEFORE drilling or elevating - touch returns first, sharp last. Cold-test the offending tooth as the final go/no-go.

blocks.text: Anesthesia for extractions follows the same principles as restorative anesthesia but has higher consequences for failure - a partially-anesthetized patient cannot tolerate the pressure and forces of luxation. Plan generously: one extra carpule of anesthetic is cheaper than a patient losing trust mid-procedure.

items: **Anterior teeth (#6-#11) - infiltration approach:** local infiltration in the buccal vestibule over the apex. 1/3 to 1/2 carpule lidocaine 2% with epi 1:100K. Add palatal infiltration (1/4 carpule) for the palatal soft tissues.
**Premolars (#4-#5, #12-#13):** buccal infiltration + palatal infiltration. 1 carpule total.

**Molars (#2-#3, #14-#15) - PSA block + buccal + palatal:** PSA targets the posterior superior alveolar nerve. Inject into the buccal vestibule distal to the 2nd molar, advance the needle 1 cm at a 45 degree angle posteriorly + medially + superiorly. Aspirate (PSA is near the pterygoid plexus - risk of hematoma). 1-2 carpules. Add buccal infiltration over the specific tooth and palatal infiltration on the lingual side.
**Greater palatine block (rarely needed):** for extensive palatal soft tissue work. 1/4 carpule slowly.
**Maxillary block (V2):** if multiple maxillary teeth need extraction. Specialist-level injection - skip in pre-doctoral chairs.

**IA (Inferior Alveolar) block - the workhorse:** patient opens wide. Palpate the coronoid notch with your thumb in the mouth, retromolar fold with your finger behind the ramus. Inject into the pterygomandibular space at the level of the mandibular foramen (about 6-10 mm above the occlusal plane of mandibular molars). Insert needle 2/3 of its length, aspirate, deposit 1 carpule. Wait 5 min for onset.
**Long buccal block:** anesthetizes the long buccal nerve (buccal soft tissues of mandibular molars). 1/4 carpule. ADD this to every IA block for mandibular molar work.

**Mental block:** anesthetizes the mental nerve (buccal mucosa of mandibular premolars and anteriors). Inject into the buccal vestibule between the apices of the 1st and 2nd mandibular premolars. 1/2 carpule.
**Gow-Gates block:** alternative when IA fails. Higher injection site (condylar neck level). Specialist-level.
**Vazirani-Akinosi (closed-mouth) block:** for trismus. Specialist-level.
**Test the soft tissues with a sharp probe.** Run the explorer along the marginal gingiva. Light touch returns first; sharp prick takes longer.
**Cold-test the offending tooth with Endo Ice.** Cold response = pulpal nerves still firing. Wait 2 more minutes; if still cold-positive, supplement.
**Intraligamentary supplementation:** insert a 27G short needle into the PDL space, mesial and distal to the tooth. Inject with pressure. 0.2 mL per site. Onset 30-60 sec; duration 30-60 min.
**Intraseptal supplementation:** insert needle into the interproximal alveolar bone. Deposit 0.2 mL. Useful when IA fails on lower molars.
**Never start the extraction** until both tests are negative.
**Lidocaine 2% with epi 1:100,000:** standard. Onset 2-5 min; duration 60-90 min pulpal, 3-5 hr soft tissue.
**Bupivacaine 0.5% with epi 1:200,000 (Marcaine):** long-acting, for >2-hour cases or post-op pain control. Onset 5-10 min; duration 5-9 hr. NOT for pediatric patients (lip-biting risk).
**Articaine 4% with epi 1:100,000 (Septocaine):** higher diffusion through bone - useful for failed IA on mandibular molars.
**Mepivacaine 3% (Carbocaine, no epi):** epi-free option for cardiac patients. Shorter duration (40 min pulpal).
**Maximum doses (adult, 70 kg):** lidocaine 2% with epi: max ~7 carpules. Bupivacaine 0.5% with epi: max ~10 carpules.

blocks.title: Anesthesia problems
blocks.body: **IA block fails consistently:** accessory innervation from the mylohyoid or long buccal nerve. Supplement with long buccal block + lingual infiltration. If still fails, try Gow-Gates. **Patient gets dizzy/nauseated after injection:** vasovagal reaction or local anesthetic toxicity. Stop, recline, give oxygen, monitor vitals. **Patient develops trismus 1-2 days after IA block:** muscle hematoma. Heat, NSAIDs, gentle jaw exercises. Resolves 7-14 days. **Persistent paresthesia of lip/tongue >24 hr:** lingual nerve or IAN injury. Document, reassure, recall at 4 weeks. **Hematoma at PSA injection site:** cold compress for 24 hr, then warm. Resolves 7-10 days.

chapters.title: Socket management - curettage, irrigation, primary closure decisions
chapters.verdict: The 60 seconds you spend on socket management determines whether the patient heals uneventfully or comes back for dry socket. Inspect for retained tissue + root tips; curette only what needs to come out (don't strip the periosteum); irrigate; compress; suture if indicated.

blocks.text: Socket management is the post-delivery cleanup phase. The tooth is out; now you decide whether the socket is ready to heal as-is, needs curettage, needs grafting (for future implant), or needs primary closure with sutures. Different cases need different management - a routine simple extraction with no granulation tissue may need only compression and a gauze pack; a surgical extraction with extensive bone removal needs irrigation, hemostasis, and primary closure.

items: **Suction the socket** to remove blood and debris. Use a small (#3) Frazier suction tip.

**Inspect with good lighting and loupes if available.** Look for: retained root fragments (especially at the apex), granulation tissue (yellow-tan, looks different from clot), sharp bone edges, perforated buccal/lingual plate, sinus communication (maxillary molars).
**Probe gently with a curette** to feel the contours of the socket. The walls should feel smooth; rough irregularities may indicate retained fragments or sharp bone needing filing.
**Granulation tissue or chronic infection:** scoop out with a Lucas curette or molt curette. Be gentle - over-curettage strips the periosteum and slows healing.

**Retained root tip (<2 mm, in apex):** if accessible, retrieve with a small root tip pick or curette. If embedded deep near vital structures (IAN, sinus), leave it and document - retrieval risk > leaving risk.
**Apical cyst or granuloma:** enucleate with a curette, send for biopsy if appearance is unusual (large >5 mm, has clear fluid, contains hair or solid material).
**Bony sequestrum:** small piece of dead bone visible in the socket. Remove with curette or rongeur.
**Sharp bone edges:** file smooth with a bone file (Sluder or Miller file). Sharp edges create discomfort under sutures and slow healing.
**Use sterile saline** in a 20 mL syringe with a blunt-tip cannula or large-gauge needle.
**Irrigate the socket** thoroughly - the saline carries away any remaining debris.
**Suction the irrigation** to confirm the socket is clean.
**Avoid hydrogen peroxide** - it can drive air into the bone marrow (rare but serious complication). Saline is the standard.

blocks.text: Compress the buccal and lingual plates firmly with finger pressure for 30 seconds. This restores socket morphology that may have expanded during delivery. Particularly important for maxillary extractions where buccal plate expansion can lead to subsequent ridge collapse.

items: **Gauze pressure:** primary hemostasis for most extractions. Patient bites firmly on folded gauze for 30 minutes.

**Gelatin sponge (Gelfoam, Surgicel) ONLY if needed:** persistent bleeding after gauze, anticoagulated patient, or socket preservation for future implant. The sponge provides a scaffold for clot formation.
**Hemostatic agent (HemCon, Surgicel):** for refractory bleeding. Apply to socket, hold pressure 5 minutes.
**Electrocautery (if available):** for capillary bleeding from soft tissue. Spot-cauterize 1-2 mm from the bleeding source.
**Surgical extraction with flap reflection:** primary closure with sutures. 3-0 chromic gut or 4-0 Vicryl, simple interrupted.
**Anticoagulated patient:** cross-suture over a gelatin sponge to retain it in the socket.
**Socket preservation graft for future implant:** suture closed with a slow-resorbing suture (4-0 Vicryl).
**Routine simple extraction:** sutures NOT required. Gauze pressure provides adequate hemostasis.
**Suture removal:** chromic gut resorbs in 7-10 days, Vicryl in 30-60 days. Both are fine to leave alone.

**Sinus communication (maxillary molars):** Valsalva test (patient closes nose, gently blows; air through socket = communication). Small (<2 mm) = primary closure + antibiotics + sinus precautions. Large (>5 mm) = OMFS referral for buccal advancement flap.
**Mandibular molar with paper-thin lingual plate:** lingual plate may have fractured during delivery. If fragment is loose, remove with the tooth. Smooth edges.
**Socket preservation graft (for planned implant 3-6 months later):** xenograft (Bio-Oss) or allograft (Puros) packed into socket, covered with collagen membrane, sutured closed.

blocks.title: Socket management problems
blocks.body: **Can't stop bleeding from a specific spot:** identify the source (cortical bleeder = direct pressure + electrocautery; ooze = gelatin sponge + suture; visible vessel = ligate or cauterize). **Suture won't hold:** tissue is friable or too tightly stretched. Use a larger-diameter suture or place additional sutures. **Patient continues bleeding hours after leaving:** see post-op surgery-ch21 chapter for management. **Socket walls collapse after extraction:** common in maxillary anterior; preserves better with a socket graft + collagen membrane.

parts.label: Non-surgical therapy
parts.tagline: Scaling and root planing - the foundational periodontal procedure that converts Stage II-III periodontitis from progressive disease to stable maintenance.

chapters.title: Scaling & root planing (SRP) - quadrant approach, healing window
chapters.verdict: SRP is the definitive non-surgical perio therapy. The goal isn't just calculus removal - it's calculus removal AND root surface smoothing to a glassy texture under direct tactile feedback. Schedule by quadrant (or half-mouth in cooperative patients) with anesthesia, ultrasonic first for gross debridement, hand instrumentation for definitive root planing, re-evaluation at 4-6 weeks.

blocks.text: Scaling and root planing is the procedural backbone of periodontal therapy. A prophy is supragingival and around the gingival margin; SRP is subgingival, into the periodontal pocket, removing calculus and bacterial biofilm from the root surface and re-establishing a clean substrate for periodontal tissue reattachment. Done well, SRP converts a Stage II-III periodontitis patient into a stable maintenance patient. Done poorly, it leaves residual calculus that perpetuates the disease.

items: **Stage I (gingivitis).** Pocket depths <=3 mm, no radiographic bone loss, bleeding on probing. Treatment is prophy + OHI, NOT SRP.
**Stage II periodontitis.** Pocket depths 4-5 mm, radiographic bone loss 15-33% of root length, clinical attachment loss 1-2 mm. **SRP indicated.**

**Stage III periodontitis.** Pocket depths >=6 mm, radiographic bone loss >33%, clinical attachment loss >=5 mm, tooth loss <=4 due to perio. **SRP first; surgical referral for sites that don't respond.**
**Stage IV periodontitis.** As Stage III plus tooth loss >=5 due to perio, masticatory dysfunction, secondary occlusal trauma. **SRP first; comprehensive treatment planning includes prosthodontics.**
**Coding:** D4341 = SRP, 4+ teeth per quadrant. D4342 = SRP, 1-3 teeth per quadrant. Bill per quadrant treated.

**Full perio charting** - 6-point probing (mesiobuccal, mid-buccal, distobuccal, mesiolingual, mid-lingual, distolingual) for every tooth. Note mobility (0/1/2/3), furcation involvement (I/II/III), recession, attached gingiva width.
**Periapical or vertical bitewing series** to evaluate bone loss pattern. CBCT for advanced or atypical cases.

**Medical history review** - anticoagulants (no contraindication for SRP but bleeding management plan needed), bisphosphonates (no contraindication for non-surgical), uncontrolled diabetes (worse outcomes; coordinate with PCP for HbA1c management).
**Discuss prognosis and time commitment.** SRP requires 2-4 visits, costs vary by insurance, and the patient must commit to 3-month maintenance afterward indefinitely.

**Schedule by quadrant or half-mouth.** Most UIC predoctoral cases: Mx in one visit, Mn in next (or one half-mouth per visit). Full-mouth disinfection (all four quadrants in 24 hours) has slightly better outcomes but requires staff support most predoctoral clinics don't have.
**Anesthesia:** lidocaine 2% w/ epi 1:100K (standard), bupivacaine 0.5% w/ epi 1:200K (long-acting for full-mouth SRP).
**Ultrasonic:** Cavitron (magnetostrictive) with 25K or 30K insert. Universal tip for supra + early subgingival; slimline for deep pockets.
**Hand instruments:** Gracey curettes - 1/2 (anteriors), 11/12 (mesial posterior), 13/14 (distal posterior). Universal scaler for supragingival. Files (Hirschfeld or Orban) for tenacious calculus.
**Mouth rinse:** chlorhexidine 0.12% for pre-procedural rinse (reduces bacterial load).
**Suction:** high-volume + Isodry if needed for moisture control.
**Visualization:** loupes (2.5x or 3.5x) + headlamp essential for sub-gingival work.

blocks.text: Step 1 - Pre-rinse + anesthesia (5-10 min per quadrant)

items: **Patient rinses with CHX 0.12%** for 30 seconds before anesthesia. Reduces bacterial aerosol from ultrasonic.

**Anesthetize the quadrant.** Mandibular: IA block + buccal infiltration + long buccal. Maxillary: posterior infiltration + mid + anterior infiltrations + palatal infiltration if treating palatal pockets.

**Verify profound anesthesia** by gently probing a 5+ mm pocket. If the patient feels sharp pain, supplement. Subgingival instrumentation without profound anesthesia destroys patient trust and willingness to return for maintenance.

blocks.text: Step 2 - Ultrasonic gross debridement (15-20 min per quadrant)

items: **Set Cavitron to medium power** with copious water flow. Lower power for thin areas (cervical of mandibular anteriors); higher for tenacious calculus.
**Use a light, feather-touch grip.** Pencil grip with finger rest on adjacent teeth. The ultrasonic does the work - pressing hard damages root surface and reduces tip efficiency.
**Move the tip in overlapping strokes** - coronal-apical, mesial-distal. Adapt the active sides to the root surface. The end of the tip is the most active; the lateral sides are less.
**Sub-gingivally:** slimline insert with the tip 1-2 mm short of pocket base. Long, sweeping strokes parallel to the root surface.

**Time-box this step.** 15-20 min ultrasonic per quadrant is enough for gross removal. Don't try to finish the case with ultrasonic alone - hand instruments are required for the smooth-glass endpoint.

blocks.text: Step 3 - Hand instrumentation (20-30 min per quadrant)

Hand instruments are how you confirm the calculus is gone and the root surface is planed smooth. The endpoint is tactile: an explorer (or curette tip) moved along the root surface feels like polished glass. Anything less - gritty texture, sticky catch, irregularity - means residual calculus or unplaned cementum.

items: **Sequence by site.** Start at the most distal tooth of the quadrant, work mesially. Within each tooth, treat each surface: mesial -> buccal -> distal -> lingual.

**Choose Gracey by site.** 1/2 = anteriors (canines and incisors, both surfaces). 11/12 = posterior mesial surfaces (lower number = mesial). 13/14 = posterior distal surfaces. The curettes are double-ended; pick the working end that adapts to the root contour.

**Adapt the working end** with the lower 1/3 of the cutting edge against the root. Working end blade angled 60-80 deg to the root surface - too closed = burnishes calculus; too open = gouges cementum.

**Stroke pattern:** firm fingers rest on adjacent teeth, pivot on the rest, vertical or oblique strokes coronal-to-apical. Overlap each stroke by 1-2 mm. Continue until the tactile endpoint is reached.
**Verify endpoint with an explorer (11-12 or shepherd's hook).** Run the tip along the root surface. Glass-smooth = done. Any catch = re-instrument.

**For furcation areas:** use a furcation file or Nabers probe to detect, then small curettes (mini-five Graceys) to access. Class II and III furcations may need surgical referral for definitive treatment.
**Irrigate each pocket** with CHX 0.12% or sterile saline using a blunt-tip syringe. Removes loosened debris.

**Patient counseling:** sensitivity to cold is expected for 2-4 weeks (cementum is now exposed; remineralization takes time). Recommend desensitizing toothpaste (Sensodyne, Colgate Sensitive) or fluoride varnish at this visit.

**OHI focus on technique.** Specific to the pocket areas treated. Brushing alone won't keep these areas clean - interdental brushes (Tepe, Curaprox) sized to the embrasure, water flosser for deeper pockets, dental floss with technique.
**Schedule next quadrant** at 1-2 weeks for the typical 2-visit SRP. Full-mouth disinfection schedule has all quadrants within 24 hours.
**Bill D4341 or D4342** per quadrant. Note clinical findings in the chart for re-evaluation reference.
**Re-chart pocket depths** at every site previously treated. Expect 1-2 mm reduction in most sites, more in deeper pockets.
**Bleeding on probing should decrease substantially.** Persistent BOP at >50% of sites = inadequate biofilm control by patient OR inadequate instrumentation.

**Sites with residual 5+ mm pockets:** consider re-SRP of that site (bill D4341/D4342 per quadrant if 4+ sites in quadrant; D4910 if maintaining), local-delivery antibiotics (Arestin), or surgical referral.
**Schedule maintenance interval** at 3-4 months. Document the new maintenance interval prominently in the chart so future providers know.
**Annual re-evaluation:** full perio re-chart every 12 months to detect progression and adjust treatment intensity.

blocks.title: Common SRP challenges
blocks.body: **Tenacious calculus that won't yield to ultrasonic or curette:** try a Hirschfeld or Orban file - heavier instruments designed for chunks. If still not budging, consider mineralized calculus that has fused with cementum; finer hand instrument work + multiple strokes. **Patient becoming uncomfortable mid-quadrant despite anesthesia:** check for inadequate anesthesia (re-test), consider whether the patient has anxiety-driven sensitivity (nitrous oxide may help), or whether you're treating a tooth with separate pulpal pathology (irreversible pulpitis from undetected caries). **Bleeding obscuring the field:** rinse with CHX, suction, light pressure on the area for 30 sec, resume.
