Excessive bleeding may indicate underlying systemic issue (uncontrolled hypertension, anticoagulant) - recheck history. **Patient won't return for maintenance:** the 3-month maintenance interval is non-negotiable for SRP outcomes. If the patient refuses, document the discussion and accept they're choosing to risk recurrence. **Pockets get deeper at re-eval despite SRP:** consider missed sites, residual calculus, occlusal trauma, host factors (smoking, diabetes), or non-plaque-induced disease (drug-induced gingival hyperplasia, aggressive periodontitis). Refer for perio specialist evaluation.

chapters.title: Perio charting - 6-point probe, mobility, furcation, recession
chapters.verdict: Perio charting is the diagnostic core of every periodontal patient encounter. Six-point probing on every tooth, plus mobility, furcation, recession, and bleeding. Done correctly, it takes 8-12 minutes for a full mouth. Done poorly, the treatment plan that follows is built on bad data.

blocks.text: A perio chart is six numbers per tooth (pocket depths) plus annotations for bleeding, mobility, furcation involvement, recession, and clinical attachment loss. Over time, serial charts show whether periodontitis is progressing, stable, or regressing under therapy.

items: **Periodontal probe:** UNC-15 (standard, marked in 1 mm increments to 15 mm) or Williams probe (1-2-3-5-7-8-9-10 mm markings).
**Furcation probe (Nabers):** curved probe specifically for furcation evaluation.
**Articulating paper** for mobility assessment.
**Light source + magnification:** loupes (2.5x or 3.5x) + chair light.
**Position the probe** parallel to the long axis of the tooth.
**Insert with light pressure (10-20 g)** - if the probe creates blanching of the marginal gingiva, you're pressing too hard.
**Walk the probe** along the gingival sulcus, stepping in 1 mm increments. The deepest reading wins for each surface.
**Six surfaces per tooth:** mesiobuccal, mid-buccal, distobuccal, mesiolingual, mid-lingual, distolingual.
**Read the depth at the gingival margin** - the millimeter mark where the probe disappears under the gingival edge.

**Record as you go, by arch and side.** UIC workflow: sweep all buccal surfaces across the maxillary arch first, then all maxillary lingual, then repeat on the mandibular. Walking by surface side (not tooth-by-tooth through all 6 surfaces) keeps probe angle and body position constant and reduces transcription errors.

blocks.text: What to chart on every tooth

items: **Pocket depths (6 per tooth).**
**Bleeding on probing (BOP):** mark each surface as bleeding within 30 sec of probing. >25% = active disease.
**Recession:** distance from the CEJ to the gingival margin. Positive value if margin is apical to CEJ.
**Clinical attachment loss (CAL):** CAL = pocket depth + recession. The 'true' measure of periodontal destruction.
**Mobility (Miller):** 0 = no mobility. 1 = horizontal <1 mm. 2 = horizontal >1 mm. 3 = horizontal + vertical (depressible).
**Furcation involvement (Glickman):** I = horizontal probe enters 1-3 mm. II = >3 mm but doesn't penetrate. III = through-and-through but soft tissue covers. IV = through-and-through, visible.
**Mucogingival defects:** keratinized gingiva width <2 mm or no attached gingiva - note these areas.
**Gingival biotype:** thin or thick.
**Probing depths <=3 mm, no bleeding:** healthy.
**Probing depths <=3 mm, with bleeding:** gingivitis. Prophy + improved OHI.
**Probing depths 4-5 mm with bleeding + bone loss:** Stage II periodontitis. SRP.
**Probing depths >=6 mm with bleeding + significant bone loss + CAL >=5 mm:** Stage III. SRP first, surgical referral for sites that don't respond.
**Probing depths >=6 mm + tooth loss >=5 due to perio + masticatory dysfunction:** Stage IV. Comprehensive treatment planning.
**Bleeding on probing >25% of sites:** active disease.

blocks.title: Charting challenges
blocks.body: **Patient gags during posterior probing:** topical anesthetic (HurriCaine spray) before probing distal molars. Position slightly upright. **Bleeding obscures the reading:** rinse with water, blot with gauze, re-probe. **Patient complains of probing pain:** significant inflammation. Take a break, reassure, gentle re-probe. If still painful, consider 1/2 carpule lidocaine infiltration before completing that quadrant. **Probe sticks at a certain depth:** probably calculus or restoration overhang. Note the depth and the obstacle. **Patient on anticoagulants:** probe gently, expect more bleeding. Document.

chapters.title: Diagnosis - AAP stages (I-IV) and grades (A-C)
chapters.verdict: The 2018 AAP classification uses a stage (I-IV, severity + complexity) + grade (A-C, progression rate) system. Stage tells you how bad it is now; grade tells you how fast it's getting worse. Together they drive treatment intensity.

blocks.text: Periodontal diagnosis since 2018 follows the AAP/EFP joint classification: every periodontitis patient gets a stage (I, II, III, IV) and a grade (A, B, C). Stage describes severity and complexity of current disease, grade describes rate of progression.
First sort - is this gingivitis or periodontitis?

items: **Periodontal health:** intact periodontium, no BOP, normal probing depths (<=3 mm), no bone loss.
**Gingivitis:** BOP present, probing depths usually <=3 mm, NO interdental clinical attachment loss, NO radiographic bone loss. Reversible.
**Periodontitis:** clinical attachment loss (CAL) >=1 mm at >=2 non-adjacent teeth, OR >=3 mm CAL at buccal/oral aspect of >=2 teeth. Not reversible.
**Stage I (initial):** CAL 1-2 mm, radiographic bone loss <15%, no tooth loss due to perio, probing depths <=4 mm. Treatment: SRP usually adequate.
**Stage II (moderate):** CAL 3-4 mm, radiographic bone loss 15-33%, probing depths <=5 mm. Treatment: SRP, possibly with adjunctive antimicrobials.

**Stage III (severe, potential for additional loss):** CAL >=5 mm, radiographic bone loss >33% to middle/apical third, tooth loss due to perio <=4 teeth, probing depths >=6 mm. Furcation involvement Class II or III. Treatment: SRP first; surgical referral for non-responsive sites.

**Stage IV (severe, potential for tooth loss):** all Stage III criteria PLUS at least one of: >=5 missing teeth due to perio, masticatory dysfunction, secondary occlusal trauma, severe ridge defects, <20 remaining teeth. Treatment: comprehensive multi-disciplinary.
**Grade A (slow):** no progression over 5 years; %bone loss / age <0.25.
**Grade B (moderate):** <2 mm bone loss over 5 years; %bone loss / age 0.25-1.0.
**Grade C (rapid):** >=2 mm bone loss over 5 years; %bone loss / age >1.0; aggressive risk factors.
**Smoking:** non-smoker = no modifier. <10/day = Grade B. >=10/day = Grade C.
**Diabetes:** non-diabetic = no modifier. HbA1c <7.0 = Grade B. HbA1c >=7.0 = Grade C.
**Generalized Stage II Grade B periodontitis** - 40% of teeth have CAL 3-4 mm, BL 25%, smoker <10/day.
**Localized Stage III Grade C periodontitis** - 5 teeth have CAL 6-7 mm with vertical bone defects, HbA1c 8.2.
**Generalized gingivitis on intact periodontium** - BOP at >25% of sites, no CAL, no bone loss.
**Stage I/II Grade A-B:** SRP + 6-month maintenance. OHI focus.
**Stage I/II Grade C:** SRP + 3-4 month maintenance + adjunctive antimicrobial. Aggressive risk factor management.
**Stage III Grade A-B:** SRP + 3-4 month maintenance; surgical referral for non-responsive 6+ mm sites.
**Stage III Grade C:** SRP + 3 month maintenance + adjunctive antimicrobials + risk factor management + surgical referral.
**Stage IV:** comprehensive treatment planning (perio + prosth + restorative). 3 month maintenance indefinitely.

blocks.title: Diagnostic edge cases
blocks.body: **Patient has good current chart but historical records show significant prior bone loss:** stage by current findings; consider stable disease (Grade A) if no progression in recent records. **Patient has primary occlusal trauma (no perio inflammation):** this is NOT periodontitis. Treatment is occlusal management. **Patient has gingival recession without periodontitis:** mucogingival defect, not periodontitis. Consider gingival graft. **Pregnancy gingivitis or medication-induced gingival overgrowth:** distinct entities.

chapters.title: Local anesthesia for perio - block + supraperiosteal infiltration choices
chapters.verdict: Subgingival instrumentation needs anesthesia. Block for the molars (IA, PSA), infiltration for the anteriors and palatal. Bupivacaine for full-mouth SRP (long-acting), lidocaine for single-quadrant.

blocks.text: Periodontal anesthesia mirrors restorative anesthesia in principles but covers a quadrant of teeth at once. The key is bilateral coverage - both buccal AND palatal/lingual surfaces need to be numb because instrumentation goes around the entire tooth circumference.

items: **Posterior infiltration** at the PSA region for #2-#3 molars: 1 carpule lidocaine 2% with epi 1:100K, slowly aspirated. Provides 60-90 min of pulpal + soft tissue coverage.
**Mid-buccal infiltration** at #4-#7 premolars and canines: 1/2 carpule per side.
**Anterior buccal infiltration** at #8-#11: 1/2 carpule total.
**Palatal infiltration** is required for palatal pocket instrumentation: 1/4 carpule at the greater palatine foramen + 1/4 carpule at the incisive papilla.
**Total maxillary quadrant:** typically 2.5-3 carpules.
**IA block:** 1 carpule. Provides coverage for #17-#21 (left) or #28-#32 (right) on the lingual.
**Long buccal block:** 1/4 carpule. Provides coverage for the buccal mucosa of the molars.
**Buccal infiltration** at #19-#20 premolars (some patients): 1/4 carpule.
**Mental block** (if treating only premolars and anteriors): 1/2 carpule.
**Total mandibular quadrant:** typically 1.5-2 carpules.
**Bupivacaine 0.5% with epi 1:200,000 (Marcaine):** preferred for full-mouth SRP appointments where you need anesthesia to last >2 hours. Onset 5-10 min, duration 5-9 hours.
**Lidocaine 2% with epi 1:100,000:** standard for single-quadrant SRP. Onset 2-5 min, duration 60-90 min pulpal + 3-5 hours soft tissue.
**Mixed protocol:** start with lidocaine for fast onset, supplement with bupivacaine if the appointment runs long.

blocks.text: Probe with a periodontal probe at the deepest pocket in the quadrant. If the patient feels pressure but not sharp pain, anesthesia is adequate for instrumentation. If they feel sharp pain, give more anesthetic - subgingival instrumentation on an inflamed pocket without adequate anesthesia is excruciating and destroys patient trust.

blocks.title: Perio anesthesia problems
blocks.body: **Patient feels pain during palatal instrumentation despite block:** palatal mucosa wasn't infiltrated. Add palatal infiltration immediately. **Anesthesia wears off mid-quadrant:** common with lidocaine on long cases. Supplement with another 1/2 carpule or switch to bupivacaine. **Patient anxious about needles:** topical first (HurriCaine 20% benzocaine for 1 min), then slow injection with distraction. Computer-controlled syringe (The Wand) is gentler if available. **Mandibular block fails:** consider intraseptal supplementation at the affected tooth (deposits anesthetic directly into bone).

chapters.title: Periodontal maintenance - every 3-4 months after SRP, what to do
chapters.verdict: Perio maintenance is the indefinite recall interval after SRP. The procedure is between a prophy and a re-SRP: full-mouth ultrasonic + hand-instrumentation, with anesthesia for any quadrants that re-developed pockets. The clinical task is identifying which quadrants need re-SRP-level instrumentation vs which are stable enough for surface debridement. Re-charting every 12 months catches progression early.

blocks.text: Periodontal maintenance (D4910) is the quarterly procedure after SRP that prevents recurrence. Studies show recurrence rates of 50-70% at 5 years without maintenance vs 10-15% with consistent 3-4 month maintenance. The 3-month interval matters: biofilm matures and becomes pathogenic again within 3 months of professional cleaning.

items: **Review the patient's last perio chart** before they arrive. Note: deepest pockets, sites with prior bleeding, mobility, furcation involvement.
**Check the prior maintenance note** - any new symptoms or complaints?
**Confirm medical history changes** - new medications (especially anticoagulants), new diagnoses (diabetes, immunosuppression).
**Patient interview (5 min):** any new sensitivity, bleeding, swelling? Brushing technique? Interproximal aids being used?
**Re-chart pocket depths.** At minimum, spot-check 3-4 sites per quadrant. Annual full re-chart (every 12 months) is the gold standard.
**Assess bleeding on probing (BOP).** Sites that bleed at maintenance = unstable. Note them for instrumentation focus.
**Plaque assessment.** Disclosing solution if needed to identify areas the patient is missing.
**Ultrasonic gross debridement** of all teeth - supragingival + early subgingival. 10-15 minutes for full mouth.
**Hand instrumentation** focused on sites with bleeding or increased pocket depth. Use Gracey curettes as in SRP technique.
**Polish** with prophy paste or air-polish (depending on what's been most effective for the patient).
**OHI reinforcement.** Demonstrate technique on areas the patient is missing.
**Schedule next maintenance** at 3-4 months. Document the interval in the chart.
**Stable quadrant** (no BOP, no pocket increase, no new clinical attachment loss): surface ultrasonic + hand instruments above the gingival margin. Patient may not need anesthesia.
**Unstable quadrant** (BOP, pocket increase >=1 mm, new mobility): treat as a localized re-SRP. Anesthetize the quadrant. Bill as D4341/D4342 instead of D4910 if 4+ sites in quadrant are involved.
**Refractory pocket** (5+ mm pocket that wasn't responsive to original SRP): consider local-delivery antibiotic (Arestin) or referral to perio specialist.
**OHI reinforcement** - patient demonstrate technique. Watch them brush. Most patients drift from the technique they learned at SRP.
**Interdental aid review** - floss vs interdental brushes vs water flosser. The right tool depends on embrasure size.
**Smoking cessation** - smokers have 3x recurrence rate. Refer to smoking cessation services.
**Diabetes control** - poorly-controlled diabetes (HbA1c >7) worsens outcomes. Encourage patient to discuss A1c management with PCP.
**Recall interval discussion** - explain why 3 months matters. Patients who try to extend to 6 months often see recurrence.
**Code D4910** for routine perio maintenance.
**Code D4341/D4342** instead if 4+ sites in a quadrant got SRP-level instrumentation.
**Code D1330** if OHI was provided.
**Note any sites with new BOP or pocket increase** for next visit's focus.
**Photo or PA if any concerning changes** were noted clinically or radiographically.

blocks.title: Maintenance challenges
blocks.body: **Patient skipped 6+ months and pockets re-developed:** treat as a re-SRP, not maintenance. Bill accordingly. Counsel about the importance of consistent recall. **Pocket depths are getting deeper despite consistent maintenance:** evaluate for: smoking (counsel cessation), diabetes (poor control), occlusal trauma, refractory bacterial flora. Consider perio specialist referral. **Patient resistant to 3-month interval ('too expensive', 'too often'):** explain the long-term cost of tooth loss vs maintenance. Some insurance covers maintenance with no out-of-pocket. **New sensitivity at recession sites:** desensitizing toothpaste (Sensodyne, Colgate Sensitive Pro-Relief), fluoride varnish at this visit. **Patient develops new disease in a previously stable mouth:** assess for systemic risk factor change (new medication causing xerostomia, new diabetes diagnosis, etc.).

chapters.title: Behavior management - tell-show-do, voice control, parental presence
chapters.verdict: Behavior management is the single most important skill in pediatric dentistry. A cooperative child finishes treatment in one visit; an uncooperative child needs three visits or sedation. The toolkit is mostly verbal and visual: tell-show-do for every instrument, voice modulation for confidence, parental presence for young children (parent OUT for school-age).

blocks.text: Children come to the dentist with predictable patterns of anxiety. Pre-school children (3-6) fear separation from parents more than the dental instruments themselves. School-age children (6-12) fear the unknown and fear judgment from peers. Adolescents have adult-level concerns about pain and aesthetics. Behavior management strategy must match developmental stage.
Frankl Behavior Rating Scale - assess and document

items: **Frankl 1 (definitely negative):** refuses treatment, cries forcefully, fearful, extreme negativism, withdrawn isolation.
**Frankl 2 (negative):** reluctance to accept treatment, uncooperative, some evidence of negative attitude (sullen, withdrawn).
**Frankl 3 (positive):** acceptance of treatment, cautious behavior at times, willingness to comply with reservation but follows directions cooperatively.
**Frankl 4 (definitely positive):** good rapport, interested in dental procedures, laughing, enjoying the situation.
**TELL:** describe what you're about to do in age-appropriate language. Use euphemisms: 'sleepy juice' for anesthesia, 'tooth cleaner' for slow-speed handpiece, 'tooth-counter' for explorer.
**SHOW:** demonstrate the instrument on a non-threatening surface first. Show the mirror by reflecting their face; show the prophy cup by spinning it in your gloved finger.
**DO:** perform the action in the smallest unit possible. Start with the safest instrument (mirror, explorer) before noisier ones (handpiece). Praise after each successful step.
**REPEAT FOR EVERY NEW INSTRUMENT.**
**Tone:** calm, confident, slightly louder than the child when getting attention.
**Pace:** slow speech when the child is anxious; normal speech when settled.
**Praise:** specific and immediate. 'You held really still - that helps me see better' beats 'Good job.'
**No false promises:** never say 'it won't hurt' or 'this is the last one.' When that promise breaks, you lose trust for the rest of the visit.

blocks.text: Parental presence - the rule of thumb

items: **Under age 6:** parent in the operatory, ideally sitting in the chair next to the child.
**Age 6-12:** parent option. School-age children often perform better with parent OUT. Discuss with parent and child.
**Age 13+:** parent in waiting room. Adolescents need autonomy.
**Always allow parents to stay if they insist.**
**Parents in the operatory have rules:** (1) don't interrupt the dentist mid-procedure, (2) reassure when prompted, (3) don't promise rewards mid-procedure, (4) silent presence is usually best.
**Visual:** ceiling-mounted TV showing cartoons, tablet videos.
**Auditory:** music (the child's choice), audiobooks.
**Imagery / storytelling:** 'Let's pretend we're on a spaceship - this is the rocket' (the suction).
**Counting / praise reinforcement:** 'I'm going to count to 5 and then we'll be done with this part.'

blocks.text: Escalation - when basic techniques aren't enough

items: **Reschedule.** Better to end on a positive note than push through and traumatize them.
**Nitrous oxide:** for mild-moderate anxiety. Effective in children who can keep a nasal mask on.
**Oral sedation (midazolam or hydroxyzine):** for moderate anxiety. Requires pediatric dentistry training.
**IV sedation or general anesthesia:** for severe anxiety, extensive treatment, or pre-cooperative children (under 3).
**Protective stabilization (papoose board):** last resort for emergency procedures. Requires written informed consent.

blocks.title: Behavior management problems
blocks.body: **Child crying inconsolably:** stop, give the child a break, allow them to sit up. Praise any cooperation. Re-engage with a simple non-threatening task. **Child refuses to open mouth:** don't pry. Ask, model with your own mouth, count to 3, ask again. If still refusing, consider whether the procedure can be deferred or nitrous is indicated. **Child has a behavioral diagnosis (autism, ADHD, anxiety):** consult parent on what works at home. Some children with autism need consistent routine; others need extra processing time. Avoid bright lights. Schedule at the child's best time of day. **Parent argues with dentist in front of child:** the child takes their cue from the parent's distress. Step outside to have any difficult conversations. **Sibling is also being seen and influencing behavior:** schedule siblings separately.

chapters.title: Local anesthesia in children - dose limits (lidocaine 4.4 mg/kg), needle phobia
chapters.verdict: Pediatric local anesthesia has different rules than adult anesthesia. Weight-based dosing (lidocaine max 4.4 mg/kg of body weight), shorter-acting agents preferred (no Marcaine), and a topical-first injection sequence to minimize needle awareness. Most pediatric anesthesia failures come from rushing the injection or under-anesthetizing the soft tissues.

blocks.text: Children metabolize local anesthetics faster than adults relative to body mass, but they have less total volume of distribution. The result: dose limits scale with weight, not age. A 20 kg child can safely receive lidocaine 88 mg (half a carpule); a 40 kg child can receive 176 mg (a full carpule). Exceed these and toxicity (seizures, cardiac arrest) becomes a real risk.

items: **Lidocaine 2% with epi 1:100K:** max 4.4 mg/kg. Each carpule = 36 mg lidocaine. So max carpules = (weight in kg * 4.4) / 36. For a 20 kg child = 2.4 carpules; for a 30 kg child = 3.7 carpules.
**Articaine 4% with epi 1:100K:** max 7 mg/kg in children (slightly different threshold). Each carpule = 68 mg.
**Mepivacaine 3% (no epi):** max 4.4 mg/kg. Each carpule = 54 mg. Used for short procedures.
**Bupivacaine (Marcaine):** NOT recommended in children. Prolonged anesthesia (5-9 hours) means children bite their lips, tongue, or cheeks before sensation returns - causing lip-biting trauma.
**Round DOWN to be safe.** A 22 kg child should not receive more than 2 carpules of lidocaine, even though the calculation gives 2.7.
**Weigh the child** (scale by front door at UIC) before any anesthesia decision.
**Calculate max dose** in mg and convert to carpules. Write it on the chart.
**Apply topical (20% benzocaine, HurriCaine):** thin layer at the injection site for 1-2 minutes. Cover with cotton roll if the child resists having the gel on the gums.
**Position the child:** semi-supine, head turned slightly away from the operator. Some children prefer parent in operatory for the injection; others prefer parent in waiting room. Ask.
**Use a 27G short needle** for most pediatric injections. Smaller-diameter needle = less initial pain.
**Distract during injection:** talk to the child about something unrelated (their school, pet, favorite TV show). Some children prefer to look at a fixed point in the ceiling.
**Insert needle slowly** with gentle pressure on the cheek to distend the tissue. Slow insertion is the single most effective way to minimize pain.
**Deposit 1-2 drops** before advancing fully - anesthetizes the injection track.
**Advance to target depth** while continuing to deposit small drops.
**Aspirate** before main injection (children's blood vessels are closer to the surface; aspiration is critical to avoid intravascular injection).
**Deposit slowly** - 1 mL over 60 seconds. Fast injection is the #1 cause of pediatric anesthesia complaint.
**Withdraw needle quickly** at the end.
**Apply gentle pressure** at the site for 30 seconds with a cotton roll. Reduces hematoma risk.

blocks.text: Special considerations - mandibular block in children

items: **Mandibular foramen is HIGHER in children** than adults relative to the occlusal plane (because the mandible hasn't grown to full adult size). Inject at the level of the occlusal plane (NOT above it) for IA block in children 6-10.
**Children's IA blocks have higher failure rate** than adults - accessory innervation is more common. Supplement with buccal infiltration over the affected tooth.

**Articaine infiltration alternative:** for single-tooth restorative work in mandibular primary molars, articaine infiltration on the buccal alone is often sufficient (better bone penetration than lidocaine). Avoids the IA block discomfort entirely.

blocks.text: Children, especially under age 6, often don't recognize numbness as numbness - they think their lip 'feels weird' and want to chew or pick at it. This causes lip-biting trauma in 5-10% of pediatric IA blocks. Counsel both child and parent before discharge: 'Your lip will feel puffy and weird for the next 2-3 hours. Don't bite or pick at it - that can hurt you. Eat soft foods, drink with a straw if possible, until the numbness goes away.'

blocks.title: Pediatric anesthesia problems
blocks.body: **Child cries during injection:** stop, hold pressure on the site, reassure. Don't restart immediately - wait 30 seconds, ask the child if they're ready. Most children tolerate the injection better if they have a sense of agency. **Anesthesia incomplete after IA block:** common in children. Supplement with buccal infiltration over the affected tooth (articaine works well here). **Lip-biting after appointment:** apply petroleum jelly to the lip immediately to prevent further damage. Heals in 5-7 days. Counsel parent about supervision until numbness wears off. **Child reports 'tingling' or 'spider crawling' sensation:** likely transient paresthesia from needle trauma. Document, reassure, recall in 2 weeks for vitality check. **Vasovagal reaction (pale, sweaty, dizzy):** lay child flat, raise legs, monitor pulse. Resolves in 5-10 minutes. Common in adolescents who haven't eaten before the appointment.

chapters.title: Stainless steel crown (SSC) - sizing, festooning, crimping, cementation
chapters.verdict: SSCs are the workhorse pediatric crown - preformed, full-coverage, glass-ionomer-cemented, ~5-7 year service life matching primary molar lifespan. Sizing is the critical decision: too small won't seat, too large won't engage contacts. Conventional preparation involves occlusal + interproximal + axial reduction; Hall technique skips prep but isn't taught at UIC. Plan 60 min for a single SSC with pulpotomy.

blocks.text: Stainless steel crowns are the pediatric dentistry equivalent of an adult crown but with the speed and durability appropriate to a tooth that will exfoliate in 2-7 years. The preformed crown is engineered to fit standard primary molar anatomy; chairside customization (festooning + crimping) adapts it to the patient's specific tooth. Cementation with glass ionomer creates a fluoride-releasing reservoir that helps prevent recurrent caries.

items: Multi-surface caries on a primary molar (MOD).
Post-pulpotomy primary molar.
Broken cusp on a primary molar.
Child with high caries risk needing durable restoration.
Tooth needs to maintain space for an erupting permanent successor.
**SSC kit:** sizes 2-7 for mandibular (D, DLM, E, EUL, F for maxillary; or by tooth number). UIC kits provide both sides + both arches.
**Boley gauge:** measures mesio-distal width of the tooth to determine size.
**Crown shears or scissors:** for trimming the cervical margin.
**Crown-crimping pliers (Howe utility pliers or specific crimping pliers):** for crimping the cervical band inward.
**Glass ionomer cement:** Fuji I (powder + liquid, hand-mix) or Ketac Cem (auto-mix capsule).
**Floss:** for gel-stage cement cleanup.
**Articulating paper:** for occlusal verification.
**Anesthetize** (see pedo-ch2 for dosing).
**Rubber dam or Isodry isolation** with cotton roll backup.
**Occlusal reduction 1.5 mm** with a tapered diamond. Match the original cuspal anatomy approximately - don't flatten the table.

**Interproximal slice (mesial and distal)** with a thin tapered diamond. Break the contact without nicking the adjacent tooth. The interproximal preparation creates a featheredge margin (no chamfer or shoulder needed for SSCs).
**Axial reduction (buccal and lingual)** to a featheredge with the same tapered diamond. Goal: vertical axial walls that allow the crown to seat without binding.
**Remove all caries** with round bur or excavator. If pulpotomy was performed before crown placement, this is already complete.
**Refine the margin** to a clean featheredge at gingival level.
**Measure the mesio-distal width** of the tooth with a Boley gauge. Match to the corresponding SSC size from the kit.
**Try the crown in** - it should seat to the gingival margin with light pressure. The cervical margin should fit snugly against the cervical featheredge without rocking.
**Try a different size if needed:** too small won't fully seat; too large allows rocking. Side-to-side play indicates oversized; resistance during seating indicates undersized.
**Check contacts** with adjacent teeth - should be tight but not separating them.
**Mark the gingival margin** with a pencil on the crown - this is where you'll trim.
**Trim the cervical margin** with crown shears, following the pencil line. Goal: the crown extends just barely subgingival (0.5-1 mm) without overextension into the sulcus.
**Smooth the trimmed edge** with a coarse diamond bur or rotary disk. A burr that scrapes the gingival margin causes irritation.
**Crimp the cervical band** inward with crown-crimping pliers. Crimp gradually around the entire circumference - this engages the cervical undercut of the tooth and creates retention.
**Re-try in.** Should now snap into place with audible/tactile click. Re-trim or re-crimp if needed.
**Dry the prepared tooth** with cotton roll. Don't desiccate (dehydration of dentin reduces glass ionomer bond).
**Mix glass ionomer cement** per manufacturer instructions. Fuji I: 1 scoop powder + 1 drop liquid, mix to creamy consistency.
**Apply cement to the intaglio surface** of the crown. Coat all interior surfaces; don't overfill (excess gets squeezed out during seating).
**Seat the crown** with firm finger pressure or have the child bite gently into MI position. Wait 3-4 min until cement reaches gel stage.
**Gel-stage cleanup (3-4 min after seating):** remove excess cement at margins with explorer + floss. Easier than chipping fully-set cement.
**Wait full set** (typically 6-8 min from mix).
**Final cleanup** with explorer + floss. Polish any rough cement at margins.
**Have the child bite into MI** with articulating paper.
**Check for high spots** - the SSC should not be the only contact (no Class II torquing of the abutment tooth).
**Adjust** with a diamond bur if needed. Primary molars don't need precise occlusal contacts; rough function is acceptable.
**Check lateral excursions** - the SSC should disclude in lateral excursions (no balancing interference).
**Tell child + parent:** the silver tooth may feel weird for a day or two. The child should resume normal eating and brushing.
**Brushing instruction:** brush the SSC like any other tooth. Use child-strength fluoride toothpaste.
**Diet:** no special restrictions after cementation. Avoid hard candies and ice that could fracture the cement.
**Recall:** 6 months for routine recall + SSC integrity check.

blocks.title: SSC problems
blocks.body: **Crown won't fully seat after trimming:** check for: residual caries on the tooth, prep walls converging too much (not parallel), inadequate interproximal preparation, crown too small. Address each. **Crown rocks side-to-side:** crown is oversized OR contacts haven't been re-established. Try the next size down, OR crimp the cervical band tighter. **Cement won't set:** mixing ratio off (too much liquid). Re-mix with proper ratio. **Gingival blanching after cementation:** crown over-extended subgingivally OR margin not festooned smooth. Adjust as needed. **Child complains of pain on biting after cementation:** check for occlusal high spots; adjust with diamond bur. Pain should resolve within 24 hours. **Cement washing out at 6-month recall:** glass ionomer can wear over time. If integrity is questionable, re-cement or replace crown.
